cases.title: Worsening Headaches and a Clumsy Right Hand
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of progressively worsening headaches now accompanied by right-hand weakness.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents to the ED with six weeks of dull, holocephalic headaches that are worse in the early morning and occasionally wake him from sleep. Over the past two weeks his wife noticed he drops objects with his right hand and his handwriting has become illegible. This morning he had a brief episode of word-finding difficulty that resolved. He denies fever, neck stiffness, trauma, or visual loss but reports two episodes of vomiting without nausea. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: 30-pack-year smoking history, drinks alcohol socially, works as an accountant. Family history: father with myocardial infarction at 70; no known cancers or neurologic disease. Review of systems is negative for weight loss, night sweats, hemoptysis, or bowel/bladder changes. He has had no prior imaging and takes no anticoagulants. He drove himself to the hospital but reports feeling 'foggy' for the last several days.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented x3 but mildly inattentive. Pupils equal and reactive; no papilledema appreciated on bedside fundoscopy. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. Motor exam shows 4/5 strength in the right upper extremity with a subtle right pronator drift; right grip is weak. Left side and both legs are 5/5. Mild right-hand dysgraphia. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. Sensation is intact. Finger-to-nose is mildly dysmetric on the right. Gait is steady. No neck stiffness, no rash, no carotid bruits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for this patient?
options: Non-contrast head CT followed by MRI brain with and without contrast
Lumbar puncture for opening pressure and CSF analysis
Carotid duplex ultrasound
EEG to evaluate for seizure focus
stages.explanation: A subacute, progressive headache with morning predominance, vomiting, and a focal deficit raises concern for a mass lesion with raised intracranial pressure. A non-contrast CT is fast and rules out hemorrhage, and MRI with and without contrast is the gold standard to characterize an intracranial lesion. LP is contraindicated before imaging when a mass with elevated ICP is suspected, given the risk of herniation. Carotid duplex evaluates large-vessel stenosis but does not explain a progressive focal mass-like presentation. EEG is appropriate for seizures but his presentation is progressive and focal rather than paroxysmal, so imaging takes priority.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: ill-defined hypodensity in the left frontal lobe with surrounding edema and mild mass effect; no hemorrhage.
MRI brain with and without contrast: 4.2 cm irregular, thick-walled ring-enhancing mass centered in the left frontal lobe crossing the genu of the corpus callosum to the right side ('butterfly' configuration).
Central non-enhancing necrosis present.
Extensive surrounding T2/FLAIR hyperintensity (vasogenic edema).
8 mm of left-to-right midline shift.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the imaging, which diagnosis is most likely?
options: Brain abscess
Glioblastoma
Meningioma
Subacute ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: A solitary, irregular, thick-walled ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis that crosses the corpus callosum (butterfly glioma) in an older adult is the classic appearance of glioblastoma. Brain abscess also rings-enhances but typically has a thin, smooth, regular wall, restricted diffusion centrally, and is usually accompanied by fever or a source of infection. Meningiomas are extra-axial, dural-based, and homogeneously enhancing without central necrosis or callosal infiltration. Subacute ischemic stroke follows a vascular territory and shows gyriform rather than thick ring enhancement and would not cross the midline through the callosum in this pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging neurosurgical consultation, which medication should be started to address his symptoms?
options: Intravenous mannitol bolus and intubation for ICP control
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics
Dexamethasone to reduce peritumoral vasogenic edema
Therapeutic heparin for presumed venous infarct
stages.explanation: Corticosteroids, classically dexamethasone, are first-line to reduce peritumoral vasogenic edema and improve neurologic symptoms in patients with brain tumors. They act within hours to days and often improve the focal deficit and headache. Mannitol and intubation are reserved for acute herniation or rapid neurologic decline, which this stable patient does not have. Antibiotics treat abscess, which the imaging argues against. Heparin is contraindicated; there is no thrombosis, and anticoagulation risks hemorrhage into a vascular, necrotic tumor. Steroids also bridge the patient to definitive surgical diagnosis and resection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish a definitive diagnosis and guide treatment?
options: Whole-brain radiation therapy without tissue diagnosis
Maximal safe surgical resection (or biopsy) with histopathologic and molecular analysis
Empiric temozolomide based on imaging alone
PET-CT to search for a systemic primary before any intervention
stages.explanation: Tissue is required for diagnosis. Maximal safe resection both relieves mass effect and provides tissue for histology and molecular markers (IDH status, MGMT promoter methylation) that define the WHO classification and guide therapy; when resection is unsafe, stereotactic biopsy is performed. Radiation or chemotherapy should not precede a tissue diagnosis. Empiric temozolomide is inappropriate without confirmed pathology and molecular data. PET-CT to hunt for a primary is reasonable only if metastasis is suspected, but a butterfly lesion in this setting is far more consistent with primary glioblastoma, and treatment hinges on resecting and analyzing the lesion itself.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype), WHO grade 4 — butterfly glioma crossing the corpus callosum
pearls: Morning headaches, vomiting, and progressive focal deficits suggest a mass with raised ICP — image before LP.
A thick, irregular ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis crossing the corpus callosum is the classic butterfly glioblastoma.
Dexamethasone rapidly reduces peritumoral vasogenic edema and is first-line for symptomatic relief.
Definitive diagnosis requires tissue; maximal safe resection both debulks and supplies molecular markers (IDH, MGMT).

cases.title: Counseling After a Brain Tumor Diagnosis
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up to discuss treatment after recent brain surgery and pathology results.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents to neuro-oncology clinic two weeks after near-total resection of a right temporal mass that had caused progressive memory difficulty and one generalized seizure. Pathology confirmed glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4. She has recovered well from surgery with resolution of her aphasia. She is fully independent and walks daily. PMH: well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypothyroidism. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (started postoperatively), and a tapering dexamethasone course. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: never-smoker, retired schoolteacher, lives with her husband, no alcohol. Family history: mother with colon cancer at 75. She asks about her prognosis and what determines whether chemotherapy will help her. Karnofsky performance status is 90. She has no current headaches, weakness, or further seizures. Her chief concern is whether she is a good candidate for the standard chemotherapy and what her tumor's molecular testing means for her.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Mental status normal with intact attention, naming, and recall of 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Speech is fluent without paraphasic errors. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; visual fields full to confrontation. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities; no pronator drift. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric, toes downgoing. Sensation intact to all modalities. Cerebellar testing normal; tandem gait steady. Well-healing right temporal craniotomy incision without erythema, swelling, or drainage. No nuchal rigidity. No papilledema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which molecular marker most directly predicts benefit from temozolomide and a more favorable prognosis in glioblastoma?
options: EGFR amplification
MGMT promoter methylation
TERT promoter mutation
Loss of chromosome 10
stages.explanation: Methylation of the MGMT (O6-methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase) promoter silences the DNA-repair enzyme that normally removes alkylating damage. With MGMT silenced, temozolomide-induced alkylation is not repaired, so methylated tumors are more chemosensitive and carry a better prognosis. EGFR amplification and chromosome 10 loss are common in IDH-wildtype glioblastoma but are not predictive of temozolomide response. TERT promoter mutations are also frequent and contribute to the grade-4 designation but do not predict chemotherapy benefit. MGMT methylation status is therefore the key predictive biomarker guiding temozolomide expectations and counseling.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & MOLECULAR
stages.content:
Histology: glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 (microvascular proliferation and necrosis present).
IDH1/IDH2: wildtype.
MGMT promoter: methylated.
EGFR: amplified.
1p/19q: non-codeleted.
Ki-67 proliferation index: 28%.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on her excellent performance status and pathology, what is the recommended standard first-line treatment?
options: Surgery alone with surveillance MRI every 2 months
Whole-brain radiation therapy alone
Concurrent radiotherapy with temozolomide followed by adjuvant (maintenance) temozolomide
Bevacizumab monotherapy
stages.explanation: The Stupp protocol — focal radiotherapy given concurrently with daily temozolomide, followed by six cycles of adjuvant temozolomide — is the standard of care after maximal safe resection in fit glioblastoma patients and is exactly what this high-performance, MGMT-methylated patient should receive. Surgery alone offers far inferior survival; glioblastoma invariably recurs without adjuvant therapy. Whole-brain radiation alone omits the concurrent and maintenance chemotherapy that confers a proven survival advantage, and focal rather than whole-brain RT is standard. Bevacizumab does not improve overall survival in newly diagnosed disease and is reserved for selected recurrent cases or symptomatic edema control.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks how her MGMT-methylated status affects her outlook compared with an unmethylated tumor. What is the most accurate statement?
options: MGMT methylation predicts greater benefit from temozolomide and longer median survival
MGMT methylation means she does not need radiation
MGMT status has no bearing on treatment, only on diagnosis
MGMT methylation indicates the tumor is benign and curable
stages.explanation: MGMT promoter methylation is both prognostic and predictive: methylated tumors respond better to temozolomide and have a longer median overall survival (roughly double that of unmethylated tumors in trial data). It does not eliminate the need for radiation, which remains a core component of the Stupp regimen. It is not merely diagnostic — it directly informs the expected benefit of alkylating chemotherapy. Critically, glioblastoma remains incurable and uniformly recurs; methylation improves but does not normalize prognosis, so she should not be told the tumor is benign or curable. Honest, realistic counseling is essential.

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE CARE
stages.question: Before starting temozolomide concurrently with radiation, which prophylaxis is specifically recommended?
options: Prophylactic therapeutic anticoagulation
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis
Routine granulocyte colony-stimulating factor with every cycle
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics throughout radiation
stages.explanation: Concurrent temozolomide with radiation causes prolonged lymphopenia, and the combination with the tapering corticosteroid raises the risk of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, so PJP prophylaxis (commonly atovaquone given her sulfa allergy, since TMP-SMX would be contraindicated) is recommended during the concurrent phase. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not given prophylactically and risks intracranial hemorrhage. G-CSF is used reactively for significant neutropenia, not routinely. Broad-spectrum antibiotic prophylaxis is not indicated and promotes resistance; targeted PJP prophylaxis is the evidence-based measure. Counts should be monitored during therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4, MGMT promoter methylated — candidate for Stupp protocol
pearls: MGMT promoter methylation predicts temozolomide benefit and is associated with longer survival.
Standard of care after maximal safe resection in fit patients is the Stupp protocol: concurrent RT + temozolomide, then adjuvant temozolomide.
MGMT improves prognosis but glioblastoma remains incurable — counsel honestly.
PJP prophylaxis is recommended during concurrent temozolomide/RT due to lymphopenia and steroid use; use atovaquone if sulfa-allergic.

cases.title: New Enhancement After Finishing Chemoradiation
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted with worsening headaches and new left-sided weakness three weeks after completing chemoradiation.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man with glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype, MGMT methylated) of the right parietal lobe is admitted from clinic. He underwent gross total resection three months ago and completed concurrent radiotherapy with temozolomide three weeks ago. Over the past week he has developed worsening headaches and mild left arm clumsiness. His outpatient surveillance MRI today, obtained four weeks after radiation, shows new and increased contrast enhancement at the resection margin with increased surrounding edema. He is on a maintenance temozolomide schedule and a dexamethasone taper. PMH: hypertension. Medications: temozolomide (adjuvant cycle 1), levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily, dexamethasone 2 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol, works from home in IT. Family history: noncontributory. He is anxious that his tumor is growing rapidly. He has had no seizures, fevers, or new systemic symptoms. His performance status remains good and he ambulates independently with the cane he has used since surgery.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, and conversant without aphasia. Cranial nerves intact; no papilledema. Motor exam: 4+/5 strength in the left upper extremity with mild left pronator drift; left lower extremity and right side 5/5. Subtle left-hand dysmetria. Reflexes brisk on the left with an equivocal left plantar response; right side normal. Sensation intact. Gait mildly wide-based, uses a cane, no truncal ataxia. Well-healed right parietal craniotomy without signs of infection. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given new enhancement within 12 weeks of completing radiation, what diagnostic possibility must be strongly considered alongside true tumor progression?
options: Radiation necrosis later in the course (radionecrosis)
Pseudoprogression
New metastatic seeding
Postsurgical gliosis
stages.explanation: Pseudoprogression is a subacute treatment-related increase in contrast enhancement and edema that typically occurs within the first 12 weeks (especially the first 4-8 weeks) after concurrent chemoradiation and then stabilizes or improves without a change in therapy. It can be radiographically indistinguishable from true progression. It is more common in MGMT-methylated tumors, as in this patient. Radiation necrosis is also treatment-related but classically occurs months to years later. New metastatic seeding is uncommon and would not be the leading early consideration. Postsurgical gliosis does not produce new, increasing nodular enhancement weeks after surgery. Recognizing pseudoprogression prevents premature, inappropriate abandonment of effective therapy.

stages.label: ADVANCED IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with perfusion and spectroscopy:
New nodular enhancement at the posterior resection margin, 1.6 cm, with increased FLAIR edema.
Dynamic susceptibility-contrast perfusion: relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) is LOW in the enhancing region.
MR spectroscopy: no elevated choline-to-NAA ratio; lactate/lipid peak present.
No restricted diffusion suggesting abscess.
MGMT promoter: methylated (known).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How do the perfusion and spectroscopy findings help distinguish pseudoprogression from true progression?
options: High rCBV and high choline/NAA favor pseudoprogression
Low rCBV and absence of elevated choline/NAA favor pseudoprogression (treatment effect) rather than tumor
Perfusion cannot differentiate the two entities
A lactate/lipid peak confirms recurrent viable tumor
stages.explanation: Viable, actively growing tumor is hypervascular and metabolically active, so true progression typically shows HIGH relative cerebral blood volume on perfusion and an ELEVATED choline-to-NAA ratio on spectroscopy. Treatment effect (pseudoprogression/necrosis) shows LOW rCBV and lacks the choline elevation, often with a lactate/lipid peak reflecting necrosis — exactly this patient's pattern, which favors treatment effect. Saying perfusion cannot differentiate is incorrect; advanced sequences add meaningful discrimination, though they are imperfect. A lactate/lipid peak indicates necrosis, not viable tumor, so it does not confirm recurrence. The constellation here points toward pseudoprogression.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His advanced imaging favors treatment effect and he is clinically stable on steroids. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediately switch to second-line bevacizumab for progression
Continue adjuvant temozolomide, optimize steroids, and repeat MRI in 4-8 weeks to reassess
Take him back to the OR for repeat gross total resection now
Start whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: When imaging and the clinical picture favor pseudoprogression in a stable patient, the standard approach is to continue planned adjuvant temozolomide, manage symptoms and edema with steroids, and repeat MRI in 4-8 weeks; true progression will continue to worsen while pseudoprogression stabilizes or regresses. Switching to bevacizumab assumes progression that has not been established and could needlessly abandon effective first-line therapy. Reoperation is not indicated for a stable patient with imaging favoring treatment effect and carries surgical risk. Whole-brain radiation is not standard for focal glioblastoma and would not be initiated for presumed treatment effect. Surgery is reserved for when tissue is truly needed to resolve the question.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the repeat MRI in 6 weeks showed continued enlargement and the distinction remained genuinely unclear, what is the definitive way to differentiate pseudoprogression from true progression?
options: Empiric dose-intensified temozolomide
Histopathologic evaluation via biopsy or resection of the enhancing region
Repeat lumbar puncture for cytology
Serum tumor marker panel
stages.explanation: When advanced imaging is equivocal and the lesion continues to enlarge, histopathology obtained by biopsy or resection of the enhancing region is the definitive arbiter, distinguishing viable tumor from treatment-related necrosis and gliosis. Empiric dose-intensified temozolomide does not answer the diagnostic question and exposes the patient to toxicity without clarity. CSF cytology is low-yield for parenchymal glioblastoma and does not reliably detect local recurrence versus necrosis. There is no useful serum tumor marker for glioblastoma. Tissue remains the reference standard whenever imaging cannot resolve progression from pseudoprogression and management hinges on the answer.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He has been on dexamethasone for several weeks to control edema. Which complication of prolonged corticosteroid use should be actively monitored and managed during this period?
options: Hypoglycemia requiring glucose supplementation
Hyperglycemia, proximal myopathy, and increased infection risk
Profound bradycardia
Hypercoagulability requiring routine anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Prolonged corticosteroids cause hyperglycemia (steroid-induced or worsened diabetes), a proximal steroid myopathy that can itself impair strength and gait, immunosuppression with heightened infection risk (including PJP), plus insomnia, mood changes, and osteoporosis — all warranting active monitoring and the lowest effective dose with a taper once edema is controlled. Steroids cause hyperglycemia, not hypoglycemia, so glucose supplementation is wrong. They do not characteristically cause bradycardia. While malignancy carries thrombotic risk, steroids are not an indication for routine anticoagulation, which is hazardous in a vascular brain tumor. Recognizing steroid toxicity is essential because a proximal myopathy could be mistaken for tumor progression, and minimizing exposure improves outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: Pseudoprogression after concurrent chemoradiation for MGMT-methylated glioblastoma
pearls: Pseudoprogression occurs within ~12 weeks of chemoradiation, mimics progression on MRI, and is more common in MGMT-methylated tumors.
Low rCBV on perfusion and no choline/NAA elevation favor treatment effect; high rCBV and high choline/NAA favor true tumor.
Stable patients with imaging favoring pseudoprogression should continue temozolomide and undergo repeat MRI in 4-8 weeks, not abandon therapy.
Tissue (biopsy/resection) is the definitive arbiter when imaging remains equivocal and the lesion keeps growing.

cases.title: First-Ever Seizure in a Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by EMS after a witnessed first-time generalized seizure.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is brought to the ED by EMS after his wife witnessed him suddenly stiffen, fall, and have generalized shaking lasting about 90 seconds, followed by confusion. He bit his tongue and was incontinent of urine. He has never had a seizure before. On recovery he reports a mild left-sided headache over the past two weeks and a 15-pound unintentional weight loss over three months. He has a chronic cough that he attributes to smoking. PMH: COPD and hypertension. Medications: tiotropium inhaler, albuterol PRN, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (hives). Social: 45-pack-year smoker, still smoking; drinks 2-3 beers per week; retired truck driver. Family history: brother with lung cancer. Review of systems notable for fatigue and occasional hemoptysis. He denies fever, recent travel, head trauma, or alcohol withdrawal symptoms. He is now postictal but rousable and increasingly alert. No prior brain imaging. No anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: Postictal but arousable, oriented to person and place after several minutes. Lateral tongue laceration noted. Pupils equal and reactive; no papilledema. Subtle left facial droop and mild left arm pronator drift on the postictal exam, persisting after he becomes fully alert. Remaining strength 5/5; reflexes symmetric, left plantar equivocal. Lungs with diminished breath sounds and a few right-sided crackles. Cardiac exam regular. No neck stiffness, no rash. No focal sensory loss. Gait deferred initially due to postictal state.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After stabilization and a normal fingerstick glucose, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step for this first-time adult seizure with a focal deficit?
options: Discharge with outpatient neurology follow-up
Neuroimaging of the brain (CT then MRI with contrast)
Start lifelong antiepileptic therapy without imaging
Immediate lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: A new-onset focal-to-bilateral seizure in an older adult with a persistent focal deficit (left facial droop, arm drift), weight loss, and a heavy smoking history mandates neuroimaging to evaluate for a structural lesion such as a brain metastasis. A non-contrast CT excludes hemorrhage acutely, and MRI with contrast best characterizes lesions. Discharging without imaging would miss a potentially serious cause. Starting lifelong antiepileptics without identifying the etiology is premature, though acute seizure management is appropriate. LP is not the first step when a mass is suspected and is contraindicated before imaging if elevated ICP is possible.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: multiple hyperdense/iso-dense nodules at gray-white junctions bilaterally with surrounding vasogenic edema; no acute hemorrhage.
MRI brain with contrast: at least five enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction, the largest 2.1 cm in the right frontal lobe with surrounding edema.
Chest X-ray: 4 cm spiculated right upper lobe mass with right hilar adenopathy.
Na 137 mEq/L, glucose 108 mg/dL.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for the intracranial findings?
options: Multifocal primary glioblastoma
Brain metastases from a lung primary
Multiple meningiomas
Septic emboli
stages.explanation: Multiple enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction in an older heavy smoker with a spiculated lung mass and hilar adenopathy are the classic picture of brain metastases, most often from lung cancer. Metastases lodge at the gray-white junction where vessel caliber abruptly narrows. Glioblastoma is usually a solitary infiltrating lesion, and multifocal primary GBM is far less common than metastatic disease in this context. Meningiomas are extra-axial and dural-based, not parenchymal gray-white nodules. Septic emboli would typically present with fever, an infectious source, and often restricted diffusion or microabscesses, which the history and imaging do not support.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is now alert with stable vitals. What is the most appropriate acute management for his seizure and cerebral edema?
options: Prophylactic phenytoin for all patients with brain metastases regardless of seizure
Start an antiseizure medication (e.g., levetiracetam) and dexamethasone for symptomatic edema
Mannitol and emergent decompressive craniectomy
Observation alone with no medications
stages.explanation: He has had a seizure attributable to brain metastases, so an antiseizure medication such as levetiracetam is appropriate, and dexamethasone reduces symptomatic peritumoral vasogenic edema. Routine prophylactic antiepileptics are NOT recommended for brain-metastasis patients who have not had a seizure, but this patient did seize, so treatment is warranted. Mannitol and decompressive surgery are reserved for impending herniation or rapid deterioration, which he does not have. Observation alone is inappropriate given a documented seizure and significant edema. He will also need oncologic staging and multidisciplinary planning for the primary tumor and brain disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in his overall workup?
options: Whole-brain radiation immediately without tissue diagnosis
Tissue diagnosis and staging of the lung primary (e.g., biopsy plus CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET)
Resect all five brain lesions surgically
Begin palliative care with no further diagnostics
stages.explanation: Confirming the diagnosis and stage drives all subsequent therapy, so biopsy of the most accessible site (often the lung primary or a node) plus systemic staging with CT and PET is the priority; molecular and PD-L1 testing of lung cancer also guides targeted therapy and immunotherapy options that can have intracranial activity. Whole-brain radiation should not precede a tissue diagnosis. Resecting five lesions is not feasible or indicated for diffuse metastatic disease; surgery is reserved for selected solitary or symptomatic lesions. Jumping straight to comfort care forgoes potentially effective, even life-prolonging, systemic and radiation options and is premature before staging.

cases.diagnosis: Brain metastases from a lung primary, presenting with first-time seizure
pearls: A first focal seizure in an older smoker with weight loss demands brain imaging to exclude a structural lesion.
Multiple enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction are the hallmark of hematogenous brain metastases.
Treat seizures that occur, but do NOT give prophylactic antiepileptics to brain-met patients who have never seized.
Obtain tissue and stage the primary before committing to brain-directed therapy; molecular markers guide systemic options.

cases.title: Progressive Headache and Imbalance in a Cancer Survivor
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of headaches, double vision, and unsteadiness in a woman with a history of breast cancer.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents to clinic with two weeks of progressive headaches, intermittent double vision, and difficulty walking straight. She was diagnosed three years ago with HER2-positive, hormone-receptor-negative invasive ductal breast carcinoma treated with mastectomy, chemotherapy, and trastuzumab; recent scans had shown stable bony and hepatic metastases. Over the past week she has noticed nausea, neck discomfort, and tingling around her mouth. She denies fever or recent infection. PMH: HER2+ metastatic breast cancer, hypothyroidism. Medications: trastuzumab-based regimen, levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, ondansetron PRN. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, occasional wine, works part-time as a librarian, married with two children. Family history: maternal aunt with breast cancer. Review of systems notable for fatigue and the mouth tingling; no bowel or bladder dysfunction. She is highly functional at home but has stopped driving because of the visual symptoms. She is anxious that her cancer has spread to her brain.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Mild bilateral abducens (CN VI) palsies with horizontal diplopia on lateral gaze. Subtle right facial sensory diminution. Fundoscopy shows blurred disc margins suggestive of early papilledema. Neck with mild meningismus. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Finger-to-nose intact, but tandem gait is unsteady and she sways with eyes closed. No long-tract sensory level. No spinal tenderness. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam, including hearing, is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her multiple cranial nerve findings, meningismus, and early papilledema in a cancer patient most suggest which process?
options: A single large parenchymal metastasis
Leptomeningeal (carcinomatous) metastatic disease
Bacterial meningitis
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
stages.explanation: Multiple, non-contiguous cranial neuropathies (bilateral CN VI palsies, trigeminal sensory loss), meningismus, headache, and signs of raised ICP in a patient with metastatic cancer are classic for leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, in which tumor cells seed the subarachnoid space and coat cranial nerves and the meninges. HER2-positive breast cancer is a well-recognized cause. A single parenchymal metastasis produces focal, localizing deficits, not scattered multilevel cranial neuropathies. Bacterial meningitis usually presents acutely with fever and toxicity, absent here. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension occurs typically in young obese women without multifocal cranial neuropathies or a cancer context.

stages.label: IMAGING & CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain and full spine with contrast: leptomeningeal enhancement coating the basal cisterns and cerebellar folia; 'sugar-coating' along the cauda equina; plus a 1.5 cm enhancing right cerebellar parenchymal metastasis.
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 28 cm H2O.
CSF: WBC 22/µL (lymphocytic), protein 110 mg/dL, glucose 38 mg/dL (serum 100).
CSF cytology: malignant epithelial cells consistent with adenocarcinoma; HER2-positive.
Gram stain and bacterial cultures: negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which CSF finding pattern is most characteristic of leptomeningeal carcinomatosis?
options: Elevated opening pressure, high protein, low glucose, and malignant cells on cytology
Normal protein and glucose with neutrophilic pleocytosis
Very low protein with elevated glucose
Xanthochromia with normal cell count
stages.explanation: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis typically produces elevated opening pressure, elevated protein, low glucose (from tumor metabolism), a lymphocytic pleocytosis, and the diagnostic finding of malignant cells on cytology — exactly this patient's profile. Repeat LPs increase cytologic yield. Normal protein/glucose with neutrophilic pleocytosis suggests bacterial infection rather than carcinomatosis. Very low protein with high glucose is not a recognized pattern for this disease. Xanthochromia with a normal cell count indicates prior subarachnoid hemorrhage. The combination of cytology-positive malignant cells with high protein and low glucose confirms the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a focal symptomatic cerebellar metastasis AND diffuse leptomeningeal disease. Which radiation strategy is most appropriate?
options: Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) alone to the cerebellar lesion
Whole-brain radiation therapy to address diffuse leptomeningeal and parenchymal disease
No radiation; rely on trastuzumab alone
Craniospinal proton therapy as first-line standard for all such patients
stages.explanation: SRS is ideal for a limited number of discrete parenchymal metastases, but it treats only focal targets and cannot address diffuse leptomeningeal spread. With multifocal cranial neuropathies and widespread leptomeningeal enhancement, whole-brain radiation (often combined with CNS-directed systemic/intrathecal therapy) is the appropriate strategy to cover the diffuse disease and symptomatic regions. SRS alone would leave the leptomeningeal disease untreated. Forgoing radiation and relying on trastuzumab alone is inadequate because trastuzumab penetrates the CNS poorly. Craniospinal irradiation is highly toxic and not the routine first-line approach for adult solid-tumor leptomeningeal disease; involved-field or whole-brain RT plus systemic options is standard.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To improve CNS control of her HER2-positive disease beyond radiation, which systemic approach is most appropriate?
options: Continue trastuzumab alone because it controls CNS disease well
Switch to a CNS-penetrant HER2-directed therapy (e.g., tucatinib-based regimen or trastuzumab deruxtecan)
Add high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely as the primary anticancer treatment
Stop all systemic therapy and pursue intrathecal antibiotics
stages.explanation: Standard trastuzumab penetrates the blood-brain barrier poorly, so CNS progression on trastuzumab calls for CNS-active HER2-directed therapy. Tucatinib (a small-molecule HER2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor) combined with trastuzumab and capecitabine, and trastuzumab deruxtecan, have demonstrated meaningful intracranial activity in HER2-positive disease. Continuing trastuzumab alone ignores its poor CNS penetration and the documented progression. Corticosteroids palliate edema and symptoms but are not antineoplastic for this tumor. Intrathecal antibiotics treat infection, not malignancy; her CSF is culture-negative with malignant cytology. Selecting CNS-penetrant targeted therapy best addresses the leptomeningeal and parenchymal HER2-positive disease.

cases.diagnosis: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis with a cerebellar parenchymal metastasis from HER2-positive breast cancer
pearls: Scattered, multilevel cranial neuropathies plus meningismus in a cancer patient suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.
Classic CSF: high opening pressure, high protein, low glucose, lymphocytic pleocytosis, and positive cytology (repeat LPs raise yield).
SRS treats discrete parenchymal lesions; diffuse leptomeningeal disease requires whole-brain RT plus CNS-directed systemic therapy.
Trastuzumab penetrates the CNS poorly — use CNS-active HER2 agents (tucatinib-based regimens, trastuzumab deruxtecan) for brain disease.

cases.title: Sudden Weakness in a Man with Skin Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute onset of right arm weakness and a severe headache.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man presents to the ED with the abrupt onset, 90 minutes ago, of right arm weakness and a sudden 'thunderclap-like' headache while at work. He also reports a brief episode of slurred speech. He has a history of cutaneous melanoma on his back excised two years ago with a positive sentinel lymph node; he was lost to oncologic follow-up. Over recent weeks he has had milder intermittent headaches and one episode of transient left-hand numbness. He denies trauma or fever. PMH: melanoma, hypertension. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; no anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Allergies: none. Social: works in construction, extensive sun exposure, social alcohol, never-smoker. Family history: father with melanoma. Review of systems notable for a 10-pound weight loss and night sweats. He has had no seizures. He drove himself partway before a coworker took over. His symptoms have been relatively stable since onset, without clear improvement.
cases.exam: Awake, alert, oriented, mildly dysarthric. Pupils equal and reactive. Right upper extremity 3/5 strength with a clear right pronator drift; right lower extremity 4+/5; left side 5/5. Mild right central facial droop. Reflexes brisk on the right with an upgoing right plantar response. Sensation grossly intact. No neck stiffness. Skin exam reveals a healed back scar and several irregular pigmented nevi. No carotid bruit. No papilledema on bedside exam. Speech fluent in content despite dysarthria. Gait deferred given the deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He presents with stroke-like deficits within the thrombolysis window. What must be excluded with the first imaging study before any consideration of thrombolytics?
options: Acute large-vessel occlusion only
Intracranial hemorrhage
Carotid dissection
Cervical spine fracture
stages.explanation: Any acute stroke-like presentation requires an immediate non-contrast head CT primarily to exclude intracranial hemorrhage, which is an absolute contraindication to thrombolysis and entirely changes management. This patient has additional red flags — a melanoma history, subacute preceding headaches, weight loss — that raise concern for hemorrhagic brain metastases mimicking stroke. Identifying large-vessel occlusion matters for thrombectomy but is assessed with vessel imaging after hemorrhage is excluded. Carotid dissection and cervical spine fracture are not the immediate priority here and do not fit the clinical picture. Detecting hemorrhage first is essential and would preclude thrombolytics.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: multiple hyperdense intra-axial lesions with surrounding edema; the largest in the left frontoparietal region shows acute hemorrhage and local mass effect.
CT angiography: no large-vessel occlusion; no aneurysm.
MRI brain with contrast: several enhancing lesions, several with hemorrhagic (T1-hyperintense, blooming on GRE/SWI) and melanin-related signal; surrounding vasogenic edema.
LDH elevated at 410 U/L.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which primary tumor is most notorious for producing hemorrhagic brain metastases like these?
options: Prostate adenocarcinoma
Melanoma (along with renal cell, choriocarcinoma, and thyroid)
Colon adenocarcinoma
Basal cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: Melanoma is a classic cause of hemorrhagic brain metastases; the mnemonic for hemorrhage-prone metastases is melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, and thyroid carcinoma (some include lung). Melanin and the friable vascularity of these lesions explain the bleeding and the T1-hyperintense/blooming MRI signal. This patient's melanoma history, multiple hemorrhagic enhancing lesions, and elevated LDH fit perfectly. Prostate and colon cancers commonly metastasize to bone and liver, respectively, and rarely cause hemorrhagic brain metastases. Basal cell carcinoma is locally invasive and essentially never metastasizes to the brain. Recognizing hemorrhagic metastases prevents misdiagnosis as primary stroke.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the CT shows intracranial hemorrhage from metastases, what is the correct decision regarding thrombolysis and acute management?
options: Administer IV tPA now since he is within the time window
Do NOT give thrombolytics; manage blood pressure, give dexamethasone for edema, and consult neurosurgery/oncology
Start therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed cardioembolism
Perform emergent lumbar puncture to confirm subarachnoid hemorrhage
stages.explanation: Intracranial hemorrhage is an absolute contraindication to thrombolysis, and the deficit is due to hemorrhagic metastases rather than ischemia, so tPA is unequivocally wrong and would be catastrophic. Appropriate care is careful blood-pressure control, dexamethasone to reduce peritumoral edema, seizure precautions, and prompt neurosurgical and oncologic consultation for definitive planning. Therapeutic anticoagulation is contraindicated in active intracranial hemorrhage and there is no evidence of cardioembolism. Lumbar puncture is unnecessary and risky here; the hemorrhage and lesions are already demonstrated on CT/MRI, and LP carries herniation risk with mass effect. Avoiding tPA is the pivotal safety decision.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After stabilization, which systemic therapy class has demonstrated meaningful intracranial activity in metastatic melanoma and should be considered with the team?
options: Empiric cytotoxic chemotherapy with dacarbazine alone
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (and BRAF/MEK inhibitors if BRAF-mutated)
Hormonal therapy
Trastuzumab
stages.explanation: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1 with or without anti-CTLA-4) have produced durable intracranial responses in melanoma brain metastases, and for BRAF-mutated tumors, BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy provides rapid CNS-active responses; molecular testing for BRAF should be obtained. Single-agent dacarbazine has limited efficacy and poor CNS activity and is not a modern standard. Hormonal therapy is irrelevant to melanoma. Trastuzumab targets HER2 in breast and gastric cancers, not melanoma. Brain-directed local therapy (SRS or surgery for the symptomatic hemorrhagic lesion) plus immunotherapy or targeted therapy, coordinated by a multidisciplinary team, is the contemporary approach for melanoma brain metastases.

cases.diagnosis: Hemorrhagic brain metastases from melanoma presenting as a stroke mimic
pearls: Always get a non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage before thrombolysis in any stroke-like presentation.
Hemorrhage-prone brain metastases: Melanoma, Renal cell, Choriocarcinoma, Thyroid (and lung).
Intracranial hemorrhage is an absolute contraindication to tPA — recognize hemorrhagic metastases as stroke mimics.
Melanoma brain metastases respond to checkpoint inhibitors and, if BRAF-mutated, to BRAF/MEK inhibitors — test for BRAF.

cases.title: A Single Brain Lesion in a Man with Kidney Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: Headaches and left-hand weakness leading to discovery of a single brain lesion in a man with treated kidney cancer.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is in the pre-operative area before a planned craniotomy. Three weeks ago he developed worsening headaches and progressive left-hand weakness. He has a history of clear-cell renal cell carcinoma treated two years ago with radical nephrectomy; recent surveillance imaging showed no evidence of extracranial disease and his primary is considered controlled. MRI revealed a single 3.2 cm enhancing lesion in the right frontal lobe (premotor/motor region) with surrounding edema and no other intracranial lesions. Systemic restaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET) found no other sites of disease. PMH: clear-cell RCC (controlled), hypertension, prior smoker. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, dexamethasone (started for edema), levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (started prophylactically pre-op). Allergies: none. Social: quit smoking 3 years ago, retired engineer, lives with spouse. Family history: noncontributory. His Karnofsky performance status is 90. He understands he is undergoing resection and asks what comes after surgery to keep the tumor from returning at that spot.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, calm. Cranial nerves intact; no facial asymmetry. Left upper extremity 4/5 with mild left pronator drift; left grip slightly weak; left lower extremity 5/5; right side 5/5. Reflexes 2+ symmetric; left plantar equivocal. Sensation intact. Cerebellar testing normal. Gait steady with a cane. No papilledema. No neck stiffness. Surgical site marked over the right frontal region. Remainder of the general exam unremarkable; well-healed flank nephrectomy scar.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features make this patient an appropriate candidate for surgical resection of his brain metastasis?
options: Multiple lesions with poor performance status
A single, surgically accessible, symptomatic lesion with controlled systemic disease and good performance status
Diffuse leptomeningeal disease
An asymptomatic deep brainstem lesion
stages.explanation: Surgical resection of a brain metastasis is favored for a single (or limited), surgically accessible, symptomatic lesion in a patient with controlled or absent systemic disease and good performance status — precisely this patient. Resection rapidly relieves mass effect, provides tissue confirmation, and, combined with radiation, improves local control and survival in selected patients. Multiple lesions with poor performance status generally favor radiation or systemic therapy over surgery. Diffuse leptomeningeal disease is not amenable to focal resection. A deep brainstem lesion carries prohibitive surgical risk and is typically managed with radiosurgery. Patient selection is the central principle for metastasis surgery.

stages.label: OPERATIVE FINDINGS & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Intraoperative: well-circumscribed, highly vascular, reddish tumor in the right frontal cortex; gross total resection achieved with neuromonitoring; motor responses preserved.
Estimated blood loss higher than typical due to tumor vascularity.
Frozen section: metastatic carcinoma.
Final pathology: metastatic clear-cell renal cell carcinoma.
Resection margins grossly negative; postoperative MRI shows no residual enhancing tumor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After gross total resection of this solitary metastasis, what is the recommended approach to maximize local control while limiting toxicity?
options: No further local therapy; surveillance MRI only
Postoperative stereotactic radiosurgery to the resection cavity
Whole-brain radiation therapy to the entire brain
Repeat surgery in 6 weeks regardless of imaging
stages.explanation: Even after gross total resection, microscopic disease in the cavity causes local recurrence, so postoperative stereotactic radiosurgery to the resection cavity is standard to improve local control while sparing the rest of the brain. Trials show cavity SRS reduces local recurrence versus observation and avoids the neurocognitive decline associated with whole-brain radiation. Surveillance alone leaves an unacceptably high local failure rate. Whole-brain radiation is no longer preferred for limited disease because of cognitive toxicity and equivalent survival to focal approaches. Routine repeat surgery without evidence of residual or recurrent tumor is not indicated. Cavity SRS optimally balances control and toxicity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding the prophylactic levetiracetam started before surgery, what is the appropriate plan if he has no seizures postoperatively?
options: Continue it indefinitely as lifelong seizure prophylaxis
Taper and discontinue it, since routine long-term prophylaxis is not recommended in seizure-free brain-tumor patients
Escalate to two antiepileptics for added protection
Switch to phenytoin for better prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Antiepileptics are sometimes used in the immediate perioperative period for supratentorial surgery, but routine long-term prophylaxis is not recommended for brain-tumor patients who have not had a seizure, because evidence does not show benefit and the drugs carry side effects. If he remains seizure-free, the appropriate plan is to taper and stop levetiracetam after the perioperative window. Indefinite prophylaxis is not supported. Adding a second agent or escalating prophylaxis increases toxicity without proven benefit. Phenytoin is an enzyme inducer with more interactions and side effects and is not preferred; levetiracetam is generally favored when an antiepileptic is needed.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His RCC was considered controlled, but if systemic disease later progresses, which systemic therapy class is most relevant for metastatic clear-cell RCC?
options: Anti-estrogen hormonal therapy
Immune checkpoint inhibitors and/or VEGF tyrosine kinase inhibitors
Trastuzumab-based HER2 therapy
Conventional alkylating chemotherapy as first-line
stages.explanation: Metastatic clear-cell RCC is treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors (often in combination) and/or VEGF-pathway tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib, or axitinib; these target the hypervascular, VHL/HIF-driven biology of clear-cell RCC. Hormonal therapy has no role in RCC. Trastuzumab targets HER2-positive breast/gastric cancers, not RCC. Classic cytotoxic chemotherapy is notably ineffective in RCC and is not first-line. Of note, the high vascularity that makes VEGF inhibitors useful also explains the brisk intraoperative bleeding seen with RCC brain metastases, reinforcing the consistency of this case.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After resection and planned cavity radiosurgery, what is the most appropriate brain-specific surveillance plan for this patient?
options: No further imaging unless new symptoms develop
Serial surveillance brain MRI with contrast to detect cavity recurrence and new distant metastases
Annual non-contrast head CT only
Repeat PET brain scans every month
stages.explanation: After local treatment of a brain metastasis, patients need serial contrast-enhanced brain MRI (typically every 2-3 months initially) to detect both local recurrence at the treated cavity and new distant intracranial metastases, which can emerge even when the original lesion is controlled. Waiting for symptoms misses asymptomatic, more treatable recurrences. Non-contrast CT is far less sensitive than contrast MRI for small enhancing recurrences and cannot reliably distinguish recurrence from post-radiosurgery change. Monthly PET of the brain is not standard, has limited spatial resolution for small lesions, and exposes the patient to unnecessary cost and radiation. Contrast MRI surveillance is the established approach.

cases.diagnosis: Solitary brain metastasis from clear-cell renal cell carcinoma — resection followed by cavity stereotactic radiosurgery
pearls: Resection is favored for a single, accessible, symptomatic brain metastasis with controlled systemic disease and good performance status.
Postoperative SRS to the resection cavity improves local control and avoids whole-brain RT's cognitive toxicity.
Do not continue antiepileptic prophylaxis long-term in seizure-free brain-tumor patients — taper after the perioperative period.
Clear-cell RCC is hypervascular (brisk bleeding) and treated systemically with checkpoint inhibitors and VEGF TKIs, not cytotoxic chemo.

cases.title: An Unexpected Finding on a Head Scan
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred to discuss a brain mass found incidentally on a scan ordered for minor head trauma.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman is referred to clinic to discuss an unexpected brain finding. Two weeks ago she slipped on ice and bumped her head; an ED CT showed no fracture or bleed but noted a possible mass, and a follow-up MRI was obtained. She feels entirely well, with no headaches, seizures, weakness, numbness, or visual changes, and her cognition is normal. PMH: hypertension, osteopenia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, calcium and vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, rare alcohol, works as a real estate agent, exercises regularly. Family history: mother with hypertension; no known brain tumors. Review of systems is unremarkable. She has had no hormonal therapy and no prior cranial radiation. She is anxious after reading about brain tumors online and wants to understand whether she needs surgery. She is fully functional and independent, drives, and has had no falls other than the recent slip. Her only symptom is mild anxiety about the finding itself.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, calm. Mental status normal; fluent speech; intact attention and memory. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; visual fields full; no papilledema. Motor strength 5/5 throughout; no pronator drift. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Sensation intact to all modalities. Cerebellar testing normal; tandem gait steady. No bruits. No scalp lesions. The neurologic exam is entirely normal, consistent with an asymptomatic, incidentally discovered lesion.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast: 1.8 cm extra-axial, dural-based mass over the left frontal convexity.
Homogeneous avid contrast enhancement with a 'dural tail.'
Broad dural attachment; the mass abuts but does not invade the brain; gray-white interface preserved (CSF cleft present).
No significant surrounding edema; no mass effect or midline shift.
No restricted diffusion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail?
options: Glioblastoma
Meningioma
Metastasis
Brain abscess
stages.explanation: An extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously and avidly enhancing mass with a dural tail and a preserved CSF cleft separating it from the brain is the classic appearance of a meningioma — the most common benign primary intracranial tumor, more frequent in women. Glioblastoma is intra-axial (within brain parenchyma), heterogeneous, and necrotic, not dural-based. Metastases are usually intra-axial at the gray-white junction and often multiple. A brain abscess shows central restricted diffusion and ring enhancement with clinical signs of infection. The dural tail and extra-axial location strongly favor meningioma, and the homogeneous enhancement without necrosis supports a benign (WHO grade 1) lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is completely asymptomatic with a small lesion and no edema or mass effect. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate craniotomy and resection
Observation with serial surveillance MRI
Empiric stereotactic radiosurgery now
Start chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Small, asymptomatic, incidentally discovered meningiomas without edema or mass effect are appropriately managed with observation and serial surveillance MRI, since many grow slowly or not at all and intervention carries risk. An initial follow-up scan in several months and then periodically allows detection of growth before symptoms develop. Immediate resection is reserved for symptomatic, large, growing, or edema-producing lesions. Radiosurgery is an option for selected small or surgically difficult lesions or for growth/symptoms, but it is not first-line for a tiny asymptomatic convexity meningioma. Chemotherapy has no role in standard meningioma management. Watchful waiting is the evidence-based choice here.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks what would change the plan toward intervention. Which finding on follow-up would most appropriately prompt treatment?
options: Stable size and no symptoms after one year
Documented interval growth or development of symptoms/edema
Her ongoing anxiety about the diagnosis
The presence of a dural tail on the original MRI
stages.explanation: Intervention is prompted by objective change — documented interval growth on serial imaging or the development of attributable symptoms or significant edema — which signals biologically active disease warranting resection or radiosurgery. Stability without symptoms supports continued observation. Patient anxiety, while important to address with reassurance and clear education, is not by itself an indication for neurosurgery on a benign asymptomatic lesion. A dural tail is a characteristic imaging feature of meningioma and helps make the diagnosis but does not itself dictate treatment. Tracking growth and symptoms over time is the principled basis for deciding when to act.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If resection were eventually performed for growth, which factor most strongly predicts a low recurrence risk?
options: WHO grade 1 histology with gross total resection (Simpson grade I)
Subtotal resection of a higher-grade tumor
Larger tumor size at diagnosis
Presence of a dural tail
stages.explanation: Recurrence risk in meningioma is driven chiefly by histologic grade and the completeness of resection: a WHO grade 1 (benign) tumor that is completely removed including its dural attachment (low Simpson grade) has the lowest recurrence rate. Subtotal resection of a higher-grade tumor carries a much higher recurrence risk. Larger size can complicate resection but is not as decisive as grade and resection extent. A dural tail is a diagnostic imaging feature and may harbor tumor cells, supporting resection of the involved dura, but it is not itself the strongest predictor of recurrence. Grade plus extent of resection are the key prognostic factors.

cases.diagnosis: Incidental WHO grade 1 (benign) convexity meningioma — managed with observation
pearls: Extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail = meningioma until proven otherwise.
Small, asymptomatic meningiomas without edema are managed with serial surveillance MRI, not immediate surgery.
Intervene for documented growth, new symptoms, or significant edema — not for patient anxiety alone.
Recurrence risk depends most on WHO grade and completeness of resection (Simpson grade).

cases.title: Both Legs Are Getting Weak
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive weakness of both legs over two months, now with difficulty walking.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman is admitted with two months of progressive bilateral lower-extremity weakness and several recent falls. She describes heaviness in both legs, worse over the past two weeks, with new urinary urgency. She has also had mild headaches. She initially thought she had a 'pinched nerve in her back.' She denies arm symptoms, back pain at a specific level, fever, or trauma. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior breast cyst (benign). Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, no alcohol, retired postal worker, lives alone. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems negative for weight loss, sensory level in the trunk that she can localize, or saddle anesthesia. She can still feel her legs but they 'don't do what she wants.' She has had no prior spine imaging. On admission the team initially considered a thoracic spinal cord lesion given the bilateral leg findings, but her exam and the lack of a clear sensory level prompted reconsideration.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented; normal language and cognition. Cranial nerves intact; no papilledema. Motor: symmetric bilateral lower-extremity weakness 3+/5 (proximal and distal), with bilateral leg spasticity; upper extremities 5/5. Reflexes brisk (3+) at both knees and ankles with bilateral Babinski signs; arm reflexes normal. No discrete spinal sensory level; light touch and proprioception in legs largely preserved. Gait spastic and unsafe. No saddle anesthesia; rectal tone intact. The combination of bilateral leg upper-motor-neuron signs without a clear cord sensory level is notable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her bilateral leg upper-motor-neuron weakness without a clear spinal sensory level could arise from a parasagittal intracranial lesion. Why does such a lesion mimic a spinal cord process?
options: It compresses the cervical nerve roots
It compresses the medial motor cortex bilaterally, where the leg representation (homunculus) lies along the interhemispheric falx
It causes a peripheral polyneuropathy
It blocks the cauda equina
stages.explanation: The leg representation of the motor and sensory homunculus lies on the medial surface of the hemispheres along the interhemispheric (parasagittal) region. A parasagittal mass, such as a falcine or parasagittal meningioma, can compress both medial frontal lobes and produce bilateral leg weakness with upper-motor-neuron signs, mimicking a thoracic cord lesion. It does not compress cervical roots, which would affect the arms. It does not cause a length-dependent peripheral polyneuropathy. It is intracranial and so does not block the cauda equina. Recognizing that bilateral leg weakness can be cortical in origin prevents anchoring solely on the spine.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI thoracic and cervical spine: no cord compression, no intramedullary lesion, no significant stenosis.
MRI brain with and without contrast: 3.4 cm extra-axial, dural-based mass arising from the superior sagittal sinus region (parasagittal), homogeneously enhancing with a dural tail.
The mass compresses the medial frontal lobes (paracentral lobules) bilaterally with adjacent vasogenic edema.
Patency of the superior sagittal sinus partially preserved.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the spine MRI is normal and the brain MRI shows a parasagittal dural-based mass, what is the diagnosis?
options: Thoracic spinal cord tumor
Parasagittal meningioma causing bilateral leg weakness
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Guillain-Barré syndrome
stages.explanation: The normal spine MRI excludes a cord lesion, and the brain MRI demonstrates a parasagittal dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail compressing the bilateral paracentral lobules — a parasagittal meningioma producing bilateral leg weakness. ALS causes mixed upper- and lower-motor-neuron signs without a mass and typically spares sensation and sphincter function with characteristic findings like fasciculations and bulbar involvement over time. Guillain-Barré is an acute areflexic ascending flaccid paralysis — the opposite of her brisk reflexes and spasticity. The imaging plus the upper-motor-neuron pattern confirms a parasagittal meningioma as the cause.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has progressive, symptomatic weakness from a sizable parasagittal meningioma with edema. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year
Dexamethasone for edema followed by surgical resection
Lumbar drain placement
Physical therapy alone
stages.explanation: A symptomatic, sizable meningioma producing progressive neurologic deficits and edema warrants definitive treatment. Dexamethasone reduces the peritumoral edema and can improve symptoms while surgical resection removes the compressive mass and is the treatment of choice for accessible, symptomatic meningiomas. Observation is inappropriate for a progressively symptomatic lesion. A lumbar drain does not address an intracranial compressive mass and could be dangerous. Physical therapy is supportive but does not treat the underlying cause; the deficits will progress without removing the mass. Parasagittal location requires careful surgical planning around the superior sagittal sinus, but resection remains the definitive intervention.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Resecting a parasagittal meningioma carries a specific surgical risk related to its location. What is the most important structure to protect?
options: The internal carotid artery
The superior sagittal sinus and draining cortical veins
The optic chiasm
The brainstem
stages.explanation: Parasagittal meningiomas arise adjacent to and may invade the superior sagittal sinus, and resection risks injury to the sinus and the draining cortical (bridging) veins. Sacrificing a patent posterior superior sagittal sinus or key cortical veins can cause venous infarction, hemorrhage, and worsened neurologic deficits, so preserving venous drainage is paramount and dictates whether involved sinus is resected. The internal carotid artery and optic chiasm are at risk in skull-base or suprasellar locations, not the parasagittal convexity. The brainstem is remote from this lesion. Protecting the venous anatomy is the location-specific surgical priority for parasagittal meningiomas.

cases.diagnosis: Parasagittal meningioma compressing the bilateral paracentral lobules, mimicking a spinal cord lesion
pearls: The leg homunculus is medial (parasagittal) — a parasagittal mass causes bilateral leg weakness mimicking a cord lesion.
Bilateral leg UMN weakness without a clear sensory level should prompt brain imaging, not just spine imaging.
Symptomatic meningiomas with deficits/edema are treated with steroids plus surgical resection.
Parasagittal meningioma surgery threatens the superior sagittal sinus and cortical draining veins — preserving venous drainage is critical.

cases.title: The Mass Has Come Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up showing recurrence of a previously resected brain tumor.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man returns to clinic for surveillance after resection of a brain tumor 14 months ago. At that time he underwent a gross total resection of a left frontal convexity mass; pathology was an atypical meningioma, WHO grade 2, and he was managed with observation (no adjuvant radiation) per a shared decision. He has been well but reports a few new mild headaches over the past month. Routine surveillance MRI shows a new nodular enhancing lesion at the prior resection margin. He has no focal deficits. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, prior atypical meningioma. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, social alcohol, works as a high-school teacher, very active. Family history: noncontributory. He asks why this tumor came back and whether he should have had radiation the first time. His performance status is excellent (KPS 90). He has had no seizures and no new weakness or visual changes. He is eager to understand the role and timing of radiation now.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, fully oriented; normal cognition and fluent language. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields full; no papilledema. Motor 5/5 throughout; no pronator drift. Reflexes 2+ symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Sensation intact. Cerebellar testing normal; gait and tandem gait steady. Well-healed left frontal craniotomy incision without erythema or tenderness. No meningismus. The exam is normal aside from the imaging finding of recurrence.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Compared with WHO grade 1 meningiomas, atypical (WHO grade 2) meningiomas are characterized by which feature?
options: Lower recurrence rate and indolent behavior
Higher recurrence rate and more aggressive behavior (e.g., elevated mitotic count, brain invasion)
Inability to enhance with contrast
Origin from neurons rather than meningothelial cells
stages.explanation: Atypical (WHO grade 2) meningiomas have a substantially higher recurrence rate and more aggressive behavior than benign grade 1 tumors, defined by features such as an increased mitotic count (4-19 per 10 high-power fields) or brain invasion, or a combination of atypical histologic features. This is why grade 2 tumors recur more readily, as in this patient. They enhance avidly like other meningiomas, so failure to enhance is incorrect. Meningiomas arise from meningothelial (arachnoid cap) cells, not neurons. The higher grade explains both his recurrence and the need to reconsider adjuvant radiation strategy.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE MRI
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast: new 1.4 cm nodular, avidly enhancing lesion at the medial margin of the prior left frontal resection cavity, with a dural tail.
No significant edema; no midline shift.
Comparison with prior post-op MRI (which showed no residual enhancing tumor): the lesion is new, consistent with local recurrence.
No distant intracranial lesions; no leptomeningeal enhancement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has a discrete, accessible local recurrence with excellent performance status. What is the most appropriate management of the recurrent lesion?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year
Re-resection (with consideration of adjuvant radiation), or radiosurgery if not resectable
Systemic cytotoxic chemotherapy as first-line
Whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: For a documented, discrete, accessible local recurrence of an atypical meningioma in a fit patient, repeat surgical resection is preferred to remove tumor, confirm grade (it can progress to grade 3), and relieve any mass effect, with adjuvant radiation considered thereafter; stereotactic radiosurgery or fractionated radiotherapy is appropriate when the lesion is not safely resectable. Observation is not appropriate for a confirmed recurrent higher-grade tumor. Systemic chemotherapy has limited efficacy in meningioma and is not first-line. Whole-brain radiation is not used for a focal recurrence given its cognitive toxicity; focal radiation techniques are preferred. Re-resection plus focal radiation best addresses recurrent grade 2 disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Regarding adjuvant radiation timing for atypical meningioma, which statement best reflects current practice and the dilemma in his case?
options: Radiation is never used for grade 2 meningiomas
Adjuvant radiation after resection of a grade 2 meningioma improves local control, and recurrence (as here) strengthens the case for radiation rather than observation alone
Radiation should replace surgery entirely as first-line for all grade 2 tumors
Radiation is only indicated for grade 1 tumors
stages.explanation: Adjuvant radiation after resection of atypical (grade 2) meningioma improves local control, and although its routine use after gross total resection has been debated, recurrence — as occurred in this patient who was observed — strengthens the rationale for radiation rather than observation alone. After re-resection, adjuvant radiotherapy is generally recommended for recurrent grade 2 disease. Saying radiation is never used, or is only for grade 1 tumors, is incorrect; higher-grade tumors are precisely those for which radiation is considered. Radiation does not replace surgery as first-line when the tumor is resectable; surgery remains the cornerstone, with radiation as an adjuvant for higher grade, subtotal resection, or recurrence.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: He asks how future surveillance should differ now that he has recurred. What is the most appropriate plan?
options: Stop imaging since the recurrence will be treated
More frequent, long-term surveillance MRI because grade 2 meningiomas recur and can progress in grade over time
Annual MRI only for two years, then stop
Replace MRI with periodic CT to reduce cost
stages.explanation: Atypical meningiomas have a high propensity to recur and can transform to higher grade, so patients require more frequent and prolonged surveillance MRI to detect recurrence early when it is most treatable — especially after a documented recurrence. Stopping imaging is inappropriate because new recurrences must be caught. A fixed short course (annual for two years then stop) underestimates the long, sometimes late recurrence risk of grade 2 tumors, which can recur years out. Contrast-enhanced MRI is far superior to CT for detecting small dural-based recurrences, so substituting CT to save cost would compromise detection. Long-term contrast MRI surveillance is the standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If the re-resection pathology showed progression to anaplastic (WHO grade 3) meningioma with a high mitotic count, how would this change the recommendation?
options: It would favor observation, since grade 3 tumors are indolent
It would make adjuvant fractionated radiotherapy strongly indicated, given the aggressive, high-recurrence behavior of grade 3 disease
It would mean no further imaging is needed
It would indicate that hormonal therapy is now first-line
stages.explanation: Anaplastic (WHO grade 3) meningiomas are malignant, with high recurrence rates and a markedly worse prognosis, so adjuvant fractionated radiotherapy after maximal safe resection is strongly indicated to improve local control — observation would be inappropriate. Grade 3 tumors are aggressive, not indolent, so the first option is wrong. Surveillance imaging becomes more important, not unnecessary, given the high recurrence risk, so stopping imaging is incorrect. Hormonal therapy is not an established standard treatment for meningioma of any grade. Escalation to grade 3 thus converts a debatable radiation decision into a clear indication for adjuvant radiotherapy and intensified follow-up.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent atypical (WHO grade 2) meningioma after gross total resection — re-resection with adjuvant radiation
pearls: Atypical (WHO grade 2) meningiomas have high recurrence rates, defined by elevated mitotic count or brain invasion.
Adjuvant radiation improves local control in grade 2 meningiomas; recurrence after observation strengthens the case for radiation.
Discrete, accessible recurrences are best treated with re-resection (to reconfirm grade), then focal radiation; avoid whole-brain RT.
Grade 2 meningiomas need more frequent, long-term contrast-enhanced MRI surveillance because they recur and can progress in grade.

cases.title: Confusion and Low Sodium in a Man With a Brain Tumor
cases.chiefComplaint: New confusion and lethargy three days into a hospital admission for a newly diagnosed brain tumor.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man was admitted four days ago after an MRI showed a 3 cm enhancing left temporal mass, likely a high-grade glioma, found during workup of new-onset focal seizures. He was started on dexamethasone and levetiracetam and is awaiting biopsy. Over the past 24 hours the nursing staff report he has become increasingly lethargic, slow to answer questions, and had one episode of disorientation overnight. He denies headache, vomiting, diarrhea, polyuria, or excessive thirst. He has been eating and drinking normally and has not been on diuretics. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, dexamethasone 4 mg every 6 hours, levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He has had no IV fluids running other than a saline lock. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, dyspnea, or new weakness beyond his baseline mild right facial droop.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable, oriented to self and place but not date. Mucous membranes moist; no axillary dryness. No jugular venous distension and no peripheral edema. Skin turgor normal; capillary refill <2 seconds. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams unremarkable. Abdomen soft and nontender. Neurologically he has a stable mild right facial droop and mild right pronator drift, unchanged from admission; no new focal deficit. No asterixis. No neck stiffness. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. Gait not tested due to drowsiness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step to evaluate his acute change in mental status?
options: Obtain a stat non-contrast head CT to rule out tumor hemorrhage
Send a basic metabolic panel including serum sodium and a point-of-care glucose
Increase the dexamethasone dose empirically for presumed worsening edema
Start empiric antibiotics for possible meningitis
stages.explanation: New confusion in an inpatient has a broad differential, but a focused, rapid, low-cost metabolic screen (sodium, glucose, calcium) catches the most common reversible causes and should come first, especially since hyponatremia is frequent in brain tumor patients. A head CT is reasonable if a structural cause like hemorrhage is suspected, but his focal exam is unchanged and CT is not the highest-yield first step before basic labs. Empirically increasing dexamethasone treats presumed edema without data and can cause harm if the cause is metabolic. Antibiotics for meningitis are unwarranted: he is afebrile with no meningismus and a clear alternative differential.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 122 mEq/L
K 4.1 mEq/L
Cl 90 mEq/L
HCO3 24 mEq/L
BUN 8 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.7 mg/dL
Glucose 138 mg/dL
Serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 540 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 68 mEq/L
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
AM cortisol 24 µg/dL
Uric acid 3.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains his hyponatremia?
options: Hypovolemic hyponatremia from poor intake
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
Hyperglycemic pseudohyponatremia
Primary polydipsia
stages.explanation: He has hypotonic hyponatremia (serum osm 258) with inappropriately concentrated urine (540 mOsm/kg), elevated urine sodium (>40 mEq/L), low BUN/uric acid, and clinical euvolemia, with normal thyroid and adrenal function. This is the classic profile of SIADH, well described with intracranial tumors. Hypovolemic hyponatremia would show clinical volume depletion, low urine sodium (<20 mEq/L), and elevated BUN, none of which are present. His glucose of 138 is near-normal and cannot cause significant pseudohyponatremia. Primary polydipsia produces maximally dilute urine (<100 mOsm/kg), the opposite of his concentrated urine.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His sodium is 122 mEq/L and he is symptomatic but not seizing. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Rapid correction with a 100 mL bolus of 3% hypertonic saline repeated to normalize sodium quickly
Aggressive isotonic (0.9%) saline at 200 mL/hr
Fluid restriction to roughly 800-1000 mL/day with close sodium monitoring
Start a loop diuretic plus oral salt tabs as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderately symptomatic chronic SIADH (confusion without seizures), first-line therapy is fluid restriction with serial sodium monitoring, correcting no faster than 6-8 mEq/L in 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination. A single weight-based dose of hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptoms such as seizures or obtundation; repeated boluses to 'normalize quickly' risk overcorrection. Isotonic saline can paradoxically worsen SIADH because the retained free water and high urine osmolality cause the salt to be excreted while water is retained. Loop diuretics plus salt are second-line adjuncts, not the initial step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
