stages.question: After 12 hours of fluid restriction his sodium has risen from 122 to 132 mEq/L. What is the most appropriate next action?
options: Continue fluid restriction unchanged since the trend is favorable
Recognize overcorrection and administer 5% dextrose in water and/or desmopressin to relimit the rise
Add tolvaptan to accelerate correction further
Begin 3% hypertonic saline to push sodium into the normal range
stages.explanation: A 10 mEq/L rise in 12 hours exceeds the safe limit of 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours and risks osmotic demyelination syndrome. The correct response is to deliberately slow or relower the sodium using free water (D5W) and/or desmopressin to keep the 24-hour rise within target. Continuing restriction unchanged ignores a dangerous overcorrection. Tolvaptan and hypertonic saline would both accelerate the rise, worsening the risk. Recognizing and reversing overcorrection is a high-yield safety principle in hyponatremia management.

cases.diagnosis: SIADH secondary to an intracranial tumor
pearls: SIADH causes euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia with concentrated urine (>100 mOsm/kg), urine Na >40 mEq/L, and low BUN/uric acid; always confirm normal thyroid and adrenal function first.
First-line treatment for chronic SIADH with mild-moderate symptoms is fluid restriction; hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptoms like seizures or coma.
Correct sodium no faster than 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours; overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome and may require D5W and/or desmopressin to relower.
Isotonic saline can worsen SIADH because high urine osmolality leads to salt excretion with water retention.

cases.title: Falling Sodium After Brain Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive hyponatremia on postoperative day five after resection of a skull base tumor.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman is in the neuro-ICU on postoperative day five after craniotomy for resection of a clival meningioma that abutted the third ventricle. Her early postoperative course was uneventful, but over the last 48 hours her serum sodium has fallen and she has become increasingly drowsy with one episode of a brief focal seizure this morning, now controlled. Nursing notes record high urine output (4.2 L over the last 24 hours) and she has felt lightheaded when sitting up. She denies excess water drinking. She has not received mannitol since the day of surgery and is on maintenance isotonic fluids. PMH: migraine, well controlled. Medications: acetaminophen, levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily (started postop), pantoprazole, subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: nonsmoker, occasional wine. Family history: noncontributory. There has been no diarrhea or vomiting. Intake and output records show that despite isotonic fluids, she has been in net negative fluid balance with rising heart rate and falling blood pressure trends over two days.
cases.exam: Drowsy, arousable to voice, oriented to self only. Mucous membranes dry; tongue furrowed. JVP is flat. Skin tenting present over the sternum; capillary refill 3 seconds. Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft. Postoperative scalp incision clean and dry. Neurologically she has no new focal motor deficit beyond mild generalized weakness; pupils equal and reactive. No peripheral edema. Postural lightheadedness limits gait testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of bedside findings is most important to characterize before treating her hyponatremia?
options: Her dietary salt intake over the past week
Her volume status, including orthostatics, mucous membranes, JVP, and fluid balance
Her serum potassium and magnesium only
Her preoperative thyroid function
stages.explanation: The single most important step in evaluating hyponatremia is determining volume status, because it separates the two leading post-craniotomy causes: SIADH (euvolemia) and cerebral salt wasting (hypovolemia). Orthostatic vital signs, mucous membranes, JVP, skin turgor, and cumulative fluid balance directly inform this. Dietary salt history is far less reliable and slow to obtain in the ICU. Potassium and magnesium are worth checking but do not distinguish the key differential. Preoperative thyroid function is relevant to general hyponatremia workup but is not the priority finding when acute postoperative volume status will drive immediate management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na 121 mEq/L
K 4.3 mEq/L
BUN 26 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Serum osmolality 255 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 620 mOsm/kg
Urine sodium 95 mEq/L
Urine output 4.2 L/24h
Uric acid 2.4 mg/dL
Hematocrit 47% (up from 41% preop)
CVP 2 mmHg
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL
AM cortisol 22 µg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and laboratory picture?
options: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)
Cerebral salt wasting (CSW)
Central diabetes insipidus
Iatrogenic hyponatremia from hypotonic fluids
stages.explanation: She has hypotonic hyponatremia with high urine sodium and high urine output, but unlike SIADH she is clearly hypovolemic: orthostatic hypotension, dry mucous membranes, flat JVP, low CVP, rising hematocrit and BUN, and net negative fluid balance. This volume depletion with renal salt wasting defines cerebral salt wasting, classically seen after intracranial surgery or subarachnoid hemorrhage. SIADH is euvolemic or mildly hypervolemic with normal/low BUN and uric acid but normal volume; here the patient is volume depleted. Diabetes insipidus produces dilute urine (low osmolality), not the concentrated urine seen here. Hypotonic fluids were not used, and her urine sodium is far too high for simple dilution.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment that distinguishes management of CSW from SIADH?
options: Strict fluid restriction to 800 mL/day
Volume and sodium repletion with isotonic (or hypertonic) saline
Tolvaptan to block ADH receptors
Free water (D5W) infusion to dilute the urine
stages.explanation: CSW is a state of renal sodium and volume loss, so the treatment is volume and sodium repletion, typically with isotonic saline (hypertonic saline if sodium is dangerously low or symptoms severe), the opposite of SIADH. Fluid restriction, the cornerstone of SIADH therapy, would dangerously worsen the hypovolemia of CSW and can precipitate hypotension and cerebral ischemia, which is precisely why distinguishing the two matters. Tolvaptan treats euvolemic/hypervolemic hyponatremia and would worsen volume depletion. D5W adds free water and would lower sodium further. Fludrocortisone is a recognized adjunct to promote sodium retention in refractory CSW.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is symptomatic (drowsy, recent seizure) with a sodium of 121 mEq/L. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Give 3% hypertonic saline (e.g., 100-150 mL bolus) and reassess sodium
Begin fluid restriction and recheck sodium in 6 hours
Administer desmopressin to retain free water
Give a 1 L bolus of D5W to treat hypovolemia
stages.explanation: With symptomatic hyponatremia (altered mental status plus a recent seizure) at 121 mEq/L, the immediate intervention is hypertonic (3%) saline in a controlled bolus to raise sodium a few mEq/L and abort neurologic symptoms, while continuing isotonic repletion for the underlying volume deficit; correction is still capped at 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours. Fluid restriction is contraindicated in CSW and is too slow for a symptomatic patient. Desmopressin retains free water and would worsen the hyponatremia. A D5W bolus provides free water and would acutely drop sodium further, risking worsened cerebral edema and seizures.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As her sodium and volume status normalize over several days, what is the expected natural history of CSW that should guide her disposition?
options: It is a permanent disorder requiring lifelong salt supplementation
It is typically self-limited, resolving over days to a few weeks as the CNS insult recovers
It invariably progresses to chronic kidney disease
It will convert to SIADH and require permanent fluid restriction
stages.explanation: Cerebral salt wasting is generally a self-limited phenomenon that resolves over days to a few weeks as the underlying CNS insult (surgery, hemorrhage) heals, so management is supportive volume and sodium repletion until renal salt handling normalizes; lifelong therapy is not required. It does not inherently cause permanent kidney disease, as the kidneys are structurally normal and the defect is in natriuretic signaling. While SIADH and CSW can be confused, CSW does not 'convert' to SIADH requiring permanent fluid restriction; conflating the two and restricting fluids would harm the patient.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral salt wasting after craniotomy
pearls: CSW and SIADH both cause hypotonic hyponatremia with high urine sodium; the key differentiator is volume status — CSW is hypovolemic (orthostasis, high BUN, rising hematocrit, low CVP).
Treat CSW with volume and sodium repletion (isotonic/hypertonic saline); fluid restriction (the SIADH treatment) is dangerous in CSW.
Symptomatic severe hyponatremia (seizure, obtundation) warrants 3% hypertonic saline regardless of cause, still limiting correction to 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours.
CSW is usually self-limited, resolving over days to weeks as the CNS insult recovers; fludrocortisone is an adjunct for refractory cases.

cases.title: Double Vision and Facial Numbness in a Man With Sinus Symptoms
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of double vision, a droopy right eyelid, and numbness over the right cheek and forehead.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man presents to clinic with two months of horizontal double vision that is worse on right gaze, accompanied by a progressively drooping right upper eyelid. Over the same period he has noticed numbness over his right forehead, cheek, and upper teeth. He reports several months of right-sided nasal stuffiness, intermittent blood-tinged nasal discharge, decreased hearing in the right ear with a sensation of fullness, and a firm lump he can feel in the right upper neck. He denies headache, fever, or weight loss. PMH: GERD. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (hives). Social: born in southern China, immigrated 20 years ago; 15-pack-year smoking history; occasional alcohol; works in construction. Family history: an uncle with 'throat cancer.' Review of systems is notable for the unilateral nasal and ear symptoms and the neck mass; no limb weakness, no vertigo, no dysphagia. He has not had prior imaging or ENT evaluation.
cases.exam: Right partial ptosis. Extraocular movements show impaired abduction and limited adduction and elevation of the right eye (deficits referable to CN III, IV, and VI); the left eye moves fully. Right pupil mid-position and minimally reactive. Diplopia on right and upward gaze. Decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the right V1 and V2 distributions; V3 and the corneal reflex are intact. Facial strength symmetric (CN VII intact). Right ear with a dull, retracted tympanic membrane and a conductive hearing pattern on Weber/Rinne. A firm, fixed 2.5 cm right level II cervical lymph node is palpable. No proptosis. Remainder of cranial nerve and limb exam normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The combination of CN III, IV, and VI palsies with V1 and V2 sensory loss but a normal V3 and sympathetic-appearing pupil findings localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Orbital apex
Cavernous sinus
Internal auditory canal
Jugular foramen
stages.explanation: The cavernous sinus contains CN III, IV, VI and the V1 and V2 branches of the trigeminal nerve, plus the internal carotid artery and its sympathetic plexus. Multiple ophthalmoplegias combined with V1/V2 sensory loss but sparing of V3 (which runs outside the sinus through foramen ovale) is the signature of a cavernous sinus syndrome. The orbital apex syndrome would additionally involve the optic nerve (CN II) with vision loss, which he lacks. The internal auditory canal carries CN VII and VIII, not the ocular motor nerves. The jugular foramen transmits CN IX, X, and XI, producing dysphagia and shoulder weakness rather than ophthalmoplegia.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
MRI head/neck with contrast: enhancing soft-tissue mass centered in the right nasopharynx (fossa of Rosenmüller) with skull base erosion and extension into the right cavernous sinus encasing the cavernous ICA.
Obliteration of the right fossa of Rosenmüller with mastoid effusion (eustachian tube obstruction).
Right retropharyngeal and level II nodal enlargement.
Nasopharyngeal biopsy: non-keratinizing undifferentiated carcinoma.
In situ hybridization positive for Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)-encoded RNA.
Plasma EBV DNA: elevated.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best accounts for the entire clinical and imaging picture?
options: Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma with perineural and skull base extension into the cavernous sinus
Tolosa-Hunt syndrome
Pituitary macroadenoma with apoplexy
stages.explanation: A southern-Chinese man with unilateral nasal obstruction, blood-tinged discharge, serous otitis from eustachian tube blockage, a neck node, and an EBV-positive non-keratinizing nasopharyngeal mass eroding the skull base into the cavernous sinus is the classic presentation of nasopharyngeal carcinoma causing a cavernous sinus syndrome. Cavernous sinus thrombosis is typically acute and febrile with proptosis, chemosis, and an infectious source, not a slowly progressive mass with a nasopharyngeal primary. Tolosa-Hunt is a diagnosis of exclusion (idiopathic granulomatous inflammation) and would not explain a nasopharyngeal mass and node. A pituitary macroadenoma is sellar/suprasellar and would not arise from the nasopharynx or cause unilateral otitis and a neck node.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond the nasopharyngeal biopsy already performed, what is the most appropriate next step in staging and management planning?
options: Excisional biopsy of the neck node for primary diagnosis
PET/CT (or CT chest/abdomen) and referral for definitive chemoradiation
Empiric high-dose IV antibiotics and anticoagulation
Immediate craniotomy for surgical resection of the cavernous sinus disease
stages.explanation: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is staged with cross-sectional and metabolic imaging (PET/CT or CT chest/abdomen/pelvis plus dedicated head/neck MRI) and treated primarily with radiation therapy, adding concurrent chemotherapy for locally advanced disease; it is highly radiosensitive. The diagnosis is already established by nasopharyngeal biopsy, so excisional neck-node biopsy is unnecessary and can complicate later neck dissection. Antibiotics and anticoagulation treat cavernous sinus thrombosis, which this is not. Surgical resection of cavernous sinus tumor extension is not the primary modality for NPC; the deep skull base location and radiosensitivity favor chemoradiation over upfront craniotomy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the primary treatment modality for locally advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma such as this?
options: Surgery alone
Concurrent chemoradiation (radiotherapy with platinum-based chemotherapy)
Immunotherapy monotherapy
Palliative care only
stages.explanation: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is exquisitely radiosensitive, and locally advanced disease (with skull base and cavernous sinus invasion plus nodal involvement) is treated with concurrent chemoradiation using platinum-based chemotherapy, often with induction or adjuvant chemotherapy. Surgery alone is not standard because of the deep, surgically inaccessible location and the tumor's radiosensitivity. Immunotherapy is used in recurrent/metastatic settings, not as upfront monotherapy for curable locoregional disease. Palliative care alone is inappropriate for potentially curable locally advanced NPC; even with cavernous sinus extension, definitive chemoradiation offers meaningful cure rates.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which test is most useful for monitoring treatment response and surveillance for recurrence in EBV-associated nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
options: Serial plasma EBV DNA levels
Monthly lumbar punctures
Serum calcium and PTH
Repeated nasopharyngeal biopsies every month
stages.explanation: Plasma EBV DNA is a validated biomarker in EBV-associated nasopharyngeal carcinoma: pretreatment levels carry prognostic value, falling levels track treatment response, and a rising level after therapy is an early marker of recurrence, making serial measurement the most useful monitoring tool here. Lumbar punctures are not used for solid-tumor surveillance and carry no role unless leptomeningeal spread is specifically suspected. Calcium and PTH monitor parathyroid disorders, irrelevant to NPC follow-up. Repeating invasive nasopharyngeal biopsies monthly is unnecessary and morbid when a noninvasive blood biomarker and imaging suffice.

cases.diagnosis: Cavernous sinus syndrome from nasopharyngeal carcinoma invasion
pearls: Cavernous sinus syndrome = ophthalmoplegia (CN III, IV, VI) plus V1/V2 sensory loss with V3 sparing; orbital apex syndrome adds CN II (vision) involvement.

Nasopharyngeal carcinoma classically presents with unilateral serous otitis media, blood-tinged nasal discharge, a neck node, and cranial neuropathies from skull base invasion; strongly associated with EBV and southern-Chinese ancestry.
NPC is highly radiosensitive — locally advanced disease is treated with concurrent chemoradiation, not upfront surgery.
Plasma EBV DNA is a useful prognostic and surveillance biomarker in EBV-associated NPC.

cases.title: A Hoarse Voice, Trouble Swallowing, and a Drooping Shoulder
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of progressive hoarseness, difficulty swallowing, and right shoulder weakness.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with three months of progressively worsening hoarseness and a sensation that food and liquids 'go down the wrong way,' with occasional coughing while drinking. She also reports a dull right-sided deep ear and upper neck ache and that her right shoulder droops and tires easily when she lifts her arm or carries bags. She denies limb weakness, facial droop, vertigo, or double vision. She has had no fevers or weight loss but notes mild unintentional weight loss from reduced oral intake due to swallowing difficulty. PMH: hypertension, osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: 20-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago; social alcohol; retired teacher. Family history: mother with breast cancer. Review of systems otherwise negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, or bowel/bladder changes. She has not had prior imaging or ENT evaluation and reports the symptoms have been steadily worsening rather than fluctuating.
cases.exam: Hoarse, breathy voice. Palate elevation is asymmetric with the uvula deviating to the left; the right palatal arch is lower. Gag reflex diminished on the right. Right vocal cord appears immobile on flexible laryngoscopy. Decreased sensation over the right posterior pharynx. There is right sternocleidomastoid weakness (impaired head turn to the left) and trapezius weakness with shoulder drop and limited shoulder shrug on the right. Tongue protrudes midline without atrophy or fasciculations (CN XII intact). No ptosis, no anisocoria, no facial sensory loss, normal hearing. Limb strength, reflexes, and gait are normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Right-sided palatal/pharyngeal weakness with a diminished gag, vocal cord paralysis, and weakness of the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius — with a normal tongue — localizes to which structure?
options: Cavernous sinus
Jugular foramen
Internal auditory canal
Foramen magnum
stages.explanation: The jugular foramen transmits cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and XI (spinal accessory). Involvement of all three produces the Vernet (jugular foramen) syndrome: loss of taste/sensation and gag from CN IX, palatal/pharyngeal/laryngeal weakness with hoarseness and dysphagia from CN X, and sternocleidomastoid/trapezius weakness from CN XI. The preserved tongue (CN XII, which exits the separate hypoglossal canal) helps localize to the jugular foramen rather than a more diffuse skull base lesion. The cavernous sinus involves ocular cranial nerves and V1/V2. The internal auditory canal carries CN VII and VIII. The foramen magnum lesion would cause long-tract and high cervical signs, not isolated IX-XI palsies.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI skull base with contrast: enhancing mass centered at the right jugular foramen with bony erosion of the foramen margins and 'salt-and-pepper' flow voids.
The mass extends into the right hypoglossal canal minimally but the carotid space is involved.
CT temporal bone: permeative-destructive erosion of the jugular foramen.
No cerebellopontine angle component.
No restricted diffusion.
Urine metanephrines: within normal limits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the imaging characteristics, which tumor most likely accounts for a vascular, enhancing jugular foramen mass with 'salt-and-pepper' appearance?
options: Vestibular schwannoma
Glomus jugulare (jugular paraganglioma)
Epidermoid cyst
Meningioma of the cerebellopontine angle
stages.explanation: A highly vascular, avidly enhancing jugular foramen mass with characteristic 'salt-and-pepper' flow voids on MRI and permeative bony erosion is the classic appearance of a glomus jugulare (jugular paraganglioma), the most common tumor of the jugular foramen and a leading cause of jugular foramen syndrome. Vestibular schwannomas arise in the internal auditory canal/CPA causing CN VIII symptoms, not lower cranial nerve palsies. Epidermoid cysts are non-enhancing, restrict on diffusion, and insinuate around structures rather than erode in this vascular pattern. CPA meningiomas are dural-based and enhance homogeneously but would not produce the salt-and-pepper flow voids or center on the jugular foramen with this vascularity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before any biopsy or surgical planning of this suspected vascular jugular foramen tumor, which step is most important?
options: Perform a percutaneous needle biopsy in clinic
Obtain catheter angiography and consider preoperative embolization; screen for catecholamine secretion
Start anticoagulation for presumed jugular vein thrombosis
Begin empiric chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Paragangliomas are highly vascular, so blind or percutaneous biopsy risks severe hemorrhage and is generally avoided; instead, catheter angiography characterizes the blood supply and enables preoperative embolization to reduce intraoperative bleeding. Because a minority are catecholamine-secreting, biochemical screening (plasma/urine metanephrines) is prudent before any intervention to avoid an intraoperative hypertensive crisis. Anticoagulation is inappropriate; there is no thrombosis, and it would increase bleeding risk in a vascular tumor. Empiric chemotherapy is not indicated for a localized paraganglioma, which is managed with surgery and/or radiotherapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which statement best reflects appropriate definitive management options for a glomus jugulare tumor causing lower cranial nerve deficits?
options: Observation is never appropriate; immediate radical resection is mandatory in all patients
Treatment is individualized among surgical resection, radiotherapy (including stereotactic radiosurgery), or observation depending on size, symptoms, and patient factors
Systemic chemotherapy is the standard first-line therapy
Lifelong antibiotics are the mainstay of therapy
stages.explanation: Glomus jugulare tumors are typically slow-growing, so management is individualized: surgical resection (often with preoperative embolization), radiotherapy including stereotactic radiosurgery for tumor control with cranial nerve preservation, or observation with serial imaging in older patients or small asymptomatic tumors. Radical resection in every patient is not mandatory and carries substantial risk of worsening lower cranial nerve deficits. Systemic chemotherapy is not standard first-line therapy for localized paraganglioma. Antibiotics have no role in treating a neoplasm. The emphasis is on balancing tumor control against cranial nerve morbidity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her right vocal cord paralysis and dysphagia with coughing on liquids, which supportive intervention is most important to prevent a serious complication?
options: Encourage thin liquids to maintain hydration
Formal swallow evaluation with aspiration precautions and dietary modification
Routine PPI escalation only
Immediate tracheostomy in all cases
stages.explanation: Vagal (CN X) injury with vocal cord paralysis and pharyngeal weakness markedly raises aspiration risk, so a formal swallow evaluation (e.g., by speech-language pathology, often with video fluoroscopy) with aspiration precautions and diet texture modification is the key step to prevent aspiration pneumonia. Encouraging thin liquids would worsen aspiration in a patient already coughing on liquids. PPI escalation addresses reflux, not neurogenic dysphagia and aspiration. Tracheostomy is not required in all cases and is reserved for severe airway compromise or unmanageable secretions, not as a routine first measure.

cases.diagnosis: Jugular foramen (Vernet) syndrome from a glomus jugulare tumor
pearls: Jugular foramen (Vernet) syndrome = CN IX, X, XI palsies: diminished gag, dysphagia/hoarseness with vocal cord paralysis, and sternocleidomastoid/trapezius weakness; a spared tongue (CN XII) points to the jugular foramen rather than diffuse skull base disease.
A vascular, avidly enhancing jugular foramen mass with 'salt-and-pepper' flow voids and permeative bone erosion is a glomus jugulare (paraganglioma).
Avoid percutaneous biopsy of suspected paragangliomas; obtain angiography for embolization and screen for catecholamine secretion before intervention.
Vagal vocal cord paralysis with dysphagia demands a formal swallow evaluation and aspiration precautions to prevent aspiration pneumonia.

cases.title: Persistent Numbness of the Chin and Lower Lip
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of numbness confined to the right chin and lower lip.
cases.history: A 62-year-old woman presents with six weeks of persistent numbness over her right chin and lower lip that she first noticed while applying lipstick. The numbness has not improved and now feels slightly worse, with occasional tingling. She denies facial pain, weakness, drooping, or numbness elsewhere on the face. She had a dental evaluation and a panoramic dental X-ray, and her dentist found no cavity or abscess to explain the symptom. PMH: estrogen-receptor-positive invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast treated 4 years ago with lumpectomy, radiation, and adjuvant endocrine therapy; she was told she was in remission. Hypertension. Medications: anastrozole 1 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, rare alcohol, retired librarian. Family history: mother with breast cancer. Review of systems notable for mild new low back ache for a month and fatigue; no weight loss reported, no headaches, no visual changes. She has had no recent dental procedures or trauma to the jaw.
cases.exam: Cranial nerves: decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the right chin and lower lip in the distribution of the mental/inferior alveolar nerve (V3 terminal branch); sensation over the right cheek (V2) and forehead (V1) is intact, and the rest of the trigeminal exam including masseter/temporalis bulk and jaw strength is normal. Facial movements symmetric (CN VII intact). No oral mucosal lesion or dental abnormality on exam. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable. Mild tenderness to palpation over the lower lumbar spine. Remainder of the cranial nerve, motor, sensory, and gait exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with a history of cancer presenting with isolated numbness of the chin and lower lip, what is the most appropriate interpretation and next step?
options: It is almost always benign; reassure and observe for 6 months
Treat as numb chin syndrome — a red flag for malignancy — and pursue imaging of the mandible/skull base and systemic restaging
Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia and start carbamazepine
Attribute it to dental disease and refer back to the dentist
stages.explanation: Numb chin syndrome (mental neuropathy) is an isolated sensory loss in the mental nerve distribution and, especially in a patient with known cancer, is a well-recognized red flag for malignancy — most often metastatic involvement of the mandible or skull base, or leptomeningeal disease, particularly from breast cancer and lymphoma. The appropriate step is dedicated imaging (mandible/skull base MRI or CT, and brain MRI) plus systemic restaging. Reassurance and observation risk missing progressive cancer. It is not trigeminal neuralgia, which causes paroxysmal lancinating pain, not steady numbness. A normal dental exam and X-ray already argue against a dental cause.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI mandible/skull base with contrast: enhancing infiltrative lesion in the right mandibular body involving the inferior alveolar (mandibular) canal and mental foramen, with marrow replacement.
MRI brain: no parenchymal or leptomeningeal enhancement.
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan: multiple lytic/sclerotic osseous lesions including lumbar vertebrae and ribs.
Alkaline phosphatase 168 U/L (elevated)
Calcium 10.0 mg/dL
CA 15-3 elevated
Bone biopsy: metastatic carcinoma consistent with breast primary; ER positive, HER2 negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the unifying diagnosis?
options: Idiopathic trigeminal sensory neuropathy
Numb chin syndrome from metastatic breast cancer to the mandible
Bell palsy
Postherpetic neuralgia
stages.explanation: The mental nerve numbness, an enhancing mandibular lesion infiltrating the inferior alveolar canal/mental foramen with marrow replacement, widespread osseous metastases, elevated tumor markers, and a bone biopsy showing ER-positive carcinoma together establish numb chin syndrome caused by metastatic breast cancer to the mandible. Idiopathic trigeminal sensory neuropathy is a diagnosis of exclusion and cannot be invoked with biopsy-proven metastasis. Bell palsy is a CN VII motor palsy, not a V3 sensory deficit. Postherpetic neuralgia follows a herpes zoster outbreak with a painful vesicular rash, which she never had, and would not cause an infiltrative bone lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Now that metastatic ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer with bone involvement is confirmed, which management combination is most appropriate?
options: Curative-intent mandibulectomy alone
Systemic therapy (e.g., CDK4/6 inhibitor plus endocrine therapy) with a bone-targeting agent, and consideration of palliative radiotherapy to symptomatic sites
Observation with repeat imaging in one year
High-dose carbamazepine for symptom control as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: Metastatic ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer is treated systemically — commonly a CDK4/6 inhibitor combined with endocrine therapy — along with a bone-modifying agent (bisphosphonate or denosumab) to reduce skeletal complications, plus palliative radiotherapy directed at symptomatic or structurally threatened sites such as the mandible or spine. Curative-intent mandibulectomy is inappropriate for widely metastatic disease. Observation for a year ignores progressive symptomatic metastatic cancer. Carbamazepine may help neuropathic symptoms but does not treat the cancer and is not definitive therapy; it addresses neuralgic pain, not the steady numbness or the underlying disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting denosumab or a bisphosphonate, which complication should be screened for and prevented?
options: Osteonecrosis of the jaw — arrange a dental evaluation and address dental disease beforehand
QT prolongation requiring continuous telemetry
Acute pancreatitis
Pulmonary fibrosis
stages.explanation: Bone-modifying agents (bisphosphonates and denosumab) carry a risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw, so a baseline dental evaluation with completion of needed dental work and good oral hygiene before and during therapy is standard preventive practice — especially relevant here given mandibular involvement. They also require monitoring and repletion of calcium and vitamin D to prevent hypocalcemia. QT prolongation, pancreatitis, and pulmonary fibrosis are not the characteristic risks of these bone agents, so they are not the screening priority before initiating therapy.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best characterizes the prognostic significance of numb chin syndrome in a cancer patient?
options: It is a benign incidental finding with no prognostic weight
It often signals advanced or progressive malignancy and is associated with a poor prognosis
It indicates the cancer is cured
It is unrelated to the underlying cancer
stages.explanation: Numb chin syndrome in a patient with known malignancy frequently heralds advanced, often disseminated or progressive disease, and carries a poor overall prognosis because it usually reflects metastatic bone involvement or leptomeningeal/perineural spread. It is not a benign incidental finding, does not indicate cure, and is directly related to the underlying cancer — in fact it can be the sentinel presenting sign that prompts discovery of metastasis. Recognizing its ominous significance drives prompt restaging and treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Numb chin syndrome from metastatic breast cancer to the mandible
pearls: Numb chin syndrome (mental neuropathy) is isolated numbness in the mental nerve (V3) distribution and is a red flag for malignancy — especially breast cancer and lymphoma — until proven otherwise.
Workup includes dedicated mandible/skull base imaging, brain MRI to assess for leptomeningeal disease, and systemic restaging; a normal dental exam should not end the evaluation.
It often reflects metastatic bone involvement or perineural/leptomeningeal spread and portends a poor prognosis.
Before bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy, arrange a dental evaluation to reduce the risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw and monitor calcium/vitamin D.

cases.title: Aching Arm and a Weak Hand After Breast Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of worsening right arm and hand pain with weakness of the hand.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with three months of deep, burning pain in the right axilla and medial forearm that radiates into the fourth and fifth fingers, now accompanied by clumsiness and weakness of the right hand. The pain is constant, worse at night, and poorly relieved by acetaminophen. She also reports tingling along the inner forearm and pinky side of the hand. She denies neck pain and the symptoms are not positional. PMH: right-sided invasive breast cancer treated 3 years ago with mastectomy, axillary node dissection, and adjuvant radiotherapy to the chest wall and axilla/supraclavicular fields; reportedly disease-free since. Hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, anastrozole 1 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker; occasional wine; works part-time as a seamstress. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: no fevers, mild fatigue, no weight loss; she notes a vague fullness in the right supraclavicular region. There is no history of recent trauma, and she reports the arm now occasionally feels swollen.
cases.exam: Right hand: weakness of the intrinsic hand muscles (interossei, thenar and hypothenar) with early wasting of the first dorsal interosseous; weak finger abduction and grip. Sensory loss to pinprick along the medial forearm and fourth/fifth digits (C8-T1 distribution). Right triceps reflex preserved; the deep tendon reflexes are otherwise symmetric. Subtle right ptosis and miosis are noted (partial Horner sign). There is firm fullness in the right supraclavicular fossa. Mild non-pitting edema of the right arm. The neck has full painless range of motion. Left upper limb and both lower limbs are neurologically normal. No skin changes of radiation fibrosis described as severe.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The pattern of pain, C8-T1 sensory loss, intrinsic hand weakness, and a partial Horner syndrome localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Upper trunk of the brachial plexus (C5-C6)
Lower trunk of the brachial plexus (C8-T1)
Median nerve at the carpal tunnel
C5-C6 cervical radiculopathy
stages.explanation: Pain in the axilla/medial arm, sensory loss over the medial forearm and fourth/fifth fingers, and weakness/wasting of the intrinsic hand muscles all map to the C8-T1 lower trunk of the brachial plexus. The associated Horner syndrome reflects involvement of the nearby sympathetic chain/stellate ganglion, indicating a lesion high near the plexus apex — a classic clue to tumor infiltration. Upper trunk (C5-C6) lesions affect the shoulder and biceps, not the hand intrinsics. Carpal tunnel affects the median-innervated thenar muscles and radial fingers, sparing the medial forearm and the ulnar digits. A C5-C6 radiculopathy would not produce C8-T1 hand findings or a Horner sign.

stages.label: IMAGING & STUDIES
stages.content:
MRI brachial plexus with contrast: enhancing infiltrative soft-tissue mass in the right lower brachial plexus extending toward the supraclavicular region and the apex of the lung; involvement of the C8-T1 components.
No cystic radiation-change appearance; the tissue is nodular and enhancing.
PET/CT: hypermetabolic right brachial plexus mass and supraclavicular nodes.
EMG/NCS: lower trunk plexopathy without myokymic discharges.
No significant lymphedema-only findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best distinguishes neoplastic brachial plexopathy from radiation-induced plexopathy in this patient?
options: Radiation plexopathy, supported by the painless presentation and myokymia
Neoplastic (tumor infiltration) plexopathy, supported by severe pain, Horner syndrome, an enhancing mass, and absence of myokymia
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Cervical disc herniation
stages.explanation: Several features favor neoplastic plexopathy over radiation injury: prominent, severe pain (radiation plexopathy is often painless or mildly painful), lower-trunk (C8-T1) predominance with Horner syndrome, an enhancing nodular mass on MRI, hypermetabolism on PET, and the absence of myokymic discharges on EMG. Radiation-induced plexopathy classically presents with less pain, often upper-trunk or diffuse involvement, T2 changes/fibrosis without a discrete enhancing mass, and characteristic myokymic discharges on EMG. Carpal tunnel and cervical disc disease do not explain a supraclavicular enhancing mass, Horner syndrome, or PET-avid nodes.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis and guide therapy?
options: Empiric physical therapy and gabapentin without further workup
Biopsy of the plexus mass/supraclavicular node and systemic restaging to confirm recurrent malignancy
Immediate surgical neurolysis of the plexus
Carpal tunnel release
stages.explanation: When imaging suggests a recurrent tumor infiltrating the plexus, tissue confirmation via image-guided biopsy of the mass or an accessible supraclavicular node, along with systemic restaging, is essential to confirm recurrence and direct oncologic therapy. Empiric symptomatic treatment alone would delay diagnosis of treatable recurrent cancer. Surgical neurolysis is not the diagnostic step and is generally not curative for infiltrative tumor; it may be considered selectively for symptom relief but not before diagnosis. Carpal tunnel release addresses an unrelated median nerve entrapment and would not help a lower-trunk neoplastic plexopathy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms recurrent breast carcinoma infiltrating the plexus. Which management approach is most appropriate?
options: Systemic oncologic therapy guided by tumor biology, with radiotherapy to the plexus and multimodal neuropathic pain control
Curative en bloc resection of the brachial plexus
Observation with serial imaging
Opioids alone with no oncologic treatment
stages.explanation: Recurrent metastatic breast cancer infiltrating the plexus is managed with systemic therapy chosen by receptor status (e.g., endocrine therapy with a targeted agent, or chemotherapy), local radiotherapy to the involved plexus for tumor control and pain, and aggressive multimodal neuropathic pain management (e.g., gabapentinoids, duloxetine, opioids as needed). En bloc resection of the plexus is not curative for infiltrative disease and causes major deficits. Observation alone neglects progressive disease. Opioids alone abandon disease-directed therapy that can both control the cancer and relieve pain.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding, if it had been present instead, would have most strongly suggested radiation-induced rather than neoplastic plexopathy?
options: Severe early pain and a Horner syndrome
Myokymic discharges on EMG and relatively painless, slowly progressive upper-plexus weakness
An enhancing nodular mass with PET avidity
Rapid progression with supraclavicular adenopathy
stages.explanation: Myokymic discharges on EMG are a hallmark of radiation-induced plexopathy, which also tends to be relatively painless, slowly progressive, and often upper-plexus or diffuse with diffuse fibrosis rather than a focal mass. Severe early pain and Horner syndrome point toward tumor infiltration, not radiation. An enhancing nodular mass with PET avidity indicates active tumor. Rapid progression with supraclavicular adenopathy likewise favors neoplastic recurrence. Recognizing myokymia and the pain profile is the classic board-level discriminator between these two entities.

cases.diagnosis: Neoplastic brachial plexopathy from recurrent breast cancer
pearls: Neoplastic plexopathy favors the lower trunk (C8-T1), is severely painful, often shows Horner syndrome and an enhancing mass, and lacks myokymia on EMG.
Radiation-induced plexopathy is typically less painful, more often upper-trunk/diffuse, shows fibrosis without a discrete mass, and characteristically has myokymic discharges on EMG.
A Horner syndrome accompanying a lower brachial plexopathy is a red flag for tumor infiltration near the plexus apex.
Confirm recurrence with image-guided biopsy and restaging; treat with systemic therapy, radiotherapy, and multimodal neuropathic pain control rather than radical resection.

cases.title: A Rapidly Growing Painful Lump in a Young Man With Skin Spots
cases.chiefComplaint: A previously stable lump on the thigh that has grown rapidly and become painful over two months.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents with a lump on the posterior right thigh that he has had for years as a soft, painless swelling, but over the past two months it has grown noticeably, become firm, and turned painful, with new shooting electric sensations down the leg and numbness on the lateral foot. He denies fever or weight loss but feels generally fatigued. PMH: neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), diagnosed in childhood, with multiple cutaneous neurofibromas and a known large plexiform neurofibroma of the right thigh that had been stable. Medications: none regularly. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, social alcohol, works as a warehouse supervisor. Family history: father and sister with NF1. Review of systems: the lump now interferes with sitting; he reports new foot drop developing. On further history he has numerous café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling. He has had no prior surgery on the mass and no recent trauma. He is anxious because the rapid change is unlike anything the mass has done before.
cases.exam: Skin shows numerous café-au-lait macules (>6, each >15 mm), axillary and inguinal freckling, and multiple soft cutaneous neurofibromas. On the posterior right thigh is a firm, fixed, tender 8 cm mass with a positive Tinel sign radiating down the leg. There is weakness of right ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop) and decreased sensation over the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot (peroneal/sciatic distribution). Lisch nodules are noted on slit-lamp by ophthalmology. The contralateral leg is normal. No regional lymphadenopathy palpable. Distal pulses intact. The previously known cutaneous neurofibromas elsewhere remain soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In an NF1 patient, a long-standing plexiform neurofibroma that becomes rapidly enlarging, painful, and produces new neurologic deficits should raise concern for what?
options: Benign growth of a neurofibroma — reassure and observe
Malignant transformation to a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST)
Simple lipoma
Deep vein thrombosis
stages.explanation: In NF1, new rapid growth, new or worsening pain (especially persistent or nocturnal), and new neurologic deficit arising in a previously stable plexiform neurofibroma are classic warning signs of malignant transformation to a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor. Plexiform neurofibromas are the precursor lesions that carry this lifetime risk. Reassurance and observation would be dangerous given these red flags. A lipoma is soft, painless, and would not cause a Tinel sign or neurologic deficit. DVT causes limb swelling and pain but not a discrete enlarging nerve mass with a Tinel sign and focal deficits.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
MRI thigh with contrast: 8 cm heterogeneous mass arising from the sciatic nerve with irregular margins, intratumoral necrosis, and peripheral enhancement; loss of the typical 'target sign.'
FDG-PET/CT: intensely hypermetabolic mass (high SUVmax) with no distant metastases.
Core-needle biopsy: high-grade spindle-cell sarcoma arising from a nerve sheath, high mitotic rate; immunostains support malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor.
Lungs clear on CT chest.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging/clinical feature most supports MPNST over a benign plexiform neurofibroma?
options: Homogeneous low metabolic activity on PET
Large size with rapid growth, ill-defined margins, intratumoral necrosis, loss of the target sign, and high FDG uptake
A clearly defined target sign with uniform low signal
Multiple small soft skin nodules
stages.explanation: Features favoring malignancy include large size and rapid growth, irregular/ill-defined margins, intratumoral necrosis, heterogeneity, loss of the benign 'target sign,' and intense FDG uptake (high SUVmax) on PET, which is useful to distinguish MPNST from benign neurofibroma and to guide biopsy of the most metabolically active area. Homogeneous low metabolic activity and a preserved target sign with uniform low signal are reassuring features of a benign neurofibroma. Multiple small soft skin nodules are typical benign cutaneous neurofibromas and do not by themselves indicate malignant transformation of the deep mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms a high-grade MPNST localized to the thigh without metastases. What is the cornerstone of curative-intent treatment?
options: Chemotherapy alone
Wide surgical resection with negative margins, often combined with radiotherapy
Radiotherapy alone
Long-term observation with serial MRI
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of curative treatment for a localized MPNST is wide local surgical resection achieving negative margins, frequently combined with radiotherapy to improve local control, particularly for large, high-grade tumors. Chemotherapy alone is not curative and has limited efficacy in MPNST; it is generally reserved for advanced/metastatic or selected high-risk cases. Radiotherapy alone does not eradicate gross disease and is adjuvant rather than definitive. Observation is inappropriate for a confirmed high-grade sarcoma, which is aggressive and prone to local recurrence and metastasis if not resected.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Compared with sporadic MPNST, how does NF1-associated MPNST generally behave, and what surveillance principle follows?
options: NF1-associated MPNST is more indolent and needs no surveillance
NF1-associated MPNST tends to be more aggressive with worse prognosis, warranting close oncologic follow-up and prompt evaluation of changing plexiform lesions
It never metastasizes
It is cured by chemotherapy in nearly all cases
stages.explanation: MPNST arising in NF1 generally behaves more aggressively and carries a worse prognosis than sporadic MPNST, and it is a leading cause of cancer death in NF1. This warrants close oncologic follow-up after treatment and a low threshold to evaluate any plexiform neurofibroma that becomes painful, rapidly enlarging, or neurologically symptomatic. It does metastasize (lung is the most common site), so it does not 'never metastasize.' Chemotherapy does not cure most cases; surgery with negative margins, often plus radiation, offers the best chance of cure for localized disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point is most appropriate for this patient and his NF1 family?
options: NF1 carries no increased cancer risk, so no special vigilance is needed
NF1 increases lifetime risk of MPNST and other tumors; report any new persistent pain, rapid growth, or neurologic change in a plexiform neurofibroma promptly
All neurofibromas should be removed prophylactically
Genetic factors play no role and family screening is unnecessary
stages.explanation: Patients with NF1 should be counseled that they have an elevated lifetime risk of MPNST and certain other tumors, and that warning signs in a plexiform neurofibroma — new persistent or nocturnal pain, rapid enlargement, a change in consistency to firm/fixed, or new neurologic deficits — warrant prompt evaluation. NF1 clearly increases cancer risk, so reassurance of 'no risk' is wrong. Prophylactic removal of all neurofibromas is neither feasible nor recommended given their number and benign behavior. NF1 is heritable (autosomal dominant), so family awareness and appropriate genetic counseling are warranted.

cases.diagnosis: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) arising in a plexiform neurofibroma in NF1
pearls: Suspect MPNST when an NF1 plexiform neurofibroma develops rapid growth, new persistent/nocturnal pain, a firm/fixed change, or new neurologic deficit.
Imaging red flags include large size, irregular margins, necrosis, loss of the 'target sign,' and intense FDG-PET uptake; PET helps target biopsy and distinguish from benign neurofibroma.
Curative treatment is wide resection to negative margins, often combined with radiotherapy; chemotherapy is reserved for advanced/high-risk disease.
NF1-associated MPNST is more aggressive than sporadic MPNST and is a leading cause of NF1 mortality, mandating vigilant surveillance.

cases.title: Headache and a Crossed Eye in a Boy Being Treated for Leukemia
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening headache and new double vision during treatment for acute leukemia.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL), currently in the induction phase of chemotherapy, develops three days of increasing headache, intermittent vomiting, and, today, double vision with a tendency to turn his head to compensate. His parents note he has been more irritable. He denies neck stiffness but the headache is worse when lying flat in the morning. PMH: ALL diagnosed five weeks ago; no prior CNS symptoms. Medications: induction chemotherapy per protocol, ondansetron, allopurinol. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents and a younger sibling; up to date on vaccines. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: no fever today (afebrile), no cough, no rash, no limb weakness, no seizures. His most recent peripheral counts show recovering marrow. He has had prior lumbar punctures at diagnosis. The team is concerned about a new neurologic complication of his leukemia versus a treatment effect, and his parents are anxious about the new eye finding.
cases.exam: Alert, irritable but consolable. Pupils equal and reactive; fundoscopy shows early papilledema. Right eye fails to abduct fully past midline (right CN VI palsy) producing horizontal diplopia; other extraocular movements intact, no ptosis. Mild neck supple without meningismus. Face symmetric, palate elevates symmetrically. Motor strength 5/5 in all limbs, reflexes normal, no clonus, plantars flexor. Gait mildly unsteady but no focal cerebellar signs. No skin petechiae today; no lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding makes an isolated CN VI palsy a nonspecific but important sign in this child, and what does the overall picture suggest?
options: CN VI palsy localizes precisely to the cavernous sinus only
CN VI palsy is a 'false localizing sign' of raised intracranial pressure; with headache, morning vomiting, and papilledema it suggests increased ICP, possibly from CNS leukemia
It indicates a benign migraine
It confirms a primary brain tumor unrelated to leukemia
stages.explanation: The abducens nerve has a long intracranial course and is vulnerable to stretch from raised intracranial pressure, making a CN VI palsy a classic 'false localizing sign.' Combined with morning-predominant headache, vomiting, and papilledema, the picture indicates increased intracranial pressure, which in a child with ALL raises strong concern for CNS involvement of leukemia (leptomeningeal infiltration). It does not localize specifically to the cavernous sinus. The systemic context and exam make benign migraine unlikely. While a primary brain tumor is possible in general, in a known ALL patient CNS leukemia is the leading concern and must be evaluated.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm CNS involvement?
options: Lumbar puncture immediately without imaging
Neuroimaging (MRI/CT) first to exclude a mass/elevated-ICP contraindication, then lumbar puncture with CSF cytology and cell count
EEG
Empiric whole-brain radiation without diagnosis
stages.explanation: With papilledema and signs of raised ICP, neuroimaging should precede lumbar puncture to exclude an intracranial mass or obstructive hydrocephalus that would make LP hazardous. Once it is safe, lumbar puncture with CSF cytology, cell count, and flow cytometry confirms CNS leukemia by demonstrating blasts in the CSF. Performing LP immediately despite papilledema risks herniation. EEG evaluates seizures, not leukemic meningeal spread. Empiric cranial radiation without a diagnosis is inappropriate; treatment must be guided by confirmed CNS involvement and protocol-directed therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: leptomeningeal enhancement; no discrete mass; mild communicating hydrocephalus.
Lumbar puncture (after imaging): opening pressure elevated.
CSF WBC 40 /µL with blasts identified on cytospin
CSF flow cytometry: B-lymphoblast population matching the marrow clone
CSF protein 65 mg/dL
CSF glucose 45 mg/dL
Peripheral blood: recovering counts, no circulating blasts
CNS status classified as CNS3 (CSF blasts present)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given CSF blasts confirming CNS leukemia, what is the mainstay of CNS-directed therapy?
options: Intrathecal chemotherapy (e.g., methotrexate, sometimes with cytarabine/hydrocortisone), with CNS-directed systemic therapy and radiation reserved for selected cases
Oral antibiotics
Surgical resection of the leptomeninges
Observation with repeat LP in one month
stages.explanation: CNS leukemia is treated with intrathecal chemotherapy (commonly methotrexate, alone or as triple therapy with cytarabine and hydrocortisone) delivered directly into the CSF, combined with CNS-penetrating systemic therapy such as high-dose methotrexate; cranial radiation is reserved for selected high-risk or refractory cases due to long-term neurocognitive and secondary-malignancy risks. Antibiotics treat infection, not leukemic infiltration. The leptomeninges cannot be surgically resected. Observation is unsafe with documented CNS3 disease, which requires prompt CNS-directed treatment to prevent progression and relapse.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Why does virtually every child with ALL receive CNS-directed prophylaxis even without overt CNS disease at diagnosis?
options: Because the CNS is a pharmacologic sanctuary site where systemic chemotherapy penetrates poorly, allowing leukemic cells to survive and seed relapse
Because all children develop meningitis without it
Because it prevents tumor lysis syndrome
Because it is required to treat anemia
stages.explanation: The central nervous system is a 'sanctuary site' protected by the blood-brain barrier, where many systemic chemotherapeutic agents reach inadequate concentrations, so residual leukemic cells can survive there and later cause CNS relapse. Routine CNS-directed prophylaxis — primarily intrathecal chemotherapy — is therefore standard for essentially all ALL patients to eradicate occult CNS disease and has dramatically reduced CNS relapse. It is not given to prevent bacterial meningitis, has nothing to do with tumor lysis syndrome (managed with hydration, allopurinol/rasburicase), and is unrelated to treating anemia.

cases.diagnosis: CNS involvement of acute lymphoblastic leukemia (CNS leukemia)
pearls: An isolated CN VI palsy is a 'false localizing sign' of raised intracranial pressure; in a leukemia patient it should prompt evaluation for CNS involvement.
When papilledema or raised ICP is present, obtain neuroimaging before lumbar puncture; CNS leukemia is confirmed by CSF blasts on cytology/flow cytometry (CNS3 disease).
Treat CNS leukemia with intrathecal chemotherapy plus CNS-penetrant systemic therapy; reserve cranial radiation for selected cases due to long-term toxicity.
The CNS is a pharmacologic sanctuary site, so essentially all ALL patients receive CNS-directed prophylaxis to prevent relapse.

cases.title: A Thirsty Toddler Who Wets Through Every Diaper
cases.chiefComplaint: Excessive thirst and very frequent, copious urination for the past two months.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought in for two months of dramatically increased thirst and urination; his parents report he drinks constantly, wakes overnight to drink, and soaks through multiple diapers including overnight, after previously being mostly dry at night. He has had mild weight loss and is more irritable. They also mention a tender bump on the side of his head and a chronically draining right ear that has not responded to antibiotic drops. PMH: recurrent otitis media; otherwise healthy, born full-term. Medications: completed several antibiotic courses for ear drainage. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents; attends daycare; immunizations up to date. Family history: noncontributory; no diabetes mellitus in the family. Review of systems: no fever currently, no polyphagia, normal stools; parents deny visual complaints but note he sometimes seems to bump into things. A scaly, seborrhea-like rash is present on the scalp and in the diaper area that has been resistant to usual treatments. There is no history of head trauma.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly irritable, appears slightly thin. Mucous membranes slightly dry. A firm, tender swelling is palpable over the right parietal/temporal skull. Right ear with chronic otorrhea and granular tissue in the canal. Skull and diaper-area skin show a yellow-brown scaly, petechial seborrheic-appearing eruption. Abdomen soft; mild hepatomegaly with the liver edge 2 cm below the costal margin; spleen tip palpable. No focal neurologic deficit; gait normal; visual fields grossly intact on confrontation. No lymphadenopathy beyond a small cervical node.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Polyuria/polydipsia with a normal glucose, in a toddler, most likely reflects what mechanism, and what is the best initial test to characterize it?
options: Diabetes mellitus; check HbA1c
A defect in water conservation (diabetes insipidus); obtain paired serum and urine osmolality/electrolytes and assess for inability to concentrate urine
Urinary tract infection; obtain urine culture only
Normal toddler behavior; reassure
stages.explanation: Copious dilute urine with intense thirst and a normal glucose points to diabetes insipidus — a failure to concentrate urine from deficient ADH (central) or renal resistance (nephrogenic). The best initial characterization is paired serum and urine osmolality with electrolytes, which in DI shows inappropriately dilute urine despite rising or high-normal serum osmolality/sodium; a water-deprivation test with desmopressin then distinguishes central from nephrogenic DI. Diabetes mellitus is excluded by normal glucose. A UTI does not cause sustained high-volume dilute polyuria with polydipsia. Dismissing it as normal behavior ignores significant pathologic polyuria with weight loss.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum Na 148 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 300 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg (inappropriately dilute)
Glucose 92 mg/dL
Urine concentrates appropriately after desmopressin (consistent with central DI)
MRI brain/pituitary: thickened, enhancing pituitary infundibulum (stalk); loss of the normal posterior pituitary 'bright spot'
Skull X-ray/CT: well-defined lytic ('punched-out') lesions of the skull with beveled edges
Skin/bone biopsy: CD1a-positive, S100-positive, Langerin (CD207)-positive histiocytes; Birbeck granules on EM

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis unifies central diabetes insipidus, pituitary stalk thickening, lytic skull lesions, chronic otorrhea, and a seborrheic rash with CD1a-positive histiocytes?
options: Craniopharyngioma
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) with CNS/pituitary involvement
Germinoma of the pineal gland
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
stages.explanation: The combination of central DI from infundibular involvement (thick, enhancing stalk; absent posterior pituitary bright spot), 'punched-out' lytic skull lesions with beveled edges, chronic refractory otorrhea, and a seborrheic/petechial rash, with biopsy showing CD1a-, S100-, and Langerin-positive histiocytes (Birbeck granules), is classic for Langerhans cell histiocytosis. The pituitary stalk is the most common CNS site, and DI is the most frequent endocrine manifestation. Craniopharyngioma is a suprasellar calcified cystic mass, not multifocal lytic bone disease with this rash. A germinoma can cause DI but lacks the lytic bone lesions and CD1a-positive histiocytosis. Nephrogenic DI would not respond to desmopressin and does not explain the systemic findings.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After establishing the diagnosis, which step is most important to determine treatment intensity?
options: Begin chemotherapy immediately without further evaluation
Stage the disease by assessing for multisystem involvement and 'risk-organ' involvement (liver, spleen, bone marrow)
Restrict fluids to treat the DI
Refer for radiation to the skull lesions as first-line
stages.explanation: LCH ranges from single-system to multisystem disease, and prognosis and therapy hinge on whether 'risk organs' (liver, spleen, hematopoietic system) are involved, so staging with imaging, blood counts, liver function, and assessment of organ involvement is essential before deciding treatment intensity. This patient already shows hepatosplenomegaly, raising concern for multisystem risk-organ disease. Starting chemotherapy without staging risks inappropriate intensity. Fluid restriction is harmful in DI (which requires desmopressin and free access to water). Radiation is not first-line for systemic pediatric LCH, which is treated with systemic chemotherapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which management plan is most appropriate for multisystem LCH with central diabetes insipidus?
options: Desmopressin for the DI plus systemic chemotherapy (e.g., vinblastine and prednisone) for multisystem disease
Desmopressin alone, since DI is the only treatable component
Antibiotics for the ear and topical steroids for the rash only
Observation, because LCH always resolves spontaneously
stages.explanation: Central DI in LCH is managed with desmopressin (DDAVP) replacement, and is usually permanent because the stalk damage rarely reverses. The multisystem disease itself requires systemic chemotherapy — classically vinblastine plus a corticosteroid — particularly with risk-organ involvement. Desmopressin alone ignores the active multisystem histiocytosis. Treating only the ear and rash addresses symptoms but not the underlying systemic disease. While some single-system LCH (e.g., isolated bone) can regress, multisystem disease with risk-organ involvement does not reliably self-resolve and needs treatment; mutation-targeted therapy (e.g., BRAF inhibitors) is an option in refractory BRAF-mutated cases.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Beyond DI, which long-term endocrine complication should prompt ongoing surveillance in a child with LCH involving the pituitary stalk?
options: Anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies, especially growth hormone deficiency
Hyperthyroidism only
Cushing disease
Precocious puberty as the sole concern
stages.explanation: Pituitary/infundibular LCH can progressively damage the anterior pituitary, so children need ongoing endocrine surveillance for anterior hormone deficiencies, most commonly growth hormone deficiency, and also potentially TSH, ACTH, and gonadotropin deficiencies. Monitoring growth and hormonal axes allows timely replacement. The typical pattern is hormone deficiency from gland destruction rather than overproduction, so isolated hyperthyroidism or Cushing disease (a cortisol-excess state) are not the expected complications. Precocious puberty is not the characteristic or sole endocrine concern; anterior pituitary hypofunction, particularly GH deficiency, is the key surveillance target.

cases.diagnosis: Langerhans cell histiocytosis with CNS/pituitary involvement causing central diabetes insipidus
pearls: Central DI with a thickened, enhancing pituitary stalk and loss of the posterior pituitary 'bright spot,' plus 'punched-out' lytic skull lesions, refractory otorrhea, and a seborrheic rash, is classic for Langerhans cell histiocytosis.
LCH is confirmed by CD1a-, S100-, and Langerin (CD207)-positive histiocytes with Birbeck granules; the pituitary stalk is the most common CNS site and DI the most common endocrine manifestation.

Stage LCH for multisystem and 'risk-organ' (liver, spleen, marrow) involvement, which dictates treatment intensity; multisystem disease is treated with systemic chemotherapy (vinblastine plus corticosteroid).
DI from LCH is usually permanent (treat with desmopressin); monitor long-term for anterior pituitary deficiencies, especially growth hormone deficiency.

cases.title: Facial Twitching and a Mass Behind the Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of intermittent right facial twitching and a recent episode of facial numbness.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman presents with about eight months of intermittent involuntary twitching of the right side of her face, initially around the eye and now spreading to the cheek and mouth. More recently she has noticed brief episodes of numbness over the right cheek and mild unsteadiness. She denies hearing loss, vertigo, headache, or fever. PMH: unremarkable; no prior surgeries. Medications: oral contraceptive pill; no others. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a teacher. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems: no diplopia, no dysphagia, no limb weakness, no weight loss. She initially attributed the twitching to stress and fatigue but became concerned as it became more frequent and the numbness appeared. She has not had prior imaging. There is no tinnitus and no facial weakness that she has noticed, though others have commented on the visible twitching.
cases.exam: Intermittent hemifacial spasm involving the right orbicularis oculi and perioral muscles. Right corneal reflex is mildly diminished and there is subtle decreased pinprick over the right V2/V3; facial strength is otherwise normal at rest (no flaccid weakness). Hearing is normal to finger rub bilaterally with normal Weber/Rinne. No nystagmus. Mild right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose and a slightly wide-based gait. Remaining cranial nerves intact; no papilledema. Motor and sensory exam of the limbs otherwise normal; reflexes symmetric. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Hemifacial spasm plus trigeminal sensory changes and ipsilateral limb ataxia point to a lesion in which region?
options: Cavernous sinus
Cerebellopontine angle (CPA)
Internal capsule
Lumbar spine
stages.explanation: The cerebellopontine angle contains cranial nerves V, VII, and VIII and lies adjacent to the cerebellum and brainstem. A CPA lesion can irritate the facial nerve (hemifacial spasm), compress the trigeminal nerve (facial numbness, diminished corneal reflex), and affect the cerebellum (ipsilateral ataxia/dysmetria). The combination here localizes to the CPA. The cavernous sinus involves the ocular cranial nerves and V1/V2 without cerebellar signs. The internal capsule produces contralateral hemiparesis/hemisensory loss, not focal facial spasm with ataxia. The lumbar spine has no relation to facial or cerebellar findings.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast: a lobulated, non-enhancing extra-axial mass in the right cerebellopontine angle insinuating around the brainstem and cranial nerves with a 'cauliflower' surface, following CSF signal on T1 and T2.
FLAIR: incomplete suppression (does not fully match CSF).
Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI): marked restricted diffusion (bright on DWI, low on ADC).
No calcification; no dural tail; minimal mass effect for size.
CT (separate): hypodense, non-calcified lesion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which CPA lesion characteristically restricts on diffusion-weighted imaging and shows incomplete FLAIR suppression, distinguishing it from an arachnoid cyst?
options: Arachnoid cyst
Epidermoid cyst
Vestibular schwannoma
Meningioma
stages.explanation: An epidermoid cyst follows CSF signal on conventional T1/T2 and so can mimic an arachnoid cyst, but it characteristically shows restricted diffusion (bright on DWI) and incomplete FLAIR suppression, plus a tendency to encase rather than displace vessels and nerves and a lobulated 'cauliflower' surface. An arachnoid cyst suppresses completely on FLAIR and does NOT restrict on diffusion (it simply displaces structures). A vestibular schwannoma is an enhancing mass centered on the internal auditory canal, usually with hearing loss. A meningioma is an enhancing, often dural-based mass with a dural tail, unlike this non-enhancing, diffusion-restricting lesion.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: A 'white epidermoid' can occasionally appear hyperintense on T1 and mimic other lesions. Which imaging feature most reliably confirms an epidermoid in this setting?
options: Avid homogeneous contrast enhancement
Restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC
A dural tail sign
Calcification on CT
stages.explanation: Restricted diffusion on DWI with corresponding low ADC is the most reliable feature confirming an epidermoid cyst, including atypical 'white epidermoids' that may look hyperintense on T1; this diffusion behavior separates epidermoids from arachnoid cysts and most other CPA masses. Epidermoids characteristically do not enhance avidly, so homogeneous enhancement argues against the diagnosis (and toward schwannoma/meningioma). A dural tail suggests meningioma. Calcification is not a defining feature of epidermoids and is more typical of other lesions; it is the diffusion restriction that clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a symptomatic CPA epidermoid cyst, what is the definitive management?
options: Surgical resection (maximal safe removal of the cyst and capsule)
Stereotactic radiosurgery as first-line
Systemic chemotherapy
Antibiotics and observation
stages.explanation: Symptomatic intracranial epidermoid cysts are treated with surgical resection, aiming for maximal safe removal of the cyst contents and capsule; because they insinuate around neurovascular structures, complete capsule removal can be difficult and recurrence may occur if remnants are left, so careful microsurgical technique is key. Radiosurgery is not the primary treatment for these benign, slow-growing, congenital inclusion cysts. Chemotherapy has no role for a benign epidermoid. Antibiotics are irrelevant unless rupture causes chemical (aseptic) meningitis; observation may be reasonable only for small, truly asymptomatic lesions, not for a symptomatic cyst causing spasm, sensory loss, and ataxia.

cases.diagnosis: Intracranial (cerebellopontine angle) epidermoid cyst
pearls: A CPA mass causing hemifacial spasm (CN VII), facial numbness/diminished corneal reflex (CN V), and ipsilateral ataxia localizes to the cerebellopontine angle.

Epidermoid cysts follow CSF signal but restrict on diffusion (bright DWI, low ADC) and show incomplete FLAIR suppression — this distinguishes them from arachnoid cysts, which suppress on FLAIR and do not restrict.
Restricted diffusion is the most reliable feature, including for atypical hyperintense 'white epidermoids'; epidermoids encase rather than displace neurovascular structures.
Definitive treatment of a symptomatic epidermoid is maximal safe surgical resection; residual capsule can cause recurrence and rupture can trigger aseptic (chemical) meningitis.

cases.title: A First Seizure in a Healthy Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Witnessed generalized convulsion at work earlier today.
cases.history: A 43-year-old right-handed accountant is brought to the ED after coworkers witnessed him stiffen, fall, and convulse for about 90 seconds during a meeting. He was confused and groggy for roughly 20 minutes afterward but is now back to baseline. He bit the side of his tongue and was briefly incontinent of urine. Before the event he recalls a strange ascending sensation in his abdomen and an unusual smell. He denies any prior seizures, head trauma, recent illness, fever, or headache, though in retrospect he describes several months of vague intermittent 'déjà vu' spells lasting seconds. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He drinks two beers on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no illicit drugs. He works long hours and sleeps adequately. Family history is notable for hypertension in his father; no family history of seizures, brain tumors, or neurocutaneous disorders. He is married with two children and has had no recent travel.
cases.exam: Awake, alert, fully oriented, and conversant. A small lateral tongue laceration is present. Pupils equal and reactive; extraocular movements full; no nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Face symmetric, speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities with normal tone and no pronator drift. Sensation intact. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Coordination and gait normal. No neck stiffness, no papilledema on fundoscopy, and no cutaneous café-au-lait macules or neurofibromas. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After the patient is stabilized and at neurologic baseline, what is the most appropriate next step in the workup of this first unprovoked seizure?
options: Start levetiracetam empirically and discharge with neurology follow-up
Obtain a fingerstick glucose, basic metabolic and toxicology screen, then neuroimaging of the brain
Perform an urgent lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis
Reassure and discharge, as a single seizure requires no further evaluation
Order EEG as the sole diagnostic test before any imaging
stages.explanation: A first unprovoked adult seizure mandates a search for provoking and structural causes. Point-of-care glucose, electrolytes (Na, Ca, Mg), renal function, and a toxicology screen exclude common metabolic/toxic triggers, and neuroimaging is required to find a structural lesion—especially with focal features (epigastric aura, olfactory hallucination, déjà vu suggesting a temporal/frontal focus). Empiric levetiracetam without workup is premature and skips identifying a treatable lesion. Lumbar puncture is unnecessary without fever, meningismus, or immunocompromise. Discharge without evaluation is unsafe given focal semiology. EEG alone helps characterize epilepsy and recurrence risk but does not exclude a structural lesion and is not the immediate priority over imaging.

stages.label: LABS & INITIAL IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose 96 mg/dL
Na 140 mEq/L
K 4.1 mEq/L
Ca 9.4 mg/dL
Mg 2.0 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL
Urine toxicology: negative
Non-contrast head CT: well-circumscribed left frontal mass with coarse, clumped calcifications and mild surrounding hypodensity; no hemorrhage or midline shift

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a calcified, well-demarcated frontal lobe mass on CT, which next investigation will best characterize the lesion?
options: Repeat non-contrast CT in 6 months
Catheter cerebral angiography
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain
PET-CT of the whole body for staging
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI is the gold-standard for characterizing an intra-axial brain mass: it defines location, infiltration, enhancement pattern, and relationship to eloquent cortex, and is essential for surgical planning. A cortically based frontal mass with calcification in an adult with a focal seizure is classic for a low-grade glioma, particularly oligodendroglioma. Repeat CT delays diagnosis of a symptomatic lesion. Angiography is for vascular lesions, not a calcified parenchymal tumor. Whole-body PET-CT is not used to characterize a primary glioma and would be premature. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis and is irrelevant to a parenchymal mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI confirms a non-enhancing, cortically based left frontal mass with calcification. After starting an antiseizure medication, what is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis and guide therapy?
options: Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue diagnosis
Maximal safe surgical resection with tissue sent for histology and molecular testing
Stereotactic radiosurgery to the lesion
Initiate temozolomide based on imaging alone
Observe with serial MRI and defer any tissue sampling indefinitely
stages.explanation: Tissue is required for diagnosis, grading, and molecular classification, and maximal safe resection both provides ample tissue and improves seizure control and progression-free survival in low-grade gliomas.
