Resection specimens allow IDH and 1p/19q testing, which define the tumor type. Empiric whole-brain radiation without a diagnosis is never appropriate and causes neurotoxicity. Radiosurgery is not standard for diffuse infiltrative gliomas and provides no tissue. Starting temozolomide on imaging alone treats without a diagnosis. Indefinite observation without any tissue is inappropriate for a symptomatic, surgically accessible lesion and forgoes prognostic molecular data.

stages.label: MOLECULAR DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Pathology shows a diffusely infiltrating glioma with cells having round nuclei, perinuclear halos ('fried-egg' appearance), and a delicate branching 'chicken-wire' capillary network. Which molecular result would confirm the specific diagnosis?
options: EGFR amplification and chromosome 10 loss
IDH mutation with intact 1p and 19q
IDH mutation with combined 1p/19q codeletion
BRAF V600E mutation with KIAA1549-BRAF fusion
MYCN amplification
stages.explanation: Oligodendroglioma is defined by the combination of an IDH mutation AND whole-arm codeletion of chromosomes 1p and 19q—both are required by the WHO classification. The fried-egg cytology and chicken-wire vasculature are classic but are diagnostically confirmed only by this molecular signature, which also predicts excellent chemoradiation response. EGFR amplification with chromosome 10 loss characterizes IDH-wildtype glioblastoma. IDH mutation with intact 1p/19q indicates IDH-mutant astrocytoma, not oligodendroglioma. BRAF alterations are seen in pilocytic astrocytoma/ganglioglioma. MYCN amplification is associated with neuroblastoma and some embryonal tumors, not oligodendroglioma.

cases.diagnosis: Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted (WHO grade 2)
pearls: A focal seizure with an epigastric/olfactory aura in an adult should prompt brain imaging to exclude a structural lesion.
A cortically based, calcified frontal lobe mass in a young-to-middle-aged adult is classic for oligodendroglioma.
Oligodendroglioma requires BOTH IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion—this molecular pair defines the tumor and predicts strong chemoradiation sensitivity.
Maximal safe resection improves seizure control and survival and supplies tissue for essential molecular classification.

cases.title: An Incidental Brain Spot in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after an MRI showed an abnormality while evaluating headaches.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman, a software engineer and mother of one, presents to neuro-oncology clinic after a brain MRI ordered for several months of intermittent dull bifrontal headaches revealed a left frontal lesion. The headaches are mild, respond to acetaminophen, and are not worse in the morning or with Valsalva. She denies seizures, focal weakness, sensory changes, vision changes, nausea, or cognitive decline. She has had no episodes of speech difficulty. Past medical history includes mild iron-deficiency anemia. Her only medications are an oral iron supplement and a daily multivitamin; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and uses no recreational drugs. She exercises regularly and works full time. Family history is negative for brain tumors, seizures, and neurocutaneous syndromes. She is married, recently considering a second pregnancy, and is highly anxious about the finding. She brings the imaging disc and is seeking guidance on whether she needs surgery or can be monitored.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, anxious but cooperative woman in no distress. Higher cognitive function intact; speech fluent with normal naming and repetition. Cranial nerves II–XII normal, including full visual fields and no papilledema. Motor strength 5/5 throughout with normal tone and no drift. Sensation to light touch, pinprick, and proprioception intact. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin coordination normal; tandem gait normal. No nystagmus. No neck stiffness. Skin without café-au-lait macules or axillary freckling.

stages.label: IMAGING INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Her outside MRI shows a non-enhancing region of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the left frontal lobe that expands the cortex and blurs the gray-white junction, without restricted diffusion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute ischemic infarct
Diffuse low-grade glioma
Cerebral abscess
Multiple sclerosis plaque
Meningioma
stages.explanation: A non-enhancing, expansile T2/FLAIR-hyperintense lesion that infiltrates and blurs the gray-white junction without diffusion restriction is the classic appearance of a diffuse low-grade glioma (often IDH-mutant astrocytoma) in a young adult. An acute infarct follows a vascular territory and restricts diffusion. An abscess shows ring enhancement and central restricted diffusion with systemic signs. An MS plaque is typically periventricular/ovoid, smaller, and may enhance when active, and the clinical picture differs. A meningioma is an extra-axial, dural-based, avidly enhancing mass—not an infiltrative non-enhancing parenchymal lesion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Before committing to a management strategy, which step is most important to establish diagnosis, grade, and prognosis of this presumed low-grade glioma?
options: Empiric radiotherapy to the lesion
Lumbar puncture for cytology
Tissue sampling (biopsy or resection) with IDH and 1p/19q molecular testing
Serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG)
Repeat MRI in 5 years
stages.explanation: Histologic and molecular characterization—particularly IDH mutation status and 1p/19q codeletion—is essential to distinguish IDH-mutant astrocytoma from oligodendroglioma, assign grade, and prognosticate; this is best obtained via surgery (resection preferred over biopsy when safe). Empiric radiotherapy without a diagnosis is inappropriate and toxic. CSF cytology is low-yield for a non-enhancing parenchymal glioma. Serum AFP/beta-hCG are markers for germ cell tumors, not gliomas. A 5-year imaging interval is far too long and risks unmonitored malignant progression; even conservative strategies require much closer surveillance.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & MOLECULAR
stages.content:
Stereotactic biopsy: diffusely infiltrating astrocytoma, low cellularity, no microvascular proliferation, no necrosis
Mitotic activity: low
IDH1 R132H: mutated
1p/19q: intact (no codeletion)
ATRX: loss of nuclear expression
Ki-67 proliferation index: 3%
CNS WHO grade: 2

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This is a young, high-functioning patient with an IDH-mutant astrocytoma. Regarding 'watchful waiting' versus early intervention, which approach is best supported by current evidence?
options: Indefinite observation with imaging only when new symptoms develop
Maximal safe resection is favored, as greater extent of resection improves outcomes even in low-grade glioma
Immediate whole-brain radiotherapy regardless of resectability
Watchful waiting for at least 10 years before any intervention
Chemotherapy alone with temozolomide, avoiding surgery entirely
stages.explanation: Modern evidence favors early maximal safe resection in low-grade glioma: greater extent of resection is associated with longer progression-free and overall survival and delays malignant transformation, while also providing definitive tissue. Pure indefinite observation and a fixed 10-year delay are outdated 'wait-and-watch' strategies now reserved at most for small, asymptomatic, surgically inaccessible lesions with close surveillance. Whole-brain radiotherapy is excessively toxic and not first-line for a focal low-grade glioma. Temozolomide alone without surgery forgoes the survival benefit of resection and is not the standard initial approach for an accessible tumor.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: She asks how her tumor's molecular profile affects her prognosis compared with other gliomas. Which statement is most accurate?
options: IDH-wildtype status would carry a better prognosis than her IDH-mutant tumor
IDH-mutant gliomas carry a markedly better prognosis than IDH-wildtype gliomas
Molecular markers have no bearing on glioma prognosis
Her intact 1p/19q means the tumor will behave like glioblastoma
MGMT methylation is irrelevant in all gliomas
stages.explanation: IDH mutation is a powerful favorable prognostic marker: IDH-mutant gliomas grow more indolently and confer substantially longer survival than IDH-wildtype tumors, which behave aggressively (an IDH-wildtype diffuse astrocytic tumor with certain features may even be classified as glioblastoma). Thus IDH-wildtype is worse, not better. Molecular markers are central—not irrelevant—to the current WHO classification and prognosis. Intact 1p/19q distinguishes astrocytoma from oligodendroglioma but does not make a grade 2 IDH-mutant tumor behave like glioblastoma. MGMT promoter methylation does carry prognostic and predictive value in gliomas.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse astrocytoma, IDH-mutant (WHO grade 2 low-grade glioma)
pearls: A non-enhancing, expansile T2/FLAIR lesion blurring the gray-white junction in a young adult suggests a diffuse low-grade glioma.
IDH mutation with intact 1p/19q and ATRX loss defines IDH-mutant astrocytoma; codeletion would instead indicate oligodendroglioma.
Early maximal safe resection—not indefinite watchful waiting—is favored in low-grade glioma because greater resection extent improves survival.
IDH mutation is a strong favorable prognostic marker compared with IDH-wildtype gliomas.

cases.title: A Wobbly Walk and Morning Headaches in a Child
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of unsteadiness and headaches, worse in the morning.
cases.history: A 9-year-old girl is brought to pediatric clinic by her parents for about six weeks of progressively worsening clumsiness and headaches. The headaches are mainly in the back of the head, are worst on waking, and have been accompanied by occasional morning vomiting that briefly relieves them. Her parents note she has begun bumping into door frames on her right side and her handwriting has deteriorated. She has had no fevers, no recent infections, no head trauma, and no seizures. She is otherwise developmentally normal and a good student, though her teacher recently noted she tilts her head. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger brother and has had no travel. Family history is unremarkable—no brain tumors, neurofibromatosis, or other neurocutaneous disorders. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no weight loss, polyuria, or polydipsia.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive girl with a subtle head tilt. Fundoscopy shows early bilateral papilledema. Extraocular movements full without gaze-evoked nystagmus at rest, but sustained right gaze elicits nystagmus. Cranial nerves otherwise intact; face symmetric. Strength 5/5 throughout. On coordination testing she has right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose and dysdiadochokinesia; heel-to-shin is impaired on the right. Gait is wide-based and unsteady with falls toward the right; tandem gait is impossible. Romberg negative. Reflexes 2+ symmetric; plantar responses flexor. No neck stiffness. Skin exam shows no café-au-lait macules.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Based on her morning headaches with vomiting, papilledema, right-sided dysmetria, and a wide-based ataxic gait, where is the lesion most likely localized?
options: Left cerebral hemisphere (motor cortex)
Right cerebellar hemisphere
Brainstem pons
Pituitary/sellar region
Spinal cord
stages.explanation: Limb ataxia (dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia) is ipsilateral to the cerebellar lesion, so right-sided appendicular signs localize to the right cerebellar hemisphere; the wide-based gait, head tilt, and gaze-evoked nystagmus support a posterior fossa cerebellar process, and morning headache with vomiting and papilledema reflect raised intracranial pressure from posterior fossa mass effect. A left motor cortex lesion would cause right-sided weakness, not ipsilateral ataxia. A pontine lesion typically causes cranial nerve palsies and crossed signs. A sellar lesion causes visual field/endocrine problems, not limb ataxia. A spinal cord lesion would not produce papilledema or appendicular cerebellar signs.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Well-circumscribed right cerebellar hemisphere mass, ~4 cm
Large cystic component with an avidly enhancing mural nodule
Minimal surrounding edema
Fourth ventricle compressed with mild obstructive hydrocephalus
No leptomeningeal enhancement; spine MRI without drop metastases

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule in a 9-year-old is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma
Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor
stages.explanation: A well-circumscribed cerebellar cyst with an enhancing mural nodule is the textbook appearance of pilocytic astrocytoma, the most common pediatric brain tumor and a WHO grade 1 lesion. Medulloblastoma is typically a solid, midline (vermian) enhancing mass with restricted diffusion and a tendency to seed CSF. Ependymoma classically arises in the fourth ventricle and squeezes through the foramina ('plastic' tumor). Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma is an infiltrative non-enhancing pontine mass without a cyst-plus-nodule pattern. AT/RT is an aggressive infant tumor that is heterogeneous and often disseminated, not a benign cyst-with-nodule.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this lesion?
options: Upfront craniospinal irradiation
Maximal safe surgical resection
Chemotherapy alone with vincristine and carboplatin
Observation with serial MRI only
Stereotactic radiosurgery to the mural nodule
stages.explanation: Gross-total surgical resection is the treatment of choice for pilocytic astrocytoma and is frequently curative, with excellent long-term survival; it also relieves the mass effect and hydrocephalus. Craniospinal irradiation is reserved for disseminated embryonal tumors like medulloblastoma and is avoided in a curable grade 1 tumor, especially in children, due to neurocognitive toxicity. Chemotherapy is used mainly for unresectable/progressive disease (e.g., optic-pathway gliomas in young children), not as first-line for a resectable cerebellar lesion. Observation is inappropriate for a symptomatic obstructing mass. Radiosurgery is not standard primary therapy when complete resection is achievable.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: After gross-total resection, the parents ask about her outlook. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Even with complete resection, 5-year survival is under 50%
Pilocytic astrocytoma is WHO grade 1, and gross-total resection is often curative with excellent long-term survival
She will require lifelong chemotherapy regardless of resection
The tumor commonly metastasizes throughout the neuraxis
Recurrence is inevitable within one year
stages.explanation: Pilocytic astrocytoma is a WHO grade 1 tumor with one of the best prognoses in neuro-oncology; gross-total resection is frequently curative and 5- and 10-year survival exceeds 90%. Therefore survival is excellent, not under 50%. Lifelong chemotherapy is not needed after complete resection. Leptomeningeal dissemination is characteristic of medulloblastoma, not pilocytic astrocytoma. Recurrence is uncommon after gross-total resection, so inevitable one-year recurrence is incorrect; close surveillance imaging is nonetheless appropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Pilocytic astrocytoma of the cerebellum (WHO grade 1)
pearls: Morning headache, vomiting, papilledema, and ataxia in a child signal a posterior fossa mass with raised intracranial pressure.
Appendicular cerebellar signs (dysmetria) are ipsilateral to the lesion.
A cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule is classic for pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO grade 1).
Gross-total resection is often curative for pilocytic astrocytoma, with excellent long-term survival.

cases.title: Morning Vomiting and a Staggering Gait in a Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of morning vomiting and worsening unsteadiness.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents for two weeks of vomiting that occurs mostly in the early morning, sometimes before he eats, and is often projectile. Over the same period he has become increasingly unsteady on his feet, walking with his legs far apart and grabbing furniture. He complains of headaches that are worse when he wakes and improve as the day goes on. His parents report he seems more tired and irritable and has started seeing 'double' when looking to the sides. He has had no fever, diarrhea, sick contacts, or recent travel. Past medical history is unremarkable; he was a full-term infant with normal development. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and an older sister; immunizations are current. Family history is unremarkable for tumors or neurocutaneous syndromes. Review of systems is otherwise negative for rash, weight loss, or focal limb weakness, though his appetite has decreased because of the nausea.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable and cooperative boy. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. He has bilateral abducens (CN VI) palsies with impaired lateral gaze and horizontal diplopia. Pupils equal and reactive. Strength is 5/5 in all extremities with normal tone. Coordination shows truncal ataxia—he cannot sit steadily without support—and bilateral appendicular dysmetria. Gait is markedly wide-based and staggering; he cannot perform tandem gait. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Neck is mildly stiff with discomfort on flexion. No focal sensory deficits. Skin shows no neurocutaneous stigmata.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has signs of raised intracranial pressure (morning vomiting, papilledema, bilateral CN VI palsies) and truncal ataxia. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture for opening pressure and CSF studies
Urgent neuroimaging of the brain (CT or MRI)
Outpatient MRI in 2 weeks
Empiric oral migraine prophylaxis and discharge
Prescribe an antiemetic and observe at home
stages.explanation: Signs of elevated intracranial pressure with focal cerebellar findings demand urgent neuroimaging to identify a posterior fossa mass and obstructive hydrocephalus before anything else. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when raised ICP/mass effect is suspected because of the risk of fatal herniation. Delayed outpatient MRI is unsafe with progressive ICP signs. Migraine prophylaxis and home antiemetics with observation ignore red-flag features (papilledema, CN VI palsies, ataxia) and risk rapid deterioration. CN VI is a false-localizing sign of raised ICP given its long intracranial course.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Midline mass arising from the cerebellar vermis filling the fourth ventricle
Homogeneous enhancement; restricted diffusion (low ADC) within the solid tumor
Marked obstructive hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles
Nodular leptomeningeal enhancement coating the cerebellar folia and along the dorsal spinal cord ('drop metastases')

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A midline cerebellar mass with restricted diffusion plus leptomeningeal drop metastases in a 7-year-old is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma
Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Hemangioblastoma
Choroid plexus papilloma
stages.explanation: A solid, midline (vermian) posterior fossa mass that restricts diffusion (reflecting dense small round blue cells) and seeds the CSF with leptomeningeal drop metastases is classic for medulloblastoma, an embryonal WHO grade 4 tumor and the most common malignant pediatric brain tumor. Pilocytic astrocytoma is a cystic mass with an enhancing nodule, off-midline, without diffusion restriction or CSF seeding. Ependymoma arises within the fourth ventricle and extrudes through the foramina but seeds less commonly and is more heterogeneous. Hemangioblastoma is an adult cyst-with-nodule tumor linked to von Hippel–Lindau. Choroid plexus papilloma is an intraventricular frond-like CSF-overproducing tumor, not a vermian solid mass with drop mets.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Given enhancement suggesting CSF dissemination, which evaluation is essential to complete staging before treatment planning?
options: Bone marrow biopsy as the primary staging test
MRI of the entire spine plus CSF cytology (lumbar CSF after it is safe)
Whole-body FDG-PET as the staging standard
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis only
No additional staging is needed once the brain MRI is done
stages.explanation: Medulloblastoma is staged for neuraxis dissemination with contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire spine and CSF cytology (lumbar sampling, typically performed after surgery once it is safe), because metastatic (M) status critically affects risk stratification and therapy. Bone marrow biopsy is not the primary CNS staging test. FDG-PET and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis are not standard for staging CNS dissemination of medulloblastoma. Stopping at the brain MRI misses spinal drop metastases and CSF involvement that change the patient from standard- to high-risk and alter radiation and chemotherapy intensity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After addressing the hydrocephalus, what is the standard multimodal treatment for an average-aged child with medulloblastoma?
options: Surgery alone with no adjuvant therapy
Maximal safe resection followed by craniospinal irradiation and chemotherapy
Chemotherapy alone, avoiding all radiation
Focal radiation to the posterior fossa only, without chemotherapy
Observation after biopsy
stages.explanation: Standard care for medulloblastoma in a child over ~3 years is maximal safe resection followed by craniospinal irradiation (with a boost to the tumor bed) and adjuvant chemotherapy, reflecting its propensity for CSF dissemination. Surgery alone is insufficient given seeding risk and high relapse. Chemotherapy alone is generally reserved for very young children in whom radiation is deferred to limit neurotoxicity, and it is not standard for a 7-year-old. Focal posterior fossa radiation without craniospinal coverage or chemo inadequately treats neuraxis spread. Observation after biopsy is inappropriate for a malignant, disseminated embryonal tumor.

cases.diagnosis: Medulloblastoma (embryonal posterior fossa tumor, WHO grade 4) with CSF drop metastases
pearls: Morning vomiting, papilledema, bilateral CN VI palsies, and truncal ataxia in a child indicate a midline posterior fossa mass with hydrocephalus.
Avoid lumbar puncture before imaging when raised ICP from a mass is suspected—herniation risk.
A solid, midline cerebellar mass that restricts diffusion and seeds the CSF is classic for medulloblastoma.
Stage the entire neuraxis (spine MRI + CSF cytology); treat with resection, craniospinal irradiation, and chemotherapy in older children.

cases.title: A Girl with Headaches and a Tilted Head
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted for worsening headache, vomiting, and lethargy.
cases.history: A 6-year-old girl was admitted to the pediatric ward after three weeks of intermittent headaches and vomiting that have intensified over the past several days. Her parents report the vomiting is worse in the morning and she has become increasingly sleepy and unsteady. She holds her head tilted to one side and resists having it moved. There has been no fever, neck rash, recent illness, or trauma, and no seizures. Her appetite is poor. Past medical history is unremarkable; she was born at term with normal development and milestones. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with both parents and attends first grade. Immunizations are current. Family history is negative for brain tumors and neurocutaneous syndromes. On the ward she has had one further episode of vomiting and appears more drowsy than on arrival, prompting urgent reassessment. Review of systems is otherwise negative for diplopia at rest, focal weakness, or sensory complaints, although she reports the room 'spins' when she sits up quickly.
cases.exam: Drowsy but arousable girl with a head tilt and reluctance to flex the neck. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilledema. Pupils equal and reactive; partial left abducens palsy with horizontal diplopia on left gaze. Mild appendicular ataxia bilaterally and a wide-based, unsteady gait. Strength 5/5 throughout with normal tone. Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. No nuchal rigidity suggestive of meningitis, but discomfort with neck movement is present. The combination of bradycardia, drowsiness, and papilledema raises concern for rising intracranial pressure. Skin without neurocutaneous stigmata.

stages.label: RECOGNITION
stages.question: Her bradycardia (HR 62), drowsiness, papilledema, and morning vomiting most likely reflect which pathophysiologic process requiring urgent attention?
options: Dehydration from vomiting alone
Obstructive hydrocephalus with rising intracranial pressure
Bacterial meningitis
Benign positional vertigo
Migraine with aura
stages.explanation: Drowsiness, bradycardia, papilledema, abducens palsy, and morning vomiting form a picture of rising intracranial pressure, most often from obstructive hydrocephalus caused by a posterior fossa/fourth-ventricular mass blocking CSF flow; bradycardia with hypertension and irregular respirations (Cushing reflex) signals dangerous ICP elevation. Dehydration does not cause papilledema or CN VI palsy. Meningitis typically presents with fever and meningismus, absent here. Benign positional vertigo and migraine do not produce papilledema, abducens palsy, or progressive drowsiness and cannot explain the focal and ICP findings.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Heterogeneously enhancing mass arising within the fourth ventricle
Tumor extends inferiorly through the foramen of Magendie toward the cervicomedullary junction and laterally through the foramina of Luschka ('plastic'/molding growth)
Marked obstructive hydrocephalus with transependymal CSF flow
No definite spinal drop metastases on screening spine MRI

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A fourth-ventricular tumor that molds and extrudes through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie is most characteristic of which diagnosis?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
Choroid plexus carcinoma
Brainstem glioma
stages.explanation: The hallmark of fourth-ventricular ependymoma is a 'plastic' tumor that conforms to and squeezes through the fourth-ventricular outlet foramina (Luschka laterally, Magendie inferiorly), often extending toward the cervicomedullary junction. Medulloblastoma is a midline vermian solid mass that restricts diffusion and seeds CSF, but does not characteristically extrude through the foramina. Pilocytic astrocytoma is a cerebellar cyst-with-nodule lesion. Choroid plexus carcinoma typically arises in the lateral ventricles in young children and is highly aggressive but not defined by foraminal molding. A brainstem glioma is an intrinsic pontine lesion, not an intraventricular molding mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is increasingly obtunded from obstructive hydrocephalus. What is the most appropriate initial management priority?
options: Immediate craniospinal irradiation
Relief of hydrocephalus and elevated ICP (e.g., CSF diversion/EVD) followed by maximal safe tumor resection
Lumbar puncture to lower intracranial pressure
Start chemotherapy before any surgery
Transfer to outpatient oncology clinic for elective surgery
stages.explanation: The immediate threat is obstructive hydrocephalus with rising ICP, so the priority is CSF diversion/ICP control (such as an external ventricular drain or endoscopic third ventriculostomy) followed by maximal safe surgical resection, which is the cornerstone of ependymoma treatment and provides tissue diagnosis. Upfront craniospinal irradiation is not the first move and is not standard for localized ependymoma. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a posterior fossa mass due to herniation risk. Chemotherapy before surgery does not address the acute ICP crisis. Elective outpatient transfer is unsafe in an obtunded child with raised ICP.

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: After gross-total resection of a localized intracranial ependymoma in a 6-year-old, what is the standard adjuvant therapy?
options: No further therapy; surgery alone is curative for all ependymomas
Focal (involved-field) radiation to the tumor bed
Craniospinal irradiation for all patients regardless of stage
High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue as first-line
Hormonal therapy
stages.explanation: For localized intracranial ependymoma, standard adjuvant treatment after maximal safe resection is focal (involved-field) radiotherapy to the tumor bed, which improves local control; extent of resection is the strongest prognostic factor. Surgery alone is generally insufficient because of high local recurrence. Craniospinal irradiation is reserved for documented disseminated (M+) disease, not routine localized cases. High-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue is not first-line and is mainly explored in very young children to delay radiation. Hormonal therapy has no role in ependymoma.

cases.diagnosis: Fourth-ventricular ependymoma with obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Bradycardia, drowsiness, papilledema, and morning vomiting signal rising ICP from obstructive hydrocephalus—a neurosurgical emergency.
A 'plastic' fourth-ventricular tumor extruding through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie is classic for ependymoma.
Manage acute obstructive hydrocephalus first (CSF diversion) before definitive resection.
Localized intracranial ependymoma is treated with maximal safe resection plus focal radiotherapy; extent of resection is the key prognostic factor.

cases.title: Confusion and a Brain Lesion in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of progressive confusion and personality change.
cases.history: A 70-year-old retired teacher is admitted after three weeks of progressive memory difficulty, word-finding trouble, and uncharacteristic apathy noticed by his wife. She reports he has become withdrawn, forgets recent conversations, and twice got lost driving familiar routes. He has had mild headaches but no seizures, fever, or weight loss. He denies recent infections or night sweats. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes; medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. He has no allergies. He is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks rarely, and has no history of immunosuppression, organ transplant, or HIV risk factors. There is no family history of brain tumors. On the ward he is medically stable but his family is anxious for answers. Before admission an outside physician considered giving corticosteroids for a brain lesion seen on a CT, but they were held pending neuro-oncology input. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no focal limb weakness reported at home.
cases.exam: Alert but inattentive elderly man, oriented to person and place but not date, with impaired short-term recall (1/3 at five minutes) and mild anomia. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields full; no papilledema. Mild right pronator drift with strength 4+/5 in the right upper extremity; otherwise 5/5. Sensation intact. Reflexes slightly brisker on the right; right plantar response equivocal. Coordination intact; gait mildly cautious but not ataxic. No meningismus. No lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or skin lesions. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His outside CT showed a deep periventricular lesion. What is the most appropriate next imaging step?
options: Repeat non-contrast CT in one month
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain
FDG-PET of the brain alone
Cerebral angiography
No further imaging; proceed directly to empiric steroids
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI is the best next test to characterize a deep periventricular brain lesion: it defines location, enhancement pattern, multiplicity, and diffusion characteristics, all of which narrow the differential. A periventricular, homogeneously enhancing lesion that crosses the corpus callosum and restricts diffusion in an older patient strongly suggests primary CNS lymphoma. Repeat CT delays diagnosis. FDG-PET alone is adjunctive, not the primary characterization tool. Angiography evaluates vascular lesions, not a parenchymal mass. Proceeding to empiric steroids without imaging or tissue is precisely what should be avoided, because steroids can shrink lymphoma and obscure the biopsy.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Contrast-enhanced MRI:
Single homogeneously enhancing periventricular mass involving the corpus callosum and crossing the midline ('butterfly' configuration)
Restricted diffusion (low ADC) within the lesion
Moderate surrounding vasogenic edema
No ring enhancement; no hemorrhage
HIV screen pending; LDH mildly elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT PITFALL
stages.question: The covering team wants to start high-dose dexamethasone now for the edema while awaiting biopsy. In a patient with suspected primary CNS lymphoma, why is this generally inadvisable before tissue diagnosis?
options: Steroids worsen vasogenic edema in lymphoma
Steroids are lympholytic and can rapidly shrink the tumor, causing nondiagnostic ('vanishing') biopsies
Steroids cause the lesion to hemorrhage
Steroids are contraindicated in all brain tumors
Steroids convert lymphoma into glioblastoma
stages.explanation: Primary CNS lymphoma is exquisitely steroid-sensitive; corticosteroids are lympholytic and can cause the enhancing tumor to shrink or transiently disappear ('ghost' or vanishing tumor), yielding a nondiagnostic biopsy and delaying definitive diagnosis. Therefore steroids should be deferred until tissue is obtained unless there is impending herniation. Steroids reduce—not worsen—vasogenic edema. They do not cause hemorrhage. They are not contraindicated in all brain tumors; they are widely used for symptomatic edema once a diagnosis is secured. They do not transform tumor histology.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Steroids are withheld. Which is the most appropriate definitive diagnostic procedure?
options: Gross-total surgical resection of the lesion
Stereotactic needle biopsy of the enhancing lesion
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue
Serum protein electrophoresis as the confirmatory test
Bone marrow biopsy as the first diagnostic step
stages.explanation: Stereotactic needle biopsy is the standard for diagnosing primary CNS lymphoma because the tumor is deep, infiltrative, and chemo/radiosensitive—aggressive resection offers no survival benefit and adds morbidity. Gross-total resection is therefore not indicated and may harm eloquent deep structures. Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue treats blindly and forfeits diagnosis. Serum protein electrophoresis evaluates plasma cell dyscrasias, not CNS lymphoma. Bone marrow biopsy is part of systemic staging once lymphoma is confirmed but is not the first diagnostic step for the brain lesion.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & STAGING
stages.content:
Stereotactic biopsy: diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, angiocentric, CD20-positive, high Ki-67
HIV antibody: negative
Slit-lamp/ophthalmologic exam: no ocular (vitreoretinal) involvement
CSF cytology: atypical lymphoid cells; flow cytometry monoclonal B-cells
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone marrow: no systemic lymphoma (disease confined to CNS)

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: Diagnosis is confirmed as primary CNS diffuse large B-cell lymphoma confined to the CNS. What is the cornerstone of first-line treatment?
options: Surgical resection followed by observation
High-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy (often with rituximab)
Whole-brain radiotherapy alone as first-line
CHOP regimen identical to systemic lymphoma without CNS-penetrant agents
Single-agent oral temozolomide
stages.explanation: The backbone of primary CNS lymphoma therapy is high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy, which crosses the blood–brain barrier and achieves high response rates, frequently combined with rituximab and other CNS-penetrant agents (induction followed by consolidation). Surgery then observation is inadequate—resection has no survival benefit. Whole-brain radiotherapy alone yields high relapse and significant neurotoxicity, so it is used selectively, not as first-line monotherapy. Standard systemic CHOP uses agents that poorly penetrate the CNS and is ineffective for PCNSL. Single-agent temozolomide is a glioma drug, not the standard for PCNSL.

cases.diagnosis: Primary CNS lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell, immunocompetent host)
pearls: A periventricular, homogeneously enhancing, diffusion-restricting lesion crossing the corpus callosum in an older adult suggests primary CNS lymphoma.
Withhold corticosteroids before biopsy in suspected PCNSL—they can cause a nondiagnostic 'vanishing' tumor.
Diagnose with stereotactic biopsy, not resection; aggressive surgery offers no survival benefit.
First-line therapy is high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy (often with rituximab), not surgery or CHOP.

cases.title: Headache and a Ring of Lesions in an Immunocompromised Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of headache, confusion, and left-sided weakness.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man with HIV is admitted for two weeks of worsening headache, intermittent confusion, and progressive left arm and leg weakness. He was diagnosed with HIV several years ago but has been poorly adherent to antiretroviral therapy and missed recent clinic visits. He has no fever at presentation but reports recent night sweats. He denies seizures, neck stiffness, or visual loss. Past medical history includes oral candidiasis and a prior episode of Pneumocystis pneumonia. He is not currently taking trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis and admits to stopping ART months ago. He has no medication allergies. He smokes half a pack daily, drinks socially, and has a remote history of injection drug use. He lives alone. Family history is noncontributory. On the ward he is alert but slowed. The key clinical problem is distinguishing between the two leading causes of a focal brain lesion in advanced HIV. Review of systems is notable for poor appetite and a 10-pound weight loss over two months.
cases.exam: Thin, chronically ill-appearing man, alert but psychomotor-slowed and oriented to person and place only. Oral thrush is present. Cranial nerves grossly intact; visual fields full; no papilledema. Left hemiparesis with strength 3/5 in the left upper and lower extremities and a left pronator drift; right side 5/5. Left-sided hyperreflexia with an upgoing left plantar response. Sensation intact. No meningismus. Coordination limited by weakness; gait not formally tested. No lymphadenopathy or focal skin lesions. Cardiopulmonary exam normal aside from mild tachycardia.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CD4 count: 38 cells/µL
HIV viral load: 220,000 copies/mL
Toxoplasma IgG serology: pending
Contrast MRI brain: two ring-enhancing lesions—one in the right basal ganglia and one at the left gray-white junction—with surrounding edema and mass effect
LDH: elevated

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: In a patient with CD4 of 38 and ring-enhancing brain lesions, what are the two most important diagnoses to differentiate?
options: Glioblastoma versus meningioma
Cerebral toxoplasmosis versus primary CNS lymphoma
Bacterial abscess versus neurocysticercosis
Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy versus stroke
Cryptococcoma versus tuberculoma
stages.explanation: In advanced AIDS (CD4 < 50–100) with ring-enhancing lesions, the two leading and most consequential diagnoses to separate are cerebral toxoplasmosis and primary CNS lymphoma (typically EBV-associated), because their treatments differ entirely. Glioblastoma versus meningioma is not the AIDS-defining concern here. Bacterial abscess and neurocysticercosis are less likely given the immune context and imaging. PML is non-enhancing demyelination, not ring-enhancing, and stroke would follow a vascular territory. Cryptococcoma and tuberculoma are possible but far less common than toxoplasmosis and lymphoma in this setting.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.question: Which combination of tests best helps distinguish toxoplasmosis from CNS lymphoma noninvasively in this patient?
options: Serum LDH and peripheral smear
CSF EBV PCR plus thallium-201 SPECT (or FDG-PET)
Blood cultures and procalcitonin
Serum cryptococcal antigen and India ink
EEG and nerve conduction studies
stages.explanation: Two adjuncts strongly favor CNS lymphoma over toxoplasmosis: a positive CSF EBV PCR (lymphoma is EBV-driven in AIDS) and increased uptake on thallium-201 SPECT or FDG-PET (lymphoma is hypermetabolic; toxoplasmosis is typically not). Together these noninvasive tests, with toxoplasma serology and imaging, guide management and the decision to biopsy. Serum LDH and smear are nonspecific. Blood cultures/procalcitonin target bacteremia, not these CNS processes. Cryptococcal antigen/India ink evaluate cryptococcal meningitis, a different entity. EEG and nerve conduction studies do not differentiate these mass lesions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Toxoplasma IgG is positive and lesions are multiple. What is the most appropriate initial management strategy?
options: Immediate brain biopsy before any treatment
Empiric anti-toxoplasmosis therapy (pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin) with repeat imaging in ~2 weeks
Start high-dose methotrexate empirically for lymphoma
Whole-brain radiation without further workup
Observation off all therapy
stages.explanation: Standard practice in AIDS with positive Toxoplasma serology and compatible multiple ring-enhancing lesions is empiric anti-toxoplasmosis therapy (pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, leucovorin) with clinical and radiographic reassessment at about two weeks; clear improvement supports toxoplasmosis, whereas failure to respond prompts biopsy to evaluate for lymphoma. Immediate biopsy is reserved for atypical cases, negative serology, single lesions with lymphoma features, or treatment failure. Empiric high-dose methotrexate or whole-brain radiation without confirming lymphoma is inappropriate. Observation off therapy risks rapid deterioration from untreated toxoplasmosis. Restarting ART is also essential.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
After 2 weeks of empiric anti-toxoplasmosis therapy:
Lesions essentially unchanged; left hemiparesis persists
Thallium-201 SPECT: focal increased uptake in the right basal ganglia lesion
CSF EBV PCR: positive
Stereotactic biopsy: EBV-positive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given failure of empiric anti-toxoplasmosis therapy plus positive CSF EBV PCR, hypermetabolic SPECT, and confirmatory biopsy, what is the diagnosis?
options: Cerebral toxoplasmosis
EBV-associated primary CNS lymphoma (AIDS-related)
Tuberculoma
Pyogenic bacterial abscess
Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy
stages.explanation: The constellation—no response to anti-toxoplasmosis therapy, positive CSF EBV PCR, increased thallium-201 SPECT uptake, and biopsy showing EBV-positive diffuse large B-cell lymphoma—confirms AIDS-related primary CNS lymphoma, which is EBV-driven in the setting of severe immunosuppression. Toxoplasmosis would have improved on therapy and is typically EBV-PCR negative and not hypermetabolic. Tuberculoma and bacterial abscess have different microbiologic and imaging features and would not show EBV-positive lymphoid pathology. PML is non-enhancing demyelination caused by JC virus, not a ring-enhancing hypermetabolic mass. Management combines methotrexate-based therapy with ART.

cases.diagnosis: EBV-associated primary CNS lymphoma (AIDS-related)
pearls: Ring-enhancing brain lesions with CD4 < 100 should be worked up as toxoplasmosis versus primary CNS lymphoma.
CSF EBV PCR positivity and increased thallium-201 SPECT/FDG-PET uptake favor lymphoma; toxoplasmosis is usually EBV-negative and not hypermetabolic.
With positive Toxoplasma serology and multiple lesions, treat empirically for toxoplasmosis and reimage in ~2 weeks; failure to respond prompts biopsy.
AIDS-related primary CNS lymphoma is EBV-driven; treat with methotrexate-based therapy and reinstate antiretroviral therapy.

cases.title: One-Sided Hearing Loss and Ringing
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradual hearing loss and ringing in the right ear.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents to ENT clinic with about a year of slowly progressive hearing loss in her right ear, accompanied by constant high-pitched ringing (tinnitus) on the same side. She first noticed difficulty hearing on the phone with her right ear and trouble localizing sounds. More recently she reports mild unsteadiness, especially when turning quickly, but no true vertigo or falls. She denies otalgia, ear discharge, facial weakness or numbness, headaches, or prior ear infections. There is no history of loud noise exposure, ototoxic drug use, or trauma. Past medical history is notable only for well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications are levothyroxine 88 mcg daily; she has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. She works as a librarian. Family history is negative for hearing disorders, brain tumors, or neurofibromatosis. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no diplopia, dysphagia, or limb weakness. She is most concerned that the hearing loss is steadily worsening despite no obvious cause.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Otoscopy shows normal external canals and tympanic membranes bilaterally. Weber test lateralizes to the LEFT (better-hearing) ear; Rinne is positive (air > bone) bilaterally, consistent with right sensorineural hearing loss. Facial sensation is intact and facial movements are symmetric (CN V and VII normal). No nystagmus at rest. Mild instability on tandem gait but Romberg negative. Remaining cranial nerves intact; no dysmetria. Strength and sensation normal in all limbs. No café-au-lait macules or cutaneous neurofibromas.

stages.label: CHARACTERIZE THE LOSS
stages.question: Her Weber lateralizes to the left ear and Rinne is positive bilaterally. How is the right-sided hearing loss best characterized?
options: Conductive hearing loss on the right
Sensorineural hearing loss on the right
Conductive hearing loss on the left
Normal hearing bilaterally
Mixed hearing loss on the left
stages.explanation: With Rinne positive bilaterally (air conduction > bone conduction, the normal pattern) and Weber lateralizing to the left, the right ear is the affected side with sensorineural hearing loss: in sensorineural loss, Weber lateralizes to the better (unaffected) ear, here the left. Conductive loss on the right would lateralize Weber to the right and give a negative Rinne on the right—neither is present. Conductive loss on the left would lateralize Weber to the left only if the left were the affected conductive ear with abnormal Rinne, which is not the case. Hearing is not normal given her symptoms and Weber finding. Mixed loss on the left is not supported.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: For unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step after audiometry?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium
Tympanometry only
Empiric oral antibiotics
Reassurance and recheck in one year
stages.explanation: Asymmetric/unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus must be evaluated for a retrocochlear lesion, and gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the internal auditory canals/cerebellopontine angle is the gold standard to detect a vestibular schwannoma. Non-contrast CT poorly visualizes small CPA/IAC tumors and soft tissue. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear function and would not explain or detect a retrocochlear cause. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without infection. Reassurance with a one-year recheck risks missing a treatable, slowly growing tumor that warrants timely diagnosis.

stages.label: IMAGING & AUDIOLOGY
stages.content:
Audiogram: right high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss; reduced word recognition score on the right
MRI IAC with gadolinium: enhancing mass in the right cerebellopontine angle extending into the internal auditory canal ('ice-cream cone' appearance), ~1.4 cm
No brainstem compression; fourth ventricle patent
Left IAC normal; no second lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An enhancing cerebellopontine angle mass extending into the internal auditory canal with this presentation is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Meningioma
Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma)
Epidermoid cyst
Cerebellar metastasis
Glomus jugulare tumor
stages.explanation: A unilateral enhancing CPA mass extending into and widening the internal auditory canal ('ice-cream cone' shape), presenting with progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus, is classic for vestibular schwannoma, the most common CPA tumor arising from the vestibular division of CN VIII. Meningioma is the second most common CPA mass but is dural-based with a broad dura attachment and a dural tail, usually not extending into the IAC the same way. An epidermoid cyst does not enhance and follows CSF/restricts diffusion. Cerebellar metastasis is intra-axial and typically multiple with a cancer history. Glomus jugulare arises at the jugular foramen with pulsatile tinnitus and lower cranial nerve signs.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 1.4 cm vestibular schwannoma with preserved facial function and serviceable contralateral hearing, which management options are most appropriate to discuss?
options: Whole-brain radiotherapy
Observation with serial MRI, stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection depending on growth and patient factors
Empiric chemotherapy
Immediate radical resection in all patients regardless of size
Systemic corticosteroids as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: Management of a small-to-moderate vestibular schwannoma is individualized among three options: serial-MRI observation (many grow slowly), stereotactic radiosurgery for tumor control, or microsurgical resection, chosen by tumor size, growth rate, hearing status, age, and patient preference. Whole-brain radiotherapy is for diffuse metastatic disease, not a benign focal schwannoma. Chemotherapy has no role in sporadic schwannoma (bevacizumab is used selectively in NF2). Mandatory radical resection for all ignores valid conservative options. Corticosteroids are not definitive therapy for a schwannoma.

cases.diagnosis: Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) of the right cerebellopontine angle
pearls: Unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss with tinnitus warrants gadolinium MRI of the IAC to exclude vestibular schwannoma.
In sensorineural loss, Weber lateralizes to the better (unaffected) ear and Rinne stays air > bone.
An enhancing CPA mass extending into the IAC ('ice-cream cone') is classic for vestibular schwannoma, arising from CN VIII.
Management options include serial MRI observation, stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection, tailored to size, growth, hearing, and patient factors.

cases.title: A Young Woman with Hearing Loss and a Family Concern
cases.chiefComplaint: Hearing difficulty and imbalance, worried about a family pattern.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college student presents to clinic with about eight months of bilateral hearing difficulty, worse on the left, with intermittent ringing in both ears and increasing trouble understanding speech in noisy rooms. She also reports mild imbalance and occasional unsteadiness. She denies otalgia, discharge, facial weakness, or facial numbness. She notes that an uncle had 'tumors on his hearing nerves' and went deaf in his thirties, and a parent had early hearing loss; she is anxious about an inherited condition. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and a daily vitamin; no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks minimally. She has noticed a couple of small painless skin nodules and was previously told she had a 'cataract' at a young age. Family history is notable for the uncle's bilateral hearing-nerve tumors and the parent's early-onset deafness. Review of systems is otherwise negative for seizures, weakness, or visual loss, though she reports mild blurred vision in one eye.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman. Otoscopy normal bilaterally. Weber does not clearly lateralize; Rinne positive bilaterally, consistent with bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Facial strength and sensation are intact (CN V and VII normal). Mild gait instability on tandem testing; Romberg negative; no resting nystagmus. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Strength and sensation normal in all limbs; reflexes symmetric. Skin shows two small subcutaneous nodules; there are NO numerous café-au-lait macules and no axillary freckling. Slit-lamp later notes a posterior subcapsular lens opacity. No Lisch nodules.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given bilateral sensorineural hearing loss in a young adult with a striking family history, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Non-contrast CT of the temporal bones
Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and internal auditory canals
Tympanometry alone
Empiric corticosteroids for sudden hearing loss
Genetic testing before any imaging
stages.explanation: Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss in a young patient with a suggestive family history requires gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and IACs to evaluate for bilateral vestibular schwannomas and other CNS tumors, which is the imaging hallmark needed to establish the diagnosis. Temporal bone CT does not adequately image small CPA/IAC tumors or other intracranial lesions. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear mechanics, not retrocochlear pathology. Empiric steroids target sudden idiopathic loss, not this chronic bilateral pattern. Genetic testing is important but follows or accompanies imaging that demonstrates the characteristic tumors; imaging is the immediate priority.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Gadolinium MRI brain and IACs:
Bilateral enhancing cerebellopontine angle masses extending into both internal auditory canals (bilateral vestibular schwannomas)
A separate dural-based enhancing mass along the left convexity (consistent with a meningioma)
No hydrocephalus
Spine MRI: a small intradural enhancing lesion (consistent with a schwannoma)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas plus a meningioma and a spinal schwannoma in a young adult are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1
Neurofibromatosis type 2
Von Hippel–Lindau disease
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Sturge–Weber syndrome
stages.explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic for neurofibromatosis type 2, an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the NF2 (merlin) tumor-suppressor gene, which also predisposes to meningiomas, other schwannomas, ependymomas, and juvenile posterior subcapsular cataracts. NF1 features café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules, and optic gliomas—not bilateral vestibular schwannomas. Von Hippel–Lindau causes hemangioblastomas, retinal angiomas, and renal cell carcinoma. Tuberous sclerosis causes cortical tubers, subependymal nodules/SEGAs, and skin findings like ash-leaf spots. Sturge–Weber is a port-wine stain with leptomeningeal angiomas, not nerve-sheath tumors.

stages.label: GENETIC COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding inheritance and family screening, which statement is most accurate for this condition?
options: It is X-linked recessive, so only males are affected
It is autosomal dominant; first-degree relatives should be offered screening and genetic counseling
It is autosomal recessive and not heritable from a single affected parent
It is acquired and carries no inherited risk
It is mitochondrial, inherited only from the father
stages.explanation: Neurofibromatosis type 2 is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern (each child of an affected parent has a 50% risk), so genetic counseling and screening of first-degree relatives—often with NF2 gene testing and surveillance MRI/audiology—are recommended; note that a substantial fraction of cases arise from de novo or mosaic mutations. It is not X-linked recessive, not autosomal recessive, not purely acquired without inherited risk, and not mitochondrial. The family history of an uncle with bilateral hearing-nerve tumors and an affected parent fits dominant transmission.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How is the management of bilateral vestibular schwannomas in this disorder best approached?
options: Immediate bilateral total resection of both tumors
Multidisciplinary, hearing-preservation-focused care (surveillance, bevacizumab in selected cases, radiosurgery or surgery individualized) at a specialized center
Whole-brain radiotherapy to all lesions
Cytotoxic chemotherapy identical to systemic cancer
No treatment is ever indicated
stages.explanation: Care for NF2-related bilateral vestibular schwannomas is multidisciplinary and prioritizes hearing and facial-nerve preservation: options include serial surveillance, bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) which can improve hearing and shrink tumors in selected patients, and individualized radiosurgery or microsurgery, ideally at an NF2 specialty center, with attention to the other tumors (meningioma, spinal schwannoma). Immediate bilateral total resection risks bilateral deafness and facial palsy and is not the default. Whole-brain radiotherapy is inappropriate for these benign tumors and carries risk of inducing/worsening tumors. Standard cytotoxic chemotherapy is not used. 'No treatment ever' is wrong; tumors are monitored and treated as needed.

cases.diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 2 with bilateral vestibular schwannomas
pearls: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic for neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2/merlin gene).
NF2 also predisposes to meningiomas, other schwannomas, ependymomas, and juvenile posterior subcapsular cataracts—distinct from NF1's café-au-lait macules and Lisch nodules.
NF2 is autosomal dominant; offer genetic counseling and screening to first-degree relatives (with a notable rate of de novo/mosaic cases).
Management is multidisciplinary and hearing-preservation-focused: surveillance, bevacizumab in selected cases, and individualized radiosurgery or microsurgery.

cases.title: Bumping Into Things on Both Sides
cases.chiefComplaint: Trouble seeing to the sides and frequent minor accidents while driving.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man presents to clinic because he keeps clipping his car's side mirrors and bumping into people approaching from the sides. Over roughly six months he has noticed he must turn his head to see things in his peripheral vision, and he recently sideswiped a mailbox he 'never saw coming.' He also reports mild, dull headaches and decreased libido and energy that he attributed to aging. He denies double vision, eye pain, flashes, or floaters. There is no galactorrhea. Past medical history includes hyperlipidemia. Medications are atorvastatin 20 mg nightly; no allergies. He does not smoke, drinks a few beers weekly, and works as a delivery driver—his vision symptoms now worry him about his job. Family history is unremarkable for tumors or endocrine disease. Review of systems reveals he has been more fatigued, with some cold intolerance and reduced shaving frequency over the past several months. He denies polyuria or excessive thirst. He has had no recent weight change.
cases.exam: Well-appearing middle-aged man in no distress. Visual acuity is mildly reduced bilaterally. Confrontation visual fields reveal bilateral temporal field loss; he misses targets in both outer (temporal) fields while nasal fields are preserved. Pupils equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements full; no ptosis or diplopia. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema; optic discs appear mildly pale. Remaining cranial nerves intact. Facial sensation and strength normal. Motor strength 5/5 throughout; sensation intact; reflexes symmetric. No galactorrhea on exam. No acromegalic features. Gait normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: His confrontation testing shows loss of both temporal visual fields (bitemporal hemianopia). Where is the lesion localized?
options: Left optic nerve
Optic chiasm (compression of crossing nasal retinal fibers)
Right optic tract
Occipital (calcarine) cortex
Retina, bilaterally
stages.explanation: Bitemporal hemianopia results from compression at the optic chiasm, where the nasal retinal fibers (which carry the temporal visual fields) cross; a sellar/suprasellar mass pressing on the chiasm classically produces this pattern. A single optic nerve lesion causes monocular vision loss, not a bitemporal pattern. An optic tract lesion causes a contralateral homonymous hemianopia. Occipital cortex lesions cause homonymous hemianopia (often with macular sparing), not bitemporal loss. Bilateral retinal disease would not produce this specific crossing-fiber pattern and would show fundus abnormalities.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to identify the cause of his chiasmal compression?
options: Non-contrast CT of the orbits
MRI of the brain with dedicated pituitary/sellar views and contrast
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Lumbar puncture
Visual evoked potentials alone
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain with dedicated pituitary/sellar imaging and contrast is the best test to evaluate a chiasmal compressive lesion, defining a sellar mass such as a pituitary macroadenoma and its relationship to the chiasm and cavernous sinuses. Orbital CT targets the orbits and poorly characterizes the sella and parasellar soft tissue. Carotid duplex (B/C) evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis, irrelevant here. Lumbar puncture does not localize or characterize a compressive sellar mass and could be hazardous with a mass. Visual evoked potentials assess conduction but do not reveal the structural cause; formal perimetry plus MRI are the priorities.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI sella with contrast: 1.8 cm sellar mass with suprasellar extension elevating and compressing the optic chiasm; no cavernous sinus invasion
Prolactin: 28 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
TSH 0.8 µIU/mL with low free T4; morning cortisol low-normal
IGF-1 normal; no clinical acromegaly
Formal perimetry: bitemporal hemianopia confirmed

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The prolactin is only mildly elevated (28 ng/mL) despite a 1.8 cm sellar mass. How should this be interpreted?
options: This is a prolactinoma and warrants a dopamine agonist as definitive therapy
The mild elevation reflects stalk effect from a non-functioning macroadenoma, not a prolactinoma
The prolactin is normal and excludes any pituitary tumor
This indicates a growth hormone-secreting tumor
This confirms Cushing disease
stages.explanation: A large sellar mass with only a mildly elevated prolactin indicates the 'stalk effect': the tumor disrupts the pituitary stalk, reducing dopaminergic inhibition and modestly raising prolactin—consistent with a non-functioning macroadenoma rather than a true prolactinoma (which usually produces markedly higher prolactin, often >200 ng/mL with a macroadenoma). Thus a dopamine agonist as definitive therapy is not appropriate here. The prolactin is mildly elevated, not normal, and a tumor is clearly present. It does not indicate a GH-secreting tumor (D; IGF-1 normal, no acromegaly) or Cushing disease. The low free T4 and low-normal cortisol also suggest hypopituitarism from compression.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this non-functioning pituitary macroadenoma causing progressive bitemporal hemianopia, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Dopamine agonist (cabergoline) as first-line definitive therapy
Transsphenoidal surgical resection to decompress the optic chiasm
Observation with repeat MRI in 2 years
Whole-brain radiotherapy
Lifelong somatostatin analog therapy
stages.explanation: A non-functioning macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm with progressive visual field loss is a surgical indication: transsphenoidal resection decompresses the chiasm and often improves vision, with hormonal evaluation and replacement as needed. A dopamine agonist is the treatment for prolactinomas, not non-functioning adenomas, and would not reliably shrink this tumor. Observation is unsafe with progressive visual compromise. Whole-brain radiotherapy is not first-line and risks hypopituitarism and optic toxicity; focused radiation is reserved for residual/recurrent disease. Somatostatin analogs are used for GH/TSH-secreting tumors, not this non-functioning adenoma.

cases.diagnosis: Non-functioning pituitary macroadenoma with chiasmal compression
pearls: Bitemporal hemianopia localizes to the optic chiasm, classically compressed by a sellar/suprasellar mass.
Dedicated contrast MRI of the sella plus formal perimetry is the workup for chiasmal compression.
A large sellar mass with only mildly elevated prolactin suggests 'stalk effect' from a non-functioning adenoma, not a prolactinoma (which usually causes markedly high prolactin).
A non-functioning macroadenoma compressing the chiasm with visual loss is treated with transsphenoidal resection; assess for and replace pituitary hormone deficiencies.

cases.title: Missed Periods and Breast Discharge
cases.chiefComplaint: I haven't had a period in five months and my breasts keep leaking milk.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with 5 months of amenorrhea and bilateral milky nipple discharge that stains her bra. She and her partner have been trying to conceive for a year without success. She also reports decreased libido and occasional mild headaches but denies visual changes. She had regular menses until about 8 months ago. She is not pregnant (home test negative) and is not breastfeeding. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only a daily multivitamin; she takes no antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or estrogen. No herbal supplements. NKDA. Social: marketing analyst, drinks alcohol socially, never smoked, no recreational drugs. Family history: mother with hypothyroidism; no known pituitary or endocrine tumors. Review of systems negative for cold intolerance, constipation, weight change, and skin or hair changes. She denies polyuria, polydipsia, and nausea. No history of chest wall trauma or shingles.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Visual acuity normal; confrontation visual fields full to finger counting in all quadrants. Extraocular movements intact, pupils equal and reactive. Thyroid normal size without nodules. Breast exam: expressible bilateral milky discharge, no masses, no skin changes, no lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams unremarkable. Skin without acne, striae, or hirsutism. No acromegalic features. Neurologic exam nonfocal with normal gait and reflexes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After confirming she is not pregnant, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order serum prolactin, TSH, and a metabolic/renal panel
Proceed directly to transsphenoidal pituitary surgery
Start combined oral contraceptives empirically
Order an OGTT with growth hormone measurement
stages.explanation: Amenorrhea plus galactorrhea points to hyperprolactinemia, so the first step is a serum prolactin along with TSH (primary hypothyroidism raises TRH and prolactin) and a renal panel (uremia impairs prolactin clearance); pregnancy must also be excluded, which was done. Surgery is premature before biochemical confirmation and imaging. Empiric combined contraceptives would mask the problem and not address the cause. An OGTT with GH evaluates acromegaly, which she has no features of. Establishing the biochemical etiology before treatment is the correct sequence.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Beta-hCG: negative
Prolactin: 168 ng/mL (normal <25)
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
FSH: 3.2 mIU/mL
LH: 2.8 mIU/mL
Estradiol: low

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a prolactin of 168 ng/mL with normal thyroid and renal function, what is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Repeat prolactin in 6 months without imaging
Pituitary MRI with and without contrast
Dexamethasone suppression test
CT head without contrast
stages.explanation: A prolactin of 168 ng/mL is well above the level expected from medications or stalk effect (usually <100-150) and strongly suggests a prolactin-secreting adenoma, so a dedicated pituitary MRI with and without contrast is the imaging test of choice to characterize tumor size and parasellar extension. Simply repeating the level delays diagnosis of a likely tumor. A dexamethasone suppression test evaluates cortisol/Cushing, not prolactin. CT poorly visualizes the pituitary and sella compared with MRI. MRI both confirms the adenoma and distinguishes micro- from macroadenoma, guiding management.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Pituitary MRI: 8 mm intrasellar enhancing lesion (microadenoma) in the left pituitary
No suprasellar extension
Optic chiasm not compressed
Cavernous sinuses uninvolved

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wishes to restore fertility. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this prolactin microadenoma?
options: Transsphenoidal resection of the adenoma
Stereotactic radiosurgery to the sella
Cabergoline, a dopamine agonist
Observation alone with annual MRI
stages.explanation: Dopamine agonists are first-line for prolactinomas of any size because lactotrophs are under dopaminergic inhibition; cabergoline is preferred over bromocriptine for greater efficacy, better tolerability, and twice-weekly dosing, and it normalizes prolactin, shrinks the tumor, and restores ovulation and fertility in most patients. Surgery is reserved for dopamine-agonist intolerance or resistance. Radiosurgery is a later-line option with slow onset and hypopituitarism risk. Observation alone would not address her symptomatic hyperprolactinemia or infertility. Medical therapy here is both diagnostic-confirmatory and definitive for most patients.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Regarding cabergoline and her plan to conceive, which counseling point is most accurate?
options: Cabergoline must be continued throughout the entire pregnancy
She should switch to a GnRH agonist once pregnant
Dopamine agonists are usually stopped once pregnancy is confirmed for a microadenoma
Pregnancy is contraindicated until the tumor is surgically removed
stages.explanation: For a microadenoma, the risk of clinically significant tumor enlargement during pregnancy is very low (about 2-3%), so dopamine agonists are typically discontinued once pregnancy is confirmed, with clinical monitoring for symptoms of tumor growth (headache, visual changes). Continuing therapy throughout pregnancy is generally reserved for large macroadenomas or symptomatic growth. A GnRH agonist would suppress the pituitary-gonadal axis and is irrational here. Surgery before conception is unnecessary because medical therapy already restores fertility safely. Visual field testing each trimester is added only if a macroadenoma is present.

cases.diagnosis: Prolactinoma (prolactin-secreting pituitary microadenoma)
pearls: Amenorrhea, galactorrhea, and infertility in a woman should prompt prolactin, TSH, and renal function plus a pregnancy test.
Prolactin >150-200 ng/mL strongly suggests a prolactinoma rather than stalk effect or medication.
Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) are first-line for prolactinomas of any size; surgery is for intolerance or resistance.
For microadenomas, dopamine agonists are usually stopped once pregnancy is confirmed, with clinical monitoring.

cases.title: Worst Headache and Sudden Double Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: I had the worst headache of my life come on out of nowhere and now I'm seeing double.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents to the ED with sudden-onset severe retro-orbital and bifrontal headache that peaked within seconds about 4 hours ago, described as the worst of his life. He developed nausea, two episodes of vomiting, and horizontal double vision shortly after. He felt lightheaded and nearly fainted standing up. He denies trauma, neck stiffness, fever, or prior similar headaches. He has been more fatigued and less interested in sex over the past year but never sought care. PMH: hypertension, untreated. Medications: none reliably; allergic to none (NKDA). Social: works in construction, drinks 2-3 beers nightly, former smoker. Family history noncontributory. On further questioning he has had occasional mild headaches and decreased peripheral vision noticed when driving. No anticoagulant use. No recent illness. ROS notable for cold intolerance and reduced shaving frequency over months.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man in moderate distress. Alert and oriented. Right eye is deviated 'down and out' with ptosis; right pupil is dilated and sluggishly reactive. Left eye movements intact. Visual fields show bitemporal deficit on confrontation. No nuchal rigidity. No focal limb weakness; reflexes symmetric. Skin cool and clammy. Cardiac exam: tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. Mucous membranes dry. No rash. Romberg deferred due to hypotension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He is hypotensive and tachycardic. Beyond IV fluids, what is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Emergent IV hydrocortisone (stress-dose glucocorticoid)
IV labetalol to control presumed hypertensive crisis
Emergent levothyroxine bolus
Lumbar puncture to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage
stages.explanation: The picture of thunderclap headache, acute ophthalmoplegia, and hemodynamic instability in someone with chronic symptoms of hypopituitarism suggests pituitary apoplexy with acute secondary adrenal insufficiency; emergent stress-dose hydrocortisone is life-saving and must not wait for confirmatory cortisol or imaging. Labetalol is dangerous given his hypotension. Levothyroxine before glucocorticoid can precipitate adrenal crisis and is never given first. An LP is unsafe before imaging with possible mass/raised pressure and would delay the critical steroid. Give hydrocortisone first, then proceed to imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Na: 129 mEq/L
K: 4.0 mEq/L
Glucose: 58 mg/dL
Cortisol (random): 2 µg/dL (inappropriately low)
TSH: 0.6 mIU/L  Free T4: 0.5 ng/dL (low)
Prolactin: 4 ng/mL (low)
Non-contrast CT head: hyperdense sellar/suprasellar mass with areas suggesting hemorrhage
MRI pituitary: hemorrhagic enlargement of a pituitary macroadenoma with displacement of the optic chiasm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The labs and MRI are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage
Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into a pituitary adenoma)
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Bacterial meningitis
stages.explanation: Hemorrhage into a pre-existing pituitary macroadenoma producing sudden headache, ophthalmoplegia, chiasmal compression, and acute panhypopituitarism (low cortisol, low free T4, low prolactin) defines pituitary apoplexy; the hyperdense sellar mass on CT and hemorrhagic adenoma on MRI confirm it. Aneurysmal SAH typically shows blood in basal cisterns, not an enhancing sellar mass with pituitary failure. Cavernous sinus thrombosis usually has chemosis, proptosis, and fever with a thrombosed sinus. Meningitis presents with fever and nuchal rigidity, not isolated cranial nerve III palsy and pituitary collapse.

stages.label: ANATOMY
stages.question: His right eye is 'down and out' with ptosis and a dilated pupil. Which structure is most likely compressed?
options: Optic chiasm
Abducens nerve (CN VI) in the cavernous sinus
Oculomotor nerve (CN III) in the cavernous sinus
Trigeminal nerve (CN V1)
stages.explanation: A 'down and out' eye with ptosis and a dilated, sluggish pupil indicates a complete oculomotor (CN III) palsy with parasympathetic involvement; lateral expansion of the hemorrhagic adenoma into the cavernous sinus compresses CN III, which is the most commonly affected nerve in apoplexy. Optic chiasm compression causes bitemporal hemianopia (also present) but not the ophthalmoplegia/ptosis. CN VI palsy causes isolated horizontal diplopia with an adducted eye, not 'down and out.' CN V1 is sensory to the forehead and would not cause these motor findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After hemodynamic stabilization and steroids, he has a progressive bitemporal field cut and worsening ophthalmoplegia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Outpatient endocrine follow-up in 2 weeks
Urgent neurosurgical evaluation for transsphenoidal decompression
Start cabergoline to shrink the mass
Whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: Progressive or severe visual deterioration and worsening neuro-ophthalmologic deficits in pituitary apoplexy are indications for urgent transsphenoidal surgical decompression to relieve pressure on the optic chiasm and cranial nerves; medical management alone is reserved for stable, mild deficits. Outpatient follow-up is unsafe with evolving deficits. Cabergoline is too slow and only useful if this were a prolactinoma, but prolactin is low here. Radiation has no role in the acute setting and would not relieve mass effect quickly. Surgery preserves vision when deficits are progressive.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: On long-term hormone replacement after recovery, which principle about glucocorticoid and thyroid dosing is correct?
options: Always replace thyroid hormone first, then add glucocorticoid
Glucocorticoid must be replaced before or with thyroid hormone
Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone) is essential
DDAVP is required in all patients post-apoplexy
stages.explanation: In central (secondary) adrenal insufficiency, glucocorticoids must be started before or together with thyroid hormone, because giving levothyroxine first increases metabolic demand and cortisol clearance, precipitating an adrenal crisis. Replacing thyroid first is the classic dangerous error. Fludrocortisone is unnecessary in secondary adrenal insufficiency because the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis is intact (ACTH does not drive aldosterone). DDAVP is needed only if central diabetes insipidus develops, which is not universal after apoplexy. Lifelong hormone monitoring and patient education on stress dosing are essential.

cases.diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into a pituitary macroadenoma with acute panhypopituitarism)
pearls: Pituitary apoplexy = thunderclap headache + ophthalmoplegia + visual loss + acute hypopituitarism; give stress-dose hydrocortisone immediately.
