CN III palsy ('down and out' eye, ptosis, dilated pupil) from cavernous sinus extension is the most common ocular finding.
Always replace glucocorticoids before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.
Urgent transsphenoidal decompression is indicated for progressive or severe visual deficits.

cases.title: Rings That No Longer Fit
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands and jaw have gotten bigger and my wedding ring doesn't fit anymore.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man reports that over the past 3 years his hands and feet have enlarged, requiring two larger ring sizes and bigger shoes. His wife notes his facial features have coarsened and his jaw appears more prominent, with new gaps between his teeth. He has frontal headaches, excessive sweating, and new snoring with daytime sleepiness. He was recently diagnosed with hypertension and 'borderline diabetes.' He also reports tingling in both hands at night. PMH: hypertension, prediabetes. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily; allergic to none (NKDA). Social: accountant, rare alcohol, never smoked. Family history: father with type 2 diabetes; no known endocrine tumors. ROS: decreased libido, joint aches in hands and knees, and a recent colonoscopy that found a tubular adenoma. No visual changes reported but he admits he has not had formal field testing. Denies galactorrhea and palpitations.
cases.exam: Large, coarse facial features with prominent brow and prognathism; macroglossia and increased interdental spacing. Hands large and 'doughy' with spade-like fingers; positive Tinel sign bilaterally. Skin thickened and oily with skin tags. Visual fields full to confrontation; no obvious bitemporal deficit. Thyroid mildly enlarged without nodules. Cardiac exam normal S1/S2, no murmur. No acne or striae. Mild bilateral knee crepitus. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single test is the best initial screen for suspected acromegaly?
options: Random serum growth hormone (GH) level
Serum insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1)
Pituitary MRI
24-hour urinary free cortisol
stages.explanation: IGF-1 is the best screening test for acromegaly because it reflects integrated GH secretion over time and, unlike GH, is not subject to large pulsatile and diurnal fluctuations; an age-matched elevated IGF-1 strongly supports the diagnosis. A random GH is unreliable because GH is secreted in pulses and can be normal or even high in healthy people. MRI is for tumor localization after biochemical confirmation, not the initial screen. Urinary free cortisol screens for Cushing syndrome, which his exam (no striae, no central obesity) does not suggest. Confirm IGF-1 elevation, then proceed to a confirmatory test.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
IGF-1: 720 ng/mL (markedly elevated for age)
Fasting glucose: 124 mg/dL
HbA1c: 6.7%
Prolactin: 38 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
TSH: 1.4 mIU/L  Free T4: normal
Phosphate: 4.9 mg/dL (high-normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: IGF-1 is elevated. What is the confirmatory test for acromegaly?
options: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) with GH measurement showing failure to suppress
Insulin tolerance test
Overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Repeat random GH level
stages.explanation: The confirmatory test for acromegaly is the OGTT with serial GH measurements: in normal individuals a 75 g glucose load suppresses GH to a very low level, whereas in acromegaly GH fails to suppress (remains elevated), confirming autonomous GH secretion. An insulin tolerance test assesses GH reserve/adrenal axis for deficiency, the opposite problem. The dexamethasone suppression test evaluates cortisol for Cushing. A repeat random GH remains uninterpretable due to pulsatility. After biochemical confirmation, pituitary MRI localizes the adenoma. The mildly elevated prolactin reflects either co-secretion or stalk effect.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
OGTT: GH nadir 4.2 ng/mL (fails to suppress below 1 ng/mL)
Pituitary MRI: 14 mm enhancing pituitary macroadenoma
No optic chiasm compression
Colonoscopy (recent): one tubular adenoma removed

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The OGTT confirms acromegaly and MRI shows a resectable macroadenoma. What is the first-line treatment?
options: Lifelong cabergoline monotherapy
Transsphenoidal surgical resection of the adenoma
External beam radiation therapy
Pegvisomant alone indefinitely
stages.explanation: Transsphenoidal surgical resection is first-line for most GH-secreting adenomas, especially a resectable macroadenoma, and offers the best chance of biochemical remission. Cabergoline has only modest GH-lowering effect and is adjunctive, useful mainly when IGF-1 is only mildly elevated or prolactin is co-secreted. Radiation is reserved for residual or recurrent disease because of slow onset and hypopituitarism risk. Pegvisomant, a GH-receptor antagonist, normalizes IGF-1 but does not shrink the tumor and is used for persistent disease after surgery. Somatostatin analogs are used pre-/post-op or when surgery is not curative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the leading cause of increased mortality in untreated acromegaly?
options: Colorectal carcinoma
Cardiovascular disease (cardiomyopathy, hypertension)
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Obstructive sleep apnea alone
stages.explanation: Cardiovascular disease, including hypertensive heart disease and acromegalic cardiomyopathy, is the leading cause of excess mortality in acromegaly, which is why early biochemical control matters. Colorectal carcinoma risk is increased and warrants colonoscopy screening, but it is not the leading cause of death. Carpal tunnel syndrome is common and morbid but not life-threatening. Obstructive sleep apnea contributes to cardiovascular risk and should be treated, but on its own it is not the primary mortality driver. Controlling GH/IGF-1 reduces cardiovascular and overall mortality.

cases.diagnosis: Acromegaly due to a GH-secreting pituitary macroadenoma
pearls: IGF-1 is the best screening test for acromegaly; random GH is unreliable due to pulsatile secretion.
Confirm with an OGTT showing failure of GH to suppress below 1 ng/mL.
Transsphenoidal surgery is first-line; somatostatin analogs, cabergoline, and pegvisomant are adjuncts.
Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death; screen for colon cancer and sleep apnea.

cases.title: Weight Gain, Bruising, and Stretch Marks
cases.chiefComplaint: I've gained weight around my middle, bruise easily, and have these purple stretch marks.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman reports 18 months of progressive central weight gain, a 'round' face, easy bruising, and wide purple abdominal striae. She has new hypertension, prediabetes, irregular menses, proximal muscle weakness (trouble climbing stairs), and low mood. She denies exogenous glucocorticoid use of any form, including inhalers, joint injections, and skin creams. PMH: hypertension, prediabetes, depression. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily; NKDA. Social: teacher, rare alcohol, never smoked, no significant occupational exposures. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes; no endocrine tumors. ROS: new acne, increased facial hair, and a recent vertebral compression fracture from minimal trauma. She denies headaches and visual changes. No flushing, palpitations, or episodic symptoms suggestive of pheochromocytoma. No recent severe stress or illness that might cause physiologic hypercortisolism.
cases.exam: Cushingoid appearance with moon facies, dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat pads, and central obesity with thin extremities. Wide (>1 cm) violaceous abdominal striae. Multiple ecchymoses on the forearms; skin thin. Mild facial hirsutism and acne. Proximal muscle weakness: difficulty rising from a squat. Visual fields full to confrontation. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal. No abdominal masses or bruits. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After excluding exogenous steroids, which is an appropriate first-line screening test for Cushing syndrome?
options: Single random serum cortisol level
Late-night salivary cortisol (or 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test)
Plasma ACTH alone
Pituitary MRI
stages.explanation: Screening for Cushing syndrome uses tests that capture cortisol dysregulation: late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, or the 1 mg overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test; two abnormal results confirm hypercortisolism. A single random serum cortisol is uninterpretable because of diurnal variation and stress. Plasma ACTH is used to determine the cause after hypercortisolism is confirmed, not to screen. Pituitary MRI comes only after biochemical confirmation and ACTH testing, because incidental pituitary lesions are common and can mislead. Establish hypercortisolism first, then localize.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
24-hour urinary free cortisol: 4x upper limit of normal
Late-night salivary cortisol: elevated
1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression: cortisol fails to suppress (8 µg/dL)
Plasma ACTH: 78 pg/mL (inappropriately normal/high)
HbA1c: 6.4%  K: 3.6 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Hypercortisolism is confirmed and ACTH is elevated. What does this indicate, and what is the next step?
options: ACTH-independent disease; obtain adrenal CT
Exogenous steroid use; stop the offending agent
Pseudo-Cushing; reassure and observe
ACTH-dependent disease; perform a high-dose dexamethasone suppression test and pituitary MRI
stages.explanation: An elevated (non-suppressed) ACTH indicates ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome, narrowing the source to a pituitary adenoma (Cushing disease) or an ectopic ACTH-secreting tumor; the next steps are a high-dose dexamethasone suppression test (pituitary tumors often suppress, ectopic usually does not) and a pituitary MRI. Low/suppressed ACTH would indicate ACTH-independent adrenal disease warranting adrenal CT. Exogenous steroids were excluded and would suppress ACTH. Pseudo-Cushing is excluded by markedly elevated UFC and clear clinical features. Distinguishing pituitary from ectopic source guides definitive therapy.

stages.label: FURTHER WORKUP
stages.content:
High-dose dexamethasone (8 mg) suppression: cortisol suppresses by >50% (favors pituitary source)
Pituitary MRI: no definite adenoma identified (or 4 mm equivocal lesion)
Chest/abdomen CT: no ectopic mass identified

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: ACTH-dependent Cushing with an equivocal/absent pituitary lesion on MRI. What is the best test to localize the ACTH source?
options: Repeat the high-dose dexamethasone suppression test
Adrenal venous sampling
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) with CRH stimulation
Whole-body PET scan as first localization step
stages.explanation: When ACTH-dependent Cushing is confirmed but the pituitary MRI is normal or shows only a small/equivocal lesion, inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) with CRH stimulation is the gold standard to distinguish a pituitary source from ectopic ACTH: a central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient (>2 at baseline, >3 after CRH) confirms Cushing disease and can lateralize the adenoma. Repeating the HDDST adds little once results are known. Adrenal venous sampling is for primary aldosteronism, not ACTH-dependent disease. Whole-body PET is reserved for hunting an ectopic source after IPSS suggests a non-pituitary origin.

stages.label: IPSS RESULTS
stages.content:
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling with CRH:
Baseline central-to-peripheral ACTH ratio: 4.2 (>2 positive)
Post-CRH central-to-peripheral ratio: 9.0 (>3 positive)
Right-to-left gradient lateralizes to the right pituitary

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: IPSS confirms a pituitary source lateralizing to the right. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Transsphenoidal resection of the pituitary adenoma
Bilateral adrenalectomy
Lifelong ketoconazole
Pituitary radiation as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for Cushing disease is selective transsphenoidal resection of the ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, which IPSS has now confirmed and lateralized; it offers the highest remission rates with preservation of normal pituitary function. Bilateral adrenalectomy is a last resort and risks Nelson syndrome from the persistent pituitary tumor. Ketoconazole and other steroidogenesis inhibitors are used for medical control before surgery or when surgery fails, not as definitive cure. Radiation is second-line for residual or recurrent disease due to slow onset and hypopituitarism risk. Postoperative cortisol monitoring confirms remission.

cases.diagnosis: Cushing disease (ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma)
pearls: Confirm hypercortisolism with two screening tests (late-night salivary cortisol, 24-h UFC, or 1 mg DST) before localizing.
Elevated/non-suppressed ACTH = ACTH-dependent disease (pituitary vs ectopic); suppressed ACTH = adrenal source.
Inferior petrosal sinus sampling with CRH is the gold standard when pituitary MRI is equivocal.
Transsphenoidal resection is the definitive treatment for Cushing disease.

cases.title: Falling Off the Growth Curve and Bumping Into Things
cases.chiefComplaint: He's stopped growing like his friends and keeps bumping into things on his sides.
cases.history: An 11-year-old boy is brought in by his parents because he has not grown in height over the past 1.5 years and is now the shortest in his class. They also note he bumps into door frames and misses objects to his sides, and he complains of intermittent morning headaches with occasional vomiting. His teachers report he holds books very close. He is more tired and thirsty than usual and gets up at night to drink water. PMH: previously healthy, immunizations up to date. Medications: none; NKDA. Social: 5th grade, lives with both parents, no toxic exposures. Family history: parents of average height; no endocrine or neurologic disease. ROS: no fever, weight stable to slightly increased, no seizures. Growth chart review shows height velocity flattening with weight tracking normally, crossing percentiles downward for height. He has not yet entered puberty. No recent trauma.
cases.exam: Short stature (height below 3rd percentile, fallen across percentiles); weight at 50th percentile. Prepubertal (Tanner 1). Visual acuity reduced; confrontation fields reveal bitemporal hemianopia. Fundoscopy: mild optic disc pallor. Extraocular movements intact. No focal motor deficits; gait normal. Skin without café-au-lait macules. Thyroid not enlarged. Abdomen soft. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal except for the visual findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Growth failure plus bitemporal hemianopia and headaches in a child most urgently warrants which study?
options: Bone age radiograph only
MRI of the brain/pituitary with and without contrast
Reassurance and repeat height in 6 months
Karyotype analysis
stages.explanation: The combination of growth arrest, bitemporal hemianopia (chiasmal compression), and morning headaches with vomiting points to a sellar/suprasellar mass, so brain/pituitary MRI with and without contrast is the urgent next step to identify the lesion and assess mass effect. A bone age film helps characterize growth disorders but cannot diagnose the mass and would dangerously delay care. Reassurance ignores red-flag neuro-ophthalmologic and headache findings. Karyotype evaluates conditions like Turner syndrome but does not explain the visual field defect. MRI guides everything that follows.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain/pituitary: 3 cm suprasellar mass, predominantly cystic with solid components
Coarse calcifications within the lesion
Compression and upward displacement of the optic chiasm
Non-contrast CT confirms dense calcification
Mass extends toward the hypothalamus and third ventricle

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A calcified, cystic suprasellar mass in an 11-year-old with growth failure and visual loss is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pituitary adenoma
Craniopharyngioma (adamantinomatous type)
Germinoma
Rathke cleft cyst
stages.explanation: A suprasellar mass that is cystic with calcification in a child, causing growth failure, visual field loss, and hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction, is classic for an adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma, which arises from Rathke pouch remnants and characteristically contains 'machine oil' fluid and calcifications. Pituitary adenomas are rare in this age group and usually lack dense calcification. Germinoma typically does not calcify and often presents with diabetes insipidus and elevated tumor markers, though it can overlap. A Rathke cleft cyst is usually small, non-calcified, and asymptomatic. The bimodal-peak childhood form fits perfectly here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: He has nocturia and excessive thirst. Before any surgery, what endocrine assessment is essential?
options: Only check a random glucose
Thyroid ultrasound
No endocrine testing is needed preoperatively
Full anterior pituitary panel plus assessment for diabetes insipidus (serum/urine osmolality, sodium)
stages.explanation: Suprasellar craniopharyngiomas frequently disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, so a full preoperative anterior pituitary panel (cortisol/ACTH, free T4/TSH, IGF-1/GH, gonadotropins) plus evaluation for central diabetes insipidus (paired serum and urine osmolality and sodium) is essential; his polyuria and polydipsia suggest DI. Checking only glucose misses the broad hormonal deficits and DI. Thyroid ultrasound does not assess central hypothyroidism. Skipping endocrine testing is dangerous because unrecognized adrenal insufficiency can be fatal perioperatively. Identifying and replacing deficient hormones, especially glucocorticoids, must precede surgery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding definitive management and its risks, which statement is most accurate?
options: Surgery (often with radiation for residual tumor) is standard; hypothalamic injury risks obesity and endocrinopathy
Chemotherapy alone is curative for craniopharyngioma
These tumors are highly malignant and metastasize widely
Observation alone is preferred given the benign histology
stages.explanation: Although histologically benign (WHO grade 1), craniopharyngiomas are managed with surgical resection, often combined with radiation for subtotal resection/residual disease, because of their critical location; injury to the hypothalamus and pituitary can cause panhypopituitarism, diabetes insipidus, and severe hypothalamic obesity, so balance of resection extent versus morbidity is key. Chemotherapy alone is not curative. They are not highly malignant or metastatic. Observation alone is inappropriate given his progressive visual loss and mass effect. Long-term endocrine and visual follow-up are essential after treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma (suprasellar)
pearls: Growth failure + bitemporal hemianopia + headache in a child should trigger sellar/suprasellar imaging.
A cystic, calcified suprasellar mass with 'machine oil' fluid is classic for adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma.
Assess the full pituitary axis and screen for diabetes insipidus before surgery; replace glucocorticoids first.
Despite benign histology, hypothalamic injury can cause obesity, panhypopituitarism, and DI.

cases.title: Fatigue, Excessive Thirst, and Dimming Vision
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm exhausted all the time, constantly thirsty and urinating, and my side vision seems off.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman reports 8 months of progressive fatigue, cold intolerance, and weight gain, along with several weeks of intense thirst and frequent urination, including waking 3-4 times nightly to void large volumes. She has noticed difficulty seeing cars approaching from the sides while driving. Menopause occurred at 51; she now also reports very low energy and decreased appetite. PMH: hypothyroidism suspected but never confirmed, hyperlipidemia. Medications: atorvastatin 20 mg daily; NKDA. Social: retired librarian, rare alcohol, never smoked. Family history: noncontributory for endocrine or neurologic tumors. ROS: occasional dull headaches, no fever, no galactorrhea. She denies polyphagia and her glucose was normal at a recent visit despite the polyuria. No prior cranial surgery or radiation. Skin feels dry. She has had no nausea or vomiting and no acute event.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing woman, pale and with dry skin. Visual acuity mildly reduced; confrontation fields show bitemporal hemianopia. Extraocular movements intact; pupils reactive. Thyroid not enlarged; reflexes with delayed relaxation. Bradycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Abdomen benign. Mucous membranes slightly dry. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam otherwise nonfocal apart from visual field defect. No hyperpigmentation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Polyuria with normal glucose plus features of hypopituitarism and bitemporal hemianopia suggest a sellar mass. Which initial labs best characterize her endocrine status?
options: HbA1c and lipid panel only
Anterior pituitary panel (cortisol/ACTH, TSH/free T4, IGF-1, gonadotropins, prolactin) plus paired serum/urine osmolality and sodium
24-hour urinary catecholamines
Random glucose alone
stages.explanation: Her constellation of hypothyroid features, fatigue, and polyuria with normal glucose points to combined anterior hypopituitarism and central diabetes insipidus, so the right workup is a full anterior pituitary panel plus paired serum and urine osmolality and sodium to document DI (high serum osmolality/sodium with inappropriately dilute urine). HbA1c/lipids and random glucose do not assess pituitary function or DI. Urinary catecholamines evaluate pheochromocytoma, which she has no features of. Characterizing all axes, especially the cortisol axis, is essential before considering any intervention.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
TSH: 0.4 mIU/L  Free T4: 0.4 ng/dL (low) → central hypothyroidism
8 AM cortisol: 3 µg/dL (low)  ACTH: low-normal
IGF-1: low for age
FSH/LH: low for a postmenopausal woman
Serum osmolality: 301 mOsm/kg  Serum Na: 146 mEq/L
Urine osmolality: 180 mOsm/kg (inappropriately dilute)
Prolactin: mildly elevated (stalk effect)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Labs confirm panhypopituitarism with central DI. After MRI shows a cystic, partially calcified suprasellar mass, what is the most likely diagnosis given her age?
options: Pituitary macroadenoma
Meningioma of the tuberculum sellae
Craniopharyngioma (adult, papillary or adamantinomatous type)
Metastatic disease to the pituitary
stages.explanation: Craniopharyngiomas have a bimodal age distribution, with a second peak in adults aged 50-70; a cystic, partially calcified suprasellar mass causing panhypopituitarism, diabetes insipidus, and chiasmal compression fits an adult craniopharyngioma (papillary subtype is more common in adults, adamantinomatous can also occur). A pituitary macroadenoma rarely calcifies and uncommonly causes DI because it arises within the sella below the stalk. A tuberculum sellae meningioma is dural-based and homogeneously enhancing without the cystic/calcified pattern. Pituitary metastasis is possible but lacks her chronic indolent course and characteristic imaging.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain/pituitary: 2.6 cm suprasellar cystic-solid mass with rim calcification
Optic chiasm elevated and compressed
Mass abuts the hypothalamus and stalk
Pituitary gland displaced inferiorly
No other intracranial lesions

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before she undergoes surgical resection, which hormone deficiency must be addressed first to prevent a perioperative crisis?
options: Central hypothyroidism with levothyroxine
Central DI with desmopressin only
Growth hormone deficiency with somatropin
Secondary adrenal insufficiency with glucocorticoids
stages.explanation: Secondary (central) adrenal insufficiency must be corrected with glucocorticoids before surgery and before thyroid hormone replacement, because untreated cortisol deficiency can cause fatal adrenal crisis under surgical stress, and giving levothyroxine first would accelerate cortisol metabolism and precipitate crisis. Starting levothyroxine first is the classic dangerous error. Desmopressin for DI is important but not the immediate life-threatening priority compared with cortisol. GH replacement is never urgent and is deferred until well after recovery. Stress-dose glucocorticoid coverage during and after surgery is mandatory.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After resection she has persistent diabetes insipidus. What is the appropriate long-term management?
options: Desmopressin (DDAVP) with monitoring of sodium and fluid balance
Fluid restriction alone
High-dose thiazide diuretic
No treatment; DI always resolves spontaneously
stages.explanation: Persistent central diabetes insipidus from hypothalamic/stalk injury is treated with desmopressin (DDAVP), a vasopressin analog, with careful monitoring of serum sodium and fluid balance to avoid both hypernatremia (under-treatment) and hyponatremia (over-treatment). Fluid restriction alone is inappropriate for central DI and risks dangerous hypernatremia and dehydration. Thiazides are used adjunctively for nephrogenic DI, not as primary therapy for central DI. DI does not reliably resolve on its own after this kind of injury; permanent replacement is often required. Lifelong endocrine follow-up for all pituitary axes is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Adult craniopharyngioma (suprasellar) with panhypopituitarism and central diabetes insipidus
pearls: Craniopharyngioma has a bimodal age peak; suspect it in older adults with a cystic, calcified suprasellar mass.
Polyuria with normal glucose plus hypopituitarism suggests central diabetes insipidus from stalk/hypothalamic involvement.
Replace glucocorticoids before levothyroxine to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis.
Persistent central DI is treated with desmopressin and close sodium monitoring.

cases.title: Unsteady Walking and a Flushed Complexion
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been getting clumsy and off-balance, and people keep saying I look ruddy.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man reports 4 months of progressive imbalance, clumsiness of the right hand, and occipital headaches worse in the morning and with coughing. He has had two episodes of vomiting without nausea on waking. A recent walk-in visit found him 'too thick-blooded.' He denies smoking, lives at low altitude, and has no chronic lung disease or sleep apnea. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none; NKDA. Social: software engineer, minimal alcohol, no testosterone or erythropoietin use. Family history: a maternal uncle who died young of a 'brain tumor' and a cousin with kidney cancer; father with vision problems in one eye. ROS: no chest pain or dyspnea; no flank pain or hematuria. He notes occasional episodes of palpitations and sweating but no clear pattern. No prior surgeries. He has not had recent dehydration or diuretic use that could explain a high hematocrit.
cases.exam: Plethoric, well-built man. Fundoscopy: blurred optic disc margins suggesting papilledema. Cerebellar signs: right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose, dysdiadochokinesia, and a wide-based ataxic gait; tandem gait impaired. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. No nystagmus at rest, mild gaze-evoked nystagmus to the right. Strength and sensation normal in all limbs; reflexes symmetric. No abdominal masses or bruits. No retinal hemorrhages on this exam. Spleen not enlarged.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Progressive cerebellar signs with morning headaches and papilledema most urgently require which study?
options: CT of the chest to evaluate erythrocytosis
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Bone marrow biopsy for polycythemia vera
Lumbar puncture for opening pressure
stages.explanation: Subacute cerebellar dysfunction, morning/Valsalva-worsened headaches, and papilledema indicate a posterior fossa mass with raised intracranial pressure, so contrast-enhanced brain MRI is the urgent test to localize and characterize the lesion. A chest CT does not address the acute neurologic threat. Bone marrow biopsy presumes polycythemia vera, but his erythrocytosis may be secondary (EPO-driven) and the brain lesion takes priority. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a posterior fossa mass and papilledema because of herniation risk. MRI guides the entire subsequent workup.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI brain: 2.5 cm cystic cerebellar lesion with an intensely enhancing mural nodule
Prominent flow voids (hypervascular nodule)
Mild obstructive hydrocephalus
Hemoglobin: 19.5 g/dL  Hematocrit: 58%
Serum erythropoietin: elevated
EPO low-oxygen causes excluded (normal SpO2, no lung disease)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A cystic cerebellar lesion with an enhancing mural nodule plus EPO-driven polycythemia is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma
Hemangioblastoma
Metastatic renal cell carcinoma
Medulloblastoma
stages.explanation: A cystic cerebellar mass with a highly vascular, enhancing mural nodule in an adult, accompanied by secondary polycythemia from tumor-produced erythropoietin, is classic for hemangioblastoma, the most common primary infratentorial tumor in adults. Pilocytic astrocytoma shares the cyst-with-nodule appearance but is typically a pediatric tumor and does not secrete EPO. Metastatic RCC can mimic it and is associated with VHL, but the EPO-driven erythrocytosis and the imaging hallmark favor hemangioblastoma. Medulloblastoma is a midline, solid, hyperdense pediatric tumor without the cyst-plus-nodule/EPO pattern. The EPO link is the key clue.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His family history (young relative with a brain tumor, a cousin with kidney cancer, father with eye disease) prompts which evaluation?
options: No further evaluation; hemangioblastomas are always sporadic
Screen for von Hippel-Lindau disease, including VHL germline testing and surveillance imaging
Test for neurofibromatosis type 2
Order a Philadelphia chromosome study
stages.explanation: CNS hemangioblastoma, especially in a young patient with a suggestive family history (relatives with CNS tumors, renal cell carcinoma, retinal lesions), should prompt evaluation for von Hippel-Lindau disease via VHL germline mutation testing and surveillance for associated lesions (retinal/CNS hemangioblastomas, RCC, pheochromocytoma, pancreatic and renal cysts). Assuming sporadic disease misses a hereditary syndrome. NF2 is associated with schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas, not hemangioblastomas. The Philadelphia chromosome is for CML and irrelevant here. His episodic palpitations/sweating also warrant pheochromocytoma screening.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for this symptomatic cerebellar hemangioblastoma?
options: Surgical resection of the lesion (mural nodule)
Phlebotomy and observation
Systemic chemotherapy
Whole-brain radiation as first-line
stages.explanation: Surgical resection, particularly complete removal of the vascular mural nodule, is the definitive treatment for a symptomatic hemangioblastoma and also resolves the EPO-driven polycythemia and the mass effect/hydrocephalus. Phlebotomy and observation treat only the erythrocytosis and ignore the progressive neurologic disease. Chemotherapy has no established curative role for these benign vascular tumors. Whole-brain radiation is not first-line; stereotactic radiosurgery may be considered for small, multiple, or surgically inaccessible lesions, especially in VHL. Resecting the nodule is curative for the solitary symptomatic lesion.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding would most concern you for an undiagnosed VHL-associated tumor during his preoperative evaluation?
options: Mildly elevated fasting glucose
Episodic hypertension with palpitations, sweating, and elevated plasma metanephrines
A small simple liver cyst
Mild iron-deficiency anemia
stages.explanation: Episodic hypertension, palpitations, and sweating with elevated plasma metanephrines suggest a pheochromocytoma, a VHL-associated tumor that, if unrecognized, can cause a hypertensive crisis under anesthesia; it must be excluded and, if present, treated with alpha-blockade before any surgery. Mildly elevated glucose is nonspecific. A simple liver cyst is incidental and benign. Iron-deficiency anemia is unrelated and actually opposite to his erythrocytosis. Catecholamine screening and alpha-blockade preoperatively are critical safety steps in suspected VHL.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebellar hemangioblastoma with secondary (EPO-mediated) polycythemia, suspected von Hippel-Lindau disease
pearls: Cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule + secondary polycythemia = hemangioblastoma (EPO-secreting).
Hemangioblastoma is the most common primary infratentorial tumor in adults.
New CNS hemangioblastoma, especially when young or familial, should trigger VHL germline testing and surveillance.
Exclude pheochromocytoma (plasma metanephrines) before surgery to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis.

cases.title: Family History and a Spot on the Retina
cases.chiefComplaint: My optometrist found something on my retina and my dad has the same gene problem.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman is referred after a routine eye exam revealed a peripheral retinal vascular lesion. She has mild intermittent headaches and occasional unsteadiness but is otherwise well. Her father was diagnosed with von Hippel-Lindau disease and had a cerebellar tumor and a kidney tumor; a paternal aunt had retinal lesions. She has had no prior imaging or genetic testing. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: combined oral contraceptive; NKDA. Social: graduate student, no tobacco, occasional alcohol. Family history: autosomal dominant pattern of CNS and renal tumors on the paternal side. ROS: no flank pain, hematuria, palpitations, or episodic sweating; no vomiting. She is concerned about her risk given the family history and wants to know about screening. No prior surgeries. Vision is currently intact in both eyes by her report. She is not pregnant.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Dilated fundoscopy: a peripheral retinal capillary hemangioblastoma (angioma) with a feeding vessel in the right eye; left eye normal. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally. Cerebellar exam: subtle impaired tandem gait, otherwise normal finger-to-nose and no dysmetria. Cranial nerves intact. No abdominal masses or bruits. No skin neurofibromas or café-au-lait macules. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. No focal motor or sensory deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a retinal hemangioblastoma and a parent with confirmed VHL, what is the most appropriate first step?
options: Reassure her; one retinal lesion is incidental
Confirm the diagnosis with VHL germline genetic testing and begin systematic surveillance
Immediately resect the retinal lesion before any workup
Start chemotherapy empirically
stages.explanation: A retinal hemangioblastoma in a person with an affected first-degree parent is clinically diagnostic of von Hippel-Lindau disease; the right step is to confirm with VHL germline genetic testing and initiate the systematic, lifelong surveillance protocol for associated tumors. Reassurance is wrong because even a single retinal angioma with this family history establishes the diagnosis. Immediate retinal resection skips characterization and full-body screening; small retinal lesions are often treated with laser/cryotherapy, not urgent resection. Empiric chemotherapy has no role. Confirming the diagnosis and screening for synchronous tumors drives management.

stages.label: GENETICS & SCREENING LABS
stages.content:
VHL germline testing: pathogenic VHL mutation (chromosome 3p25) confirmed
Plasma free metanephrines: normal
Urinalysis: no hematuria
Hemoglobin: 13.6 g/dL (normal)
Fasting glucose: 88 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Von Hippel-Lindau disease is confirmed. Which set of tumors is she at lifelong risk of developing?
options: Vestibular schwannomas and meningiomas
Optic gliomas and cutaneous neurofibromas
Cardiac rhabdomyomas and subependymal nodules
CNS/retinal hemangioblastomas, clear cell renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic/renal cysts and tumors
stages.explanation: VHL is an autosomal dominant tumor-suppressor syndrome (VHL gene, 3p25) predisposing to CNS and retinal hemangioblastomas, clear cell renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma, pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors and cysts, endolymphatic sac tumors, and epididymal/broad ligament cystadenomas. Vestibular schwannomas and meningiomas characterize NF2. Optic gliomas and neurofibromas characterize NF1. Cardiac rhabdomyomas and subependymal nodules characterize tuberous sclerosis. Recognizing the VHL tumor spectrum is essential because RCC is a major cause of mortality and demands lifelong surveillance.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: Which surveillance plan best matches recommended VHL monitoring?
options: Annual chest X-ray only
A single baseline brain MRI with no further imaging
Regular dilated ophthalmologic exams, periodic MRI of the brain and full spine, abdominal imaging (MRI/CT) of the kidneys/adrenals/pancreas, and plasma/urine metanephrines
Colonoscopy every 10 years as the principal screen
stages.explanation: VHL surveillance is multi-organ and lifelong: regular dilated retinal exams (starting in childhood), periodic contrast MRI of the brain and entire spine for hemangioblastomas, abdominal imaging (MRI or CT) for renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic/adrenal lesions, and biochemical screening (plasma or urinary metanephrines) for pheochromocytoma; audiology may be added for endolymphatic sac tumors. A chest X-ray is irrelevant. A single brain MRI fails the lifelong, multi-organ requirement. Colonoscopy is not a VHL screen. Coordinated, recurring screening detects tumors early, when they are most treatable.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI: two small asymptomatic cerebellar hemangioblastomas
Full-spine MRI: a small enhancing intramedullary cervical hemangioblastoma
Abdominal MRI: a 1.2 cm enhancing renal lesion plus pancreatic cysts
No adrenal mass; metanephrines normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her CNS hemangioblastomas are small and asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate management of these lesions?
options: Serial imaging surveillance, treating lesions that grow or become symptomatic (surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery)
Resect all lesions immediately regardless of symptoms
Whole-CNS radiation now
Start a tyrosine kinase inhibitor for the brain lesions
stages.explanation: Small, asymptomatic CNS hemangioblastomas in VHL are managed with serial imaging surveillance, intervening (microsurgical resection or stereotactic radiosurgery) only when lesions enlarge, become symptomatic, or develop associated cysts, because patients harbor multiple lesions over a lifetime and prophylactic removal of all of them carries unacceptable cumulative morbidity. Resecting everything immediately is excessive and harmful. Whole-CNS radiation is inappropriate for benign, multifocal lesions. Systemic TKIs such as belzutifan (an HIF-2alpha inhibitor) are emerging for VHL-associated tumors, but a TKI is not standard for small asymptomatic brain hemangioblastomas. Surveillance with selective treatment is the standard.

cases.diagnosis: Von Hippel-Lindau disease (germline VHL mutation) with multiple CNS hemangioblastomas and a retinal angioma
pearls: A retinal or CNS hemangioblastoma with an affected first-degree relative is clinically diagnostic of VHL.
VHL spectrum: CNS/retinal hemangioblastomas, clear cell RCC, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic/renal lesions.
Surveillance is lifelong and multi-organ: eyes, brain/spine MRI, abdominal imaging, and metanephrines.
Small asymptomatic CNS hemangioblastomas are watched; treat those that grow or become symptomatic.

cases.title: A Curving Spine and a Changing Walk
cases.chiefComplaint: My back has been curving and I keep tripping when I walk.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy is admitted for evaluation after his pediatrician noted worsening scoliosis and his parents reported a change in his gait over the past year. He describes vague mid-back pain that is worse at night and sometimes wakes him. He has also noticed his right leg feels weaker and 'catches' when walking, and he has dropped objects from his right hand. No bowel or bladder incontinence yet, though he reports occasional urinary hesitancy. PMH: unremarkable; no trauma. Medications: none; NKDA. Social: 10th grader, plays no contact sports recently due to fatigue. Family history: noncontributory; no neurocutaneous disorders. ROS: no fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. He has no skin lesions. Symptoms have been slowly progressive rather than acute. He denies recent infection. Scoliosis was not present at a sports physical 18 months ago, raising concern for a secondary cause.
cases.exam: Right thoracic scoliosis on forward-bend (Adam's) test. Gait mildly spastic with right circumduction. Strength: right lower extremity 4/5, mild right hand intrinsic weakness; left side 5/5. Reflexes brisk in the right lower extremity with sustained clonus at the right ankle; right plantar response upgoing (Babinski). Sensory: decreased pinprick and temperature in a band over the trunk with relative preservation of vibration/proprioception. No spinal tenderness to percussion. No café-au-lait macules or cutaneous neurofibromas.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Rapidly progressive scoliosis with upper motor neuron signs and night-waking back pain in a teen suggests what, and what imaging is best?
options: Idiopathic adolescent scoliosis; standing spine radiographs only
A spinal cord lesion; MRI of the entire spine with and without contrast
Muscular dystrophy; obtain a CK and muscle biopsy
Mechanical back strain; lumbar CT
stages.explanation: New, rapidly progressive scoliosis combined with upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski), a sensory level, and night/awakening pain is a red flag for a structural spinal cord lesion, so contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire spine is the test of choice to identify an intramedullary tumor. Calling it idiopathic scoliosis ignores the neurologic deficits and atypical pain. Muscular dystrophy causes lower motor neuron/myopathic patterns without a sensory level or upgoing toes. Mechanical strain with a lumbar CT cannot evaluate the cord and misses the diagnosis. Painful, progressive scoliosis with myelopathy always warrants spinal MRI.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI whole spine: fusiform expansile intramedullary lesion spanning C7-T4
Lesion is eccentric within the cord with patchy, irregular enhancement
Associated intramedullary cysts (syrinx) above and below
Cord is widened; no extramedullary component
No leptomeningeal disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A pediatric, eccentric, fusiform intramedullary cord lesion with patchy enhancement and an associated syrinx is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Ependymoma
Meningioma
Astrocytoma (low-grade, e.g., pilocytic)
Schwannoma
stages.explanation: In children, the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor is an astrocytoma, which is typically eccentric within the cord, fusiform/infiltrative with ill-defined margins and patchy enhancement, and may have an associated syrinx. Ependymoma is the most common intramedullary tumor in adults and tends to be central, well-circumscribed, and homogeneously enhancing. Meningioma and schwannoma are extramedullary (intradural-extramedullary), not intramedullary, and would not expand the cord from within. The patient's age and the eccentric, infiltrative pattern point to astrocytoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the primary initial management for this intramedullary spinal cord astrocytoma?
options: Immediate radical gross-total resection regardless of margins
Empiric high-dose radiation without tissue diagnosis
Observation alone given the benign location
Maximal safe surgical resection/biopsy with intraoperative neuromonitoring to establish histology and decompress
stages.explanation: Management of an intramedullary astrocytoma centers on surgery with the goal of maximal safe resection (or at least biopsy) using intraoperative neurophysiologic monitoring, both to obtain histologic grading and to decompress the cord while preserving function; because astrocytomas are infiltrative, a complete resection is often not achievable without unacceptable deficits. Radical gross-total resection at any cost risks severe neurologic injury given the infiltrative margins. Radiation without tissue forgoes essential grading and is reserved as adjuvant therapy for higher-grade or residual disease. Observation alone is inappropriate given his progressive myelopathy. Histology then guides adjuvant therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His progressive scoliosis is most directly explained by which mechanism?
options: Asymmetric paraspinal muscle weakness/imbalance from the cord lesion (neuromuscular scoliosis)
Primary vertebral bone tumor eroding the spine
Vitamin D deficiency
Poor posture and heavy backpacks
stages.explanation: His rapidly progressive scoliosis is neuromuscular in origin: the intramedullary cord lesion produces asymmetric weakness and imbalance of paraspinal and trunk musculature, leading to a structural curve, which is why new or rapidly worsening scoliosis with neurologic signs must prompt cord imaging. A primary vertebral bone tumor is extramedullary/osseous and not what the MRI shows. Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets/osteomalacia, not this pattern. Posture and backpacks do not cause progressive structural scoliosis with myelopathy. Recognizing scoliosis as a presenting sign of intramedullary tumor is high-yield.

cases.diagnosis: Intramedullary spinal cord astrocytoma (cervicothoracic)
pearls: New or rapidly progressive scoliosis with UMN signs and night pain warrants whole-spine MRI to rule out a cord tumor.
Astrocytoma is the most common intramedullary cord tumor in children; ependymoma is most common in adults.
Astrocytomas are eccentric and infiltrative with patchy enhancement; ependymomas are central and well-circumscribed.
Management is maximal safe resection/biopsy with neuromonitoring; infiltrative margins limit gross-total resection.

cases.title: Burning Hands and Dropped Coffee Cups
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands feel like they're burning and I can't feel hot from cold, and I keep dropping things.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents with about 10 months of bilateral hand and forearm numbness with a burning quality, plus difficulty distinguishing hot from cold water, leading to a minor burn he did not feel. He has dropped cups and has new neck pain. He reports the deficits started in a 'cape-like' distribution across the shoulders and upper arms. There is no weakness in the legs early on, though he now notes mild leg stiffness. PMH: unremarkable; no trauma or prior neck surgery. Medications: occasional ibuprofen; NKDA. Social: graphic designer, no tobacco, social alcohol. Family history: noncontributory; no neurocutaneous syndromes. ROS: no bowel/bladder symptoms initially, recent mild urinary urgency. No fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. Symptoms have been slowly progressive. He denies B12 deficiency risk factors and has no diabetes.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable. Cranial nerves intact. Motor: mild intrinsic hand weakness bilaterally; legs 5/5 with subtle increased tone. Sensory: loss of pain and temperature in a 'cape-like' distribution over both shoulders and arms (C4-T2), with preserved light touch, vibration, and proprioception (dissociated sensory loss). Reflexes: reduced at the upper extremities, brisk at the knees with bilateral Babinski. Gait mildly spastic. No spinal deformity. No tenderness to palpation over the spine. No skin café-au-lait macules.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 'cape-like' dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature lost, touch/proprioception spared) localizes the lesion to which structure, and what imaging is best?
options: Peripheral nerves; obtain nerve conduction studies first
Central cervical spinal cord (e.g., crossing spinothalamic fibers); MRI of the cervical spine with and without contrast
Dorsal columns only; check B12 and copper
Brain cortex; obtain a brain MRI
stages.explanation: Loss of pain and temperature with preserved touch and proprioception in a cape-like distribution reflects damage to the centrally crossing spinothalamic fibers near the central canal of the cervical cord (a central cord/syringomyelic pattern), so cervical spine MRI with and without contrast is the test of choice to find an intramedullary lesion or syrinx. Peripheral nerve studies would not explain a dissociated, suspended sensory level with UMN leg signs. Dorsal column disease impairs vibration/proprioception, the opposite pattern. Brain MRI does not fit a segmental cord syndrome. The dissociated pattern is the key localizer.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: well-circumscribed, centrally located intramedullary mass from C3-C6
Homogeneous enhancement with a 'cap sign' (hemosiderin caps at the poles)
Associated rostral and caudal syrinx
Cord symmetrically expanded
Lesion has clear margins/cleavage plane with the cord

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A centrally located, well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing intramedullary mass with a polar 'cap sign' in a 42-year-old is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Ependymoma
Astrocytoma
Hemangioblastoma
Metastasis
stages.explanation: Ependymoma is the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor in adults and is classically central, well-circumscribed, and homogeneously enhancing, often with a hemosiderin 'cap sign' at the tumor poles and an associated syrinx, with a surgical cleavage plane that allows en bloc resection. Astrocytoma is eccentric and infiltrative, more common in children, and lacks a clean plane. Hemangioblastoma is highly vascular with flow voids, often dorsal/pial-based and associated with VHL. Metastasis is uncommon intramedullary and usually shows a primary cancer history with surrounding edema. The central, encapsulated, cap-signed appearance defines ependymoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the preferred treatment for this well-circumscribed intramedullary ependymoma?
options: Subtotal debulking followed by routine radiation
Radiation alone without surgery
Gross-total microsurgical resection using the cleavage plane with intraoperative neuromonitoring
Observation with serial MRI
stages.explanation: Because spinal ependymomas are well-circumscribed with a clear cleavage plane, gross-total microsurgical resection with intraoperative neurophysiologic monitoring is the preferred treatment and is frequently curative, in contrast to infiltrative astrocytomas. Planned subtotal debulking with routine radiation undertreats a resectable tumor; radiation is reserved for incompletely resected, anaplastic, or recurrent disease. Radiation alone is inferior when complete resection is achievable. Observation is inappropriate given his progressive myelopathy and deficits. Complete excision via the cleavage plane offers the best functional and oncologic outcome.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional evaluation is appropriate after diagnosing a spinal ependymoma, particularly a myxopapillary or higher-grade lesion?
options: Bone marrow biopsy
Echocardiography
Skeletal survey for lytic lesions
Imaging of the entire neuraxis (brain and complete spine) to assess for CSF dissemination
stages.explanation: Ependymomas can disseminate through the cerebrospinal fluid, so imaging of the entire neuraxis (brain and complete spine MRI), and CSF cytology in selected cases, is appropriate to detect 'drop metastases' and to guide treatment and surveillance, especially for myxopapillary (commonly conus/cauda equina) or higher-grade tumors. A bone marrow biopsy is irrelevant to CNS staging. Echocardiography has no role here. A skeletal survey screens for systemic bone disease, not CSF spread. Neuraxis staging informs whether craniospinal therapy or closer follow-up is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Intramedullary spinal cord ependymoma (cervical) presenting with a central cord syndrome
pearls: Cape-like dissociated sensory loss (pain/temp lost, touch/proprioception spared) localizes to the central cervical cord.
Ependymoma is the most common adult intramedullary tumor: central, well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing, with a 'cap sign.'
A surgical cleavage plane makes gross-total resection feasible and often curative for spinal ependymoma.
Stage the whole neuraxis because ependymomas can seed the CSF ('drop metastases').

cases.title: Progressive Leg Stiffness and a Band Around the Trunk
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradually worsening leg stiffness, imbalance, and a tight band-like sensation around the lower chest over several months.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman presents to her neurologist with eight months of slowly progressive lower-extremity stiffness and difficulty walking. She describes a constricting 'band' across her lower chest and upper abdomen, and tingling in both feet that has ascended over time. She has had two near-falls and notes mild urinary urgency in the last month. There is no acute back trauma. Past medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She has no drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine socially. She worked as a librarian and is now retired. Family history is notable for a mother with osteoporosis; no known cancers or neurofibromatosis. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, night sweats, prior malignancy, recent infection, or bowel incontinence. She denies neck pain, arm symptoms, or visual changes. She is otherwise independent in activities of daily living but has reduced her walking distance significantly.
cases.exam: Alert, fluent, cranial nerves intact. Upper extremities normal in strength, tone, and reflexes. Lower extremities show spastic increased tone bilaterally with strength 4/5 in hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are 3+ with bilateral sustained ankle clonus and extensor plantar responses. There is a sensory level to pinprick and temperature at approximately T8, with reduced vibration and proprioception in the toes. Gait is spastic and unsteady; Romberg is mildly positive. No spinal tenderness, no saddle anesthesia, and rectal tone is normal. No skin café-au-lait macules or subcutaneous nodules.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly localizes this patient's lesion to the thoracic spinal cord rather than the brain or peripheral nerves?
options: Bilateral lower-extremity tingling
A discrete sensory level at T8 with spastic paraparesis and hyperreflexia
Urinary urgency
A positive Romberg sign
stages.explanation: A discrete sensory level with upper-motor-neuron signs below it (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantars) is the hallmark of a myelopathy and localizes the lesion to the cord at that level — here, around T8. Bilateral tingling and urinary urgency are nonspecific and occur in many lesions including peripheral neuropathy and brain disease. A positive Romberg indicates dorsal column or proprioceptive dysfunction but does not localize to a spinal level by itself. Only the combination of a sensory level with corticospinal signs pinpoints a transverse cord process at a defined segment, making thoracic cord the key localization.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Plain radiographs of the thoracic spine
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies
MRI of the thoracic spine with and without gadolinium
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
stages.explanation: MRI of the thoracic spine with and without contrast is the gold standard for evaluating a suspected compressive myelopathy; it characterizes the cord, the canal, and any enhancing mass. Plain radiographs cannot visualize the cord or soft-tissue masses and would miss an intradural lesion. EMG/NCS evaluates peripheral nerve and muscle, not the central cord, and would be inappropriate for upper-motor-neuron signs. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when a compressive lesion is suspected because it risks neurologic worsening and rarely changes initial management. MRI directly answers the localization question raised by the exam.

stages.label: MRI THORACIC SPINE
stages.content:
T1 post-gadolinium: well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing intradural extramedullary mass at T8, ~1.8 cm
Broad dural base with a 'dural tail' sign
The mass displaces and compresses the spinal cord to the right with cord T2 signal change
CSF cleft visible between mass and cord (extramedullary location)
No bony destruction; vertebral marrow signal normal
No additional spinal or intracranial lesions on screening sequences

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the imaging, which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Spinal meningioma
Spinal cord ependymoma
Metastatic epidural deposit
Schwannoma of a thoracic nerve root
stages.explanation: A dural-based, homogeneously enhancing intradural extramedullary mass with a dural tail in a 63-year-old woman is classic for spinal meningioma, which favors the thoracic spine in women. Ependymoma is intramedullary (within the cord), expanding it rather than displacing it with a CSF cleft. Metastatic epidural disease is extradural, typically destroys bone, and arises with a known primary — none present here. Schwannoma is also intradural extramedullary but usually arises from a nerve root, may widen the neural foramen ('dumbbell' shape), and lacks a broad dural base/dural tail. The dural attachment strongly favors meningioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for this symptomatic lesion?
options: Observation with serial MRI in 6 months
Upfront stereotactic radiosurgery
Empiric high-dose dexamethasone alone
Microsurgical resection of the tumor
stages.explanation: A symptomatic, accessible intradural extramedullary meningioma causing progressive myelopathy is treated with microsurgical resection, which is usually curative (Simpson grade-dependent) and relieves cord compression. Observation is appropriate only for small, incidental, asymptomatic lesions — not for a progressive deficit. Radiosurgery is reserved for poor surgical candidates, residual/recurrent tumor, or surgically inaccessible lesions, not as first-line for a resectable symptomatic mass. Dexamethasone may transiently reduce cord edema but is not definitive and does not address the mechanical compression. Early decompression also offers the best chance of neurologic recovery before deficits become fixed.

cases.diagnosis: Thoracic spinal meningioma (intradural extramedullary) causing compressive myelopathy
pearls: A sensory level plus UMN signs below it localizes a transverse process to the spinal cord at that segment.
Spinal meningiomas are most common in the thoracic spine of middle-aged to older women and are intradural extramedullary with a dural tail.
A CSF cleft between mass and cord distinguishes extramedullary lesions from intramedullary tumors like ependymoma.
Symptomatic, resectable spinal meningiomas are treated with microsurgical resection, which is often curative.

cases.title: Sudden Back Pain With Weak Legs and No Urination
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe mid-back pain for two weeks now accompanied by leg weakness and inability to urinate for the past 12 hours.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man presents to the emergency department with two weeks of progressively severe, constant mid-thoracic back pain that is worse at night and when lying flat, unrelieved by acetaminophen. Over the last two days both legs have become weak and heavy, and today he cannot urinate and feels his abdomen is full. He also reports numbness from the level of his umbilicus downward. Past medical history is significant for prostate cancer diagnosed three years ago, treated with radiation, with a recently rising PSA. Medications include leuprolide injections and tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (40 pack-years), quit 10 years ago. He lives with his wife and was fully ambulatory until this week. Review of systems is positive for 5 kg unintentional weight loss over two months and constipation. He denies fever, chills, recent trauma, or prior back surgery. He is anxious about losing the ability to walk.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, lying still. Cranial nerves and upper extremities are normal. Lower extremities show symmetric weakness 3/5 in hip flexion, knee extension, and ankle movement. Tone is increased and patellar reflexes are brisk with bilateral upgoing toes. There is a sensory level to pinprick at T10. Palpation reveals focal tenderness over the lower thoracic spinous processes. The bladder is palpable and distended; post-void residual catheterization returns 700 mL. Rectal tone is reduced. No saddle anesthesia is appreciated above the perineum, and there is no spinal deformity or overlying skin change.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate step in this patient with a known cancer history and an evolving myelopathy?
options: Obtain plain thoracic spine radiographs
Start urgent dexamethasone and arrange emergent whole-spine MRI
Schedule outpatient MRI within one week
Begin physical therapy and oral analgesics
stages.explanation: This is metastatic epidural spinal cord compression (MESCC), a neuro-oncologic emergency. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) reduce vasogenic edema and may preserve function, and emergent MRI of the entire spine localizes the compression and detects additional levels. Plain radiographs are insensitive for epidural disease and cannot guide treatment. Outpatient or delayed MRI risks irreversible paraplegia — ambulatory status at treatment is the strongest predictor of outcome. Physical therapy and analgesics do not address the compressive emergency. Steroids should not be delayed while awaiting imaging when suspicion is high.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL
Calcium 11.4 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 280 U/L
PSA 96 ng/mL
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL
MRI whole spine: T9–T10 epidural soft-tissue mass with diffuse vertebral marrow replacement, posterior displacement and compression of the spinal cord, and effacement of the thecal sac

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The MRI shows an epidural mass with vertebral marrow replacement compressing the cord. Which mechanism best explains this presentation?
options: Intramedullary metastasis within the spinal cord
Leptomeningeal spread along the cord surface
Vertebral body metastasis with posterior extension into the epidural space
A primary intradural meningioma
stages.explanation: The most common cause of MESCC is a vertebral body metastasis (here from prostate cancer) that grows posteriorly into the epidural space, compressing the cord from outside the dura — consistent with marrow replacement and an epidural mass. Intramedullary metastasis would be within the cord substance, not the epidural space, and is far rarer. Leptomeningeal disease coats the pia/arachnoid with thin enhancement and nodules, not a bulky epidural mass with bony destruction. A meningioma is intradural, dural-based, and does not replace vertebral marrow. Prostate cancer characteristically produces osteoblastic vertebral metastases, fitting the elevated alkaline phosphatase and PSA.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After steroids, which combination represents the best definitive management for an appropriate surgical candidate with a single compressive level and good performance status?
options: Radiation therapy alone
Chemotherapy alone
Surgical decompression followed by postoperative radiation therapy
Vertebroplasty alone
stages.explanation: For a fit patient with a single area of compression and limited spinal disease, decompressive surgery followed by postoperative radiotherapy preserves ambulation better than radiation alone (Patchell trial). Radiation alone is reserved for radiosensitive tumors, multiple levels, poor surgical candidates, or paraplegia present >24–48 hours. Chemotherapy alone is too slow for an acute cord emergency and is used only for highly chemosensitive tumors. Vertebroplasty addresses vertebral pain/instability from compression fractures but does not decompress the cord. Definitive treatment must rapidly relieve the compression to preserve neurologic function.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor most strongly predicts this patient's chance of regaining or maintaining the ability to walk?
options: His pretreatment PSA level
His ambulatory and neurologic status at the time treatment is initiated
The presence of hypercalcemia
His age
stages.explanation: Neurologic/ambulatory status at the start of treatment is the single best predictor of functional outcome in MESCC — patients treated while still ambulatory are far more likely to retain walking ability, underscoring the urgency of diagnosis and intervention. PSA reflects tumor burden but not neurologic recovery. Hypercalcemia signals advanced disease and must be treated but is not the determinant of ambulation. Age influences surgical candidacy but is far weaker than baseline neurologic function. This is why MESCC is an emergency: every hour of delay can convert a treatable deficit into permanent paraplegia.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic epidural spinal cord compression from prostate cancer (T9–T10)
pearls: Nocturnal/recumbent back pain in a cancer patient is MESCC until proven otherwise; image the whole spine emergently.
Give dexamethasone immediately on clinical suspicion — do not wait for MRI.
Ambulatory status at treatment start is the strongest predictor of walking outcome.
Surgery plus postoperative radiation outperforms radiation alone in fit patients with a single compressive level (Patchell).

cases.title: Worsening Weakness in a Frail Woman With Widespread Cancer
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted with worsening leg weakness and back pain in the setting of widely metastatic cancer.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma, EGFR wild-type) is admitted for progressive bilateral leg weakness over five days and severe lumbar back pain. Her cancer was diagnosed 14 months ago and has progressed through two lines of chemotherapy; she now has known liver, brain, and multilevel bony metastases. She has lost 12 kg in three months and spends more than half the day in bed. Medications include extended-release morphine 30 mg twice daily, ondansetron, and dexamethasone 4 mg daily for brain edema. No drug allergies. She has a 30-pack-year smoking history and quit at diagnosis. She lives alone; her daughter is her caregiver and healthcare proxy. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, anorexia, and new difficulty standing from a chair; she denies fever, recent falls, or seizures. Her ECOG performance status is 3. Goals-of-care discussions have begun but no formal directive is documented. She is alert and able to participate in decisions.
cases.exam: Cachectic, fatigued but oriented. Cranial nerves intact. Upper extremities normal. Lower extremities show 3/5 strength proximally and distally with increased tone, hyperreflexia at the knees, and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Sensory level to pinprick at L1. Marked tenderness over the lumbar spinous processes. Bladder is palpable; she reports incomplete emptying. Rectal tone is mildly reduced. She is too weak to stand unassisted. No meningismus, no papilledema, and upper-extremity coordination is preserved.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step on this admission?
options: CT of the lumbar spine without contrast
Emergent MRI of the whole spine with and without contrast
Bone scan
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: New progressive paraparesis with a sensory level and a UMN pattern in a cancer patient mandates emergent whole-spine MRI to confirm and map metastatic epidural cord compression and to detect additional, often clinically silent, levels. CT lacks soft-tissue and cord detail and underestimates epidural disease. A bone scan localizes bony metastases but does not assess cord compression and is too slow. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with suspected compressive cord disease and could worsen the deficit. Whole-spine MRI also informs whether radiation alone, surgery, or palliative measures are feasible by defining the number of levels and degree of instability.

stages.label: MRI WHOLE SPINE
stages.content:
Epidural soft-tissue masses compressing the cord/conus at T11 and L1 (two non-contiguous levels)
Diffuse vertebral marrow metastatic replacement at multiple levels
Pathologic compression deformity of L1 without gross spinal instability (SINS low-intermediate)
No single isolated level amenable to simple decompression
Known brain metastases unchanged from prior imaging

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given two non-contiguous compressive levels, ECOG 3 performance status, and rapidly progressive systemic disease, what is the most appropriate definitive local therapy?
options: Multilevel decompressive laminectomy with instrumented fusion
Palliative external-beam radiation therapy to the involved levels with continued steroids
Stereotactic radiosurgery as the sole curative modality
Observation with analgesia only
stages.explanation: With multiple non-contiguous levels, poor performance status (ECOG 3), and progressive disseminated disease, this patient is a poor surgical candidate; palliative radiotherapy plus steroids is the most appropriate definitive local therapy to relieve pain and stabilize neurologic function. Extensive multilevel surgery with fusion carries high morbidity and would not be expected to improve survival or function in someone this frail. Radiosurgery is excellent for selected oligometastatic, radioresistant lesions but is not the 'sole curative' option here given diffuse disease. Observation alone abandons a treatable, reversible cause of suffering and decline; radiation can still palliate even when cure is impossible.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding corticosteroids in this patient already on dexamethasone 4 mg daily, what is the best approach?
options: Stop steroids entirely to avoid side effects
Continue the current low maintenance dose unchanged
Escalate to a higher dexamethasone dose to reduce cord edema, then taper as radiation takes effect
Switch to high-dose IV methylprednisolone using the acute spinal-trauma protocol
stages.explanation: Acute cord compression warrants escalation of dexamethasone above her maintenance dose to reduce vasogenic edema and protect neurologic function, with a planned taper once radiotherapy controls the tumor. Stopping steroids removes edema control and risks abrupt deterioration plus adrenal insufficiency. Continuing only the low brain-edema dose under-treats the new cord emergency. The high-dose methylprednisolone spinal-cord-injury (NASCIS) protocol is for acute traumatic SCI, not neoplastic compression, and carries excess infection/complication risk without benefit here. Dexamethasone is the steroid of choice for malignant cord compression because of its low mineralocorticoid activity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient says her priority is comfort and remaining at home with her daughter. What is the most appropriate next step in care planning?
options: Insist on aggressive surgery to maximize survival regardless of her wishes
Involve palliative care, complete radiation for symptom control, optimize pain regimen, and document goals of care including code status
Discharge immediately without further intervention
Pursue a third line of systemic chemotherapy as the main focus
stages.explanation: Patient-centered care integrates her stated goals: engaging palliative care, delivering symptom-directed radiation, optimizing analgesia, and documenting goals of care (including code status and proxy) best honors comfort and home priorities while still treating reversible suffering. Overriding her autonomy with aggressive surgery is unethical and clinically inappropriate given ECOG 3. Immediate discharge without treating the painful, function-limiting compression neglects palliation. A third chemotherapy line after progression through two lines in an ECOG 3 patient is unlikely to help and conflicts with her comfort-focused goals. Aligning treatment with documented values is central to high-quality oncologic care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Three days into radiation she develops a new fever, hyperglycemia to 320 mg/dL, and worsening proximal weakness. Which complication of her therapy is most likely contributing?
options: Radiation myelitis
Corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia and increased infection risk, plus possible steroid myopathy
Tumor lysis syndrome
Spinal epidural hematoma
stages.explanation: High-dose corticosteroids commonly cause hyperglycemia, immunosuppression predisposing to infection (explaining fever), and proximal steroid myopathy that can mimic or compound weakness — the most likely contributors here. Radiation myelitis is a delayed complication occurring months to years after treatment, not within days. Tumor lysis syndrome arises with rapidly proliferating, chemo-sensitive tumors (e.g., lymphoma/leukemia) and causes metabolic derangements, not this picture. Spinal epidural hematoma would produce acute focal worsening and pain, typically tied to a procedure or coagulopathy, not fever and hyperglycemia. Recognizing steroid toxicity guides glucose control, infection workup, and a careful steroid taper.

cases.diagnosis: Multilevel metastatic epidural spinal cord compression from NSCLC in a patient with poor performance status (palliative management)
pearls: Surgery for MESCC benefits fit patients with a single level; multilevel disease and poor performance status favor palliative radiotherapy.
Dexamethasone is escalated for malignant cord compression, then tapered as radiation takes effect — the trauma methylprednisolone protocol does not apply.
Align local therapy intensity with performance status and documented goals of care.
Watch for steroid toxicity: hyperglycemia, infection, and proximal myopathy can all worsen function.

cases.title: Double Vision, a Drooping Face, and a Numb Chin
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive double vision, facial weakness, and headaches over three weeks in a woman with a history of breast cancer.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman with a history of hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer treated four years ago is admitted for three weeks of worsening headaches, intermittent horizontal double vision, and new right-sided facial droop. She also reports numbness over her chin and lower lip, nausea, and a few episodes of vomiting. Over the past week she has had difficulty hearing on the left and tingling around the mouth. Medications include anastrozole and as-needed ondansetron. No drug allergies. She has no smoking history and drinks rarely. She lives with her husband and two adult children. Review of systems is positive for low back pain and intermittent lower-extremity tingling; she denies fever, neck stiffness with photophobia at onset, recent travel, or sick contacts. Her last surveillance imaging two years ago showed no metastatic disease. She is frustrated by progressive, seemingly unrelated neurologic deficits that do not fit a single anatomic location.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, mildly uncomfortable. Multiple cranial nerve findings: right abducens palsy (impaired abduction, diplopia on right gaze), right peripheral facial weakness (CN VII), reduced sensation in the right mental nerve distribution ('numb chin'), and decreased left-sided hearing (CN VIII). Mild neck stiffness. Fundi without frank papilledema. Motor exam shows normal strength in arms; lower extremities have mildly depressed ankle reflexes and reduced pinprick in a patchy distribution. Gait cautious. No focal cerebellar dysmetria. The deficits are multifocal and cannot be explained by a single intra-axial lesion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What pattern do these combined findings — multiple cranial neuropathies, the 'numb chin sign,' headache, and patchy lumbar radicular signs — most suggest?
options: A single brainstem mass lesion
Diffuse seeding of the leptomeninges and CSF spaces affecting multiple nerve roots and cranial nerves
Peripheral demyelinating polyneuropathy (Guillain–Barré)
Bilateral cavernous sinus thrombosis
stages.explanation: Simultaneous involvement of several non-contiguous cranial nerves plus spinal nerve roots, with headache and the malignancy-associated 'numb chin sign,' points to a process coating the entire neuraxis — leptomeningeal carcinomatosis. A single brainstem mass cannot produce deficits spanning CN VI, VII, VIII, the mental nerve, and lumbar roots at once. Guillain–Barré causes ascending areflexic weakness with albuminocytologic dissociation, not isolated multifocal cranial palsies plus headache. Cavernous sinus thrombosis affects CN III, IV, V1/V2, and VI on one side with proptosis/chemosis, not CN VIII or lumbar roots. The multifocal, neuraxis-wide pattern is the key clue to leptomeningeal spread.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate first imaging study?
options: Non-contrast head CT
Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and the entire spine
CT myelogram
PET-CT of the body
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain and whole spine is the imaging test of choice for suspected leptomeningeal disease, revealing nodular or linear pial/subarachnoid enhancement, cranial nerve enhancement, and 'sugar-coating' of nerve roots — and it should be obtained before lumbar puncture to avoid post-LP dural enhancement artifact. Non-contrast CT is insensitive to leptomeningeal enhancement. CT myelogram is invasive and reserved for patients who cannot undergo MRI. PET-CT evaluates systemic disease burden but does not reliably show leptomeningeal involvement. MRI also guides the safest site for subsequent CSF sampling and detects hydrocephalus before LP.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & SPINE / FIRST CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain: diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement along sulci and basal cisterns; enhancement of CN VII/VIII complex on the left
MRI spine: nodular enhancing deposits coating the cauda equina nerve roots
No large mass lesion; mild communicating ventricular enlargement
First CSF: opening pressure 24 cm H2O; WBC 38/µL (lymphocytic); protein 110 mg/dL; glucose 38 mg/dL (serum 110)
Cytology: NEGATIVE for malignant cells on first sample

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: MRI strongly suggests leptomeningeal disease, but the first CSF cytology is negative. What is the best next step to confirm the diagnosis?
