options: Conclude there is no leptomeningeal disease and stop the workup
Repeat lumbar puncture with a high-volume CSF sample and send cytology (up to two or three taps)
Proceed directly to whole-brain radiation without confirmation
Obtain a brain biopsy
stages.explanation: A single negative CSF cytology does not exclude leptomeningeal carcinomatosis — sensitivity is only ~50–60% on the first tap but rises to ~80–90% with repeated, high-volume sampling. With supportive MRI and a malignant CSF profile (high protein, low glucose, pleocytosis), the correct step is to repeat the LP with adequate volume, ideally two to three times. Declaring no disease ignores strong corroborating evidence. Treating with radiation before any confirmation is premature when a confirmatory test is readily available and would establish prognosis and guide therapy. Brain biopsy is invasive, low-yield for leptomeningeal disease, and not indicated when CSF can confirm it.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Repeat cytology confirms malignant cells. Which statement about treatment and prognosis of leptomeningeal carcinomatosis is most accurate?
options: It is generally curable with surgery
Prognosis is poor; treatment is usually palliative and may include focal radiation to symptomatic sites, intrathecal or CNS-penetrant systemic therapy, and CSF diversion for hydrocephalus
Antibiotics are the mainstay of therapy
Treatment is contraindicated and only hospice is appropriate
stages.explanation: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis carries a poor prognosis (untreated median survival weeks); management is palliative and multimodal — radiation to bulky/symptomatic sites, intrathecal chemotherapy or CNS-penetrant systemic agents, and ventriculoperitoneal shunting for symptomatic hydrocephalus, integrated with palliative care. It is not surgically curable because disease coats the entire neuraxis. Antibiotics treat infectious meningitis, not malignant meningitis. While prognosis is grim, treatment is not categorically contraindicated; selected patients gain meaningful symptom control and modest survival benefit, so care is individualized rather than reflexively limited to hospice. Goals-of-care discussions remain central throughout.

cases.diagnosis: Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis from recurrent breast cancer
pearls: Multifocal cranial neuropathies plus radiculopathies across the neuraxis suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis; the 'numb chin sign' is a red flag for malignancy.
Get gadolinium MRI of brain and whole spine BEFORE lumbar puncture to avoid post-LP enhancement artifact.
One negative CSF cytology does not rule it out — repeat high-volume taps raise sensitivity to ~90%.
Classic malignant CSF: elevated protein, low glucose, lymphocytic pleocytosis, and positive cytology.

cases.title: A Young Girl With a Crossed Eye and Clumsy Walking
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of a new inward-turning eye, facial asymmetry, and increasing clumsiness when walking.
cases.history: A 6-year-old previously healthy girl is brought to clinic by her parents for six weeks of progressive symptoms: her right eye has begun to turn inward, she has a subtle facial droop, and she has become unsteady, tripping and reaching for walls. Her parents also noticed slurred speech and occasional choking on liquids. She has had mild morning headaches but no clear vomiting. She was born at term with normal development and milestones; she is in kindergarten and was previously athletic. Medications: none. No drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. No recent illness, travel, or trauma. Family history is unremarkable; no neurocutaneous disorders. Review of systems is negative for seizures, fever, weight loss, and rash. The rapid emergence of multiple brainstem-referable signs over weeks in a school-aged child is the central concern, prompting urgent neurologic evaluation and imaging.
cases.exam: Alert, interactive child. Cranial nerves: right esotropia with impaired abduction (CN VI palsy) and a right peripheral facial weakness (CN VII); mild dysarthria and a reduced gag with palatal asymmetry suggesting lower cranial nerve involvement. Pupils equal and reactive; fundi without definite papilledema. Motor: mild left-sided drift with hyperreflexia and an extensor plantar on the left (long-tract signs). Coordination: dysmetria on finger–nose and a wide-based, ataxic gait. Sensation grossly intact. The combination of multiple cranial neuropathies plus contralateral long-tract signs localizes to the brainstem (pons).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The pattern of multiple cranial nerve palsies with crossed long-tract signs localizes the lesion to which structure?
options: Cerebral cortex
Pons (brainstem)
Cerebellar hemisphere alone
Cervical spinal cord
stages.explanation: Coexisting cranial neuropathies (CN VI, VII, lower CNs) with contralateral or ipsilateral long-tract motor signs and ataxia is the classic 'crossed' picture of an intrinsic brainstem (pontine) lesion, where cranial nerve nuclei and descending tracts are in close proximity. A cortical lesion would produce contralateral hemiparesis or seizures, not multiple cranial nerve palsies. Isolated cerebellar disease causes ataxia and dysmetria but not cranial nerve palsies or corticospinal signs. A cervical cord lesion produces myelopathy below the neck without cranial nerve involvement. The convergence of brainstem nuclei and tracts explains the multifocal but anatomically unified deficits.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic study?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium
Lumbar puncture first
Electroencephalogram
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast is the study of choice to characterize a suspected brainstem tumor, defining its location, extent, and infiltrative nature. Non-contrast CT is insensitive in the posterior fossa due to beam-hardening artifact and would miss an infiltrative pontine lesion. Lumbar puncture before imaging is dangerous when a posterior fossa mass and possible obstructive hydrocephalus are suspected, risking herniation. EEG evaluates seizures, which are not the issue here. MRI is also critical because the radiographic appearance of a diffusely expansile pontine lesion can establish the diagnosis without biopsy in many cases.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Diffusely expansile, infiltrative T2/FLAIR-hyperintense mass centered in the pons
The lesion engulfs and encases the basilar artery
More than 50% of the pons is involved; minimal/patchy enhancement
Mild effacement of the fourth ventricle without significant hydrocephalus
Radiographic appearance characteristic of a diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the MRI appearance, which diagnosis and molecular feature are most consistent?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma, BRAF-fusion (typically cystic and well-circumscribed)
Diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27M-mutant (DIPG)
Medulloblastoma arising in the fourth ventricle
Brainstem cavernous malformation
stages.explanation: A diffusely infiltrative, expansile pontine mass engulfing the basilar artery in a child is the hallmark of diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma, now classified as diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27M-mutant — a WHO grade 4 tumor defined by the histone H3 K27M mutation. Pilocytic astrocytoma is typically a well-circumscribed, cystic, strongly enhancing lesion (often cerebellar) with an excellent prognosis — the opposite radiographic behavior. Medulloblastoma is an enhancing midline cerebellar/fourth-ventricular mass causing early hydrocephalus, not an infiltrative pontine lesion. A cavernoma shows a 'popcorn' hemorrhagic appearance with a hemosiderin rim, not diffuse infiltration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard primary treatment for diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma?
options: Gross total surgical resection
Focal radiation therapy for palliation/temporary disease control
Curative high-dose multi-agent chemotherapy
Observation with serial imaging
stages.explanation: Because DIPG diffusely infiltrates the pons and encases vital structures, it is unresectable; focal radiation therapy is the standard treatment and provides temporary symptom relief and disease control but is not curative. Gross total resection is impossible without devastating brainstem injury and is not attempted. No chemotherapy regimen has demonstrated cure or durable benefit, though clinical trials are encouraged. Observation is inappropriate for a rapidly progressive grade 4 tumor with neurologic decline. Median survival remains under one year despite radiation, so early integration of palliative care and honest prognostic counseling with the family is essential.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement best reflects appropriate counseling and care planning for the family?
options: Reassure them this tumor is usually curable with surgery
Provide honest prognostic information, emphasize palliative goals, and involve pediatric palliative care early alongside radiation
Recommend immediate diagnostic biopsy as mandatory before any treatment in all cases
Defer all discussion of prognosis until the tumor recurs
stages.explanation: Honest, developmentally appropriate prognostic communication with early pediatric palliative care integration — while delivering focal radiation for symptom control — is the cornerstone of DIPG care given its uniformly poor prognosis. Falsely reassuring the family that it is curable is unethical and inaccurate. Biopsy is increasingly used for molecular confirmation and trial enrollment but is not universally mandatory when imaging is classic; it should be individualized rather than declared compulsory. Deferring prognostic discussion denies the family the information needed to make decisions and plan, which contradicts good palliative and family-centered practice.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27M-mutant (diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma)
pearls: Multiple cranial neuropathies with long-tract signs and ataxia localize to the pons.
A diffusely infiltrative pontine mass engulfing the basilar artery in a child is classic DIPG (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27M-mutant, WHO grade 4).
DIPG is unresectable; focal radiation is palliative standard of care with median survival under one year.
Contrast this with pilocytic astrocytoma — well-circumscribed, cystic, enhancing, and curable by resection.

cases.title: Headaches That Vanish When He Lies Down
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of severe headaches that come on with certain head positions and a transient episode of blurred vision and near-collapse.
cases.history: A 31-year-old man is admitted after several weeks of intermittent, severe headaches that worsen when he stands or tilts his head forward and improve when he lies down. The headaches are accompanied by nausea and, on two occasions, transient bilateral visual blurring and a sense he was about to pass out. Today he had a brief 'drop' episode while bending to tie his shoes. He has no prior neurologic history. Medications: none. No drug allergies. He works as a graphic designer, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not use tobacco or illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory; no known tumor syndromes. Review of systems is positive for short-term memory lapses over the last month and one episode of vomiting; he denies seizures, fever, neck trauma, or focal weakness. The positional nature of his headaches and the transient visual/syncopal symptoms raise concern for intermittent obstruction of cerebrospinal fluid flow.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented; appears uncomfortable when upright. Cranial nerves intact; visual acuity normal at rest; early bilateral papilledema is noted on fundoscopy. No focal motor or sensory deficit; strength 5/5 throughout. Reflexes normal and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Coordination intact; gait steady at the moment of exam. Mild difficulty with serial sevens and short-term recall. No meningismus. Positional provocation reproduces his headache. The overall picture suggests raised intracranial pressure with a positional, intermittent component.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Positional headaches, transient visual obscurations, early papilledema, and drop attacks in a young adult most suggest which mechanism?
options: Migraine with aura
Intermittent obstruction of CSF flow with episodic raised intracranial pressure
Orthostatic hypotension
Tension-type headache
stages.explanation: Headaches that change with head position, transient visual obscurations, papilledema, and drop attacks indicate intermittent CSF outflow obstruction causing episodic intracranial hypertension — classically from a mobile or strategically located intraventricular lesion that transiently blocks flow. Migraine with aura produces stereotyped visual phenomena without papilledema and is not positionally obstructive. Orthostatic hypotension causes lightheadedness on standing but not papilledema or positional headache relief when supine. Tension headaches are band-like, non-positional, and lack papilledema or visual obscurations. The signs of raised ICP plus a positional trigger are the central clue to a ventricular obstructing lesion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic study?
options: Lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure first
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Carotid ultrasound
Tilt-table testing
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast is the best test to identify a structural intraventricular lesion and assess ventricular size before any CSF sampling. Lumbar puncture performed first is dangerous with suspected obstructive hydrocephalus and a possible intraventricular mass because it can precipitate herniation. Carotid ultrasound evaluates extracranial vascular disease, which does not explain papilledema or positional headaches in a young man. Tilt-table testing assesses orthostatic/syncopal physiology but would miss a structural cause. MRI both localizes the lesion (e.g., at the foramen of Monro) and demonstrates the pattern of ventricular enlargement.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Well-circumscribed, lobulated intraventricular mass within the lateral ventricle attached near the septum pellucidum at the foramen of Monro
Mass is heterogeneous with cystic spaces and small calcifications; 'bubbly/soap-bubble' appearance
Moderate enhancement after gadolinium; broad attachment to the septum/ventricular wall
Dilation of the lateral ventricles consistent with obstructive hydrocephalus at the foramen of Monro
No brainstem or extra-axial lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A lobulated, calcified, mildly enhancing intraventricular mass attached near the septum pellucidum at the foramen of Monro in a young adult most likely represents which tumor?
options: Central neurocytoma
Ependymoma of the fourth ventricle
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma
Choroid plexus carcinoma
stages.explanation: A lateral-ventricular mass attached to the septum pellucidum at the foramen of Monro with calcification and a 'soap-bubble' appearance in a young adult (20s–40s) is classic for central neurocytoma, a WHO grade 2 neuronal tumor (synaptophysin-positive). Fourth-ventricular ependymoma occurs in the posterior fossa and tends to extrude through foramina ('plastic' tumor), not the lateral ventricle/septum. SEGA arises at the foramen of Monro too but occurs in tuberous sclerosis patients, who also have cortical tubers and subependymal nodules — absent here, with no neurocutaneous history. Choroid plexus carcinoma is aggressive, occurs mainly in young children, and shows invasive features.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He develops worsening headache and lethargy with progressive ventricular enlargement. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start acetazolamide and observe
Urgent CSF diversion (e.g., external ventricular drain) to relieve acute obstructive hydrocephalus
High-dose dexamethasone alone
Immediate whole-brain radiation
stages.explanation: Acute deterioration from obstructive hydrocephalus is a neurosurgical emergency; urgent CSF diversion (such as an external ventricular drain) relieves the dangerously elevated intracranial pressure and stabilizes the patient before definitive tumor surgery. Acetazolamide modestly reduces CSF production but is far too slow and inadequate for acute obstruction. Dexamethasone helps peritumoral vasogenic edema but does not relieve mechanical CSF blockage at the foramen of Monro. Whole-brain radiation has no role in the acute emergency and is not first-line for a resectable grade 2 tumor. Immediate decompression of CSF prevents herniation while planning resection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After stabilization, what is the definitive treatment of choice for central neurocytoma?
options: Maximal safe surgical resection
Primary chemotherapy
Lifelong observation without intervention
Stereotactic radiosurgery as first-line for all cases
stages.explanation: Maximal safe surgical resection is the definitive treatment for central neurocytoma; gross total resection offers excellent local control and often cure for this typically benign (WHO grade 2) tumor, and it relieves the CSF obstruction. Primary chemotherapy is not standard for an initially resectable tumor. Observation alone is inappropriate given symptomatic hydrocephalus and progressive disease. Radiosurgery/radiotherapy is reserved for residual or recurrent disease, atypical (higher proliferative index) tumors, or inoperable cases — not as universal first-line therapy. Extent of resection is the strongest predictor of recurrence-free survival, so surgery is prioritized.

cases.diagnosis: Central neurocytoma at the foramen of Monro with obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Positional headaches with transient visual obscurations and drop attacks suggest intermittent CSF obstruction and raised ICP.
Central neurocytoma: young adult, lateral ventricle attached to the septum pellucidum, calcified 'soap-bubble' mass, synaptophysin-positive (WHO grade 2).
Acute obstructive hydrocephalus needs urgent CSF diversion before definitive tumor surgery.
Maximal safe resection is definitive; extent of resection predicts recurrence — radiation is reserved for residual/atypical/recurrent disease.

cases.title: A Toddler Whose Head Is Growing Too Fast
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in for a rapidly enlarging head, vomiting, and increasing fussiness over the past few weeks.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric emergency department because his head has been growing rapidly and his parents note he has become irritable, vomits in the mornings, and seems less interested in play. His head circumference has crossed upward across two percentile lines over two months at well-child visits. He was born at term without complications and met early milestones, but his parents feel his development has plateaued. Medications: none. No drug allergies. Immunizations up to date. No history of trauma, prematurity, or prior infection. Family history is unremarkable with no neurocutaneous syndromes. Review of systems is positive for poor feeding, intermittent vomiting, and increased sleepiness; he has had no fever, seizures, or rash. The combination of accelerating macrocephaly with signs of raised intracranial pressure in a toddler is the central concern prompting urgent neuroimaging.
cases.exam: Irritable but consolable toddler. Head circumference well above the 97th percentile with a tense, bulging anterior fontanelle and prominent scalp veins. 'Sunsetting' of the eyes (impaired upgaze) is present. Pupils equal and reactive; fundi difficult to visualize but suggest fullness of the discs. Anterior fontanelle does not pulsate normally. Tone is mildly increased; he moves all extremities. No focal weakness, no meningismus, and no skin findings such as hypopigmented macules or facial lesions. The findings indicate raised intracranial pressure and likely hydrocephalus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A bulging fontanelle, sunsetting eyes, and accelerating head growth in a toddler indicate which underlying problem?
options: Benign familial macrocephaly
Raised intracranial pressure from hydrocephalus
Dehydration
Simple febrile illness
stages.explanation: A tense bulging fontanelle, sunsetting eyes (from dorsal midbrain pressure), morning vomiting, and rapidly crossing head-circumference percentiles are classic signs of raised intracranial pressure due to hydrocephalus in a young child. Benign familial macrocephaly produces a large but proportionate head with normal development and no ICP signs. Dehydration causes a sunken, not bulging, fontanelle. A simple febrile illness does not explain progressive macrocephaly, sunsetting, or developmental plateau. Recognizing the ICP/hydrocephalus pattern drives urgent imaging to find the cause of impaired CSF dynamics.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to evaluate this child urgently?
options: Skull radiographs
MRI of the brain with and without contrast (CT if MRI not immediately available)
Cranial ultrasound only, then discharge
Lumbar puncture before imaging
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast best characterizes the ventricles and any mass causing hydrocephalus; CT is an acceptable rapid alternative if MRI is not immediately available to assess ventricular size and detect a lesion. Skull radiographs may show split sutures but cannot identify the cause. Cranial ultrasound can be a quick bedside screen while the fontanelle is open but is insufficient as the sole study and discharging without definitive imaging would be unsafe. Lumbar puncture before imaging is contraindicated with suspected obstructive hydrocephalus and possible mass, given herniation risk. Definitive cross-sectional imaging is needed.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Markedly enlarged ventricles (hydrocephalus)
Lobulated, strongly and homogeneously enhancing intraventricular mass with a 'cauliflower'/frond-like surface within the lateral ventricle (atrium/trigone)
Mass arises from the choroid plexus
No definite brain parenchymal invasion; well-circumscribed margins
Findings consistent with a choroid plexus tumor causing both CSF overproduction and obstruction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A well-circumscribed, frond-like, avidly enhancing choroid plexus mass in a 2-year-old causing hydrocephalus is most consistent with which diagnosis, and by what dual mechanism does it cause hydrocephalus?
options: Choroid plexus papilloma; CSF overproduction plus obstruction of CSF flow
Medulloblastoma; purely obstructive at the fourth ventricle
Ependymoma; from spinal seeding
Germinoma; via the pineal region only
stages.explanation: A benign, well-circumscribed, frond-like ('cauliflower') avidly enhancing mass arising from the choroid plexus in a young child is a choroid plexus papilloma (WHO grade 1), which causes hydrocephalus by both overproducing CSF and mechanically obstructing CSF flow/absorption — the classic dual mechanism. Medulloblastoma is a fourth-ventricular midline cerebellar tumor causing obstruction but does not arise from lateral-ventricle choroid plexus or overproduce CSF. Ependymoma typically arises in the fourth ventricle and extrudes through foramina; spinal seeding is a metastatic feature, not the primary cause here. Germinoma favors the pineal/suprasellar region, not the lateral-ventricle choroid plexus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment of choice for a choroid plexus papilloma?
options: Whole-brain radiation
Complete surgical resection
Chemotherapy alone
Permanent shunt placement alone without addressing the tumor
stages.explanation: Complete surgical resection is both diagnostic and curative for choroid plexus papilloma (WHO grade 1); removing the tumor stops CSF overproduction and relieves obstruction, and gross total resection generally requires no adjuvant therapy. Whole-brain radiation is avoided in young children due to neurocognitive toxicity and is unnecessary for a benign, resectable tumor. Chemotherapy is reserved for malignant choroid plexus carcinoma, not papilloma. Shunting alone treats the hydrocephalus symptomatically but leaves the CSF-overproducing tumor in place; many patients no longer need a shunt after the papilloma is resected. Surgery addresses both the mass and the underlying CSF derangement.

cases.diagnosis: Choroid plexus papilloma of the lateral ventricle causing hydrocephalus
pearls: Sunsetting eyes, bulging fontanelle, and accelerating head growth signal raised ICP from hydrocephalus in infants/toddlers.
Choroid plexus papilloma causes hydrocephalus by BOTH CSF overproduction and obstruction.
It appears as a frond-like ('cauliflower'), avidly enhancing intraventricular mass, most often in the lateral ventricle in children.
Complete resection is curative for the WHO grade 1 papilloma; chemotherapy is reserved for choroid plexus carcinoma.

cases.title: Thunderous Headaches That Come and Go With Head Position
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden, severe headache triggered by head movement, with one brief loss of consciousness.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents to the emergency department after recurrent episodes of abrupt, severe global headache that come on suddenly when he moves or bends his head and resolve within minutes when he keeps still or lies down. Today, while gardening and looking down, he had the worst such headache followed by a brief loss of consciousness and a fall, then rapid recovery. He has had several milder positional headaches over the past two months, sometimes with nausea and transient leg weakness ('legs gave out'). Medications: occasional ibuprofen. No drug allergies. He works in construction, drinks socially, and does not use tobacco or drugs. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for intermittent blurred vision during the headaches and one episode of urinary incontinence during a severe attack; he denies fever, neck stiffness at onset, focal numbness, or prior similar episodes before two months ago. The positional, intermittent thunderclap-like headaches with transient loss of consciousness are the concerning features.
cases.exam: At presentation he is alert, oriented, and comfortable lying flat. Cranial nerves intact; pupils equal and reactive; fundi show no definite papilledema at this moment. No focal motor or sensory deficit; strength 5/5 and reflexes symmetric with flexor plantars. Coordination and gait normal during the symptom-free interval. No meningismus and no neck rigidity. Provoking head flexion in the ED briefly reproduces a severe headache with pallor that resolves on lying back. The intermittent, positional nature with normal interval exam is characteristic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Sudden positional headaches that resolve when lying still, with transient loss of consciousness, classically suggest which mechanism?
options: Subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured aneurysm
A mobile lesion intermittently obstructing CSF flow at the foramen of Monro causing acute, transient hydrocephalus
Cluster headache
Vasovagal syncope alone
stages.explanation: Intermittent, position-dependent thunderclap-like headaches that abate when the patient is still, sometimes with transient loss of consciousness ('drop attacks'), classically result from a ball-valve lesion intermittently obstructing CSF at the foramen of Monro, causing acute transient hydrocephalus and ICP spikes — the textbook behavior of a third-ventricular lesion. Aneurysmal SAH causes a sustained worst-headache-of-life with meningismus, not episodes that fully resolve with position changes. Cluster headache is unilateral, orbital, with autonomic features and is not positionally obstructive. Vasovagal syncope explains transient loss of consciousness but not the positional severe headaches and visual obscurations. The positional intermittency is the key clue.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging in the ED?
options: Non-contrast head CT (followed by MRI with contrast)
Lumbar puncture first to rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Plain skull films
stages.explanation: A non-contrast head CT is the fastest, most available study to detect acute hydrocephalus and a hyperdense third-ventricular lesion and to exclude hemorrhage, and it should be followed by MRI with contrast for definitive characterization. Lumbar puncture first is contraindicated when obstructive hydrocephalus and an obstructing mass are suspected, given herniation risk; imaging must precede LP here. Carotid ultrasound assesses extracranial vessels and would not explain positional hydrocephalus. Plain skull films cannot evaluate ventricles or intracranial masses. CT triages the emergency and guides whether urgent CSF diversion is needed before MRI characterizes the lesion.

stages.label: CT THEN MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT: well-defined, hyperdense rounded lesion at the anterosuperior third ventricle near the foramen of Monro
Bilateral lateral ventricle dilation (acute obstructive hydrocephalus)
MRI: T1-hyperintense, T2-variable spherical cyst at the roof of the third ventricle, ~1.2 cm, attached near the foramen of Monro
No subarachnoid blood; no parenchymal mass
Appearance characteristic of a colloid cyst of the third ventricle

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A well-defined, often T1-hyperintense spherical cyst at the roof of the third ventricle near the foramen of Monro is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Colloid cyst of the third ventricle
Craniopharyngioma
Pituitary macroadenoma
Pineal germinoma
stages.explanation: A spherical, well-circumscribed cyst at the anterosuperior third ventricle near the foramen of Monro, frequently hyperdense on CT and T1-hyperintense on MRI due to proteinaceous content, is the classic colloid cyst — notorious for intermittent CSF obstruction and risk of sudden death. Craniopharyngioma is a suprasellar, often calcified, cystic-solid tumor causing visual field and endocrine deficits, not a third-ventricular roof cyst. Pituitary macroadenoma arises in the sella, causing bitemporal hemianopia and hormonal effects. Pineal germinoma sits in the pineal region and causes Parinaud syndrome. The location and signal characteristics make colloid cyst the clear answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why is this lesion considered dangerous, and what is the appropriate management given his acute hydrocephalus?
options: It is benign and never requires treatment; discharge with reassurance
It can cause acute obstructive hydrocephalus and sudden death; symptomatic/large cysts with hydrocephalus warrant CSF diversion and surgical removal
It is best treated with chemotherapy
It requires only long-term anticoagulation
stages.explanation: A colloid cyst can intermittently block CSF at the foramen of Monro, producing acute obstructive hydrocephalus and, rarely, sudden death — so a symptomatic cyst with hydrocephalus requires urgent CSF diversion (e.g., EVD) and surgical removal (endoscopic or microsurgical resection), which is generally curative. Calling it always benign and discharging him ignores a documented risk of fatal deterioration. Chemotherapy has no role for this non-neoplastic cyst. Anticoagulation is irrelevant and could be harmful. While small incidental asymptomatic cysts may be observed, this patient's hydrocephalus and syncope mandate intervention. Surgical resection eliminates the recurrent obstruction risk.

cases.diagnosis: Colloid cyst of the third ventricle with intermittent obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Positional thunderclap-like headaches with drop attacks suggest a ball-valve lesion obstructing the foramen of Monro.
Colloid cysts sit at the anterosuperior third ventricle, are often hyperdense on CT/T1-hyperintense on MRI, and can cause sudden death.
Image (CT/MRI) before LP whenever obstructive hydrocephalus is suspected.
Symptomatic colloid cysts with hydrocephalus need CSF diversion and surgical removal; small asymptomatic ones can be monitored.

cases.title: A Teen With Seizures, Skin Spots, and a Growing Brain Lesion
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up for new morning headaches and worsening seizures in a teenager with a known genetic condition and a brain lesion that has enlarged on surveillance imaging.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy with known tuberous sclerosis complex presents for follow-up after surveillance MRI showed enlargement of a lesion near the foramen of Monro. Over two months he has had new morning headaches, intermittent nausea, blurred vision, and an increase in seizure frequency despite stable antiseizure medication. His condition was diagnosed in childhood after infantile seizures; he has facial angiofibromas, hypomelanotic macules, and a history of renal angiomyolipomas. Medications: levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily; no drug allergies. He attends school with an individualized education plan and has mild intellectual disability. Family history: a parent with similar skin findings, consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance. Review of systems is positive for headaches, vomiting once, and visual blurring; he denies fever or focal weakness. The growth of a foramen-of-Monro lesion with new ICP symptoms in a TSC patient is the central concern, suggesting transformation of a subependymal nodule.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative teenager. Skin shows facial angiofibromas across the malar region, several hypomelanotic 'ash-leaf' macules, and a shagreen patch on the lower back. Cranial nerves intact; early bilateral papilledema noted on fundoscopy. No focal motor or sensory deficit; strength 5/5, reflexes symmetric, plantars flexor. Mild cognitive impairment consistent with baseline. No meningismus. Coordination and gait normal. The neurocutaneous stigmata plus signs of raised intracranial pressure point to an enlarging intraventricular lesion causing early obstruction at the foramen of Monro.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a patient with tuberous sclerosis, new headaches and an enlarging lesion at the foramen of Monro most strongly suggest which complication?
options: A new cortical tuber
Growth of a subependymal nodule into a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA) causing CSF obstruction
A cortical dysplasia
A renal angiomyolipoma metastasis to brain
stages.explanation: In TSC, subependymal nodules near the foramen of Monro can enlarge into a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA), which can obstruct CSF flow and cause raised intracranial pressure — exactly this clinical picture of an enlarging foramen-of-Monro lesion with new ICP symptoms. Cortical tubers are static developmental lesions in the cortex and do not obstruct CSF or typically enlarge. Cortical (focal) dysplasia relates to epileptogenesis, not ventricular obstruction. Angiomyolipomas are benign renal/visceral lesions and do not metastasize to the brain. The location at the foramen of Monro and interval growth are the hallmarks of SEGA transformation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic study?
options: Repeat skin examination only
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium to characterize the lesion and assess for hydrocephalus
Plain skull radiographs
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast characterizes the enlarging lesion (size, enhancement, growth rate) and evaluates ventricular size for obstruction — essential for deciding on intervention in a TSC patient with new ICP symptoms. A skin exam confirms known TSC stigmata but adds nothing to the intracranial question. Plain skull films cannot assess the ventricles or the mass. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when obstructive hydrocephalus from a foramen-of-Monro lesion is suspected because of herniation risk. Serial contrast MRI is also the standard surveillance tool in TSC specifically to detect SEGA growth before it becomes critically obstructive.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
Enhancing mass at the right foramen of Monro measuring 2.4 cm, enlarged from 1.3 cm one year prior
Multiple subependymal nodules along the lateral ventricle walls, some calcified
Numerous cortical/subcortical tubers and radial migration lines
Mild enlargement of the lateral ventricles (early obstruction at the foramen of Monro)
Findings consistent with a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma in tuberous sclerosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a growing but not acutely critical SEGA with early ventricular enlargement, which medical therapy specifically targets the underlying pathway and can shrink SEGAs?
options: An mTOR inhibitor such as everolimus
High-dose corticosteroids
A new anticonvulsant (e.g., switching to lacosamide)
Methotrexate
stages.explanation: TSC results from TSC1/TSC2 mutations causing constitutive mTOR pathway activation; mTOR inhibitors such as everolimus specifically target this pathway and can shrink SEGAs, offering a medical alternative or adjunct to surgery for growing tumors not requiring emergent decompression. Corticosteroids reduce edema transiently but do not treat the tumor's molecular driver or durably shrink it. Changing anticonvulsants addresses seizures but not the enlarging SEGA or hydrocephalus. Methotrexate is not used for SEGA. Everolimus is FDA-approved for SEGA in TSC and can avoid or delay surgery, though close monitoring of tumor size and ventricles is required.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If the SEGA instead caused acute, symptomatic obstructive hydrocephalus, what would be the most appropriate management?
options: Start everolimus and wait several weeks for the tumor to shrink
Surgical resection of the SEGA (with CSF diversion as needed) to relieve the obstruction
Increase the antiseizure medication only
Begin whole-brain radiation
stages.explanation: Acute symptomatic obstructive hydrocephalus from a SEGA is a surgical problem: surgical resection of the tumor, with CSF diversion (e.g., EVD or shunt) as needed, relieves the obstruction and is the definitive treatment in this setting. Everolimus shrinks SEGAs slowly over weeks to months and is inappropriate when the patient needs immediate decompression. Increasing antiseizure medication does nothing for the mechanical CSF blockage. Whole-brain radiation has no role for this benign (WHO grade 1) tumor and carries unacceptable neurocognitive toxicity in an adolescent. The acuity of hydrocephalus dictates surgery over slow medical therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate long-term surveillance recommendation for this patient?
options: No further imaging is needed once treated
Periodic surveillance brain MRI to monitor for SEGA growth, with multidisciplinary care for skin, renal, and neuropsychiatric manifestations
Annual chest CT only
Discontinue all medications and reassess in five years
stages.explanation: TSC is a multisystem disorder requiring periodic surveillance brain MRI to detect SEGA growth (SEGAs typically grow during childhood/adolescence) plus coordinated monitoring of renal angiomyolipomas, skin lesions, cardiac rhabdomyomas, and neuropsychiatric/epilepsy needs (TAND). Stopping imaging after treatment risks missing recurrence or new growth. A chest CT alone neglects the brain and kidneys (lymphangioleiomyomatosis screening is relevant mainly in adult women). Discontinuing medications and waiting five years abandons necessary seizure control and surveillance. Lifelong, organized multidisciplinary follow-up is the standard of care in tuberous sclerosis complex.

cases.diagnosis: Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA) in tuberous sclerosis complex
pearls: SEGAs arise at the foramen of Monro from subependymal nodules in TSC and can obstruct CSF flow.
TSC is driven by TSC1/TSC2 mutations and mTOR activation; everolimus can shrink growing SEGAs and may avoid surgery.
Acute symptomatic hydrocephalus from a SEGA requires surgical resection (with CSF diversion), not slow medical therapy.
TSC needs lifelong multidisciplinary surveillance: serial brain MRI plus renal, skin, cardiac, and neuropsychiatric monitoring.

cases.title: An Infant With Jerking Spasms and Pale Skin Patches
cases.chiefComplaint: Evaluation for clusters of sudden body jerks that began in infancy and developmental concerns, with notable skin findings.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl is brought to clinic for evaluation of a long history of episodic seizures and developmental delay. As an infant she had clusters of sudden flexor 'jackknife' spasms occurring in runs, especially on waking, which her pediatrician at the time treated; since then she has had ongoing seizures and slower-than-expected development. Her parents have also noticed pale patches on her skin since birth and small bumps developing on her face. She was born at term without complications. Medications: currently on an antiseizure medication; no drug allergies. Immunizations up to date. Family history: a maternal uncle with epilepsy and 'skin spots.' Review of systems is positive for developmental delay and behavioral difficulties (features of autism spectrum); she denies recent fever or trauma. The combination of infantile spasms, characteristic skin findings, and developmental delay raises concern for a neurocutaneous syndrome, prompting examination and brain imaging.
cases.exam: Active but easily distracted child with limited language for age. Skin: multiple hypomelanotic 'ash-leaf' macules over the trunk and limbs (highlighted with Wood's lamp), early facial angiofibromas across the cheeks/nose, and a subtle shagreen patch over the lower back. Cranial nerves intact; fundi normal without papilledema. No focal weakness; strength and tone grossly normal. Reflexes symmetric. Coordination age-appropriate. No meningismus. The neurocutaneous skin findings combined with a history of infantile spasms and developmental delay strongly suggest a specific syndrome.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Infantile spasms, hypomelanotic macules, facial angiofibromas, and developmental delay together most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Sturge–Weber syndrome
Incontinentia pigmenti
stages.explanation: The triad of infantile spasms, hypomelanotic 'ash-leaf' macules with facial angiofibromas, and developmental delay is classic for tuberous sclerosis complex, an autosomal dominant neurocutaneous disorder (TSC1/TSC2). Neurofibromatosis type 1 features café-au-lait macules, axillary/inguinal freckling, neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules — not ash-leaf macules or angiofibromas, and it is not classically associated with infantile spasms. Sturge–Weber presents with a facial port-wine stain in the V1 distribution and leptomeningeal angiomas, not these findings. Incontinentia pigmenti causes a vesicular-to-pigmented swirling rash along Blaschko lines in females, a different dermatologic pattern. The specific skin lesions clinch TSC.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate the central nervous system involvement?
options: MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Non-contrast head CT only
Genetic testing instead of any imaging
Electroencephalogram alone
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast best demonstrates the CNS lesions of TSC — cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, white-matter radial migration lines, and any subependymal giant cell astrocytoma — and is the preferred neuroimaging study. Non-contrast CT can show calcified subependymal nodules but is far less sensitive for tubers and migration lines and exposes a child to radiation. Genetic testing supports the diagnosis but does not replace imaging needed to assess for SEGA and structural lesions guiding management. EEG is important for characterizing seizures (and earlier showed hypsarrhythmia in infantile spasms) but does not visualize structural brain lesions. MRI is the key structural study.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & EEG HISTORY
stages.content:
MRI: multiple cortical and subcortical tubers
Subependymal nodules along the lateral ventricle walls, several calcified
White-matter radial migration lines
No enhancing foramen-of-Monro mass at present (no SEGA)
Prior infancy EEG (historical): hypsarrhythmia (chaotic high-amplitude background) consistent with infantile spasms

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding the infantile spasms she had as an infant, which first-line treatment is preferred specifically in tuberous sclerosis complex?
options: Vigabatrin
Phenytoin
Ethosuximide
Carbamazepine
stages.explanation: Vigabatrin is the preferred first-line therapy for infantile spasms in tuberous sclerosis complex and is notably effective in this population (ACTH/corticosteroids are first-line for infantile spasms generally). Phenytoin is not used for infantile spasms and can worsen certain seizure types. Ethosuximide treats absence seizures only and is ineffective for spasms. Carbamazepine can aggravate infantile spasms and generalized epilepsies and is inappropriate here. Recognizing TSC-specific therapy is high-yield: vigabatrin's efficacy in TSC-associated spasms is a classic board point, with monitoring for its retinal toxicity (visual field constriction).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate ongoing management plan for this child with TSC?
options: Treat the current seizures and discharge with no further follow-up
Establish multidisciplinary surveillance: periodic brain MRI for SEGA, renal imaging, skin and cardiac evaluation, ophthalmologic monitoring, and neurodevelopmental support
Focus solely on skin treatments
Begin prophylactic whole-brain radiation
stages.explanation: TSC is a multisystem disorder requiring coordinated lifelong surveillance: periodic brain MRI to detect SEGA growth, renal imaging for angiomyolipomas, cardiac evaluation (rhabdomyomas), ophthalmologic exams (retinal hamartomas; vigabatrin monitoring), dermatologic care, and neurodevelopmental/behavioral support (TAND). Treating seizures alone and discharging neglects the high risk of evolving brain, kidney, heart, and developmental complications. Focusing only on skin ignores life-threatening visceral and CNS manifestations. Whole-brain radiation has no role and would be harmful. Organized multidisciplinary follow-up per TSC guidelines optimizes outcomes across all affected organ systems.

cases.diagnosis: Tuberous sclerosis complex
pearls: Infantile spasms + hypomelanotic (ash-leaf) macules + facial angiofibromas = tuberous sclerosis complex (autosomal dominant, TSC1/TSC2).
MRI shows cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, and radial migration lines; watch for SEGA at the foramen of Monro.
Vigabatrin is preferred for infantile spasms in TSC; infantile spasms show hypsarrhythmia on EEG.
Manage TSC with lifelong multidisciplinary surveillance across brain, kidney, heart, eyes, skin, and development.

cases.title: Skin Spots and Bumps in a Young Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother is worried about multiple flat brown skin spots that have increased in number over the past few years.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric clinic by his mother, who reports that he was born with a few flat brown birthmarks, but these have grown in number and size, and he now has more than ten. Over the last year she has also noticed several small soft bumps on his trunk and freckling in his armpits and groin. He is otherwise healthy, meets developmental milestones, though his teacher notes he struggles with reading and math compared with peers. There is no headache, vomiting, seizures, or vision complaints reported by the family. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He is up to date on immunizations. He lives with both parents and a younger sister. Family history is notable for a father with 'lots of birthmarks,' learning difficulties, and a benign nerve tumor removed from his back in adulthood. The boy's growth has tracked along the 40th percentile for height and 50th for weight.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative boy in no distress. Skin reveals 12 café-au-lait macules greater than 5 mm in diameter, axillary and inguinal freckling, and several small soft cutaneous neurofibromas over the trunk. Slit-lamp examination shows multiple raised tan iris hamartomas. Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally with normal color vision; pupils are equal and reactive, and fundoscopy shows sharp optic disc margins. There is no proptosis. Cranial nerves are intact and there is no focal motor or sensory deficit. There are no bony deformities of the limbs or spine, and gait is normal. Cardiac and abdominal exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most specific for establishing this child's underlying diagnosis?
options: Soft cutaneous bumps and axillary freckling alone
Six or more café-au-lait macules over 5 mm plus axillary/inguinal freckling and iris hamartomas
Learning difficulties and a positive family history
A single large café-au-lait macule with irregular borders
stages.explanation: The NIH/revised diagnostic criteria for neurofibromatosis type 1 require two or more features; this boy meets several: ≥6 café-au-lait macules >5 mm (prepubertal cutoff), axillary and inguinal freckling (Crowe sign), cutaneous neurofibromas, and Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas). This combination is highly specific. Soft bumps and freckling alone (choice 1) are suggestive but incomplete without the macule count. Learning difficulties and family history (choice 3) support but do not by themselves establish NF1. A single large macule with irregular 'coast of Maine' borders (choice 4) suggests McCune-Albright/fibrous dysplasia, not NF1, whose macules have smooth 'coast of California' borders.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The raised tan lesions seen on slit-lamp examination of the iris are best described as which of the following?
options: Kayser-Fleischer rings
Brushfield spots
Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas)
Retinal astrocytic hamartomas
stages.explanation: The pigmented, dome-shaped lesions on the iris are Lisch nodules, melanocytic hamartomas characteristic of NF1 that increase in frequency with age and are best seen on slit-lamp examination. Kayser-Fleischer rings (choice 0) are copper deposits at the corneal limbus seen in Wilson disease. Brushfield spots (choice 1) are speckled iris findings associated with Down syndrome. Retinal astrocytic hamartomas (choice 3) are associated with tuberous sclerosis, not NF1, and are found on the retina rather than the iris.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond confirming the diagnosis, which surveillance evaluation is most important to perform routinely in this child given his age?
options: Annual abdominal ultrasound for pheochromocytoma
Annual ophthalmologic examination including visual acuity and visual fields
Annual echocardiography for cardiac rhabdomyoma
Annual renal ultrasound for angiomyolipoma
stages.explanation: Children with NF1 require annual ophthalmologic examinations during early childhood because optic pathway gliomas, the most common CNS tumor in NF1, typically present before age 6–8 and may be asymptomatic initially. Detecting subtle visual acuity or field loss prompts MRI and management. Pheochromocytoma (choice 0) does occur in NF1 but is rare in childhood and screened by blood pressure plus symptom-directed testing, not routine ultrasound. Cardiac rhabdomyoma (choice 2) and renal angiomyolipoma (choice 3) are features of tuberous sclerosis, not NF1.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI with and without contrast (obtained after a subtle drop in visual acuity was noted):
Fusiform enlargement of the right optic nerve with mild contrast enhancement
No extension into the optic chiasm
Scattered T2-hyperintense foci in the basal ganglia and cerebellar white matter (unidentified bright objects)
No hydrocephalus or midline shift
Visual acuity now 20/40 in the right eye, 20/20 left

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: An MRI obtained after a subtle visual change reveals a fusiform enhancing lesion of the right optic nerve. What is the most appropriate initial management of this lesion?
options: Immediate gross total surgical resection of the optic nerve
Upfront whole-brain radiation therapy
Serial clinical and ophthalmologic monitoring with chemotherapy reserved for progression
Begin high-dose corticosteroids indefinitely
stages.explanation: NF1-associated optic pathway gliomas are typically low-grade (pilocytic) and often indolent; many remain stable or even regress. The standard approach is observation with serial MRI and formal ophthalmologic assessment, reserving chemotherapy (carboplatin/vincristine or a MEK inhibitor such as selumetinib) for documented radiographic or visual progression. Immediate resection (choice 0) would sacrifice vision and is not indicated for a stable monocular tumor. Radiation (choice 1) is avoided in young NF1 patients due to risks of moyamoya, secondary malignancy, and cognitive injury. Indefinite corticosteroids (choice 3) are not disease-directed therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 1 with associated optic pathway glioma
pearls: NF1 is diagnosed clinically; ≥6 café-au-lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal), axillary/inguinal freckling, ≥2 neurofibromas, Lisch nodules, optic glioma, distinctive bony lesions, and a first-degree relative are the cardinal criteria.
Lisch nodules are iris melanocytic hamartomas best seen on slit-lamp exam and are nearly pathognomonic for NF1.
Optic pathway glioma is the most common CNS tumor in NF1; annual eye exams in early childhood are essential, and asymptomatic stable lesions are observed rather than treated.
Avoid radiotherapy in NF1 optic gliomas because of heightened risks of secondary tumors, moyamoya vasculopathy, and neurocognitive harm.

cases.title: A Preschooler Who Stopped Gaining Weight and Bumps Into Things
cases.chiefComplaint: Her parents report progressive weight loss despite a good appetite and that she has begun bumping into objects on her left side.
cases.history: A 5-year-old girl with known neurofibromatosis type 1 presents to pediatric clinic for evaluation of failure to thrive and new visual concerns. Over the past six months her parents report she has lost weight and become notably thin despite eating normally; her cheeks have hollowed and she seems unusually energetic and alert. In the last two months they have noticed she frequently bumps into doorframes and furniture on her left and holds books very close to her face. She has occasional morning headaches but no vomiting or seizures. Past medical history includes NF1 diagnosed at age 2 (multiple café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, a first-degree relative). She takes a daily multivitamin and has no drug allergies. Developmental milestones were appropriate. She attends kindergarten. Family history is significant for NF1 in her mother. Review of systems is otherwise negative for polyuria, polydipsia, fever, or night sweats. Her growth chart shows weight dropping from the 50th to the 5th percentile while height and head circumference remain stable.
cases.exam: Thin, alert, euphoric-appearing girl with markedly reduced subcutaneous fat and a normal-appearing face otherwise. Visual acuity is 20/100 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left; there is a right relative afferent pupillary defect. Confrontation fields show a bitemporal pattern of loss. Fundoscopy reveals right optic disc pallor. Extraocular movements are full. Cutaneous exam shows numerous café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling. There is no proptosis. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal, strength and reflexes are symmetric, and gait is steady. The abdomen is soft with no organomegaly; there is no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of profound weight loss with preserved appetite, euphoric affect, and stable linear growth in this young child is best characterized as which syndrome?
options: Cachexia from occult malignancy elsewhere
Diencephalic syndrome
Anorexia nervosa
Adrenal insufficiency
stages.explanation: Diencephalic syndrome is a classic presentation of hypothalamic/optic-chiasmatic tumors in young children: severe emaciation despite normal or increased caloric intake, a paradoxically alert and euphoric demeanor, and often preserved linear growth. It results from hypothalamic dysfunction affecting energy homeostasis. General cancer cachexia (choice 0) usually accompanies anorexia and an ill-appearing child, not euphoria. Anorexia nervosa (choice 2) is implausible at age 5 and involves reduced intake and distorted body image. Adrenal insufficiency (choice 3) causes weakness, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and salt craving rather than euphoria with preserved appetite.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given her NF1 history and the bitemporal visual field loss with a right afferent pupillary defect, where is the lesion most likely located?
options: Left occipital cortex
Optic chiasm and hypothalamus
Right cavernous sinus
Bilateral lateral geniculate nuclei
stages.explanation: Bitemporal field loss localizes to the optic chiasm, where crossing nasal retinal fibers are compressed; the asymmetric acuity loss and right afferent pupillary defect indicate additional anterior right optic nerve involvement. Combined with diencephalic syndrome, this points to an optic-chiasmatic/hypothalamic glioma, the characteristic location in NF1. Occipital cortex injury (choice 0) produces homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing, not bitemporal loss. A cavernous sinus lesion (choice 2) affects cranial nerves III, IV, V1–V2, and VI, causing ophthalmoplegia rather than chiasmal field loss. Bilateral lateral geniculate lesions (choice 3) are exceedingly rare and would not cause diencephalic syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI with and without contrast:
Lobulated enhancing suprasellar mass involving the optic chiasm and hypothalamus, 2.8 cm
Thickening and enhancement of the right optic nerve
No CSF flow obstruction; ventricles normal in size
Serum cortisol (AM): 9 µg/dL
TSH 2.1 µU/mL, free T4 1.0 ng/dL
Serum Na 139 mEq/L, glucose 88 mg/dL
IGF-1 low-normal for age
beta-hCG and AFP: within normal limits

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Imaging confirms a suprasellar mass involving the chiasm and hypothalamus. Tumor markers are negative. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed directly to whole-brain and spinal radiotherapy
Perform aggressive gross total resection to debulk the hypothalamus
Initiate carboplatin/vincristine chemotherapy (or a MEK inhibitor) as first-line therapy
Observe with repeat MRI in one year given NF1
stages.explanation: This symptomatic, progressive optic-chiasmatic/hypothalamic glioma causing visual loss and diencephalic syndrome requires treatment. In young children, first-line therapy is chemotherapy (classically carboplatin and vincristine, or increasingly a MEK inhibitor such as selumetinib in NF1) to control growth while sparing vision and avoiding radiation toxicity. Radiotherapy (choice 0) is avoided in young NF1 patients due to vasculopathy, secondary tumors, and cognitive injury. Aggressive resection (choice 1) of a chiasmatic-hypothalamic glioma risks devastating endocrine and visual injury and is not standard. Observation (choice 3) is inappropriate given documented symptomatic progression.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complication should be specifically monitored for given the tumor's location and the planned therapy?
options: Precocious puberty and other hypothalamic-pituitary endocrinopathies
Restrictive cardiomyopathy
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Inflammatory bowel disease
stages.explanation: Hypothalamic-chiasmatic tumors and their treatment commonly disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, producing central precocious puberty, growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, and occasionally diabetes insipidus; lifelong endocrine surveillance is essential. Restrictive cardiomyopathy (choice 1) is unrelated to this tumor or carboplatin/vincristine/MEK-inhibitor therapy. Primary hyperparathyroidism (choice 2) is not associated with hypothalamic gliomas or NF1. Inflammatory bowel disease (choice 3) has no link to optic pathway/hypothalamic glioma management.

cases.diagnosis: Optic pathway/hypothalamic glioma with diencephalic syndrome in NF1
pearls: Diencephalic syndrome—emaciation with preserved appetite, euphoria, and normal linear growth—should prompt brain MRI for a hypothalamic/chiasmatic tumor in young children.
Bitemporal hemianopia localizes to the optic chiasm; pair it with an afferent pupillary defect to identify added optic nerve involvement.
Optic pathway gliomas in NF1 are usually low-grade; first-line treatment of progressive disease in young children is chemotherapy or a MEK inhibitor, not radiation.
Hypothalamic-pituitary endocrinopathies (including precocious puberty and GH deficiency) require lifelong surveillance after chiasmatic-hypothalamic tumor treatment.

cases.title: A Boy Who Cannot Look Up and Has Worsening Headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: He was admitted for two weeks of progressively severe morning headaches, double vision, and inability to look upward.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy is admitted to the pediatric ward after two weeks of escalating morning headaches accompanied by vomiting, and his parents note he 'can't look up at the ceiling' and complains of double vision. Over the same period he has become lethargic and his school performance has declined. There is no fever, neck stiffness, recent trauma, or rash. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He was previously healthy and active in soccer. Immunizations are current. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; there are no pets and no recent travel. Family history is unremarkable for cancer or neurologic disease. On further questioning, his parents report his eyes seem to 'jump' when he tries to look up and that his eyelids appear retracted. He has had no seizures. Appetite has decreased over the past week, and he has had one episode of urinary incontinence. There is no history of headaches before this illness.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable boy. Pupils are 5 mm, poorly reactive to light but constrict with near effort (light-near dissociation). There is paralysis of upgaze with convergence-retraction nystagmus on attempted upward saccades and bilateral eyelid retraction. Visual acuity is grossly intact. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilledema. The remainder of the cranial nerves are intact. Strength is 5/5 throughout with normal tone; reflexes are symmetric; plantar responses are flexor. Gait is mildly unsteady. There is no nuchal rigidity. Cardiac exam reveals bradycardia without murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The boy's upgaze palsy, light-near dissociation, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and lid retraction constitute which named syndrome, and where does it localize?
options: Weber syndrome — ventral midbrain
Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome — tectal/pretectal region
Horner syndrome — sympathetic chain
Foville syndrome — dorsal pons
stages.explanation: The tetrad of vertical (up) gaze palsy, pupillary light-near dissociation, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and eyelid retraction (Collier sign) defines Parinaud syndrome, localizing to the dorsal midbrain (tectal/pretectal region). A mass here, often a pineal-region tumor, compresses the vertical gaze center and pretectal pupillary fibers. Weber syndrome (choice 0) involves the ventral midbrain causing CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis. Horner syndrome (choice 2) is miosis, ptosis, and anhidrosis from sympathetic disruption. Foville syndrome (choice 3) is a pontine syndrome with ipsilateral facial palsy and gaze palsy, not vertical gaze loss with light-near dissociation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the bradycardia, papilledema, and morning headaches with vomiting, what mechanism most likely explains his elevated intracranial pressure?
options: Communicating hydrocephalus from impaired arachnoid granulation absorption
Obstructive hydrocephalus from compression of the cerebral aqueduct
Pseudotumor cerebri (idiopathic intracranial hypertension)
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
stages.explanation: A pineal/tectal-region mass compresses the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius, blocking CSF flow from the third to the fourth ventricle and producing noncommunicating (obstructive) hydrocephalus—hence morning headaches, vomiting, papilledema, and the Cushing response (bradycardia/hypertension). Communicating hydrocephalus (choice 0) results from impaired CSF absorption (e.g., after hemorrhage/meningitis) without a focal obstructing mass. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (choice 2) occurs typically in obese women without a mass or focal midbrain signs. Venous sinus thrombosis (choice 3) raises ICP but would not selectively cause Parinaud syndrome and aqueductal obstruction.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Brain MRI with and without contrast:
Lobulated, heterogeneously enhancing pineal-region mass, 3.5 cm, compressing the tectum and cerebral aqueduct
Marked dilation of the lateral and third ventricles (obstructive hydrocephalus)
Restricted diffusion within the mass and a 'frond-like' appearance
Serum beta-hCG: 4 mIU/mL (near normal)
Serum AFP: 2 ng/mL (normal)
MRI of the entire spine: multiple enhancing leptomeningeal nodules along the thoracic and lumbar cord

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient has symptomatic obstructive hydrocephalus from the pineal mass. What is the most appropriate immediate management of his hydrocephalus?
options: Emergent lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (often with concurrent endoscopic biopsy)
Immediate high-dose mannitol alone and discharge planning
Start acetazolamide and observe
stages.explanation: Symptomatic obstructive hydrocephalus from a pineal-region mass is best managed with CSF diversion via endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV), which bypasses the aqueductal block and frequently allows simultaneous endoscopic biopsy for tissue diagnosis. Lumbar puncture (choice 0) is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a posterior mass because of herniation risk. Mannitol alone with discharge (choice 2) is only a temporizing measure and ignores definitive CSF diversion. Acetazolamide with observation (choice 3) is inadequate for acute obstructive hydrocephalus and risks neurologic deterioration. Tissue diagnosis and tumor-directed therapy follow CSF diversion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Biopsy returns a malignant small round blue cell tumor with rosettes; tumor markers are negative and spinal MRI shows drop metastases. Which diagnosis and staging implication best fit?
options: Pineocytoma — typically benign and well-circumscribed
Pineal germinoma — markedly elevated beta-hCG and exquisitely radiosensitive
Pineoblastoma — a malignant embryonal (WHO grade 4) tumor with propensity for CSF dissemination
Tectal pilocytic astrocytoma — indolent and rarely metastasizes
stages.explanation: A malignant small round blue cell tumor with rosettes (Homer Wright/Flexner-Wintersteiner) in the pineal region, negative germ cell markers, and leptomeningeal drop metastases is pineoblastoma, a WHO grade 4 embryonal tumor that disseminates through CSF—hence the need for craniospinal staging and craniospinal irradiation plus chemotherapy. Pineocytoma (choice 0) is a well-differentiated, indolent tumor without drop metastases. Germinoma (choice 1) is radiosensitive but usually shows mildly elevated beta-hCG and lacks an embryonal small-round-cell histology with rosettes. Tectal pilocytic astrocytoma (choice 3) is low-grade, indolent, and does not seed the neuraxis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Considering the histology and the spinal drop metastases, which staging and treatment principle is most appropriate?
options: Local resection alone is curative; no further imaging needed
Craniospinal MRI and CSF cytology are required, and treatment includes craniospinal irradiation plus chemotherapy
Focal radiation to the pineal region only, sparing the spine
Chemotherapy alone with no radiation given the patient's age
stages.explanation: Pineoblastoma readily seeds the neuraxis, so complete staging requires craniospinal MRI and CSF cytology; given confirmed dissemination, therapy combines maximal safe resection, craniospinal irradiation, and multiagent chemotherapy. Local resection alone (choice 0) ignores the high metastatic potential and is not curative. Focal pineal radiation sparing the spine (choice 2) would undertreat documented drop metastases. Chemotherapy alone (choice 3) is insufficient for cure in pineoblastoma; radiation is integral, though strategies are modified to limit toxicity in very young children—this 10-year-old can receive craniospinal irradiation.

cases.diagnosis: Pineoblastoma with Parinaud syndrome, obstructive hydrocephalus, and CSF dissemination
pearls: Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome—upgaze palsy, light-near dissociation, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and lid retraction—signals a tectal/pineal-region lesion.
Pineal masses compress the cerebral aqueduct, causing obstructive hydrocephalus; treat with endoscopic third ventriculostomy (often with biopsy), never lumbar puncture.
Always check beta-hCG and AFP in pineal-region tumors to distinguish radiosensitive germ cell tumors from pineal parenchymal tumors.
Pineoblastoma is a WHO grade 4 embryonal tumor that seeds CSF—require craniospinal staging and treat with craniospinal irradiation plus chemotherapy.

cases.title: A Teen With Excessive Thirst and Trouble Looking Up
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports months of excessive thirst and frequent urination, with recent difficulty looking upward and double vision.
cases.history: A 16-year-old boy presents to the outpatient clinic with his mother for evaluation of four months of intense thirst and frequent urination, including waking multiple times nightly to drink and void. He drinks several liters of cold water daily. In the last six weeks he has developed double vision when looking up and intermittent headaches. He denies polyphagia, weight gain, fever, or recent illness. His past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He is a sophomore in high school with previously good grades, now slipping. He denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use and is not sexually active. Family history is negative for diabetes mellitus or endocrine disease. Review of systems is notable for fatigue and decreased growth velocity over the past year, with no axillary or pubic hair changes recently. His parents report his thirst is not satisfied by sugary drinks specifically and that his urine is consistently pale and dilute.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, appropriately developed adolescent boy. Pupils are mid-sized with light-near dissociation; there is impaired upgaze with convergence-retraction nystagmus and mild bilateral lid retraction. Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally and fundoscopy shows flat optic discs without papilledema. Remaining cranial nerves are intact. Strength, sensation, reflexes, and coordination are normal, and gait is steady. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. Thyroid is normal; there is no gynecomastia. Tanner stage is appropriate for age. Skin is without café-au-lait macules. There is no neck stiffness and no focal weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His polyuria with dilute urine, intense thirst, and nocturia—unrelieved by sugary drinks—most likely reflects which underlying disturbance?
options: Diabetes mellitus with osmotic diuresis
Central diabetes insipidus from hypothalamic-pituitary involvement
Primary polydipsia (psychogenic)
Renal tubular acidosis
stages.explanation: Persistent polyuria with consistently dilute, pale urine, intense thirst, and nocturia in an adolescent with new neuro-ophthalmologic signs strongly suggests central diabetes insipidus from a hypothalamic-pituitary lesion impairing ADH secretion. Diabetes mellitus (choice 0) causes osmotic diuresis but urine would contain glucose and the patient would typically have hyperglycemia and weight loss with polyphagia. Primary polydipsia (choice 2) is a diagnosis of exclusion and would not explain the upgaze palsy or growth deceleration. Renal tubular acidosis (choice 3) causes metabolic acidosis and electrolyte disturbances, not isolated dilute polyuria with neurologic signs.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The combination of central diabetes insipidus plus Parinaud syndrome in a 16-year-old boy should raise concern for a lesion in which two regions?
options: Cerebellum and fourth ventricle
Suprasellar/hypothalamic region and the pineal/dorsal midbrain region
Temporal lobe and hippocampus
Frontal lobe and corpus callosum
stages.explanation: Central DI implicates the suprasellar/hypothalamic-pituitary axis, while Parinaud syndrome implicates the pineal/dorsal midbrain. Synchronous lesions in both the suprasellar and pineal regions ('bifocal' midline tumors) are a hallmark of intracranial germ cell tumors, classically germinoma, in adolescents. Cerebellum/fourth ventricle (choice 0) would cause ataxia and fourth-ventricular hydrocephalus, not DI. Temporal lobe/hippocampus (choice 2) would cause seizures or memory problems. Frontal lobe/corpus callosum (choice 3) would cause behavioral or gait changes, not this midline endocrine-plus-tectal picture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum Na 146 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 302 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg
Simultaneous urine specific gravity 1.003
Water deprivation followed by desmopressin: urine osmolality rises to 480 mOsm/kg (responds to DDAVP)
Serum beta-hCG: 38 mIU/mL (elevated)
Serum AFP: 3 ng/mL (normal)
MRI brain: enhancing suprasellar mass with loss of the posterior pituitary bright spot AND a separate enhancing pineal-region mass; mild aqueductal narrowing
CSF beta-hCG: mildly elevated; CSF AFP normal

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Imaging shows bifocal suprasellar and pineal masses; serum and CSF beta-hCG are mildly elevated with normal AFP. Which tumor type and key biological feature best fit?
options: Non-germinomatous germ cell tumor — markedly elevated AFP requiring chemotherapy before resection
Germinoma — modestly elevated beta-hCG with normal AFP and exquisite radiosensitivity
Craniopharyngioma — calcified cystic mass that is radioresistant
Pineoblastoma — embryonal tumor without tumor-marker elevation
stages.explanation: A bifocal midline tumor with modestly elevated beta-hCG and normal AFP is characteristic of intracranial germinoma; germinomas may secrete low-level beta-hCG (from syncytiotrophoblastic cells) but not AFP, and they are exquisitely radiosensitive and chemosensitive with excellent cure rates. Markedly elevated AFP (choice 0) defines an NGGCT (yolk sac/embryonal carcinoma component), which behaves more aggressively. Craniopharyngioma (choice 2) is a calcified suprasellar cystic mass without marker elevation and is not bifocal. Pineoblastoma (choice 3) is an embryonal pineal tumor without germ cell marker elevation and would not explain the suprasellar mass with DI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Markers confirm a germinoma. Regarding his diabetes insipidus and definitive therapy, which statement is most accurate?
options: Diabetes insipidus will resolve once chemotherapy begins, so no replacement is needed
He requires desmopressin for central DI, and the germinoma is treated primarily with radiation (often reduced-dose with chemotherapy), not upfront resection
Gross total surgical resection is the curative first-line treatment
Fluid restriction is the mainstay of managing his polyuria
stages.explanation: Central DI from posterior pituitary/hypothalamic involvement requires desmopressin (DDAVP) replacement and rarely recovers; meanwhile germinoma is managed primarily with radiotherapy—classically craniospinal or whole-ventricular irradiation, frequently combined with chemotherapy to allow dose reduction—achieving high cure rates without the need for upfront resection. Expecting DI to resolve with chemotherapy (choice 0) is incorrect. Gross total resection (choice 2) is not first-line for radiosensitive germinoma. Fluid restriction (choice 3) is dangerous in central DI, risking hypernatremia and dehydration; the problem is ADH deficiency, not water excess.

cases.diagnosis: Pineal/suprasellar germinoma presenting with central diabetes insipidus and Parinaud syndrome
pearls: A bifocal suprasellar-plus-pineal midline tumor in an adolescent is classic for intracranial germinoma.
Germinomas may secrete low-level beta-hCG but not AFP; markedly elevated AFP indicates a non-germinomatous germ cell tumor.
Central diabetes insipidus from posterior pituitary involvement requires desmopressin and usually does not recover with tumor treatment.
Germinomas are exquisitely radiosensitive and chemosensitive—treated primarily with radiation (often reduced-dose plus chemotherapy) rather than upfront resection.

cases.title: A Teenager With Headaches, Thirst, and Falling Grades
cases.chiefComplaint: He was admitted for worsening headaches, excessive urination and thirst, and new vision changes.
cases.history: A 14-year-old boy is admitted to the pediatric ward for three months of progressive headaches, marked polyuria and polydipsia, fatigue, and recently noted blurred vision and difficulty looking upward. His parents add that he has become withdrawn and his grades have declined sharply. He has had no fevers, seizures, or trauma. He vomited twice this morning. Past medical history is unremarkable, with no medications and no known allergies. He is in eighth grade, denies substance use, and is not sexually active. Family history is negative for cancer or endocrine disorders. On review of systems, he reports new cold intolerance and decreased energy, and his mother notes he seems to have stopped growing over the past year. He drinks large volumes of water and wakes nightly to urinate, with urine described as very pale. There is no history of head injury. He has had no rash or joint complaints.
cases.exam: Tired-appearing adolescent boy. Pupils show light-near dissociation; upgaze is impaired with convergence-retraction nystagmus and lid retraction. Visual fields show bitemporal defects; visual acuity is mildly reduced bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals early papilledema. Other cranial nerves are intact. Strength, tone, reflexes, and coordination are normal; gait is steady. He appears prepubertal for age (Tanner 2). Thyroid is normal to palpation. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There are no café-au-lait macules. No nuchal rigidity is present. Abdomen is benign without masses.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given polyuria/polydipsia, bitemporal field loss, Parinaud signs, and delayed puberty, which initial diagnostic battery is most appropriate?
