Whole-brain radiotherapy alone without chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Grade 3 IDH-mutant astrocytoma is treated after maximal safe resection with radiotherapy followed by adjuvant alkylating chemotherapy (temozolomide, or PCV in some protocols), which improves progression-free and overall survival versus radiation alone. Observation is inappropriate for a grade 3 malignancy. Bevacizumab is an anti-angiogenic agent used for recurrent high-grade glioma symptom control, not first-line adjuvant therapy. Whole-brain radiation alone is outdated and inferior; focal radiation combined with chemotherapy is standard, and chemotherapy is essential for the survival benefit in these tumors.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient asks how her IDH-mutant status affects prognosis compared with an IDH-wildtype tumor. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: IDH status has no effect on prognosis
IDH-mutant tumors carry a markedly better prognosis with longer median survival than IDH-wildtype tumors of similar grade
IDH-mutant tumors have a worse prognosis than IDH-wildtype tumors
IDH-mutant tumors are benign and require no treatment
stages.explanation: IDH-mutant gliomas have a markedly more favorable prognosis and substantially longer median survival than IDH-wildtype tumors of comparable grade, which is why molecular testing reshaped glioma classification. Saying IDH status has no prognostic effect contradicts the central role of this biomarker. Claiming IDH-mutant tumors fare worse reverses the established biology. Calling them benign is dangerously inaccurate: they are still malignant, infiltrative tumors that require resection, radiation, and chemotherapy, though their course is more indolent than IDH-wildtype disease.

cases.diagnosis: Anaplastic astrocytoma (astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, WHO grade 3).
pearls: IDH mutation status is the single most important prognostic and treatment-defining marker in adult diffuse gliomas.
Grade 3 astrocytoma shows mitotic activity but lacks the necrosis/microvascular proliferation that define grade 4.
1p/19q codeletion defines oligodendroglioma; its absence with ATRX loss points to astrocytoma.
Standard therapy for grade 3 IDH-mutant astrocytoma is maximal safe resection, then radiotherapy plus adjuvant alkylating chemotherapy.

cases.title: A 59-Year-Old Man with Worsening Headaches One Year After a Brain Tumor
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive headaches, right arm weakness, and fatigue eleven months after treatment for a brain tumor.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man returns to oncology clinic with two weeks of worsening headaches, increasing right arm weakness, and fatigue. Eleven months ago he underwent gross total resection of a left frontal glioblastoma followed by concurrent chemoradiation and adjuvant temozolomide, which he completed three months ago. His most recent surveillance MRI four weeks earlier showed a new enhancing area at the resection margin. Past medical history includes the glioblastoma, hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily, and reflux on omeprazole 20 mg daily. He takes levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily for tumor-related seizures and a dexamethasone taper. No known drug allergies. He is a retired teacher, married with supportive family, a never-smoker, and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. His ECOG performance status has declined from 0 to 1 over a month. He is alert and asks frankly about 'what comes next,' expressing a wish to maintain quality of life and remain functional at home as long as possible.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and fully oriented with mildly slowed processing. Speech is fluent. Cranial nerves are intact without papilledema. Motor exam shows 4/5 weakness of the right upper extremity with a right pronator drift; right lower extremity is 5-/5. Tone is mildly increased on the right. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are brisker on the right with an equivocal right plantar response. Coordination is limited on the right by weakness. Gait is mildly slow but independent. There is a well-healed left frontal craniotomy scar. No neck stiffness, no new visual field cut on confrontation, and no signs of infection at the surgical site.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A new enhancing area at the resection margin within months of completing chemoradiation could represent true tumor recurrence or treatment-related pseudoprogression/radiation necrosis. Which imaging approach best distinguishes them?
options: Repeat non-contrast CT head
Advanced MRI techniques such as perfusion (rCBV) and/or MR spectroscopy, with or without amino-acid PET
Plain skull radiographs
Carotid angiography
stages.explanation: Advanced MRI with perfusion (recurrent tumor shows elevated rCBV) and MR spectroscopy (elevated choline/creatine in tumor), often supplemented by amino-acid PET, best differentiates true progression from pseudoprogression/radiation necrosis, which typically show low perfusion. Non-contrast CT cannot characterize enhancement physiology and is insensitive here. Skull radiographs provide no useful information about the parenchymal lesion. Carotid angiography evaluates vasculature, not tumor versus treatment effect, and carries unnecessary risk in this setting.

stages.label: ADVANCED IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with perfusion and spectroscopy:
Enlarging nodular enhancement at the posterior resection margin, now 2.4 cm (previously 1.1 cm)
MR perfusion: elevated relative cerebral blood volume within the enhancing region
MR spectroscopy: elevated choline/NAA ratio
Increased surrounding T2/FLAIR edema with mild midline shift
Findings most consistent with true tumor progression rather than treatment effect

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the enlarging, high-perfusion, choline-elevated enhancing lesion with new neurologic decline, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Radiation necrosis (treatment effect)
Recurrent (progressive) glioblastoma
Postoperative gliosis without active tumor
Cerebral abscess at the surgical site
stages.explanation: An enlarging enhancing lesion with elevated rCBV and choline, plus worsening neurologic deficits, indicates recurrent/progressive glioblastoma rather than treatment effect. Radiation necrosis typically shows low perfusion and a more 'soap-bubble/Swiss-cheese' enhancement and would not show high choline and rising rCBV. Postoperative gliosis does not enlarge or demonstrate hyperperfusion. A cerebral abscess would usually present with rim enhancement and restricted diffusion centrally, often with fever or surgical-site signs, none of which are present, and the perfusion/spectroscopy pattern here fits tumor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient has symptomatic recurrent GBM with vasogenic edema and declining function but wishes to preserve quality of life. Regarding bevacizumab, which statement best informs shared decision-making?
options: Bevacizumab reliably cures recurrent glioblastoma and should be started for a survival benefit
Bevacizumab can improve symptoms and reduce steroid dependence and edema, improving progression-free survival, but has not demonstrated a clear overall survival benefit in recurrent GBM
Bevacizumab is contraindicated in all brain tumors due to bleeding risk
Bevacizumab is equivalent to repeat gross total resection for all recurrences
stages.explanation: Bevacizumab, an anti-VEGF antibody, can palliate symptoms by reducing vasogenic edema and steroid requirements and improves progression-free survival in recurrent GBM, but randomized data have not shown a clear overall survival benefit, making it most useful for symptom control and quality of life. It is not curative. It is not absolutely contraindicated in brain tumors; intracranial hemorrhage risk is low in practice, though wound-healing and thrombotic risks warrant caution. It is not equivalent to repeat resection, which is considered separately when feasible and beneficial; the two are different modalities with different roles.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient is started on bevacizumab. Which monitoring/counseling point is most important during therapy?
options: Monitor for hypertension, proteinuria, impaired wound healing, thromboembolism, and bleeding/perforation
Routine weekly bone marrow biopsies are required
Discontinue all antiseizure medication once bevacizumab starts
Bevacizumab eliminates the need for any further imaging surveillance
stages.explanation: Bevacizumab's class-specific toxicities include hypertension, proteinuria, impaired wound healing, arterial/venous thromboembolism, and bleeding or GI perforation, so blood pressure, urine protein, and clinical vigilance for these events are essential. Weekly bone marrow biopsies are unnecessary; bevacizumab is not primarily myelosuppressive. Antiseizure medication should be continued in a patient with tumor-related seizures, not stopped. Imaging surveillance remains necessary; bevacizumab can normalize enhancement (pseudoresponse) without true tumor control, so ongoing monitoring, including non-enhancing/FLAIR progression, is even more important.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Alongside disease-directed therapy, what additional step best supports this patient's stated goals of maintaining function and quality of life?
options: Defer all goals-of-care discussions until he becomes nonverbal
Early integration of palliative care and advance care planning
Enroll him in a phase I trial without discussing prognosis
Escalate dexamethasone indefinitely to high doses regardless of side effects
stages.explanation: Early palliative care integration alongside oncologic treatment improves quality of life and symptom control and supports advance care planning aligned with his stated goals, and is recommended for high-grade glioma. Delaying goals-of-care discussions until he loses capacity removes his voice from decisions and is inappropriate. Enrolling in a trial without honest prognostic discussion violates informed consent and his expressed wishes. Indefinite high-dose dexamethasone causes myopathy, hyperglycemia, infection, and other harms; steroids should be used at the lowest effective dose, with bevacizumab often allowing tapering.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent (progressive) glioblastoma, managed with bevacizumab for symptom control and early palliative care integration.
pearls: Distinguish true recurrence from pseudoprogression/radiation necrosis using perfusion (rCBV) and spectroscopy, sometimes amino-acid PET; tumor shows high perfusion and choline.
Bevacizumab improves progression-free survival and palliates edema/steroid need in recurrent GBM but lacks a proven overall survival benefit.
Watch bevacizumab toxicities: hypertension, proteinuria, poor wound healing, thromboembolism, and bleeding/perforation.
Integrate palliative care early in high-grade glioma to support quality of life and goal-concordant care.

cases.title: A 55-Year-Old Man with an Incidental Fourth Ventricle Mass
cases.chiefComplaint: An asymptomatic posterior fossa mass found on MRI ordered for unrelated dizziness.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is referred to neuro-oncology after an MRI obtained for a few weeks of mild positional dizziness incidentally revealed a small mass in the fourth ventricle. He has no headaches, nausea, gait disturbance, double vision, or swallowing difficulty, and the dizziness has since resolved. He denies prior neurologic symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on rosuvastatin 10 mg nightly. He had an appendectomy in his twenties. No known drug allergies. He works as an insurance adjuster, is a never-smoker, drinks one or two beers on weekends, and denies illicit drugs. Family history is notable for hypertension and a brother with colon polyps; no known CNS tumors or genetic syndromes. He is highly anxious after reading about brain tumors online and wants to know whether he needs urgent surgery. He has brought the disc with his imaging and is eager to understand whether this lesion is dangerous and what monitoring or treatment, if any, is appropriate.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, fully oriented, and in no distress. Cranial nerves are intact, including normal extraocular movements without nystagmus, intact facial sensation and strength, normal palatal elevation, and a symmetric gag. Fundi are normal without papilledema. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with normal tone and no drift. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses are flexor. Coordination is intact with normal finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin; there is no dysmetria. Gait and tandem gait are normal, and Romberg is negative. There are no signs of raised intracranial pressure and no cerebellar or brainstem deficits on a careful exam.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Well-circumscribed 1.6 cm intraventricular mass in the inferior fourth ventricle
T1 iso- to hypointense, T2 hyperintense, no diffusion restriction
Little to no contrast enhancement
No hydrocephalus; fourth ventricular outflow preserved
No surrounding edema, no invasion of the brainstem
No leptomeningeal disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A well-circumscribed, non-enhancing or minimally enhancing fourth-ventricular mass in a middle-aged adult without edema or hydrocephalus is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Ependymoma (WHO grade 2/3)
Subependymoma (WHO grade 1)
Medulloblastoma
Choroid plexus carcinoma
stages.explanation: A subependymoma is a benign, slow-growing WHO grade 1 tumor that classically arises in the fourth ventricle (or lateral ventricles) in middle-aged or older adults, is well-circumscribed, shows little or no enhancement, and is frequently incidental. Ependymoma tends to enhance more, can cause hydrocephalus, and often presents symptomatically in younger patients. Medulloblastoma is an aggressive, enhancing, restricted-diffusion posterior fossa tumor of children and young adults, not an incidental indolent adult mass. Choroid plexus carcinoma is a rare, aggressive, avidly enhancing pediatric tumor and does not fit this benign, non-enhancing, asymptomatic adult lesion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a benign, slow-growing tumor that may be safe to observe rather than resect immediately?
options: Marked contrast enhancement and restricted diffusion
Absence of enhancement, absence of hydrocephalus, sharp margins, and no neurologic deficits
Extensive peritumoral edema
Rapid interval growth on prior imaging
stages.explanation: Lack of enhancement, no hydrocephalus, sharp margins, and a normal neurologic exam all point to an indolent, benign lesion (subependymoma) that can often be observed with serial imaging. Marked enhancement and restricted diffusion suggest a more aggressive, cellular tumor that would warrant intervention. Extensive peritumoral edema indicates an infiltrative or higher-grade process, again favoring treatment. Rapid interval growth would be a clear indication to intervene; its absence (a stable, slow lesion) is precisely what supports conservative management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this asymptomatic patient with an incidental fourth-ventricular subependymoma, no hydrocephalus, and a normal exam, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate gross total resection of the tumor
Conservative management with serial MRI surveillance
Stereotactic radiosurgery to the lesion
Empiric chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic, incidentally discovered subependymomas without hydrocephalus or mass effect are appropriately managed with serial MRI surveillance, given their benign, slow-growing nature; surgery is reserved for symptomatic or growing lesions. Immediate resection of a fourth-ventricular lesion carries real risk to the brainstem and cranial nerves and is unjustified for an asymptomatic grade 1 tumor. Radiosurgery is not indicated for an indolent, benign, asymptomatic lesion and adds risk without clear benefit. Chemotherapy has no role in a WHO grade 1 subependymoma, which is not chemosensitive and does not require systemic therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What finding on follow-up would most clearly change the plan from observation to surgical resection?
options: Stable tumor size on annual MRI
Development of obstructive hydrocephalus or new brainstem/cerebellar symptoms
The patient's ongoing anxiety about having a brain lesion
An incidental small unrelated meningioma elsewhere
stages.explanation: New obstructive hydrocephalus or new brainstem/cerebellar symptoms (gait change, cranial neuropathy, intractable headache) indicate the tumor is now causing harm and warrant surgical resection. Stable size on surveillance supports continued observation, not surgery. Patient anxiety, while important to address with reassurance and counseling, is not by itself an indication to operate on a benign, asymptomatic lesion in a high-risk location. An unrelated incidental meningioma is managed on its own merits and does not change the indication for resecting the subependymoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this subependymoma did eventually require surgery and was gross-totally resected, what is the expected outcome and the role of adjuvant therapy?
options: It will recur rapidly and require chemoradiation
Gross total resection is typically curative; adjuvant radiation or chemotherapy is generally not required for a WHO grade 1 subependymoma
Lifelong temozolomide is mandatory after any resection
Whole-brain radiation is standard after resection
stages.explanation: Subependymoma is WHO grade 1 and gross total resection is typically curative, so adjuvant radiation or chemotherapy is generally unnecessary; surveillance imaging suffices. Rapid recurrence requiring chemoradiation describes a high-grade tumor, not this indolent benign lesion. Lifelong temozolomide has no role in a grade 1 tumor that is not chemosensitive. Whole-brain radiation is not standard and would expose the patient to needless toxicity for a benign, fully resected tumor; it is reserved for far more aggressive disease.

cases.diagnosis: Incidental fourth ventricular subependymoma (WHO grade 1), managed conservatively with surveillance.
pearls: Subependymoma is a benign WHO grade 1 tumor, often incidental, classically in the fourth or lateral ventricles of middle-aged/older adults.
Imaging hallmarks: well-circumscribed, little or no enhancement, no edema, often no hydrocephalus.
Asymptomatic subependymomas without hydrocephalus can be observed with serial MRI; surgery is reserved for symptoms or growth.
Resection is curative when needed, but the benign natural history favors avoiding risky fourth-ventricular surgery in asymptomatic patients.

cases.title: A 17-Year-Old with Recurrent Seizures Despite Multiple Medications
cases.chiefComplaint: Frequent focal seizures with altered awareness despite three antiseizure medications.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy is referred to the epilepsy clinic for drug-resistant seizures. Since age 12 he has had stereotyped episodes of a rising epigastric sensation, an odd familiar feeling, lip-smacking, and brief unresponsiveness lasting about a minute, occurring several times weekly. He has tried levetiracetam, lamotrigine, and oxcarbazepine at adequate doses without sustained control. He has no developmental delay, normal school performance, and no significant head trauma or CNS infection. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Current medications are lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily and oxcarbazepine 600 mg twice daily. No known drug allergies. He lives with both parents, is a high-school junior, denies alcohol or drugs, and plays soccer. Family history is negative for epilepsy or brain tumors. He has not been able to obtain a driver's license because of his seizures, which is a major concern for him and his family. A high-resolution epilepsy-protocol MRI was obtained, showing a cortically based lesion in the temporal lobe, prompting referral for surgical evaluation.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, fully oriented, and interactive with normal affect and fluent speech. Cranial nerves are intact, including full visual fields to confrontation. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with normal tone and no drift. Sensation is intact to all modalities. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with flexor plantar responses. Coordination is normal, and gait including tandem is normal. Memory and language testing at the bedside are normal. There are no neurocutaneous stigmata such as ash-leaf spots or cafe-au-lait macules. The interictal neurologic examination is entirely normal, consistent with a focal epileptogenic lesion rather than a diffuse or progressive process.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His seizures (rising epigastric aura, deja vu, automatisms, brief impaired awareness) localize to which region, guiding the imaging and surgical workup?
options: Occipital lobe
Mesial temporal lobe
Primary motor cortex
Cerebellum
stages.explanation: A rising epigastric aura, deja vu, oroalimentary automatisms (lip-smacking), and brief impaired awareness are classic features of focal seizures arising from the mesial temporal lobe, directing the epilepsy-protocol MRI and surgical evaluation toward that region. Occipital seizures present with visual phenomena such as flashing lights or scotomata, not epigastric/psychic auras. Primary motor cortex seizures cause focal clonic motor activity, often with a Jacksonian march, not automatisms with autonomic auras. The cerebellum coordinates movement and is not an epileptogenic cortical region; it does not generate these semiologies.

stages.label: EPILEPSY MRI
stages.content:
Epilepsy-protocol MRI:
Cortically based, wedge-shaped lesion in the left temporal neocortex
Multicystic 'bubbly' multinodular appearance with a soap-bubble pattern
T2/FLAIR hyperintense with a partial FLAIR-bright rim
Little or no contrast enhancement
No surrounding vasogenic edema, no mass effect
Mild remodeling of the overlying inner skull table

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A cortically based, 'bubbly' multicystic, minimally enhancing temporal lesion without edema in a young patient with chronic drug-resistant focal epilepsy is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma
Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET)
Herpes encephalitis
Cavernous malformation
stages.explanation: A dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor is a benign (WHO grade 1) cortically based 'bubbly' multicystic lesion that classically presents as long-standing, drug-resistant partial epilepsy in children and young adults, with little enhancement and no edema. Glioblastoma is an aggressive, enhancing, necrotic, edema-rich tumor of older adults, not an indolent epileptogenic cortical lesion. Herpes encephalitis causes acute febrile encephalopathy with temporal edema and restricted diffusion, not chronic stable epilepsy with a bubbly cortical mass. A cavernous malformation shows a 'popcorn' appearance with a hemosiderin rim and blooming on gradient-echo, not a multicystic bubbly cortical pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this young man with drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy and a DNET concordant with his seizure focus, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Add a fourth antiseizure medication and continue medical therapy indefinitely
Surgical resection of the lesion (lesionectomy/tailored resection), which can both control seizures and provide diagnosis
Whole-brain radiation therapy
Systemic chemotherapy with temozolomide
stages.explanation: Surgical resection is the treatment of choice for a DNET causing drug-resistant epilepsy: lesionectomy or a tailored resection often achieves excellent, frequently seizure-free outcomes and confirms the diagnosis, given the benign grade 1 nature of the tumor. Adding a fourth medication after failing three appropriate agents defines drug resistance and is unlikely to help; surgery should be considered. Whole-brain radiation is inappropriate and harmful for a benign focal lesion. Chemotherapy has no role in a WHO grade 1 DNET, which is not a chemosensitive malignancy and does not require systemic therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most accurate prognostic counseling after complete resection of a DNET?
options: The tumor is malignant and will recur aggressively requiring chemoradiation
DNET is benign (WHO grade 1) with an excellent prognosis; complete resection often yields long-term seizure freedom and recurrence is uncommon
Seizures will certainly persist regardless of surgery
Lifelong high-dose steroids are required after resection
stages.explanation: DNET is a WHO grade 1 benign tumor with an excellent prognosis; gross total resection frequently produces long-term seizure freedom and recurrence is uncommon, so adjuvant therapy is generally unnecessary. It is not a malignant tumor and does not require chemoradiation. While not every patient becomes completely seizure-free, outcomes after resection of a concordant DNET are generally very good, so categorically predicting persistent seizures is inaccurate. Lifelong high-dose steroids are not part of management and would cause unnecessary harm.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before surgery, his neurosurgeon discusses minimizing risk to memory and language. Which preoperative step best reduces the chance of a postoperative deficit in dominant temporal lobe surgery?
options: Proceed directly to a standard anterior temporal lobectomy without further testing
Obtain functional/lateralization studies (e.g., neuropsychological testing and language/memory lateralization such as fMRI or Wada) and consider intraoperative mapping
Order a whole-body PET-CT to plan the resection
Start prophylactic chemotherapy to shrink the lesion first
stages.explanation: In dominant-hemisphere temporal surgery, preoperative neuropsychological testing plus language/memory lateralization (fMRI or the Wada test) and consideration of intraoperative mapping help tailor the resection and reduce the risk of memory or language deficits. Proceeding to a standard lobectomy without functional assessment increases the risk of an avoidable deficit. Whole-body PET-CT is irrelevant for a primary CNS grade 1 tumor that does not metastasize systemically. Prophylactic chemotherapy has no role in a benign DNET and would not facilitate safe resection.

cases.diagnosis: Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET) causing drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy.
pearls: DNET is a benign WHO grade 1 cortically based tumor presenting as chronic, drug-resistant partial epilepsy in young patients.
Classic imaging: cortical 'bubbly' multicystic lesion, T2/FLAIR bright, minimal enhancement, no edema.
Failure of two or more appropriate antiseizure medications defines drug-resistant epilepsy and should prompt surgical evaluation.
Resection of a DNET concordant with the seizure focus often achieves seizure freedom with an excellent overall prognosis.

cases.title: A 19-Year-Old Woman with Years of Spells of Deja Vu and Confusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Several years of recurrent brief spells of deja vu, an odd smell, and momentary confusion.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student is evaluated for several years of recurrent brief spells consisting of a sudden sense of deja vu, an unpleasant odd smell, and a few seconds of staring with momentary confusion, after which she is briefly disoriented. The spells were initially infrequent but have become more noticeable, occurring a few times per month, and once progressed to a generalized convulsion at a party. She has no developmental delay and is an honors student. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and a daily multivitamin. No known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol occasionally at social events and denies tobacco and illicit drugs. Family history is negative for epilepsy or brain tumors. She is worried about how seizures might affect school and driving. Her primary physician ordered a brain MRI after the convulsion, which demonstrated a cystic temporal lesion with an enhancing mural nodule, and she was referred for further evaluation and management of a probable low-grade tumor.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, fully oriented, and pleasant with normal affect and fluent speech. Cranial nerves are intact, including full visual fields and normal fundi. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with normal tone and no drift. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with flexor plantar responses. Coordination is normal, and gait including tandem is normal. Bedside cognitive and memory testing are normal. There are no neurocutaneous findings. The interictal neurologic examination is normal, consistent with a focal epileptogenic temporal lesion rather than a diffuse or rapidly progressive disease process.

stages.label: BRAIN MRI
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Right medial temporal cystic lesion, 2.8 cm, with a solid mural nodule
The mural nodule shows avid enhancement; the cyst wall enhances minimally
T2/FLAIR hyperintense cyst with mild adjacent signal change
Little surrounding edema, mild mass effect
No necrosis, no leptomeningeal disease
Cortically based location involving gray matter

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A cortically based temporal cystic lesion with an enhancing mural nodule in a young patient with chronic temporal lobe seizures is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma
Ganglioglioma
Metastatic carcinoma
Multiple sclerosis plaque
stages.explanation: A ganglioglioma is a low-grade (usually WHO grade 1) glioneuronal tumor that classically presents as a cortically based, cyst-with-enhancing-mural-nodule lesion in the temporal lobe of children and young adults with chronic seizures, and carries an excellent prognosis. Glioblastoma is an aggressive necrotic tumor of older adults, inconsistent with this indolent multi-year seizure history and benign imaging. Metastatic carcinoma is unlikely in a healthy 19-year-old without a known primary and typically shows multiple lesions with marked edema. A multiple sclerosis plaque is a white-matter demyelinating lesion, not a cortically based cystic mass with a mural nodule causing seizures.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond MRI, which test best characterizes her seizures and helps confirm the epileptogenic focus before surgery?
options: Electroencephalogram (EEG)
Lumbar puncture for cytology
Whole-body PET-CT for staging
Genetic testing for a hereditary cancer syndrome
stages.explanation: An EEG characterizes the seizure type and helps confirm and localize the epileptogenic focus, important for surgical planning when a concordant lesion is present. Lumbar puncture for cytology is not indicated for a typical low-grade cortically based glioneuronal tumor without leptomeningeal disease and adds risk without benefit here. Whole-body PET-CT for staging is unnecessary because ganglioglioma is a primary CNS tumor that does not metastasize systemically. Genetic testing for a hereditary cancer syndrome is not routinely indicated for a sporadic ganglioglioma in the absence of a suggestive family or personal history.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this young woman's ganglioglioma causing recurrent seizures?
options: Whole-brain radiation therapy
Gross total surgical resection
Lifelong escalation of antiseizure medications without surgery
Systemic chemotherapy with temozolomide
stages.explanation: Gross total surgical resection is the treatment of choice for ganglioglioma; it is often curative for this low-grade tumor and provides excellent seizure control, especially when the lesion is concordant with the epileptogenic focus. Whole-brain radiation is inappropriate for a benign, well-circumscribed, surgically accessible tumor and risks long-term cognitive harm in a young patient. Indefinite medication escalation alone misses the opportunity for a potentially curative resection that also addresses seizures. Chemotherapy has no role in a typical WHO grade 1 ganglioglioma, which is not a chemosensitive high-grade malignancy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should you counsel her about prognosis after complete resection of a ganglioglioma?
options: The tumor is highly aggressive with poor survival
Ganglioglioma is typically benign (WHO grade 1) with an excellent prognosis and high rates of seizure control after gross total resection
Recurrence is inevitable within a year
She will require lifelong chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Ganglioglioma is usually a WHO grade 1 tumor with an excellent prognosis; gross total resection yields high long-term survival and good seizure control, and recurrence is uncommon. Describing it as highly aggressive with poor survival is inaccurate and would cause unwarranted distress. Recurrence within a year is not expected after complete resection of a typical grade 1 ganglioglioma. Lifelong chemotherapy is not indicated for this benign tumor; adjuvant therapy is generally reserved for the rare anaplastic (higher-grade) variants or incomplete resection with progression.

cases.diagnosis: Ganglioglioma (low-grade glioneuronal tumor) presenting with chronic temporal lobe seizures.
pearls: Ganglioglioma is a low-grade glioneuronal tumor; classic look is a cortical cyst with an enhancing mural nodule in the temporal lobe.
It commonly presents with chronic, often drug-resistant seizures in children and young adults.
Gross total resection is usually curative and provides excellent seizure control; recurrence is uncommon.
Most gangliogliomas are WHO grade 1 with an excellent prognosis; chemoradiation is reserved for rare anaplastic variants.

cases.title: A 21-Year-Old Man with New Seizures and a Surface Brain Lesion
cases.chiefComplaint: A first seizure and a superficial temporal brain lesion seen on MRI.
cases.history: A 21-year-old man presents after a witnessed first-time generalized seizure preceded by an unusual rising sensation. He recalls a few months of occasional brief 'spacing-out' episodes that he ignored. He denies headaches, weakness, or visual changes. Past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. After the seizure he was started on levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily. No known drug allergies. He is a college student studying engineering, drinks alcohol occasionally, denies tobacco and recreational drugs, and exercises regularly. Family history is negative for brain tumors or epilepsy, with no neurocutaneous syndromes. An MRI obtained after the seizure showed a superficial, cortically based temporal lesion abutting the leptomeninges, raising concern for a low- to intermediate-grade tumor, and he was referred to neuro-oncology. He is otherwise healthy and high-functioning, asking detailed questions about what kind of tumor this might be, whether surgery is needed, and what targeted treatment options could exist if it is a specific molecular subtype.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, fully oriented, and conversant with normal affect and fluent speech. Cranial nerves are intact with full visual fields and normal fundi. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with normal tone and no drift. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with flexor plantar responses. Coordination is intact, and gait including tandem is normal. Bedside cognition is normal. There are no neurocutaneous stigmata. The interictal neurologic examination is normal, consistent with a focal cortically based lesion. There are no signs of raised intracranial pressure and no focal deficits to suggest deep or eloquent involvement on this initial assessment.

stages.label: BRAIN MRI
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast:
Superficial cortically based temporal lesion, 3.0 cm, with a solid enhancing component and an associated cyst
A 'dural tail' adjacent enhancement and broad leptomeningeal contact
T2/FLAIR signal change in adjacent cortex, minimal edema
No central necrosis, mild mass effect
Cortical/peripheral location typical of a superficial hemispheric tumor

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A superficial, cortically based temporal tumor with a cyst, enhancing nodule, leptomeningeal contact, and a dural tail in a young adult with seizures is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA)
Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype
Meningioma
Central neurocytoma
stages.explanation: Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma classically arises superficially in the temporal lobe of children and young adults, presents with seizures, and shows a cortically based cyst with an enhancing mural nodule and frequent leptomeningeal contact and a dural tail. Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype is a tumor of older adults with necrosis and marked edema, not this indolent superficial cystic lesion in a 21-year-old. Meningioma is extra-axial and dural-based with a CSF cleft, whereas this is a cortically based intra-axial tumor. Central neurocytoma is an intraventricular tumor near the foramen of Monro, not a peripheral cortical lesion with leptomeningeal contact.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of this superficial temporal tumor?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year
Maximal safe surgical resection with histopathology and molecular testing
Upfront whole-brain radiation
Stereotactic radiosurgery without biopsy
stages.explanation: Maximal safe surgical resection is the standard initial approach; it relieves the seizure-causing lesion, frequently allows gross total resection of a superficial tumor, and provides histology plus molecular testing (notably BRAF V600E) that guide prognosis and any adjuvant or targeted therapy. Observation is inappropriate for a symptomatic, enhancing tumor of uncertain grade. Upfront whole-brain radiation is not indicated without a tissue diagnosis and is excessive for a likely resectable low- to intermediate-grade tumor. Radiosurgery without biopsy forgoes the essential diagnosis and the benefit of resection in a young patient with seizures.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & MOLECULAR
stages.content:
Resection specimen:
Pleomorphic astrocytic tumor with spindle and giant cells, xanthomatous (lipidized) cells, eosinophilic granular bodies, and reticulin deposition
Low mitotic count; no necrosis
BRAF V600E mutation: present
CDKN2A/B: intact
Ki-67: 4%
Integrated diagnosis: pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma, WHO grade 2, BRAF V600E-mutant

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The tumor is BRAF V600E-mutant. What is the significance of this molecular finding for management?
options: It has no clinical relevance
It identifies a potential targeted therapy option with BRAF (and MEK) inhibitors, relevant for residual, recurrent, or higher-grade disease
It mandates immediate whole-brain radiation
It indicates the tumor is a metastasis from melanoma
stages.explanation: BRAF V600E mutations are common in PXA and open the door to targeted therapy with BRAF inhibitors (often combined with MEK inhibitors), which can be valuable for residual, recurrent, or anaplastic disease not controlled by surgery. The mutation is therefore clinically meaningful, not irrelevant. It does not mandate whole-brain radiation; management is driven by extent of resection, grade, and tumor behavior. While BRAF V600E also occurs in melanoma, its presence in a primary cortically based astrocytic tumor with characteristic PXA histology indicates a primary brain tumor, not a melanoma metastasis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After gross total resection of a WHO grade 2 PXA with low Ki-67 and intact CDKN2A/B, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Immediate chemoradiation regardless of resection extent
Surveillance MRI with consideration of further therapy (including BRAF-targeted agents) if there is recurrence or progression
Lifelong bevacizumab
No follow-up imaging is needed
stages.explanation: After gross total resection of a grade 2 PXA with favorable features, surveillance MRI is appropriate, reserving additional therapy, including BRAF/MEK inhibitors or radiation, for recurrence or progression; many patients do well with resection alone. Immediate chemoradiation is not warranted after complete resection of a grade 2 tumor with low proliferation. Lifelong bevacizumab has no role in this setting and carries unnecessary toxicity. Forgoing follow-up imaging is unsafe because PXA can recur or progress (including anaplastic transformation), so ongoing surveillance is essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Three years later, surveillance MRI shows a new enhancing nodule at the resection site; biopsy reveals increased mitoses and necrosis consistent with anaplastic (WHO grade 3) PXA, still BRAF V600E-mutant. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue observation since the original tumor was grade 2
Re-resection if feasible followed by adjuvant therapy, with BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy as an option given the persistent BRAF V600E mutation
Hormonal therapy
Reassure the patient that anaplastic transformation does not affect prognosis
stages.explanation: Anaplastic transformation to grade 3 PXA warrants active treatment: maximal safe re-resection when feasible plus adjuvant therapy (radiation and/or chemotherapy), and the persistent BRAF V600E mutation makes BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy a meaningful option, particularly for unresectable or progressive disease. Continued observation is inappropriate once the tumor has become higher grade and is progressing. Hormonal therapy has no role in PXA. Reassurance that transformation does not affect prognosis is false, since anaplastic (grade 3) PXA behaves more aggressively and carries a worse prognosis than grade 2 disease.

cases.diagnosis: Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA), WHO grade 2, BRAF V600E-mutant.
pearls: PXA is a superficial, cortically based temporal tumor of children/young adults presenting with seizures, often a cyst with an enhancing nodule and leptomeningeal contact.
Histology shows pleomorphic/xanthomatous cells, eosinophilic granular bodies, and reticulin; most are WHO grade 2.
BRAF V600E is frequent in PXA and offers a targeted therapy option (BRAF +/- MEK inhibitors) for residual/recurrent/anaplastic disease.
Gross total resection is the mainstay; surveillance is appropriate, with adjuvant therapy reserved for recurrence, progression, or anaplastic features (and CDKN2A/B loss portends worse behavior).

cases.title: A 2-Year-Old Girl with Vomiting, Unsteadiness, and Eye Crossing
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of vomiting, increasing unsteadiness, and a newly crossed eye.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is admitted for two weeks of intermittent vomiting, increasing unsteadiness with frequent falls, and a recently noticed inward turning of the right eye. Her parents report she has become more irritable and less playful, and her head circumference has crossed percentiles upward at recent visits. There is no fever or recent illness. She was born at term with normal development until this illness, walking by 13 months. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. She lives with her parents and an older sibling, attends daycare, and is up to date on immunizations. Family history is unremarkable, with no known cancer predisposition syndromes. On the ward she is noted to have a bulging anterior fontanelle remnant feel on palpation of the sutures and sun-setting eyes intermittently. An urgent MRI is obtained given the concern for a posterior fossa mass with hydrocephalus, and the oncology and neurosurgery teams are consulted for an aggressive-appearing infant brain tumor.
cases.exam: The toddler is irritable but consolable, with intermittent sun-setting eyes and a right sixth-nerve palsy (esotropia, impaired abduction). The anterior fontanelle region feels full. Fundoscopy is limited but suggests early disc fullness. There is truncal ataxia with inability to sit steadily and a wide-based unsteady gait when supported. Tone is mildly decreased centrally; she moves all extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric. There is no nuchal rigidity and no rash. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is grossly intact. Findings localize to the posterior fossa with signs of raised intracranial pressure and early hydrocephalus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this acutely ill toddler with signs of raised ICP from a posterior fossa mass, what is the most important immediate priority while arranging definitive imaging?
options: Begin outpatient developmental therapy
Stabilize the child and manage raised intracranial pressure, with neurosurgical evaluation for CSF diversion
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed meningitis
Perform an immediate lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
stages.explanation: The priority is stabilization and management of raised intracranial pressure, with urgent neurosurgical evaluation for CSF diversion (e.g., EVD) if obstructive hydrocephalus is confirmed, while definitive imaging proceeds. Outpatient therapy ignores a life-threatening emergency. Antibiotics for meningitis are not indicated without infectious features (no fever or nuchal rigidity), and the picture is a mass lesion. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a posterior fossa mass and obstructive hydrocephalus because removing CSF below the obstruction can precipitate fatal herniation.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & SPINE
stages.content:
MRI brain and total spine with contrast:
Large heterogeneous posterior fossa mass (4.0 cm) centered in/around the fourth ventricle
Avid heterogeneous enhancement with areas of necrosis and restricted diffusion
Obstructive hydrocephalus with dilated lateral and third ventricles
Nodular leptomeningeal enhancement along the spinal cord (drop metastases)
Mass effect on the brainstem

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Pathology of the resected tumor shows a poorly differentiated embryonal tumor with rhabdoid cells. Loss of which protein on immunohistochemistry confirms the diagnosis?
options: GFAP
INI1 (SMARCB1)
Synaptophysin
EMA
stages.explanation: Loss of nuclear INI1 (SMARCB1) expression is the defining diagnostic feature of atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT), an aggressive embryonal tumor of infancy with rhabdoid cells. GFAP is an astrocytic marker that may be focally positive but does not define AT/RT and is not lost in a diagnostic way. Synaptophysin marks neuronal/neuroendocrine differentiation and can be variably positive but is not the confirmatory test. EMA marks epithelial and some other tumors and is not specific or confirmatory; the hallmark of AT/RT is the loss of INI1/SMARCB1.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the embryonal histology, rhabdoid cells, INI1/SMARCB1 loss, and presentation in a young child, what is the diagnosis?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma
Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT)
Ependymoma
Subependymoma
stages.explanation: An aggressive posterior fossa embryonal tumor in a very young child with rhabdoid cells and loss of INI1/SMARCB1 is diagnostic of atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT). Pilocytic astrocytoma is a benign WHO grade 1 tumor (often cystic with a mural nodule) and does not show rhabdoid cells or INI1 loss. Ependymoma arises from ependymal lining and retains INI1, lacking rhabdoid morphology. Subependymoma is a benign indolent adult tumor, entirely inconsistent with this aggressive infant tumor with drop metastases.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the leptomeningeal (spinal) dissemination and aggressive biology, what is the most appropriate treatment framework for this child's AT/RT?
options: Surgery alone is curative and no further therapy is needed
Maximal safe resection followed by intensive multimodal therapy (high-dose/multi-agent chemotherapy, with radiotherapy as age and protocol permit), often on a clinical trial
Observation with serial imaging
Hormonal therapy
stages.explanation: AT/RT is highly aggressive and treated with maximal safe resection followed by intensive multimodal therapy, multi-agent and often high-dose chemotherapy (sometimes with autologous stem-cell rescue) and radiotherapy where age and protocol allow, frequently within clinical trials given the poor prognosis. Surgery alone is never curative for this disseminated embryonal tumor. Observation is inappropriate for a rapidly progressive malignancy with drop metastases. Hormonal therapy has no role; AT/RT is not a hormone-responsive tumor, and aggressive cytotoxic and radiation strategies are the backbone of treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why is radiotherapy decision-making particularly difficult in this 2-year-old?
options: Radiation is completely safe at this age with no consequences
Craniospinal radiation in very young children causes significant long-term neurocognitive, endocrine, and growth toxicity, so timing and dose must be weighed against the aggressive disease
Radiation is never used in pediatric brain tumors
Young children are resistant to all radiation effects
stages.explanation: Radiotherapy in very young children, especially craniospinal irradiation, carries substantial long-term toxicity, including neurocognitive impairment, endocrinopathy, and growth/developmental harm, so clinicians must balance these risks against the urgent need to control an aggressive, disseminated tumor; strategies may delay, reduce, or focally tailor radiation. Claiming radiation is completely safe ignores well-documented late effects. Saying radiation is never used in pediatric brain tumors is false; it is an important modality, including for AT/RT. The notion that young children are resistant to radiation effects is the opposite of reality, as the developing brain is especially vulnerable.

cases.diagnosis: Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT) of the posterior fossa with loss of INI1/SMARCB1 and leptomeningeal dissemination.
pearls: AT/RT is an aggressive embryonal posterior fossa tumor of infancy; loss of INI1/SMARCB1 is the defining marker.
Posterior fossa masses with obstructive hydrocephalus need urgent ICP management and CSF diversion; lumbar puncture is contraindicated.
Stage the neuraxis with MRI brain and total spine (and CSF) because embryonal tumors disseminate via leptomeningeal drop metastases.
Treatment is intensive multimodal therapy, but radiotherapy in very young children must be weighed against severe neurocognitive and endocrine late effects.

cases.title: Progressive Imbalance and Headaches in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening unsteadiness, occipital headaches, and intermittent nausea over several months.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man presents with 6 months of gradually worsening gait unsteadiness, a sense of imbalance when turning quickly, and dull occipital headaches that are worse in the morning and occasionally wake him. He reports intermittent nausea but no vomiting, and recent difficulty with fine coordination of his right hand. There is no diplopia, weakness, or numbness. Past medical history is notable for multiple skin lesions removed in the past (described as 'warts' on his face and hands) and a history of a benign thyroid nodule. He takes no regular medications. Allergies: none. Social history: works as an accountant, drinks alcohol socially, never smoked, no recreational drugs. Family history is significant for a mother who had breast cancer at age 41 and endometrial cancer at 48, and a maternal uncle with thyroid cancer. On review of systems he notes a large head circumference noted since childhood. He denies seizures, visual changes, or hearing loss.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Macrocephaly noted. Multiple small papular skin lesions (trichilemmomas) on the central face and oral mucosal papillomatosis. Cranial nerves intact; no papilledema on initial fundoscopy. Right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose and dysdiadochokinesia. Wide-based, unsteady gait with difficulty on tandem walking, falling to the right. Romberg negative. Strength 5/5 throughout, normal tone, reflexes 2+ and symmetric, plantars downgoing. Sensation intact to all modalities. No nystagmus at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the localizing signs, where is the lesion most likely located?
options: Left cerebral hemisphere
Right cerebellar hemisphere
Brainstem (pons)
Cervical spinal cord
stages.explanation: Right-sided appendicular dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, and a wide-based gait with falling toward the right localize to the right cerebellar hemisphere (cerebellar signs are ipsilateral to the lesion). The morning headaches and nausea suggest raised posterior fossa pressure. A left cerebral hemisphere lesion would produce contralateral (right-sided) weakness or cortical signs, not pure ipsilateral ataxia. A pontine lesion would typically cause crossed signs, cranial neuropathies, or long-tract findings, which are absent. A cervical cord lesion would cause myelopathic signs (hyperreflexia, sensory level, weakness), none of which are present, and would not explain isolated appendicular ataxia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Non-contrast head CT
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium
Lumbar puncture for CSF cytology
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain with and without contrast is the imaging modality of choice for a suspected posterior fossa lesion; the cerebellum is poorly visualized on CT due to beam-hardening artifact. Non-contrast CT may miss subtle cerebellar pathology and lacks the resolution to characterize the lesion. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated before imaging when a posterior fossa mass with possible obstructive hydrocephalus is suspected, given herniation risk. Carotid duplex evaluates anterior circulation atherosclerosis and is irrelevant to a subacute progressive cerebellar syndrome in a young man.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without contrast:
Right cerebellar hemisphere mass, non-enhancing
T2/FLAIR hyperintense with a characteristic laminated, parallel 'tiger-stripe' (corduroy/striated) pattern of alternating bands
Mass effect on the fourth ventricle with mild effacement; no significant hydrocephalus
No diffusion restriction
No surrounding vasogenic edema
No leptomeningeal enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The striated 'tiger-stripe' appearance of a non-enhancing cerebellar mass is most characteristic of which lesion?
options: Medulloblastoma
Cerebellar pilocytic astrocytoma
Dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma (Lhermitte-Duclos disease)
Hemangioblastoma
stages.explanation: The laminated 'tiger-stripe' (striated/corduroy) pattern of a non-enhancing, T2-hyperintense cerebellar mass that follows the cerebellar folial architecture is pathognomonic for dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma, or Lhermitte-Duclos disease (LDD). Medulloblastoma is an enhancing, restricting, midline (vermian) mass in children that often seeds CSF. Pilocytic astrocytoma classically appears as a cystic mass with an avidly enhancing mural nodule. Hemangioblastoma is a strongly enhancing nodule, frequently cystic, and is associated with von Hippel-Lindau, not the striated pattern. The combination of imaging plus the patient's mucocutaneous findings strongly points to LDD.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the trichilemmomas, oral papillomas, macrocephaly, and strong family history, which syndrome and germline gene should be evaluated?
options: Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome (VHL gene)
Cowden syndrome / PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (PTEN gene)
Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2 gene)
Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 gene)
stages.explanation: Adult-onset Lhermitte-Duclos disease is considered a pathognomonic feature of Cowden syndrome, an autosomal dominant PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome. The trichilemmomas, oral mucosal papillomatosis, macrocephaly, thyroid nodule, and a family history of early breast, endometrial, and thyroid cancers all fit Cowden syndrome, warranting PTEN germline testing and cancer surveillance (breast, thyroid, endometrial, renal, colon). VHL causes hemangioblastomas, retinal angiomas, RCC, and pheochromocytoma. NF2 causes bilateral vestibular schwannomas and meningiomas. Li-Fraumeni (TP53) predisposes to sarcomas, breast cancer, and brain tumors but lacks the mucocutaneous hamartomas and is not linked to LDD.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has mild but stable symptoms and no hydrocephalus. What is the most appropriate management of the cerebellar lesion?
options: Immediate gross-total resection followed by craniospinal radiation
Surgical decompression only if symptomatic mass effect/hydrocephalus develops, with serial MRI surveillance
Whole-brain radiation therapy
Systemic temozolomide chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Lhermitte-Duclos disease is a benign (WHO grade 1) hamartomatous lesion that grows slowly. Management is guided by symptoms: surgical decompression/resection is reserved for progressive mass effect, brainstem compression, or obstructive hydrocephalus, while minimally symptomatic, stable lesions are followed with serial MRI. Radiation is not used for this benign lesion and craniospinal radiation (appropriate for CSF-seeding tumors like medulloblastoma) is inappropriate. Whole-brain radiation is for metastatic disease. Temozolomide is a chemotherapy for malignant gliomas and has no role in a benign hamartoma. This patient's mild, stable symptoms without hydrocephalus favor surveillance plus genetic evaluation.

cases.diagnosis: Lhermitte-Duclos disease (dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma) in the setting of Cowden syndrome (PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome)
pearls: The laminated 'tiger-stripe'/corduroy T2 pattern of a non-enhancing cerebellar mass following folial architecture is pathognomonic for Lhermitte-Duclos disease.
Adult-onset Lhermitte-Duclos disease is a pathognomonic feature of Cowden syndrome — send PTEN germline testing and initiate cancer surveillance.
LDD is a benign WHO grade 1 hamartoma managed by symptom-driven decompression and MRI surveillance, not radiation or chemotherapy.
Cowden syndrome surveillance targets breast, thyroid, endometrial, renal, and colorectal cancers; mucocutaneous trichilemmomas and macrocephaly are key clues.

cases.title: Morning Headaches and Double Vision in a Schoolboy
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of morning headaches with occasional vomiting and trouble looking up.
cases.history: A 12-year-old boy is brought in by his mother for 6 weeks of headaches that are worse in the morning and sometimes associated with vomiting before breakfast. Over the past two weeks his teacher noted he seems clumsy and his mother observed he tilts his chin down and complains of 'double vision' when looking up. He has had no fevers, weight loss, or seizures. Birth and developmental history are normal; he is a good student. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: occasional acetaminophen with partial relief. Allergies: none. Immunizations up to date. Social history: lives with both parents and a younger sister, plays soccer. Family history is noncontributory with no neurocutaneous disorders or brain tumors. Review of systems is negative for hearing changes, limb weakness, numbness, polyuria, or polydipsia. His appetite has decreased slightly because of the morning nausea, and his mother is worried the headaches are becoming more frequent.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative boy. Mild bilateral papilledema on fundoscopy. Impaired upgaze with convergence-retraction nystagmus on attempted upward saccades; pupils show light-near dissociation. No facial weakness; hearing intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all limbs with normal tone. Reflexes 2+ symmetric, plantars flexor. Mild gait unsteadiness but no clear appendicular dysmetria. Remainder of cranial nerve exam normal. No nuchal rigidity. Skin exam shows no café-au-lait macules or other neurocutaneous stigmata.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of impaired upgaze, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and light-near dissociation is known as which syndrome, and where does it localize?
options: Weber syndrome — cerebral peduncle
Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome — tectum of the midbrain
Wallenberg syndrome — lateral medulla
Foster Kennedy syndrome — frontal lobe
stages.explanation: Impaired vertical (up) gaze, convergence-retraction nystagmus, and pupillary light-near dissociation constitute Parinaud syndrome, localizing to the dorsal midbrain (tectum), specifically compression of the pretectal vertical gaze centers. Weber syndrome (ventral midbrain) causes ipsilateral CN III palsy with contralateral hemiparesis. Wallenberg (lateral medullary) syndrome causes crossed sensory loss, Horner syndrome, dysphagia, and ataxia. Foster Kennedy syndrome involves ipsilateral optic atrophy with contralateral papilledema from a frontal/olfactory groove mass and does not produce vertical gaze palsy. The morning headaches, vomiting, and papilledema indicate raised intracranial pressure from this dorsal midbrain region.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best initial imaging study to evaluate this child?
options: Non-contrast head CT only
MRI brain with and without contrast
Plain skull radiographs
Catheter cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: MRI brain with and without contrast best characterizes the midbrain/tectal region and the ventricular system in a child with signs of a dorsal midbrain mass and raised ICP. CT alone is limited in the posterior fossa and midbrain by artifact and poor soft-tissue contrast, although it may be obtained first emergently to assess ventricular size. Skull radiographs cannot evaluate parenchyma. Catheter angiography is invasive and reserved for suspected vascular lesions, not a tectal mass; it is not a first-line study here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without contrast:
Focal expansion of the tectal plate (quadrigeminal plate) by a T2-hyperintense, non-enhancing lesion, ~1.2 cm
Aqueduct of Sylvius is compressed/effaced
Marked dilation of the lateral and third ventricles (triventricular hydrocephalus); fourth ventricle normal in size
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no other mass
Basic metabolic panel and CBC within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The imaging shows a non-enhancing tectal lesion causing aqueductal obstruction and triventricular hydrocephalus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pineoblastoma
Tectal (tectal plate) glioma — a low-grade astrocytoma
Germinoma of the pineal region
Medulloblastoma
stages.explanation: A small, non-enhancing, T2-hyperintense lesion intrinsic to the tectal plate causing aqueductal stenosis and obstructive (triventricular) hydrocephalus is the classic appearance of a tectal glioma, typically a low-grade (often pilocytic-type) astrocytoma with an indolent course. Pineoblastoma and germinoma arise in the pineal region (posterior to the tectum), usually enhance, and germinomas elevate CSF/serum tumor markers (beta-hCG, AFP). Medulloblastoma arises in the fourth ventricle/vermis, enhances, restricts diffusion, and seeds CSF. The non-enhancing, intrinsic tectal location and indolent presentation make tectal glioma the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His hydrocephalus is symptomatic but the tectal lesion is small, non-enhancing, and indolent. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (CSF diversion) with MRI surveillance of the lesion
Immediate resection of the tectal lesion
Craniospinal irradiation
Empiric chemotherapy with carboplatin and vincristine
stages.explanation: Tectal gliomas are typically indolent low-grade lesions; the morbidity comes from obstructive hydrocephalus, not the tumor itself. The standard of care is CSF diversion — endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) is preferred over a shunt for this triventricular obstructive pattern — combined with serial MRI surveillance of the lesion, which often remains stable for years. Direct resection of a tectal lesion risks injuring the midbrain and is reserved for atypical, enlarging, or enhancing tumors. Radiation and chemotherapy are reserved for documented progression or higher-grade behavior, not for a small stable indolent tectal glioma.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful ETV with resolution of symptoms, what is the appropriate long-term plan?
options: No further imaging needed once symptoms resolve
Serial surveillance MRIs to monitor lesion size, treating progression if it occurs
Begin prophylactic anticonvulsants indefinitely
Repeat lumbar punctures every 3 months
stages.explanation: Because tectal gliomas can occasionally enlarge or transform, long-term serial surveillance MRI is required even after the hydrocephalus is treated, so that progression (enhancement, growth, new neurologic deficits) can be detected and treated with surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy as indicated. Stopping imaging risks missing growth that could recur obstruct CSF flow. Prophylactic anticonvulsants are not indicated as these lesions rarely cause seizures and the patient has none. Serial lumbar punctures are unnecessary and potentially hazardous; ETV has already restored CSF circulation and there is no CSF infection or seeding to monitor.

cases.diagnosis: Tectal (tectal plate) glioma causing aqueductal obstruction and triventricular hydrocephalus, managed with CSF diversion
pearls: Parinaud (dorsal midbrain) syndrome — upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, light-near dissociation — points to a tectal/pretectal lesion.
A small non-enhancing tectal mass causing aqueductal stenosis and triventricular hydrocephalus is the classic tectal glioma, usually low-grade and indolent.
Treat the hydrocephalus, not the tumor: endoscopic third ventriculostomy with MRI surveillance is first-line; resection/radiation are reserved for progression.
Triventricular hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and third ventricles with a normal fourth) localizes the obstruction to the cerebral aqueduct.

cases.title: Sudden Facial Droop and Limb Weakness on Opposite Sides
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt onset of double vision, right-sided facial weakness, and left-sided limb clumsiness.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman was admitted after the sudden onset, two days ago, of horizontal double vision, right-sided facial droop, and clumsiness of her left arm and leg. Symptoms began acutely while she was at rest, were maximal within an hour, and have partially fluctuated since. She recalls a brief, milder episode of transient facial tingling and unsteadiness about a year ago that resolved spontaneously and was never investigated. She has no vascular risk factors: no hypertension, diabetes, or smoking. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as a teacher. Family history: no strokes, aneurysms, or known vascular malformations. Review of systems is negative for headache at onset, neck pain, fevers, recent trauma, or oral contraceptive use. She is right-handed. She denies prior anticoagulant use and has no history of bleeding disorders.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. Right CN VI palsy with diplopia on right lateral gaze and a right lower-motor-neuron facial weakness (cannot fully close right eye, flattened right nasolabial fold). Left-sided weakness (4/5) in arm and leg with mildly increased tone and a left extensor plantar response. Sensation grossly intact. Right-sided facial findings with left-sided body findings = crossed pattern. No aphasia, normal visual fields. Mild left-sided dysmetria. Gait not formally tested due to weakness. No meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has right CN VI and VII palsies with left hemiparesis ('crossed signs'). Where does this localize?
options: Right cerebral cortex
Right internal capsule
Pons (right side, at the level of the CN VI/VII nuclei)
Left midbrain
stages.explanation: Crossed signs — ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits with contralateral long-tract (motor) findings — localize to the brainstem. Right CN VI and CN VII involvement with a left hemiparesis localizes to the right pons at the level of the abducens and facial nuclei, where the facial nerve loops around the abducens nucleus before the corticospinal tract decussates in the medulla. A cortical or internal capsule lesion would cause contralateral face and body weakness on the same side (no crossed pattern) with an upper-motor-neuron facial pattern. A left midbrain lesion would give different cranial nerve involvement (e.g., CN III) and would not explain right CN VI/VII palsies.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging to characterize a suspected pontine lesion in this patient?
options: Non-contrast head CT alone
MRI brain with and without contrast, including gradient-echo/susceptibility-weighted sequences
CT angiography of the head and neck only
Transcranial Doppler
stages.explanation: MRI with contrast plus blood-sensitive sequences (gradient-echo [GRE] or susceptibility-weighted imaging [SWI]) is essential to characterize a pontine lesion and to detect blood-degradation products. CT is insensitive in the brainstem due to artifact and cannot reliably show small hemorrhages or characterize a mass. CT angiography evaluates large-vessel stenosis/aneurysm but will not characterize an intra-axial pontine lesion or its hemosiderin. Transcranial Doppler assesses flow velocities and is not diagnostic for a structural pontine lesion. SWI/GRE is key to identifying the hemosiderin rim that distinguishes a cavernoma from a neoplasm.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without contrast (incl. SWI):
Right pontine lesion, ~1.4 cm, with mixed-signal 'popcorn' / 'mulberry' appearance and internal locules of varying T1/T2 signal
Complete peripheral rim of low signal (hemosiderin) on SWI/GRE
Small focus of acute/subacute blood within the lesion
Minimal surrounding edema; no significant mass effect
No abnormal nodular or ring enhancement; no associated large draining vein on this study
No diffusion restriction outside the lesion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A pontine lesion with a 'popcorn' appearance and a complete peripheral hemosiderin rim on SWI/GRE is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Cerebral cavernous malformation (cavernoma) with hemorrhage
Hemorrhagic metastasis
Pontine glioma
Capillary telangiectasia
stages.explanation: The 'popcorn'/'mulberry' lesion with mixed-signal locules and a complete surrounding hemosiderin rim (blooming on SWI/GRE) is the classic appearance of a cavernous malformation (cavernoma) that has bled. A complete low-signal rim and lack of significant edema or enhancement argue against neoplasm. Hemorrhagic metastasis typically shows nodular/ring enhancement, surrounding vasogenic edema, and an incomplete hemosiderin rim, usually with a known primary. Pontine glioma is an expansile, often non-hemorrhagic infiltrative mass. Capillary telangiectasia is a tiny, brush-like faintly enhancing lesion that blooms on SWI but lacks the popcorn locules and overt hemorrhage. The clinical picture of recurrent small bleeds fits a brainstem cavernoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is neurologically stable and improving. What is the most appropriate initial management of this symptomatic brainstem cavernoma after a single hemorrhage?
options: Immediate microsurgical resection regardless of accessibility
Stereotactic radiosurgery as first-line treatment
Conservative management with serial MRI and reserve surgery for repeated/disabling hemorrhages or a lesion reaching a pial surface
Therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent thrombosis
stages.explanation: Most brainstem cavernomas are managed conservatively after a first hemorrhage, with serial MRI and surgery reserved for recurrent symptomatic hemorrhages, progressive deficits, or when the lesion reaches a pial/ependymal surface (making resection safer). Brainstem surgery carries high risk, so the bleeding history and surgical accessibility drive the decision. Immediate resection 'regardless of accessibility' is inappropriate given the eloquent location. Stereotactic radiosurgery is controversial, not first-line, and may not reliably reduce rebleeding while risking radiation injury to the brainstem. Anticoagulation is contraindicated — it would increase hemorrhage risk in a lesion that has just bled.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She mentions a sibling had a 'brain bleed' from a similar lesion. What additional consideration applies to multiple or familial cavernomas?
options: They are caused by chronic hypertension and require only blood-pressure control
Multiple cavernomas suggest a familial syndrome (CCM1/KRIT1, CCM2, CCM3 genes); genetic counseling and screening of relatives are appropriate
They mandate lifelong anticoagulation
They are premalignant and require resection of all lesions
stages.explanation: Multiple cavernomas, especially with a family history, suggest familial cerebral cavernous malformation syndrome, inherited autosomal dominantly via mutations in CCM1 (KRIT1), CCM2, or CCM3 (PDCD10). This warrants genetic counseling, MRI screening (including SWI to detect numerous small lesions) of at-risk relatives, and avoidance of provoking factors. Cavernomas are not caused by chronic hypertension (that classically causes deep hypertensive hemorrhages and Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms). Anticoagulation is harmful, not indicated. Cavernomas are benign vascular malformations, not premalignant, so prophylactic resection of all lesions is not warranted; management is symptom- and risk-driven.

cases.diagnosis: Brainstem (pontine) cavernous malformation with hemorrhage producing crossed signs
pearls: Crossed signs (ipsilateral cranial nerve palsy + contralateral hemiparesis) localize to the brainstem; pontine lesions affect CN VI/VII with contralateral motor signs.
A 'popcorn'/'mulberry' lesion with a complete hemosiderin rim that blooms on GRE/SWI is the signature of a cavernoma — not a neoplasm.
Brainstem cavernomas are usually managed conservatively; surgery is reserved for repeated/disabling hemorrhages or when the lesion reaches a pial surface.
Multiple/familial cavernomas point to CCM1 (KRIT1)/CCM2/CCM3 mutations — pursue genetic counseling and screen relatives; never anticoagulate a fresh bleed.

cases.title: New Headaches and a Firm Scalp Bump in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of progressive headaches with a recently noticed firm area over the scalp.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with 3 months of progressively worsening dull, holocranial headaches, worse when lying flat, and a firm fullness he recently noticed over the right side of his head. He reports mild intermittent left-hand clumsiness but no seizures or speech difficulty. He has known metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma diagnosed 3 years ago (Gleason 9), treated with androgen-deprivation therapy; his most recent PSA had been rising. He also has hypertension and osteoarthritis. Medications: leuprolide depot injections, lisinopril 20 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social history: retired electrician, former smoker (quit 10 years ago, 25 pack-years), occasional alcohol. Family history: father with 'bone cancer,' mother with hypertension. Review of systems notable for chronic low back and hip pain (known bone metastases) and recent fatigue. He denies fevers, weight loss beyond baseline, visual loss, or recent head trauma. He has had no falls.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. A firm, mildly tender, non-mobile fullness palpable over the right parietal region; overlying skin intact. No papilledema. Subtle left pronator drift with mildly increased left-sided reflexes; plantar responses flexor. Strength otherwise 5/5; sensation intact. Cranial nerves intact. Speech fluent, no neglect. Gait normal. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable. The remainder of the neurologic exam is nonfocal aside from the subtle left-sided upper-motor-neuron signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a known prostate cancer patient with new headaches, focal signs, and a palpable cranial fullness, what is the best next diagnostic step?
options: Reassurance and follow-up in 3 months
MRI brain with and without contrast
Plain skull radiograph only
Empiric high-dose corticosteroids without imaging
stages.explanation: A cancer patient with new progressive headaches, focal neurologic signs, and a palpable cranial mass needs prompt MRI brain with and without contrast to evaluate for intracranial metastasis, dural disease, or skull involvement. Reassurance is unsafe given red-flag features (positional headache, focal deficit, known malignancy with rising PSA). A skull radiograph is insensitive and cannot assess intracranial extent or the brain parenchyma. Empiric steroids without imaging may transiently help symptoms but delays diagnosis and can confound future histologic evaluation (e.g., if lymphoma were in the differential); imaging should come first.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without contrast:
Right parietal extra-axial, dural-based enhancing mass (~3 cm) with a broad dural attachment and a 'dural tail'
Underlying calvarial signal abnormality with marrow replacement; focal sclerotic/blastic change in the adjacent skull
Mild mass effect on the underlying cortex with vasogenic edema
No intra-axial parenchymal metastasis identified
Findings could mimic meningioma
PSA 88 ng/mL (previously 12); alkaline phosphatase elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The dural-based enhancing mass with a dural tail closely mimics meningioma. What feature most favors a dural METASTASIS over a meningioma in this patient?
options: Presence of a dural tail
Homogeneous contrast enhancement
Adjacent calvarial marrow involvement/destructive-blastic skull change with a known primary cancer and rising PSA
Extra-axial location
stages.explanation: A dural tail, homogeneous enhancement, and an extra-axial location are all typical of meningioma and therefore do NOT distinguish it from a dural metastasis, which can mimic meningioma exactly. What favors metastasis here is the adjacent calvarial marrow replacement with blastic/sclerotic skull change in a patient with known prostate adenocarcinoma and a sharply rising PSA — prostate cancer characteristically produces osteoblastic bone (and dural) metastases. Meningiomas may cause hyperostosis but not marrow-replacing destructive lesions, and the strong clinical context (metastatic prostate cancer, rising PSA) makes dural metastasis far more likely. Tissue or correlation with systemic disease resolves the ambiguity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis and guide therapy?
options: Immediate craniotomy with gross-total resection
Multidisciplinary evaluation with biopsy (or correlation with systemic restaging) to confirm metastasis before definitive treatment
Start temozolomide empirically
Begin whole-brain radiation immediately without tissue confirmation
