stages.explanation: Because the lesion mimics meningioma but the clinical context strongly suggests dural metastasis, the right step is multidisciplinary evaluation — confirming the diagnosis via biopsy or by correlating with systemic restaging (bone scan, CT, PSA trend) — before committing to treatment, since management differs greatly between a benign meningioma and metastatic prostate cancer. Immediate gross-total resection is not first-line for a presumed metastasis with systemic disease and may be unnecessary if systemic/radiation therapy suffices. Temozolomide is for malignant gliomas, not prostate metastasis. Whole-brain radiation may be appropriate for multifocal disease, but committing without confirming the diagnosis and defining the extent of disease is premature; symptomatic dural masses are often treated with focal radiation and steroids.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Diagnosis is confirmed as dural metastasis from prostate adenocarcinoma. He has symptomatic edema. Which initial management is most appropriate?
options: Corticosteroids for edema plus focal radiotherapy to the dural lesion, with optimization of systemic prostate cancer therapy
Observation alone
Bilateral carotid stenting
Prophylactic anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Symptomatic dural metastasis with vasogenic edema is treated with corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone) to reduce edema and mass effect, plus focal radiotherapy to the lesion for durable local control, while systemic therapy for the prostate cancer is escalated (the rising PSA on ADT signals progression/castration resistance, so options like a next-generation antiandrogen, chemotherapy, or radiopharmaceuticals are considered). Observation is inappropriate given symptomatic mass effect. Carotid stenting addresses carotid stenosis, irrelevant here. Anticoagulation is not indicated and could increase bleeding risk in a vascular metastatic lesion. Surgery is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty or large symptomatic lesions not controlled otherwise.

cases.diagnosis: Intracranial dural metastasis from prostate adenocarcinoma (meningioma mimic)
pearls: Dural metastases can perfectly mimic meningioma (dural tail, homogeneous enhancement, extra-axial); the deciding clues are adjacent calvarial marrow involvement and a known primary.
Prostate cancer classically produces osteoblastic (sclerotic) bone and dural metastases; a sharply rising PSA signals progression/castration resistance.
Confirm a presumed dural metastasis (biopsy or systemic restaging) before definitive treatment, since meningioma and metastasis are managed very differently.
Symptomatic dural metastases are treated with steroids for edema plus focal radiotherapy, alongside escalation of systemic therapy.

cases.title: A Tender Lump on the Scalp Months After Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: An enlarging, tender lump on the top of the head noticed over the past two months.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman presents with a gradually enlarging, mildly tender lump on the vertex of her scalp that she first noticed two months ago. It does not move easily under the skin and has slowly grown. She denies headache, visual changes, weakness, numbness, or seizures. Her history is significant for hormone-receptor-positive invasive ductal breast carcinoma treated 6 years ago with lumpectomy, radiation, and adjuvant endocrine therapy; she was considered in remission. She also has osteoporosis and hypothyroidism. Medications: anastrozole 1 mg daily, alendronate weekly, levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: sulfa (hives). Social history: widowed, lives alone, never smoked, no alcohol. Family history: mother with breast cancer at 70, sister healthy. Review of systems notable for new mild low back pain over the past month and some fatigue; she denies fevers, weight loss, cough, or abdominal pain. She has had no trauma to the head and no skin infection at the site.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented. A firm, fixed, mildly tender mass (~2.5 cm) over the vertex/parietal scalp; it feels affixed to the underlying skull and is not freely mobile over bone. Overlying skin intact without ulceration or erythema. No fluctuance. No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is entirely nonfocal: cranial nerves intact, strength 5/5, sensation intact, reflexes symmetric, gait normal, no papilledema. Mild midline lumbar tenderness on back exam. Breast/chest wall exam shows post-surgical changes without a new mass.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A fixed scalp mass adherent to the skull in a woman with prior breast cancer is most concerning for what, and what is the best initial imaging?
options: Benign sebaceous cyst — no imaging needed
Skull (calvarial) metastasis — obtain CT/MRI of the head to assess for an underlying bony lesion
Cellulitis — start oral antibiotics
Lipoma — reassure and observe
stages.explanation: A new, firm, fixed scalp mass that feels adherent to the underlying skull in a patient with a history of breast cancer should be considered a calvarial (skull) metastasis until proven otherwise, and imaging (CT to characterize bone, MRI to assess soft tissue/intracranial extent) is the appropriate next step. A sebaceous cyst is mobile within the skin, not fixed to bone, and usually has a punctum. Cellulitis presents with erythema, warmth, and tenderness with overlying skin changes, absent here. A lipoma is soft, mobile, and subcutaneous, not fixed to bone. The fixation to bone plus oncologic history mandates imaging rather than reassurance.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head (bone windows) + MRI brain:
Vertex calvarial lytic/destructive lesion (~2.6 cm) with cortical breakthrough and an associated soft-tissue scalp component
Extension to the outer table with a small epidural soft-tissue rim; no significant brain parenchymal invasion or edema
MRI: enhancing marrow-replacing lesion in the diploic space
Labs: Calcium 10.9 mg/dL (elevated), Alkaline phosphatase 168 U/L (elevated)
CBC and renal function normal
Tumor marker CA 15-3 elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The lesion is a lytic, destructive calvarial mass with marrow replacement and a soft-tissue component. In a former breast cancer patient, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Meningioma with hyperostosis
Osteoma
Calvarial (skull) metastasis from breast carcinoma
Paget disease of bone
stages.explanation: A lytic, destructive, marrow-replacing calvarial lesion with cortical breakthrough and a soft-tissue scalp component, plus hypercalcemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and a rising CA 15-3, is most consistent with a calvarial metastasis from breast carcinoma (breast and lung are common sources of skull metastases). Meningioma typically causes reactive hyperostosis (bone thickening), not lytic destruction with marrow replacement, and is an intracranial dural lesion. Osteoma is a benign dense bony overgrowth, not lytic/destructive. Paget disease produces a 'cotton-wool' mixed lytic-sclerotic skull with cortical thickening and skull enlargement, usually without a focal soft-tissue mass or the systemic tumor markers seen here.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She has new back pain and hypercalcemia. What is the most appropriate next step to define disease extent?
options: Systemic restaging with body imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan or PET) and spine MRI
Immediate craniotomy to resect the skull lesion
Repeat scalp exam in 6 months
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: The picture suggests metastatic recurrence of breast cancer; new back pain plus hypercalcemia raises concern for additional bony metastases (including the spine, with risk of cord compression). Systemic restaging — CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, bone scan or PET, and dedicated spine MRI — defines the extent of disease and guides systemic and local therapy. Immediate craniotomy is not first-line for a metastatic calvarial lesion and does not address systemic disease. Reassessing in 6 months would dangerously delay treatment of metastatic recurrence and possible spinal involvement. Antibiotics are inappropriate as there is no infection. Biopsy may also be needed if recurrence is not otherwise confirmable.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Restaging confirms multifocal bone metastases. Which set of interventions is most appropriate for this calvarial metastasis and overall bone disease?
options: Surgical resection of all bone lesions
Local radiotherapy to symptomatic sites, a bone-modifying agent (bisphosphonate or denosumab), management of hypercalcemia, and re-evaluation/escalation of systemic therapy
Observation with calcium supplementation
Whole-brain radiation as the sole therapy
stages.explanation: Management of metastatic breast cancer to bone combines local radiotherapy to symptomatic/at-risk sites (including the painful calvarial lesion and any threatened spine), a bone-modifying agent (bisphosphonate such as zoledronic acid, or denosumab) to reduce skeletal-related events, treatment of hypercalcemia (IV fluids, bisphosphonate ± calcitonin), and re-evaluation of systemic endocrine/targeted/chemotherapy given progression on an aromatase inhibitor. Resecting all bone lesions is neither feasible nor indicated. Observation with added calcium would worsen hypercalcemia and ignore progressive disease. Whole-brain radiation treats parenchymal brain metastases, not a calvarial bone lesion, and would not address systemic bone disease.

cases.diagnosis: Calvarial (skull) metastasis from breast carcinoma with multifocal bone metastatic recurrence
pearls: A fixed scalp mass adherent to bone in a cancer patient is a calvarial metastasis until proven otherwise — image with CT (bone) and MRI.
Breast and lung cancers are the most common sources of skull metastases; lytic marrow-replacing destruction distinguishes metastasis from meningioma's hyperostosis.
New bone metastases with hypercalcemia and back pain warrant systemic restaging and spine MRI to exclude cord compression.
Bone-directed therapy combines focal radiotherapy, a bone-modifying agent (bisphosphonate/denosumab), hypercalcemia treatment, and systemic therapy escalation.

cases.title: Acute Numb Saddle and Inability to Urinate
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of worsening low back pain with new numbness 'down there' and inability to pass urine.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents to the ED with severe lower back pain that has worsened over the past week and, in the last two days, new numbness around the buttocks and groin, weakness in both legs, and inability to urinate today. He also notes some fecal incontinence. PMH: non-small cell lung cancer diagnosed 8 months ago, currently on systemic therapy; hypertension; former heavy smoker (40 pack-years). Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, oncologic therapy per oncology, oxycodone for pain. No anticoagulants. No drug allergies. He lives with his wife. Review of systems notable for 6 kg weight loss, night pain that wakes him, and progressive difficulty walking. No fever or chills, no recent trauma, no prior back surgery. He reports the back pain is worse when lying flat and is not relieved by rest. He has not voided in over 10 hours and feels bladder fullness. No abdominal pain, hematuria, or recent instrumentation.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, in pain. Lumbar spine tender to percussion over the lower lumbar region. Bilateral lower-extremity weakness (hip flexion and ankle dorsiflexion 4-/5), more diffuse than a single root. Reduced sensation in a saddle distribution (perineum, perianal, posterior thighs). Absent ankle jerks; diminished knee jerks. Decreased anal sphincter tone with absent bulbocavernosus reflex. Palpable, distended bladder; post-void residual not yet measured. Plantar responses downgoing. No upper-extremity deficit; no neck signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, areflexia, decreased anal tone, and urinary retention in a cancer patient most strongly suggest which syndrome?
options: Acute transverse myelitis at the thoracic cord
Cauda equina syndrome
Guillain-Barré syndrome
Lumbar facet arthropathy
stages.explanation: The combination of saddle (perineal/perianal) anesthesia, bilateral lower-extremity weakness, areflexia, decreased anal sphincter tone, and acute urinary retention is the classic presentation of cauda equina syndrome—compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus. It is a surgical emergency. Thoracic transverse myelitis would produce a sensory level on the trunk with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, upgoing toes), not saddle anesthesia with areflexia. Guillain-Barré causes ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia but typically spares the bladder early and lacks saddle sensory loss and focal back pain. Lumbar facet arthropathy causes mechanical pain without neurologic deficits or sphincter dysfunction. The deficits localize to the cauda equina.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Outpatient lumbar spine radiographs
Emergent MRI of the whole spine (urgent lumbar with thoracic/cervical screening)
Nerve conduction studies/EMG
Empiric physical therapy and reassessment in one week
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency; emergent MRI of the spine (urgent lumbar imaging, with thoracic/cervical screening because metastatic disease is often multilevel) is required to identify the compressive lesion and guide immediate intervention. Plain radiographs lack sensitivity for soft-tissue/epidural compression and would delay care. EMG/nerve conduction studies are not appropriate in the acute setting and would not localize a compressive lesion in time. Delaying with physical therapy risks irreversible sphincter and motor loss—time to decompression strongly influences recovery. In a known cancer patient with these red-flag findings, rapid imaging and surgical/oncologic mobilization are paramount.

stages.label: MRI SPINE & LABS
stages.content:
MRI: epidural soft-tissue mass at L3-L4 with a destructive L3 vertebral body lesion compressing the thecal sac and cauda equina nerve roots
Additional metastatic deposits at T8 and L5 without cord compression
Pathologic compression fracture of L3
Bladder scan: 720 mL retained urine
WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL
Calcium 11.2 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 190 U/L
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms metastatic epidural compression of the cauda equina. Along with urgent bladder decompression, what is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start corticosteroids (dexamethasone) and obtain emergent neurosurgery and radiation oncology consultation for decompression
Begin outpatient opioid titration and discharge
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
Schedule elective surgery in 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Metastatic epidural compression of the cauda equina is an emergency. Immediate steps include high-dose corticosteroids (dexamethasone) to reduce edema and emergent consultation with neurosurgery (for surgical decompression/stabilization of the destructive L3 lesion) and radiation oncology (for urgent radiotherapy). Urinary retention requires immediate catheterization (bladder decompression). Outpatient opioid titration ignores the surgical emergency and risks permanent deficits. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with an epidural compressive mass (risk of worsening compression/herniation of roots and is non-therapeutic here). Elective surgery in 2 weeks is far too late—decompression should occur urgently, ideally within hours, because neurologic recovery depends on the speed of intervention.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The patient undergoes urgent decompression and stabilization followed by radiotherapy. Which factor most strongly predicts his chance of regaining bladder and motor function?
options: His age at presentation
The duration and severity of neurologic deficit before decompression
The specific opioid used for pain control
His baseline blood pressure
stages.explanation: The strongest predictor of neurologic recovery in cauda equina syndrome (and metastatic cord/cauda compression) is the duration and severity of the deficit before decompression—particularly pre-treatment ambulatory and sphincter status and how rapidly intervention occurs. Patients decompressed early, while still ambulatory and before complete sphincter loss, have markedly better outcomes, which is why this is a time-critical emergency. Age has a minor influence compared with the timing/severity of deficit. The choice of opioid affects analgesia, not neurologic recovery. Baseline blood pressure is unrelated to root recovery in this context. Emphasizing rapid recognition and decompression is the key teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from metastatic epidural spinal cord compression (lung cancer to L3)
pearls: Saddle anesthesia + bilateral leg weakness + areflexia + decreased anal tone + urinary retention = cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency.
Obtain emergent MRI of the spine (screen multiple levels in cancer patients) and check a post-void residual/bladder scan.
Treat with immediate dexamethasone, bladder catheterization, and emergent neurosurgery + radiation oncology consultation.
Neurologic recovery depends most on the speed of decompression and pre-treatment deficit severity—act within hours.

cases.title: Rapidly Worsening Leg Weakness and a Sensory Level
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive weakness and numbness of both legs over ten days with new band-like chest tightness.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man admitted for cancer staging develops rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness and numbness over 10 days, now unable to stand. He reports a band-like sensation around the mid-chest and new urinary urgency progressing to retention. PMH: recently diagnosed small cell lung cancer with brain metastases on a prior MRI; 50 pack-year smoking history; COPD. Medications: oncologic therapy pending, albuterol/ipratropium inhalers, oxycodone. No anticoagulants; no drug allergies. He lives alone. Review of systems: 8 kg weight loss, fatigue, and a chronic cough. No fever, no recent trauma, no prior spine surgery. The weakness has been ascending and accelerating, and he now notes difficulty sensing temperature in his legs. He denies neck pain. There is no preceding viral illness or vaccination. He has had no back trauma and no prior radiation to the spine.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented. Spastic paraparesis: lower-extremity strength 2/5 proximally and distally, with increased tone. Hyperreflexia at the knees and ankles, sustained ankle clonus, bilateral extensor plantar responses. A sensory level to pinprick at approximately T6. Proprioception reduced in the toes. Upper extremities normal. Spine non-tender to palpation; no focal vertebral percussion tenderness. Palpable distended bladder. No saddle anesthesia. Cranial nerves intact. The picture is an upper-motor-neuron myelopathy with a thoracic sensory level.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Spastic paraparesis, hyperreflexia, extensor plantars, and a T6 sensory level indicate which localization?
options: Cauda equina (lower motor neuron) lesion
Thoracic spinal cord (intramedullary or compressive) lesion
Bilateral peroneal neuropathies
Parasagittal cerebral lesion
stages.explanation: Upper-motor-neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantars) with a discrete thoracic sensory level localize to the thoracic spinal cord. The absence of saddle anesthesia and the presence of hyperreflexia argue against a cauda equina (lower motor neuron) process, which would cause areflexia and saddle sensory loss. Bilateral peroneal neuropathies cause foot drop without a sensory level, sphincter involvement, or UMN signs. A parasagittal cerebral lesion can cause bilateral leg weakness but would not produce a spinal sensory level or the spinal cord pattern of sphincter dysfunction; it may also affect cognition or produce cortical signs. The thoracic cord is the site requiring urgent imaging.

stages.label: MRI SPINE
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine with contrast: fusiform expansion of the cord at T5-T7 with an intramedullary (within-cord) enhancing lesion
Ring-like/avid enhancement of the intramedullary nodule with extensive surrounding cord T2 edema disproportionate to lesion size
No epidural mass and no significant vertebral or bony destruction
No leptomeningeal enhancement on this study
Cord signal returns to normal above and below the affected segment

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An enhancing within-cord (intramedullary) lesion with disproportionate edema and no epidural/bony component in a patient with small cell lung cancer most likely represents:
options: Epidural metastasis compressing the cord
Intramedullary spinal cord metastasis
Spinal cord infarction
Primary spinal ependymoma
stages.explanation: An enhancing lesion located within the cord substance (intramedullary), with edema out of proportion to lesion size and no epidural or bony involvement, in a patient with known small cell lung cancer is most consistent with an intramedullary spinal cord metastasis—a rare site of spread, most often from lung cancer. Epidural metastasis compresses the cord from outside, typically with a vertebral/bony lesion, which is absent here. Spinal cord infarction (e.g., anterior spinal artery) presents acutely with a vascular territory pattern, restricted diffusion, and usually no nodular enhancement early. Primary ependymoma is possible but, given disseminated cancer and the classic 'edema disproportionate to lesion' sign, metastasis is far more likely. Tissue/CSF and systemic context support the diagnosis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After starting corticosteroids, what is the most appropriate next step to confirm extent and guide therapy?
options: Open cord biopsy of the lesion as the immediate first step
Image the entire neuraxis (brain and complete spine) with contrast and consider CSF cytology to assess for additional CNS disease
Begin empiric antibiotics for spinal abscess
Proceed directly to surgical resection of the intramedullary lesion
stages.explanation: Intramedullary metastasis frequently coexists with brain metastases and leptomeningeal disease, so imaging the entire neuraxis (contrast MRI of brain and full spine) and considering CSF cytology defines the burden of CNS disease and informs treatment (radiotherapy, systemic therapy). This patient already has known brain metastases, increasing the likelihood of multifocal CNS spread. Open cord biopsy carries high risk of neurologic injury and is usually unnecessary when there is a known primary and characteristic imaging; it is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without features of infection (no fever, no abscess on imaging). Surgical resection of an intramedullary metastasis is rarely first-line and is high risk; radiotherapy and systemic therapy are mainstays.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Neuraxis imaging shows the intramedullary lesion plus stable treated brain metastases. What is the most appropriate management of the cord lesion?
options: Gross-total surgical resection of the intramedullary metastasis
Corticosteroids plus focal radiotherapy to the cord lesion, integrated with systemic therapy for small cell lung cancer
Observation alone given the poor prognosis
Intrathecal chemotherapy as the sole treatment
stages.explanation: For symptomatic intramedullary spinal cord metastasis, corticosteroids reduce cord edema and focal radiotherapy provides local control and can stabilize or improve neurologic function; this is integrated with systemic therapy, since small cell lung cancer is chemo- and radiosensitive. Surgical resection of an intramedullary metastasis is high risk and not standard, generally reserved for solitary lesions with diagnostic uncertainty or when radiation is not feasible. Observation alone abandons a chance to preserve function and palliate—rapidly progressive myelopathy warrants active treatment. Intrathecal chemotherapy targets leptomeningeal disease and does not adequately treat a parenchymal (intramedullary) cord lesion; it is not appropriate as sole therapy here. Multimodal palliative-intent therapy is the standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite treatment, which complication of his myelopathy most requires proactive nursing and medical attention to prevent serious harm?
options: Mild hypertension
Neurogenic bladder with retention leading to urinary tract infection/urosepsis and pressure injuries from immobility
Transient mild hyperglycemia from steroids alone
Self-limited muscle soreness
stages.explanation: A thoracic myelopathy with paraparesis produces neurogenic bladder (retention) and immobility, so proactive bladder management (catheterization, monitoring residuals) to prevent urinary tract infection/urosepsis, plus meticulous skin care and repositioning to prevent pressure injuries, DVT prophylaxis, and bowel regimens, are essential to avoid serious complications. Mild hypertension is not the priority complication and is readily managed. Steroid-induced hyperglycemia should be monitored but is not the dominant life-threatening risk compared with urosepsis and pressure injury. Muscle soreness is trivial. Anticipating the predictable complications of immobility and neurogenic bladder is a core management principle for any patient with significant myelopathy and reduces morbidity and mortality.

cases.diagnosis: Intramedullary spinal cord metastasis from small cell lung cancer causing rapidly progressive thoracic myelopathy
pearls: Intramedullary cord metastasis is rare; suspect it with an enhancing within-cord lesion and edema disproportionate to lesion size—lung cancer is the most common source.
UMN signs with a thoracic sensory level localize to the cord; distinguish from epidural compression (bony/epidural mass) and cauda equina (areflexia, saddle anesthesia).
Image the entire neuraxis (brain + spine) and consider CSF cytology—intramedullary mets often accompany brain or leptomeningeal disease.
Treat with steroids and focal radiotherapy plus systemic therapy; surgery is rarely first-line for intramedullary metastases.

cases.title: Years of Low Back Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic, dull low back pain for over two years, worse at night, with recent tingling in the feet.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman presents with more than 2 years of persistent dull low back pain that is worse at night and when lying down, sometimes waking her. Over the past few months she has noticed intermittent tingling in both feet and mild difficulty with prolonged standing. No bowel or bladder dysfunction. PMH: unremarkable; no prior cancer. Medications: occasional ibuprofen 400 mg as needed; no daily medications. No drug allergies. She is a graphic designer, exercises regularly, nonsmoker, drinks rarely. Family history: noncontributory; no neurofibromatosis. Review of systems: no fever, weight loss, or night sweats; menses regular. She has tried physical therapy and NSAIDs with minimal relief, which prompted further evaluation. The pain does not radiate in a clear single-nerve pattern and is not consistently positional with activity. She denies trauma and has had no prior spine imaging. No saddle numbness, no leg weakness she can detect.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable at rest. Lumbar spine with mild midline tenderness over the lower lumbar region; no deformity. Straight-leg raise mildly positive bilaterally for back discomfort. Strength 5/5 in both legs; reflexes 2+ and symmetric except trace-diminished ankle jerks. Sensation mildly reduced to light touch over the soles. Normal anal tone; bulbocavernosus reflex present. Gait normal; tandem intact. No saddle anesthesia. No upper motor neuron signs. Remainder of neurologic exam unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Chronic nocturnal back pain worse when recumbent, unrelieved by conservative care, in a young adult, is a red flag that should prompt which next step?
options: Continue NSAIDs and physical therapy for 6 more months
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the lumbar spine
Plain lumbar radiographs only
Reassurance that this is mechanical back pain
stages.explanation: Nocturnal back pain that worsens when lying down and is refractory to conservative therapy is a red flag for a neoplastic or inflammatory process and warrants contrast-enhanced MRI of the lumbar spine, which best evaluates intradural and intramedullary pathology. Continuing NSAIDs/physical therapy delays diagnosis of a potentially serious lesion. Plain radiographs are insensitive for intradural soft-tissue tumors and would likely be normal. Reassurance is inappropriate when red-flag features are present—nocturnal, recumbency-worsened, treatment-refractory pain with new sensory symptoms suggests an underlying structural lesion. MRI with gadolinium is the key step to characterize a possible intradural mass.

stages.label: MRI LUMBAR SPINE
stages.content:
Well-circumscribed intradural, extramedullary mass within the lumbar thecal sac at the level of the conus/filum terminale (L2-L3 region)
Lobulated, sausage-shaped lesion arising from the filum terminale
Avid, fairly homogeneous post-contrast enhancement
T2 hyperintense with possible hemorrhagic/cystic foci
Mild scalloping of the posterior vertebral bodies; widening of the spinal canal
No leptomeningeal nodularity elsewhere on this study

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An enhancing, sausage-shaped intradural-extramedullary mass arising from the filum terminale in a young adult is most consistent with:
options: Schwannoma of a lumbar nerve root
Myxopapillary ependymoma of the filum terminale
Meningioma
Herniated lumbar disc
stages.explanation: A lobulated, sausage-shaped, avidly enhancing intradural-extramedullary mass arising from the filum terminale in a young adult is the classic appearance of a myxopapillary ependymoma (WHO grade II/the characteristic filum terminale ependymoma). Posterior vertebral scalloping and canal widening reflect a slow-growing intradural tumor. A schwannoma typically arises from a nerve root and may have a dumbbell shape extending through a foramen; it is in the differential but is less specifically tied to the filum. Meningioma is more common in the thoracic spine of older women and arises from dura, not the filum. A herniated disc is extradural, does not enhance avidly as an intradural mass, and would not explain a filum-based lesion. The location and morphology point to myxopapillary ependymoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before proceeding to surgery for this presumed filum terminale tumor, which additional study is most important to obtain?
options: PET-CT of the whole body to find a primary cancer
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuraxis (brain and complete spine) to assess for additional lesions or CSF drop metastases
Bone scan of the lumbar spine
CT myelogram of the lumbar spine only
stages.explanation: Myxopapillary ependymomas can disseminate through the CSF and produce drop metastases, so contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire neuraxis (brain and complete spine) is essential before surgery to detect additional lesions and to plan the extent of treatment and follow-up. Whole-body PET-CT is used to hunt for a systemic primary or metastatic carcinoma; this is a primary CNS tumor in a young patient without a known cancer, so PET is not the priority. A bone scan evaluates osseous metastatic disease and would not detect intradural CNS tumor spread. A lumbar-only CT myelogram is invasive, limited to one region, and inferior to whole-neuraxis MRI for characterizing intradural disease and detecting distant seeding. Complete neuraxis MRI directly informs the surgical and surveillance plan.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why is en bloc gross-total resection (rather than piecemeal removal) particularly emphasized for this tumor?
options: Because piecemeal removal causes more blood loss only
Because capsular violation/piecemeal resection increases the risk of CSF tumor seeding and local recurrence; en bloc removal of the encapsulated tumor reduces recurrence
Because en bloc resection avoids the need for any follow-up imaging
Because myxopapillary ependymomas are radioresistant and surgery is irrelevant
stages.explanation: Myxopapillary ependymomas of the filum are encapsulated but can seed the CSF if the capsule is violated. En bloc gross-total resection of the intact tumor maximizes cure and minimizes the risk of CSF dissemination and local recurrence, whereas piecemeal removal that breaches the capsule increases seeding and recurrence rates—this is the central surgical principle. The concern is not merely blood loss. En bloc resection does not eliminate the need for surveillance imaging—follow-up MRI is still required to detect recurrence or drop metastases. The tumors are not irrelevant to surgery; resection is the primary treatment, with radiotherapy reserved for subtotal resection, recurrence, or dissemination.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After gross-total en bloc resection, what is the most appropriate follow-up plan?
options: No further imaging; consider her cured permanently
Surveillance MRI of the spine (and consideration of full neuraxis imaging) to monitor for local recurrence or CSF drop metastases
Immediate whole-spine radiotherapy regardless of resection completeness
Routine lifelong chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Even after gross-total resection, myxopapillary ependymomas can recur locally or seed the CSF, so surveillance MRI of the spine—with consideration of full neuraxis imaging given the seeding potential—is essential to detect recurrence or drop metastases early. Declaring permanent cure with no imaging is unsafe because late recurrence occurs. Whole-spine radiotherapy is not automatic after a complete en bloc resection; it is reserved for subtotal resection, capsular violation, recurrence, or disseminated disease. Routine lifelong chemotherapy is not standard for this tumor; chemotherapy plays a limited, salvage role. Structured imaging surveillance balances early detection against unnecessary treatment toxicity.

cases.diagnosis: Myxopapillary ependymoma of the filum terminale (intradural lumbar mass)
pearls: Nocturnal, recumbency-worsened, treatment-refractory back pain in a young adult is a red flag—get a contrast MRI to look for an intradural tumor.
A lobulated, enhancing, sausage-shaped mass arising from the filum terminale is the classic myxopapillary ependymoma; expect canal widening and vertebral scalloping.
En bloc gross-total resection is key—violating the capsule risks CSF seeding and recurrence.
Follow with surveillance MRI (consider full neuraxis); reserve radiotherapy for subtotal resection, recurrence, or dissemination.

cases.title: Hand Clumsiness and a Burn That Went Unnoticed
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of clumsy hands and not feeling a burn on his forearm.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with several months of progressive hand clumsiness, mild upper-extremity weakness, and an episode where he burned his forearm on a stove without feeling it until he saw a blister. He notes a 'cape-like' loss of temperature and pain sensation across his shoulders and arms. No bowel/bladder symptoms. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. No drug allergies. He is an engineer, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: father had 'kidney tumors' and died of a brain tumor in his 50s; a sister had eye problems requiring laser treatment. Review of systems: occasional headaches, no weight loss, no visual loss. He denies trauma. He recalls episodic flushing and palpitations a few times but attributed them to stress. There is no history of meningitis or prior spine injury. He has not had prior imaging. He notes intermittent dull interscapular pain.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented. Dissociated sensory loss: reduced pain and temperature in a cape-like (suspended) distribution over the shoulders and arms, with preserved light touch and proprioception. Mild intrinsic hand muscle weakness and atrophy; diminished upper-extremity reflexes. Lower extremities with mild hyperreflexia. No facial findings. Fundoscopic exam shows a small peripheral retinal vascular lesion in one eye. Gait normal. No scoliosis appreciated. Blood pressure elevated; no abdominal bruit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A cape-like dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature lost, light touch preserved) with hand weakness localizes to which structure?
options: Dorsal columns of the spinal cord
Central spinal cord (region of the central canal/crossing spinothalamic fibers)
Peripheral sensory nerves (length-dependent)
Parietal cortex
stages.explanation: Dissociated sensory loss—loss of pain and temperature with preserved light touch and proprioception in a suspended, cape-like distribution—reflects disruption of the decussating spinothalamic fibers near the central canal of the cervical cord (a central cord/syrinx pattern). Hand weakness/atrophy and reduced upper-limb reflexes reflect involvement of anterior horn cells, with corticospinal involvement producing lower-limb hyperreflexia. Dorsal column lesions impair proprioception and vibration, not selective pain/temperature loss. Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy causes distal stocking-glove loss, not a suspended cape-like pattern. A parietal cortical lesion causes contralateral cortical sensory loss, not a bilateral suspended dissociated pattern. The findings indicate a central cord process, classically a syrinx, prompting cord imaging.

stages.label: MRI SPINE
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine with contrast: an intramedullary, avidly and homogeneously enhancing nodule in the dorsal cervical cord
Prominent flow voids at the periphery of the lesion (highly vascular)
A large associated syrinx (syringomyelia) spanning several cord segments
The enhancing nodule abuts the pial surface posteriorly
No leptomeningeal disease elsewhere

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A highly vascular, intramedullary enhancing nodule with flow voids and an associated syrinx in a young adult is most consistent with:
options: Spinal hemangioblastoma
Astrocytoma of the cord
Multiple sclerosis plaque
Spinal arteriovenous fistula
stages.explanation: An avidly enhancing, highly vascular intramedullary nodule with peripheral flow voids and a large associated syrinx is the classic appearance of a spinal hemangioblastoma—a benign, vascular tumor that often abuts the pial surface and produces a syrinx. Cord astrocytoma is typically infiltrative with less discrete, heterogeneous enhancement and lacks prominent flow voids. A multiple sclerosis plaque is a short-segment T2 lesion that may enhance transiently but is not a vascular nodule with flow voids and would not produce a large syrinx with this morphology. A spinal AV fistula causes cord edema and enlarged perimedullary veins (serpentine flow voids) without a discrete enhancing tumor nodule. The vascular nodule-plus-syrinx pattern points to hemangioblastoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His family history (renal tumors, retinal lesion, a relative's brain tumor) and his own retinal finding and hypertension suggest a syndrome. What evaluation is most appropriate?
options: Reassure that spinal hemangioblastomas are always sporadic

Evaluate for von Hippel-Lindau disease: genetic testing (VHL gene) and surveillance including brain/spine MRI, abdominal imaging for renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic lesions, retinal exam, and plasma/urine metanephrines for pheochromocytoma
Order only a skin biopsy
Begin antihypertensives and provide no further workup
stages.explanation: Multiple hemangioblastomas or a hemangioblastoma with suggestive features—retinal hemangioblastoma, family history of renal tumors and CNS tumors, episodic flushing/palpitations, and hypertension—should prompt evaluation for von Hippel-Lindau disease. This includes VHL genetic testing and comprehensive surveillance: brain and spine MRI (CNS hemangioblastomas), abdominal imaging for renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors/cysts, dilated retinal examination, and plasma or urine metanephrines to screen for pheochromocytoma (relevant before any surgery/anesthesia). Assuming the lesion is sporadic ignores clear syndromic clues. A skin biopsy is irrelevant. Treating hypertension without screening for pheochromocytoma and other VHL tumors would miss life-threatening, treatable conditions. VHL workup is essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Pheochromocytoma is excluded. For his symptomatic spinal hemangioblastoma with a syrinx, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Microsurgical resection of the enhancing tumor nodule
Direct surgical drainage of the syrinx alone, leaving the nodule
Embolization of the nodule as definitive cure in all cases
Empiric chemotherapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for a symptomatic spinal hemangioblastoma is microsurgical resection of the tumor nodule; because the syrinx is produced by the vascular nodule, removing the nodule typically leads to syrinx resolution—so draining the syrinx alone is inadequate and the cavity would recur. Resection is feasible since these tumors are well-circumscribed and often pial-based. Preoperative embolization can reduce vascularity for large lesions but is an adjunct, not a stand-alone cure for most. Radiosurgery is an option for surgically inaccessible or multiple lesions but is not the first choice for an accessible symptomatic nodule. Chemotherapy has no established role for hemangioblastoma. Resecting the nodule addresses both tumor and syrinx.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If genetic testing confirms VHL, what is the most appropriate long-term plan for him and his family?
options: No surveillance needed once the spinal lesion is removed
Lifelong multidisciplinary surveillance (serial CNS MRI, abdominal imaging, retinal exams, biochemical screening for pheochromocytoma) and genetic counseling/testing for at-risk relatives
Annual chest radiographs only
Surveillance for the patient but no family testing
stages.explanation: Confirmed VHL is an autosomal dominant syndrome predisposing to multiple, often metachronous tumors, so lifelong multidisciplinary surveillance is required: serial brain and spine MRI for hemangioblastomas, abdominal imaging for renal cell carcinoma and pancreatic lesions, dilated retinal exams for retinal hemangioblastomas, and periodic biochemical screening for pheochromocytoma. Genetic counseling and testing should be offered to at-risk first-degree relatives so affected individuals can begin early surveillance. Stopping surveillance after removing one lesion ignores the high risk of new tumors. Chest radiographs do not address the relevant organs. Limiting testing to the patient and excluding family members fails the at-risk relatives who could benefit from early detection and treatment.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal hemangioblastoma with syringomyelia, prompting diagnosis of von Hippel-Lindau disease
pearls: Cape-like dissociated sensory loss (pain/temperature lost, touch preserved) signals a central cord/syrinx; image the cervical cord.
A highly vascular intramedullary enhancing nodule with flow voids and an associated syrinx is the classic spinal hemangioblastoma.
Hemangioblastoma + retinal lesion + family history of renal/CNS tumors → evaluate for VHL (gene testing, CNS/abdominal imaging, retinal exam, metanephrines).
Resect the tumor nodule to treat the lesion and resolve the syrinx; exclude pheochromocytoma before surgery, and arrange lifelong VHL surveillance and family testing.

cases.title: Relentless Headache and Confusion After Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of worsening headache, nausea, and new confusion with double vision.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman with treated breast cancer is admitted for 2 weeks of progressively severe headache, nausea, intermittent vomiting, and new confusion. She also reports transient double vision and brief episodes of facial numbness. PMH: metastatic ER-positive breast cancer with prior bone and liver metastases; hypertension. Medications: endocrine therapy, antihypertensive, antiemetics as needed. No anticoagulants; no drug allergies. She lives with her husband. Review of systems: low-grade headaches becoming constant and worse when lying down, plus new urinary incontinence and gait unsteadiness. No fever or neck stiffness initially. She has had multiple cranial-nerve complaints over the past month—diplopia, then a different facial sensory change—suggesting more than one nerve is involved. She denies recent trauma. There is no rash. Her family notes she has become slower in her thinking over the past 10 days. She has had no recent intracranial surgery or shunt.
cases.exam: Drowsy but rousable, oriented to person and place, slowed responses. Bilateral papilledema. Multiple cranial neuropathies: a partial right CN VI palsy (diplopia), reduced sensation in the left V2 distribution, and mild left lower facial weakness. Neck supple early in course. Motor 5/5 with no clear focal limb deficit; gait unsteady and wide-based. Reflexes symmetric. The pattern of multiple, multifocal cranial neuropathies with signs of raised ICP is striking. No focal hemiparesis; no cerebellar dysmetria.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Multiple simultaneous cranial neuropathies at different levels, headache, and raised-ICP signs in a cancer patient should raise suspicion for what process?
options: A single brain parenchymal metastasis
Leptomeningeal (carcinomatous) involvement seeding multiple cranial nerves
Migraine with aura
Bell palsy
stages.explanation: The hallmark of leptomeningeal (carcinomatous) disease is the involvement of multiple neuraxis levels simultaneously—classically several cranial neuropathies at once (e.g., CN VI, V, VII) plus headache and signs of raised intracranial pressure, sometimes with spinal radiculopathies. A single parenchymal metastasis typically produces a focal deficit referable to one location, not scattered multifocal cranial nerve involvement. Migraine with aura does not cause fixed multiple cranial neuropathies or papilledema. Bell palsy is an isolated peripheral CN VII palsy and cannot explain CN VI and V findings or raised ICP. In a patient with metastatic breast cancer, multifocal cranial neuropathies strongly suggest carcinomatous meningitis, warranting MRI and CSF studies.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN & CSF
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: diffuse leptomeningeal and cranial-nerve enhancement; sulcal/cisternal enhancement with nodularity along cranial nerves
Communicating hydrocephalus with enlarged ventricles and effaced sulci; transependymal flow
Lumbar puncture (after imaging): opening pressure 32 cm H2O
CSF protein 145 mg/dL (elevated)
CSF glucose 32 mg/dL (low; serum glucose 110 mg/dL)
CSF WBC 28/µL (lymphocytic pleocytosis)
CSF cytology: malignant epithelial cells consistent with adenocarcinoma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement, communicating hydrocephalus, elevated CSF protein, low glucose, pleocytosis, and positive cytology, what is the diagnosis?
options: Bacterial meningitis
Carcinomatous (neoplastic) meningitis with communicating hydrocephalus
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
stages.explanation: Positive CSF cytology showing malignant adenocarcinoma cells, with diffuse leptomeningeal/cranial-nerve enhancement, elevated CSF protein, low glucose (consumed by tumor), lymphocytic pleocytosis, and high opening pressure, confirms carcinomatous (neoplastic) meningitis; impaired CSF resorption from leptomeningeal infiltration causes communicating hydrocephalus. Bacterial meningitis usually shows neutrophilic pleocytosis, very high protein, fever/neck stiffness, and positive Gram stain/culture—not malignant cytology. Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with the classic triad at normal opening pressure without malignant cells or leptomeningeal enhancement. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension causes raised pressure but with normal CSF constituents, normal imaging (no leptomeningeal enhancement), and typically occurs in young obese women. The malignant cytology is diagnostic.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The first CSF cytology had been negative in a different patient with the same clinical picture. What is the most appropriate way to maximize the diagnostic yield of CSF cytology?
options: Accept the single negative result and exclude leptomeningeal disease
Send a larger blood-culture set instead of CSF
Repeat the lumbar puncture, sending a high-volume CSF sample and obtaining serial (up to three) samples, processing them promptly
Proceed directly to brain biopsy
stages.explanation: CSF cytology is the gold standard for diagnosing leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, but a single sample has limited sensitivity; yield improves substantially with repeat lumbar punctures (up to three samples), a high-volume CSF aliquot, and prompt processing of fresh specimens. A single negative cytology therefore does NOT exclude the disease, so accepting it is wrong—especially when MRI shows leptomeningeal enhancement. Blood cultures are irrelevant to detecting malignant cells in the CSF. Brain biopsy is invasive and not the appropriate way to diagnose a CSF-based, leptomeningeal process; meningeal/cisternal disease is sampled via CSF, not parenchymal biopsy. In this patient, cytology is already positive, but understanding how to maximize yield (volume, repetition, rapid processing) is high-yield and clinically essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has symptomatic communicating hydrocephalus and confirmed leptomeningeal disease. To deliver intrathecal chemotherapy and address raised pressure, which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Repeated daily lumbar punctures indefinitely for both drug delivery and CSF drainage
Placement of an Ommaya reservoir for reliable intrathecal chemotherapy delivery (and CSF sampling), with CSF diversion as needed for symptomatic hydrocephalus
Whole-brain radiotherapy alone with no CSF access device
Observation with analgesics only
stages.explanation: An Ommaya reservoir (ventricular access device) provides reliable, repeated intraventricular delivery of intrathecal chemotherapy and easy CSF sampling, ensuring more uniform drug distribution than repeated lumbar punctures, which are uncomfortable, technically variable, and yield inconsistent drug distribution. For symptomatic communicating hydrocephalus, CSF diversion (e.g., a shunt, sometimes with an on/off valve to allow intrathecal therapy) addresses raised pressure. Repeated indefinite daily LPs are impractical and inferior for drug delivery. Whole-brain radiotherapy can palliate bulky or symptomatic sites but does not, by itself, treat the entire neuraxis or relieve hydrocephalus and is typically combined with other measures. Observation with analgesics alone neglects treatable raised pressure and active leptomeningeal disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: While planning treatment, which counseling point about prognosis and goals of care is most accurate?
options: Carcinomatous meningitis is typically curable with intrathecal chemotherapy

Carcinomatous meningitis carries a poor prognosis; treatment (intrathecal/systemic therapy, focal radiotherapy, CSF diversion) is largely palliative, aiming to stabilize neurologic function and quality of life
No treatment changes outcomes, so comfort care alone is the only option
Prognosis is excellent and similar to a single brain metastasis
stages.explanation: Carcinomatous meningitis carries a poor prognosis (often months), and therapy—intrathecal and/or systemic chemotherapy, focal radiotherapy to symptomatic/bulky sites, and CSF diversion for hydrocephalus—is largely palliative, aiming to stabilize or improve neurologic function and quality of life rather than cure. It is not typically curable, so claiming cure is inaccurate. However, it is also incorrect that treatment never helps; appropriate therapy can relieve symptoms (e.g., shunting for hydrocephalus, radiotherapy for cranial neuropathies) and modestly prolong meaningful function, so comfort care alone is not the only option and should be a shared, goals-of-care decision. The prognosis is far worse than a single resectable brain metastasis. Honest, goal-concordant counseling is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Carcinomatous (leptomeningeal) meningitis from metastatic breast cancer with communicating hydrocephalus
pearls: Multiple simultaneous cranial neuropathies plus headache and raised ICP in a cancer patient = leptomeningeal carcinomatosis until proven otherwise.
CSF shows high protein, low glucose, lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated opening pressure, and—diagnostically—malignant cytology; MRI shows leptomeningeal/cranial-nerve enhancement.
Communicating hydrocephalus arises from impaired CSF resorption; an Ommaya reservoir enables reliable intrathecal chemo, with CSF diversion for symptomatic hydrocephalus.
Prognosis is poor; treatment is palliative—focus on neurologic stabilization, symptom relief, and goals-of-care discussions.

cases.title: A Slowly Growing Forehead Bump and New Headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless firm swelling over the forehead with mild morning headaches for several months.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents to neuro-oncology clinic with a firm, non-tender swelling over the right frontal region that he first noticed about 8 months ago and feels has slowly enlarged. He also reports dull bifrontal headaches, worse on waking, for the past 2 months, occasionally with mild nausea. He denies seizures, focal weakness, visual change, or trauma. Importantly, as a child he received cranial radiation at age 6 for tinea capitis of the scalp (a historical practice), and he recalls scalp X-ray treatments at a clinic in his home country. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works as an accountant, never-smoker, social alcohol, no drugs. Family history: no known brain tumors, but a maternal aunt had breast cancer at 60. Review of systems is otherwise negative; no weight loss, fevers, or night sweats. He is anxious because an internet search linked his childhood treatment to brain tumors and wants to know whether the lump is dangerous.
cases.exam: Alert, fully oriented, no distress. A 3 cm firm, immobile, non-tender mass is palpable over the right frontal bone with overlying intact skin and a small area of alopecia consistent with old scalp treatment. No bruit. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; visual fields full to confrontation and fundi show sharp disc margins without papilledema. Motor 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes 2+ symmetric, gait normal, no pronator drift. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Skin exam: no café-au-lait macules or neurofibromas.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's history most strongly raises concern for a radiation-induced intracranial neoplasm?
options: Social alcohol use and an accountant occupation
A maternal aunt with breast cancer at age 60
Childhood cranial/scalp irradiation decades earlier with a new dural-based mass
Headaches that improve when lying down
stages.explanation: Prior ionizing radiation to the head — even low-dose treatments like historical scalp irradiation for tinea capitis — is the single best-established environmental risk factor for meningioma, with tumors arising after a long latency (often 20-40 years). A new dural-based mass in this context is highly suggestive. Alcohol and a sedentary job are not meningioma risk factors. A single aunt with later-onset breast cancer is weak family history and does not implicate a hereditary tumor syndrome. Morning-predominant headaches that reflect raised intracranial pressure are typically worse, not better, when lying flat, so that phrasing is incorrect and not the key risk factor. The combination of remote irradiation and a new dural lesion is what should focus the differential here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Plain skull radiograph only
Excisional biopsy of the scalp mass in clinic
Whole-body PET-CT to search for a primary cancer
MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced brain MRI is the imaging study of choice for a suspected meningioma; it characterizes a dural-based, avidly and homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass, shows the dural tail, and assesses brain and venous-sinus involvement and surrounding edema. A plain skull film cannot characterize an intracranial soft-tissue mass. Blind clinic excision is inappropriate before imaging defines whether the lesion is purely extracranial or has intracranial extension. Whole-body PET-CT is not the initial test for a presumed primary CNS tumor and would add cost and radiation before the lesion is even characterized. MRI both confirms the diagnosis and guides whether surgery or surveillance is appropriate.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without gadolinium:
4.1 cm right frontal extra-axial, dural-based mass with homogeneous avid enhancement
Prominent enhancing 'dural tail' sign along adjacent dura
Hyperostosis of the overlying frontal bone
Moderate surrounding T2/FLAIR vasogenic edema in the right frontal lobe
Mass effect with 4 mm midline shift; no hydrocephalus
No restricted diffusion to suggest abscess
A second 8 mm dural-based enhancing nodule along the left convexity

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the imaging, which finding makes a radiation-induced (rather than sporadic) meningioma more likely, and what does the second lesion suggest?
options: A solitary lesion with calcification confirms sporadic disease
The dural tail proves the lesion is malignant
Multiplicity of meningiomas is more characteristic of radiation-induced disease
Hyperostosis indicates metastatic carcinoma to bone
stages.explanation: Radiation-induced meningiomas are more often multiple, occur at younger ages, and carry a higher rate of atypical/aggressive histology and recurrence than sporadic tumors; the second dural-based nodule fits this pattern. A solitary calcified lesion is more typical of an indolent sporadic meningioma and is not a marker that excludes radiation etiology. The dural tail is characteristic of meningioma but is not a marker of malignancy. Hyperostosis is a classic meningioma feature reflecting reactive bony change, not carcinomatous metastasis. Other supportive clues to a radiation etiology include onset within a previously irradiated field and a documented latency of years to decades, both present here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The dominant mass is symptomatic with edema and mass effect. What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in 12 months for both lesions
Surgical resection of the symptomatic dominant mass with histologic grading, plus a short course of corticosteroids for edema
Empiric whole-brain radiotherapy
Start cytotoxic chemotherapy with temozolomide
stages.explanation: A symptomatic meningioma with edema, mass effect, and midline shift warrants maximal safe surgical resection, which relieves mass effect and provides tissue for WHO grading — especially important here given the higher rate of atypical histology in radiation-induced tumors; a short corticosteroid course controls peritumoral edema perioperatively. Observation is reserved for small, asymptomatic, incidentally found meningiomas, not a 4 cm mass causing shift. Whole-brain radiotherapy is inappropriate and would add radiation to an already radiation-injured brain. Temozolomide has no established role in primary meningioma management and is not standard first-line therapy. Surgery is therefore both diagnostic and therapeutic in this scenario.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Pathology returns as WHO grade 2 (atypical) meningioma after gross-total resection. What is the most appropriate long-term plan?
options: No further imaging needed after gross-total resection
Immediate re-operation to remove the asymptomatic 8 mm lesion
Lifelong systemic chemotherapy to prevent recurrence
Serial surveillance MRI, with consideration of adjuvant radiotherapy given atypical (grade 2) histology, and monitoring of the smaller lesion
stages.explanation: Atypical (WHO grade 2) meningiomas recur more frequently than grade 1 tumors, so structured surveillance MRI is essential, and adjuvant radiotherapy is considered — strongly after subtotal resection and increasingly even after gross-total resection of grade 2 disease. The small contralateral nodule can be followed radiographically while it remains asymptomatic. Stopping imaging ignores the high recurrence risk. Operating on an 8 mm asymptomatic lesion is not indicated when it can be safely observed. Systemic chemotherapy is not a standard preventive therapy for meningioma. Surveillance intervals are tightened for grade 2 disease, and any regrowth prompts earlier re-evaluation for repeat surgery or focused radiation.

cases.diagnosis: Radiation-induced (WHO grade 2, atypical) meningioma arising decades after childhood scalp irradiation
pearls: Prior cranial irradiation — even low-dose historical scalp treatments — is the strongest environmental risk factor for meningioma, with latency often spanning 20-40 years.
Radiation-induced meningiomas tend to be multiple, present at younger ages, and show higher rates of atypical/anaplastic histology and recurrence than sporadic tumors.
Contrast-enhanced MRI showing a dural-based, homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass with a dural tail and hyperostosis is the imaging signature of meningioma.
Symptomatic meningiomas with mass effect are treated with maximal safe resection and WHO grading; atypical (grade 2) tumors warrant close MRI surveillance and consideration of adjuvant radiotherapy.

cases.title: Forgetfulness and Word-Finding Trouble After Brain Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive short-term memory problems and mental slowing developing months after brain radiation.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman returns to neuro-oncology clinic reporting that over the past 6-9 months she has become increasingly forgetful — misplacing keys, forgetting recent conversations, and struggling to recall names and words. Her husband says she repeats questions and has trouble following multi-step instructions. Fourteen months ago she completed whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT) for multiple small brain metastases from breast cancer; her systemic disease is currently in remission on endocrine therapy. She denies headache, focal weakness, seizures, incontinence, or gait disturbance. Mood is mildly low but she denies anhedonia or suicidality and sleeps adequately. PMH: hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer, hypertension. Medications: letrozole 2.5 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily; she finished dexamethasone months ago. Allergies: none. Social: retired teacher, lives with husband, never-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: mother with hypertension; no early dementia. She is frightened that her cancer has returned to her brain and is also worried she is developing Alzheimer disease.
cases.exam: Pleasant, cooperative, fully oriented to person, place, and time. Speech fluent with occasional word-finding pauses. On bedside cognitive testing she has difficulty with delayed recall (1 of 3 words at 5 minutes) and serial subtraction; clock draw is mildly disorganized. Cranial nerves intact; visual fields full, no papilledema. Motor 5/5, normal tone, no drift; sensation intact; reflexes symmetric. Gait normal with intact tandem; no ataxia, no urinary complaints. No frontal release signs. PHQ-9 score is mildly elevated but not in the major-depression range.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step to evaluate her new cognitive decline?
options: Reassure her this is normal aging and follow up in a year
Start a cholinesterase inhibitor empirically for Alzheimer disease
Obtain contrast-enhanced brain MRI to exclude tumor recurrence/progression before attributing symptoms to treatment effects
Refer directly to hospice given presumed disease progression
stages.explanation: New cognitive decline in a patient treated for brain metastases must first be evaluated with contrast-enhanced MRI to exclude recurrent or progressive tumor, leptomeningeal disease, and radiation necrosis before attributing symptoms to radiation-induced cognitive change. Calling it normal aging is unsafe given her cancer history and objective memory deficits. Empiric Alzheimer therapy is premature without excluding structural causes and before a dementia diagnosis is established. Hospice is inappropriate — her systemic disease is in remission and no progression has been demonstrated. MRI also screens for treatable mimics such as hydrocephalus, ensuring a structural cause is ruled out first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with/without gadolinium:
No new or enhancing parenchymal lesions; prior metastases not visible (treated)
No leptomeningeal enhancement
Diffuse symmetric periventricular T2/FLAIR white-matter hyperintensity (leukoencephalopathy)
Mild generalized volume loss for age
No restricted diffusion, no necrosis with central enhancement
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
B12 540 pg/mL
CMP within normal limits
MMSE 25/30 (deficits in delayed recall and attention)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given normal metabolic labs, no tumor recurrence, and diffuse white-matter changes, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Recurrent metastatic disease
Radiation-induced cognitive decline (delayed radiation neurotoxicity / leukoencephalopathy)
Vitamin B12 deficiency dementia
Major depressive disorder fully explaining her deficits
stages.explanation: The picture — subacute decline months after WBRT, prominent memory and attention/processing-speed deficits, diffuse leukoencephalopathy on MRI without tumor recurrence, and a normal metabolic workup — is classic for delayed radiation-induced cognitive decline. Recurrence is excluded by the absence of enhancing or leptomeningeal disease. B12 deficiency is ruled out by a normal level. Depression can impair cognition, but hers is only mild and does not account for the objective leukoencephalopathy and memory deficits, though it may be a minor contributor. The temporal link to prior whole-brain radiation and the diffuse white-matter signal change together make delayed radiation neurotoxicity the unifying explanation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic intervention has the best evidence for treating radiation-induced cognitive decline in this setting?
options: High-dose corticosteroids indefinitely
A benzodiazepine to improve memory consolidation
Memantine, an NMDA-receptor antagonist shown to delay cognitive decline after whole-brain radiation
Bevacizumab as first-line for diffuse leukoencephalopathy
stages.explanation: Memantine, an NMDA-receptor antagonist, has randomized-trial evidence (RTOG 0614) showing it delays the time to cognitive decline and helps preserve processing speed, executive function, and memory after whole-brain radiation, making it the best-supported pharmacologic option here. Indefinite high-dose steroids cause serious toxicity and are not a treatment for radiation-induced cognitive decline. Benzodiazepines impair memory consolidation and worsen cognition rather than improving it. Bevacizumab is used for focal radiation necrosis with surrounding edema, not for diffuse radiation-related cognitive decline, and is not first-line here. Memantine is generally well tolerated and titrated up over several weeks.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Her sister is about to receive WBRT for brain metastases and asks how to lower her own risk of this complication. What technique reduces memory decline by sparing a key structure?
options: Adding concurrent whole-brain chemotherapy
Increasing the total radiation dose to shorten treatment
Routine prophylactic surgical biopsy of the hippocampus
Hippocampal-avoidance whole-brain radiotherapy, often combined with memantine
stages.explanation: Hippocampal-avoidance WBRT uses intensity-modulated planning to limit dose to the neural stem-cell niche in the hippocampus; trials (e.g., NRG CC001) show better preservation of memory and quality of life, and combining it with memantine is now a standard neuroprotective approach. Adding whole-brain chemotherapy increases neurotoxicity rather than preventing it. Escalating the dose worsens normal-tissue injury and cognitive risk. Surgical hippocampal biopsy is not a preventive measure and would itself cause injury. Sparing the dentate gyrus stem-cell niche thought to underlie new memory formation is why this combination has become the preferred strategy when whole-brain radiation is required.

cases.diagnosis: Delayed radiation-induced cognitive decline (radiation leukoencephalopathy) after whole-brain radiotherapy
pearls: Always exclude tumor recurrence, leptomeningeal disease, and metabolic causes with MRI and labs before attributing post-radiation cognitive decline to treatment effect.
Delayed radiation-induced cognitive decline classically presents months to years after WBRT with memory, attention, and processing-speed deficits plus diffuse leukoencephalopathy on FLAIR imaging.
Memantine delays post-WBRT cognitive decline (RTOG 0614) and is the best-evidenced pharmacologic intervention.
Hippocampal-avoidance WBRT (NRG CC001), ideally with memantine, reduces memory loss by sparing the hippocampal neural stem-cell niche.

cases.title: Calf Swelling and Breathlessness in a Patient with a Brain Tumor
cases.chiefComplaint: New unilateral leg swelling and acute shortness of breath in a hospitalized glioma patient.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man admitted 6 days ago after craniotomy for a newly diagnosed left frontal high-grade glioma develops acute dyspnea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain this morning. The covering team is called when he becomes tachycardic. Over the past 2 days he had noticed his right calf was swollen and tender but attributed it to lying in bed. He has been relatively immobile postoperatively. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: dexamethasone 4 mg every 6 hours (tapering), levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily, insulin sliding scale, lisinopril 10 mg daily; he received subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis intermittently but several doses were held around surgery. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), no alcohol. Family history: father had a myocardial infarction. He denies hemoptysis. The neurosurgery note documents no new intracranial hemorrhage on the most recent postoperative CT. He is anxious and short of breath.
cases.exam: Anxious, tachypneic, speaking in short sentences. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally without wheeze or focal crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur; jugular venous pressure mildly elevated. Right calf is swollen with 3 cm greater circumference than the left, warm, tender along the deep venous tract, with mild pitting edema; left leg normal. Neuro: awake, oriented, mild expressive aphasia stable from baseline, right pronator drift unchanged, no new deficit. Surgical incision clean and dry without hematoma. No chest wall tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given hypoxia, tachycardia, pleuritic pain, and a swollen tender calf, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: D-dimer to rule out venous thromboembolism
Empiric antibiotics for presumed pneumonia
Echocardiogram as the sole diagnostic test
CT pulmonary angiography (with bilateral lower-extremity Doppler) and supplemental oxygen
stages.explanation: This is a high-pretest-probability presentation for pulmonary embolism with a likely concurrent DVT, so CT pulmonary angiography is the definitive test, paired with lower-extremity Doppler and oxygen support. A D-dimer is unhelpful when pretest probability is high and is invariably elevated in postoperative cancer patients, so neither a positive nor a negative result would change management. Empiric antibiotics do not fit clear lungs and a swollen calf. Echocardiography alone may show right-heart strain but cannot confirm or exclude PE and is not the primary diagnostic test. Definitive imaging is needed promptly given his hypoxia and instability risk.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT pulmonary angiography: filling defects in the right lower-lobe segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries; right ventricle not enlarged (RV:LV < 1)
Lower-extremity Doppler: acute occlusive DVT in the right popliteal and femoral veins
Noncontrast head CT (same day): postoperative changes, no new intracranial hemorrhage
Troponin: mildly elevated
BNP: mildly elevated
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, Platelets 233 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR > 60
INR 1.0

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this PE best classified and what does it imply for management?
options: Massive PE requiring immediate systemic thrombolysis
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
Acute submassive/low-risk PE with DVT; hemodynamically stable, candidate for anticoagulation
Septic pulmonary emboli requiring antibiotics
stages.explanation: He is normotensive with right-lower-lobe segmental PE, no RV enlargement, and only mildly elevated cardiac biomarkers — consistent with acute, hemodynamically stable PE (low-to-intermediate risk) plus DVT, for which anticoagulation is the cornerstone. Massive PE requires hypotension or shock, which he does not have, so thrombolysis is not indicated and is especially hazardous after recent craniotomy. CTEPH is a chronic condition, inconsistent with this acute postoperative presentation. Septic emboli would suggest an infectious source such as endocarditis, not present here. Risk stratification by hemodynamics, RV size, and biomarkers guides monitoring intensity; here stability favors straightforward anticoagulation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Cancer patients have a high VTE rate, but this man is days out from craniotomy for a brain tumor. What is the most appropriate anticoagulation decision?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., LMWH or a DOAC) is indicated; the proven VTE generally outweighs bleeding risk, with timing/dose individualized after confirming no active intracranial bleed
Avoid all anticoagulation indefinitely because any brain tumor is an absolute contraindication
Aspirin 81 mg daily alone
Permanent IVC filter as the definitive and sole therapy
stages.explanation: Most patients with brain tumors and confirmed acute VTE should receive therapeutic anticoagulation, because the risk from the thromboembolism usually exceeds the intracranial bleeding risk; LMWH and certain DOACs are both acceptable in cancer-associated VTE. The recent postoperative CT confirms no active hemorrhage, supporting initiation with individualized timing. A blanket prohibition is incorrect — brain tumor alone is not an absolute contraindication. Aspirin is inadequate for treating acute DVT/PE. An IVC filter is reserved for true contraindications to anticoagulation or recurrent VTE despite therapy, not as primary stand-alone treatment. Therapeutic anticoagulation is therefore the standard of care here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which scenario would most appropriately favor an IVC filter (with deferral of anticoagulation) instead of starting anticoagulation now?
options: Stable postoperative imaging with no bleeding and normal platelets
Mild anxiety about taking injections
A documented acute intratumoral or postoperative intracranial hemorrhage making anticoagulation hazardous
A family history of myocardial infarction
stages.explanation: An IVC filter is indicated when there is a genuine contraindication to anticoagulation — most relevantly here, an acute intracranial or intratumoral hemorrhage that makes anticoagulation dangerous in the short term, providing mechanical protection from further PE until anticoagulation can be safely started. Stable imaging with normal platelets actually supports proceeding with anticoagulation. Patient anxiety about injections is addressed with education or an oral agent, not a filter. A family history of MI is irrelevant to the bleeding-versus-clotting decision. Importantly, a filter is a temporizing mechanical measure, not a substitute for anticoagulation, which should still follow once bleeding risk resolves.

cases.diagnosis: Cancer-associated venous thromboembolism (acute DVT with submassive pulmonary embolism) in a postoperative glioma patient
pearls: Brain tumor patients — especially with high-grade glioma and after surgery/immobility — have a markedly elevated risk of VTE; maintain a low threshold for CT pulmonary angiography.
D-dimer is unhelpful in high-pretest-probability or postoperative cancer patients; go directly to definitive imaging.
A brain tumor is NOT an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation — confirmed acute VTE usually warrants therapeutic anticoagulation, as clotting risk typically outweighs bleeding risk.
Reserve IVC filters for true contraindications to anticoagulation (e.g., active intracranial hemorrhage) or recurrent VTE despite adequate therapy.

cases.title: High Sugars and Weak Legs During a Hospital Stay
cases.chiefComplaint: Rising blood glucose and new difficulty rising from a chair in a patient on long-term steroids.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman with a known brain metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma has been hospitalized for symptom control and is on chronic high-dose dexamethasone (8 mg daily for 7 weeks) for peritumoral edema. The nursing team reports persistently elevated fingerstick glucose readings and the patient now complains of difficulty standing from a seated position and climbing the few steps to the bathroom. She also notes a round, fuller face and easy bruising on her arms. She denies fever, cough, dysuria, or chest pain, but mentions her appetite has surged. PMH: lung adenocarcinoma with brain metastasis, mild osteopenia, no prior diabetes. Medications: dexamethasone 8 mg daily, ondansetron as needed, omeprazole 20 mg daily, acetaminophen; no diabetes medications. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: former smoker (30 pack-years), lives with her daughter. Family history: mother with type 2 diabetes. She is worried she is becoming weaker and asks whether her steroids could be causing her new problems.
cases.exam: Cushingoid facies with mild facial rounding and supraclavicular fullness; thin skin with several ecchymoses on the forearms. Proximal muscle weakness: 4/5 hip flexors and shoulder abductors bilaterally with preserved distal strength (5/5); she rises from the chair only by pushing with her arms. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; sensation intact; no fasciculations. No focal cranial nerve deficit; speech normal. Lungs clear; abdomen soft with central adiposity; lower-extremity edema mild. No oral thrush.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her proximal weakness with intact sensation and reflexes, in the setting of chronic dexamethasone, is most consistent with which complication?
